**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** multi-chain (any chain the affected wallet supports)
**First documented:** systematic 2023 onward; canonical case Atomic Wallet June 2023
**Aliases:** "wallet supply-chain attack", "trojanised wallet update", "self-custodial wallet compromise"

## Description

A class of attacks in which the wallet software *itself* is compromised — at build time, at distribution time, or via a malicious update channel — such that a large fraction of the wallet's end-user installation base produces extraction events without any individual user-side compromise. The on-chain manifestation is a coordinated burst of extractions across thousands of distinct end-user wallets within a short window. The defender-side question is structurally different from T11.001 (vendor compromise affects organisational customer): T11.002's victims are individual self-custodial users whose security model assumed the wallet software they installed was authentic.

T11.002 is distinct from T4 (Access Acquisition) because the user did *not* sign any phishing-driven authority grant; the wallet itself is malicious or compromised, and the entry vector is the user's reasonable trust in their installed wallet software. It is also distinct from T11.001 because the affected entity is the wallet vendor's distribution channel, not a third-party signing platform used by an organisational customer.

## Observed indicators

- Coordinated burst of extraction events across thousands of distinct end-user wallet addresses, all running the same wallet software, within a short window.
- Source-cluster of the extracted funds traces back to a small set of attacker-controlled destination addresses.
- Vendor-side disclosure of build-pipeline compromise, malicious update, or compromised distribution channel at the affected wallet vendor.
- Litigation / class-action filings by affected end users targeting the wallet vendor — public-record artefact even in cases where the technical cause is not fully publicly disclosed.

## Detection signals

- Real-time on-chain: cluster outflow events by wallet-software fingerprint (transaction-construction conventions can sometimes identify the originating wallet); alert on burst patterns of structurally-similar extractions across thousands of distinct senders.
- Vendor-side: build-pipeline integrity monitoring; signed-binary verification at distribution time; user-side checksum verification for installed wallet binaries.
- Off-chain CTI: monitor wallet-vendor disclosure channels; cross-reference user-reported losses on social media against on-chain extraction patterns.

## Real-world examples

- **Atomic Wallet — multi-chain — 2023-06-03** — Loss \~\$35M initial / \~\$100M+ total across approximately 5,500 compromised end-user wallets. Elliptic attributed the incident to OAK-G01 Lazarus Group on June 6, 2023; FBI confirmed the DPRK / TraderTraitor attribution subsequently. The technical cause was not fully disclosed publicly, but per public reporting and the litigation record, the wallet software itself was the vector — affected users had not interacted with phishing dApps or signed unauthorised payloads. Funds were laundered through Garantex (Russian sanctioned exchange) post-extraction. Class-action lawsuit filed against Atomic Wallet and its proprietor. See worked example at `examples/2023-06-atomic-wallet.md`. Source: `[ellipticatomic2023]`.
- **Ledger Connect Kit — npm supply-chain — 2023-12-14** — Loss \~\$600K. The Ledger Connect Kit JavaScript library, used by hundreds of dApps to integrate Ledger hardware-wallet flows, was compromised when an attacker phished credentials from a former Ledger employee with access to the npm package. The attacker published malicious versions (1.1.5, 1.1.6, 1.1.7) that injected drainer code into any dApp that loaded the affected versions during the ~5-hour window. This is a *supply-chain* T11.002 sub-case where the compromised software is a wallet-integration library rather than a wallet binary, but the failure mode is structurally identical (user trust in installed/embedded wallet-related code). See worked example at `examples/2023-12-ledger-connect-kit.md`. Sources: industry post-mortems published by Ledger and dApp partners.
- **3CX VOIP-app supply-chain — 2023-03-29** — Cross-context T11.002-adjacent: a corporate VOIP application's build pipeline was compromised by OAK-G01 / TraderTraitor in a multi-stage chain (`[mandiant3cx2023]`). The downstream attack surface included financial-sector employees with crypto access, and the case is canonically documented as the precursor to the Bybit-class custody-vendor compromise pattern. Not a wallet-software case per se but informs the same defensive baseline (signed binaries, build-pipeline integrity).
- **BeaverTail / InvisibleFerret npm supply-chain — 2023 onward** — DPRK-attributed Contagious Interview / Wagemole campaign distributing malware via npm packages disguised as legitimate developer tooling. The malware (`[unit42beavertail2023]`) targets developer machines that hold crypto-wallet credentials and signing keys; secondary effect is wallet-software-adjacent compromise via developer-environment access. Per Unit 42 and SecurityScorecard reporting, the campaign continues at v0.1 with rolling new package names. Adjacent rather than canonical T11.002 — the affected software is developer tooling, not the wallet binary itself.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2017-06-bithumb.md`](../examples/2017-06-bithumb.md) — Bithumb employee-laptop compromise + downstream phishing wave
- [`examples/2017-12-nicehash.md`](../examples/2017-12-nicehash.md) — NiceHash mining-marketplace wallet compromise
- [`examples/2021-04-easyfi.md`](../examples/2021-04-easyfi.md) — EasyFi admin-key compromise
- [`examples/2022-08-slope-phantom.md`](../examples/2022-08-slope-phantom.md) — Slope Wallet (Solana)
- [`examples/2022-12-ankr.md`](../examples/2022-12-ankr.md) — Ankr deployer-key compromise and malicious upgrade
- [`examples/2023-08-steadefi.md`](../examples/2023-08-steadefi.md) — Steadefi deployer-key compromise and malicious-upgrade extraction
- [`examples/2024-01-concentric.md`](../examples/2024-01-concentric.md) — Concentric Finance multisig signing-key social-engineering compromise
- [`examples/2024-05-gala-games.md`](../examples/2024-05-gala-games.md) — Gala Games admin-key compromise emergency-recovery
- [`examples/2024-06-coinstats-snap.md`](../examples/2024-06-coinstats-snap.md) — CoinStats MetaMask-Snap-related compromise
- [`examples/2024-06-holograph.md`](../examples/2024-06-holograph.md) — Holograph deployer-key compromise unlimited-mint-and-dump
- [`examples/2024-06-loopring.md`](../examples/2024-06-loopring.md) — Loopring Smart Wallet 2FA-bypass guardian-recovery exploit
- [`examples/2024-06-uwu-bittensor.md`](../examples/2024-06-uwu-bittensor.md) — Bittensor `bittensor` PyPI supply-chain coldkey-exfiltration compromise
- [`examples/2024-08-bittensor-coldkey-cohort.md`](../examples/2024-08-bittensor-coldkey-cohort.md) — Bittensor PyPI compromise
- [`examples/2024-08-nexera.md`](../examples/2024-08-nexera.md) — Nexera (formerly AllianceBlock) NXRA proxy-admin compromise
- [`examples/2024-09-deltaprime.md`](../examples/2024-09-deltaprime.md) — DeltaPrime admin-key compromise + malicious upgrade
- [`examples/2024-10-tapioca.md`](../examples/2024-10-tapioca.md) — Tapioca DAO DSO logic flaw + key compromise
- [`examples/2025-02-infini.md`](../examples/2025-02-infini.md) — Infini neobank treasury drain
- [`examples/2025-04-zksync-airdrop.md`](../examples/2025-04-zksync-airdrop.md) — ZKsync unclaimed-airdrop pool drain
- [`examples/2025-09-uxlink.md`](../examples/2025-09-uxlink.md) — UXLINK multisig delegate-call hijack
- [`examples/2026-01-polymarket-trader-tooling-supply-chain.md`](../examples/2026-01-polymarket-trader-tooling-supply-chain.md) — Polymarket trader-tooling supply-chain compromise
- [`examples/2024-12-solana-web3js-npm-supply-chain.md`](../examples/2024-12-solana-web3js-npm-supply-chain.md) — `@solana/web3.js` v1.95.6/1.95.7 backdoor (CVE-2024-54134) via npm-publisher credential phishing
- [`examples/2025-03-solflare-base-x-homograph-cve.md`](../examples/2025-03-solflare-base-x-homograph-cve.md) — Solflare / `base-x` Unicode-homograph CVE-2025-27611 (six-year-undetected wallet-library address-validation bypass)

### Adjacent cohorts (proposed T11.x sub-classes — see TAXONOMY-GAPS.md)

- [`examples/2022-12-lastpass-vault-cohort.md`](../examples/2022-12-lastpass-vault-cohort.md) — LastPass encrypted-vault exfiltration → multi-year crypto-drain cohort. **Adjacent cohort, not T11.002 proper** — third-party-storage-service-at-rest seed-phrase exfiltration; proposed T11.x cold-storage-seed-phrase-exfiltration-at-rest sub-class.
- [`examples/2022-04-icloud-metamask-seed-phrase-cohort.md`](../examples/2022-04-icloud-metamask-seed-phrase-cohort.md) — iCloud-backup MetaMask seed-phrase cohort. **Adjacent cohort, not T11.002 proper** — implicit-cloud-custody via default-on iOS Backup; proposed T11.x cold-storage-seed-phrase-exfiltration-at-rest sub-class.
- [`examples/2020-01-trezor-kraken-rdp-downgrade.md`](../examples/2020-01-trezor-kraken-rdp-downgrade.md) — Trezor RDP-downgrade voltage-glitch seed extraction (Kraken Security Labs disclosure). **Adjacent cohort, not T11.002 proper** — physical-access hardware-side seed extraction (capability anchor); proposed T11.x hardware-wallet-supply-chain / physical-access compromise sub-class.
- [`examples/2025-counterfeit-ledger-nano-s-plus-cohort.md`](../examples/2025-counterfeit-ledger-nano-s-plus-cohort.md) — Counterfeit Ledger Nano S Plus hardware-wallet supply-chain compromise cohort. **Adjacent cohort, not T11.002 proper** — brand-impersonation-driven hardware substitution (deployed-attack anchor); proposed T11.x hardware-wallet-supply-chain / physical-access compromise sub-class.
- [`examples/2023-2026-fake-firmware-update-phishing-cohort.md`](../examples/2023-2026-fake-firmware-update-phishing-cohort.md) — Fake hardware-wallet firmware-update / recovery-app phishing cohort. **Adjacent cohort, not T11.002 proper** — brand-trust-leveraged active phishing for seed-phrase exfiltration; proposed T11.x hardware-wallet-supply-chain / physical-access compromise sub-class.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1.
- Vendor coverage: limited. Wallet-vendor-side build-pipeline integrity monitoring; user-side binary-verification tooling; on-chain cluster-attribution providers for post-event tracking.

## Mitigations

- **Wallet vendors (highest-leverage):** signed-binary distribution with hardware-security-module-backed signing keys; reproducible builds; runtime integrity verification of installed binary against signed reference; separation of build pipeline from update-distribution pipeline; mandatory breach-disclosure SLA in user agreements.
- **End users:** install wallet software only from canonical sources (vendor-published checksums, signed binaries); prefer hardware-wallet integrations for high-balance holdings (T11.002 cannot extract from a hardware wallet that requires per-transaction user confirmation, even if the host wallet software is compromised — provided the hardware wallet's display shows the actual destination).
- **Regulators / certification bodies:** wallet-software certification programs that require build-pipeline integrity, signed binaries, and breach-disclosure SLAs as a precondition for certification.

## Citations

- `[ellipticatomic2023]` — Atomic Wallet primary forensic write-up.
- `[chainalysis2024dprk]` — broader OAK-G01 context.

## Discussion

T11.002 is the canonical "self-custodial does not equal self-secured" case study. End users of self-custodial wallets typically reason that they hold their own keys and therefore are not exposed to centralised-custody breach risk; T11.002 demonstrates that the wallet software that holds those keys is itself a single point of failure if compromised. The structural lesson — hardware-wallet integration for high-balance holdings — has been widely absorbed by the industry post-Atomic-Wallet, but the T11.002 attack surface remains for users who do not use hardware wallets.

A live debate is whether wallet vendors should bear additional regulatory or contractual obligations for build-pipeline integrity. OAK does not opine; it documents that the attack class is real, the affected end users have legal recourse via class action (slow), and the operational-security layer (signed binaries, reproducible builds, certified distribution) is the highest-leverage mitigation surface.
docs.techniques/T11.003-multisig-contract-man:
# OAK-T11.003 — In-Use Multisig Smart-Contract Manipulation

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T11 (Custody and Signing Infrastructure), OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit) — partial cross-classification because the on-chain artefact is a contract modification
**Maturity:** observed
**Chains:** EVM
**First documented:** 2024 (WazirX canonical case)
**Aliases:** "multisig hijack", "in-flight multisig modification"

## Description

A class of attacks in which an attacker, having obtained access to one or more signers of an in-use custodial multisig wallet, uses that access not merely to withdraw funds but to **modify the smart contract controlling the multisig itself** — for example, by changing the signer set, replacing the multisig's logic with a copy that requires only the attacker's signature, or upgrading a proxy to point at attacker-controlled implementation logic. Once the contract has been modified, subsequent extractions require no further signatures from the legitimate signer set. T11.003 is a sub-case of T11.001 (the entry vector is typically a third-party-vendor compromise that gives the attacker partial signing authority) but is documented as a standalone Technique because the on-chain artefact and the post-event mitigation are distinct.

The defender's signal lives at the *contract-modification event* on-chain: a multisig that was operating with a 3-of-5 + 1 threshold and then suddenly accepts unilateral signatures has been modified, and that modification event itself is detectable as a contract-state change.

## Observed indicators

- A multisig contract upgrade or signer-set modification event that is *not* preceded by a publicly-announced governance / operational-change rationale from the operator.
- Subsequent extraction transactions that satisfy the new (attacker-modified) authorisation predicate but that would not have satisfied the prior predicate.
- Off-chain disclosure from the affected operator that the multisig was hijacked rather than merely drained — typically lagging the on-chain artefact by hours.

## Detection signals

- Real-time on-chain: monitor for multisig-modification or proxy-upgrade events at known custodial-multisig contracts; alert on modifications that are not paired with the operator's published change-control announcements.
- Pre-event: maintain an inventory of high-value custodial-multisig contracts with their published authorisation predicates; treat any modification event as a candidate T11.003 incident pending operator-side disclosure.
- Cross-reference: combine T11.003 modification-event monitoring with T7.003 cross-chain bridge laundering monitoring to surface end-to-end attack chains within hours of the contract-modification event.

## Real-world examples

- **WazirX / Liminal multisig — Ethereum — 2024-07-18** — Loss \~\$234.9M. WazirX's multisig used a 3-of-5 + 1 authorisation predicate (5 WazirX signers + 1 Liminal Custody signer; 3 WazirX + 1 Liminal required to authorise transactions). The attackers obtained partial multisig access via the third-party custody surface (T11.001 entry vector), then **altered the smart contract controlling the multisig** so that further extractions did not require additional WazirX signatures. Once the contract was modified, the attackers drained the wallet without further WazirX involvement. OAK-G01 Lazarus Group attribution via global analysis. WazirX subsequently froze trading and entered restructuring; users received \~85% recovery via the restructuring scheme by October 2025. See worked example at `examples/2024-07-wazirx.md` (pending v0.x). Sources: `[wazirxwiki2024]`, `[crystalwazirx2024]`.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2026-04-drift-protocol-durable-nonces-dprk.md`](../examples/2026-04-drift-protocol-durable-nonces-dprk.md) — Drift Protocol durable-nonces DPRK exploit (\$285M); Solana-native variant where Security-Council members were tricked into pre-signing durable-nonce transactions that subsequently transferred admin authority — structurally analogous to multisig-contract-manipulation in that the on-chain authority surface is reshaped after the social-engineering event, but the manipulation primitive is Solana's `durable-nonces` rather than EVM contract-level upgrade. Listed here as the closest available v0.1 anchor; canonical home may shift to a Solana-native sub-Technique in a future revision.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1.
- Vendor coverage: protocol-monitoring services with custodial-multisig watchlists (Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender, BlockSec PhalconHQ); custom organisational SOC integrations.

## Mitigations

- **Custodial multisig operators:** governance / change-control review for any contract-modification event; mandatory delay between modification proposal and modification execution; runtime-invariant monitoring that pauses operations on modification-event detection pending out-of-band verification.
- **Third-party custody vendors:** the T11.003 entry vector is typically T11.001 (vendor compromise giving partial multisig access); mitigations at the vendor layer are the same as T11.001 mitigations.
- **Risk teams:** cross-reference custodial-multisig modification events against the operator's published change-control history; treat any unannounced modification as a P0 incident pending operator-side disclosure.

## Citations

- `[wazirxwiki2024]` — Wikipedia summary of the WazirX hack including the multisig-modification mechanism.
- `[crystalwazirx2024]` — Crystal Intelligence forensic analysis of the attack vector.
- `[chainalysis2024dprk]` — broader OAK-G01 context.

## Discussion

T11.003 is structurally important because it changes the defender's reasoning about *what an in-use multisig protects against*. A naive risk model treats a multisig as protecting against any single-signer compromise via the threshold predicate; T11.003 demonstrates that a sufficient-signer compromise plus contract-modification capability collapses the threshold predicate entirely. Contributors writing future T11.003 examples should preserve the contract-modification event as the canonical detection-and-attribution surface — once the contract is modified, the on-chain transaction record reads as legitimate, and only the modification-event itself is the unambiguous attribution signal.

A live design question for custodial-multisig vendors is whether modification authority should be removed from the multisig itself and delegated to a separate, slower-pace governance contract with a mandatory delay. Several post-WazirX custody-vendor architectures have adopted this pattern; OAK does not endorse a specific architecture but documents the design choice as a primary T11.003 mitigation surface.
docs.techniques/T11.005-operator-side-fake-pl:
# OAK-T11.005 — Operator-side Fake-Platform Fraud

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T11 (Custody and Signing Infrastructure)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** chain-agnostic (deposit substrate is BTC / ETH / stablecoins on-chain; the fraud "platform" is off-chain operator-controlled UI/database)
**First documented:** 2014 (OneCoin) at the modern threshold; pre-pig-butchering-era (2014–2018) MLM-Ponzi cohort plus 2018-onward multi-asset-wallet Ponzi plus 2020-onward fake-CEX / pig-butchering cohort
**Aliases:** "fake-CEX", "pig-butchering platform", "fake custodian", "investment-fraud platform", "MLM fake-cryptocurrency Ponzi", "rug platform"

## Description

End-to-end operator-controlled platform fraud where the *platform itself* is the vehicle of theft — a fake centralised exchange, fake custodial wallet / asset manager, fake yield product, or pig-butchering investment site whose deposit-acceptance, balance-display, and withdrawal-blocking are all operator-controlled UI/database fictions. There is no real exchange backend, no real custody, no segregated client funds. The operator collects retail deposits in BTC / ETH / stablecoins, displays fictitious balance growth in a database-backed UI, and rejects withdrawals (or extracts a "tax" / "verification fee" before further blocking) until the cohort exits.

T11.005 is **structurally distinct** from adjacent custody-and-signing-class Techniques and rug-pull classes:

- **T11.001** (Third-Party Signing-Vendor UI / Signing-Flow Compromise) — the *real* platform's vendor surface is breached and the customer organisation is the on-chain victim. T11.005 inverts this: there is no real platform; the platform itself is the fraud.
- **T11.002** (Wallet-Software Distribution Compromise) — substituted binary on a real wallet brand. T11.005's platform never had a benign predecessor.
- **T11.003** (In-Use Multisig Smart-Contract Manipulation) — manipulation of a real on-chain multisig. T11.005 typically operates without any on-chain protocol primitive at all.
- **T5.005** (Treasury-Management Exit) — a real operator's exit on a real DeFi platform; the operator had a fiduciary relationship with the protocol. T11.005 had no such relationship.
- **T4 phishing** — phishing acquires access to a *real* user's holdings on a *real* platform. T11.005's users deposit voluntarily into an operator-controlled wallet they believe to be a legitimate exchange / custodian.
- **T2.x rug-pull tokens** — token-side liquidity-establishment fraud. T11.005's vehicle is a *platform*, not a token; there is no AMM pool, no token contract, often no on-chain protocol whatsoever; the deposit destination is a single operator-controlled wallet and the "balance" is a Postgres row.

The cohort aggregates the **largest retail-attack surface in crypto by aggregated dollar loss**. FBI IC3 reported \$5.8B in pig-butchering-class losses in the U.S. alone in 2024; Chainalysis estimates pig-butchering revenue grew approximately 40% YoY in 2024.

T11.005 has three structurally-distinct sub-patterns plus one historical-anchor framing:

- **T11.005.001 — Fake-CEX / pig-butchering platform.** No real exchange backend; balance display and "trade" functionality are entirely UI-side fictions; withdrawals are blocked or gated behind escalating "tax" / "verification fee" demands; the deposit destination is a single operator-controlled wallet. Anchored in the 2020–2026 BeurAx-class cohort and the DOJ June 2025 Philippines compound \$225M USDT seizure / DOJ October 2025 Chen Zhi / Prince Group \$15B Bitcoin forfeiture (the largest in U.S. history).
- **T11.005.002 — Fake-custodian / fake-asset-manager fraud.** The operator presents an MLM "wealth management" or "AI-trading" yield product backed by a custodial wallet they control; the on-chain receivables are real but the trading / yield generation is fictitious; payouts to early entrants are funded entirely from later entrants' deposits (Ponzi structure). Anchored across PlusToken (2018-2019), HyperVerse / HyperFund (DOJ + SEC 2024), Forsage (SEC 2022), CryptoFX (SEC 2024), CoinDeal-Chandran (SEC + DOJ 2023).
- **T11.005.003 — Compound-operated investment-fraud platforms.** The fake-CEX / fake-investment-platform pattern of T11.005.001 distributed at industrial scale through forced-labour scam compounds in Southeast Asia (Cambodia, Burma / Myanmar, Laos), with romance-scam feeder narrative as the user-acquisition surface. Anchors: Chen Zhi / Prince Group (DOJ October 2025); Ly Yong Phat / O-Smach Resort cohort (OFAC September 2024); Huione Group laundering substrate (FinCEN section 311 final rule October 2025).
- **Historical anchor (pre-pig-butchering era):** OneCoin (Ruja Ignatova / Karl Sebastian Greenwood, 2014–2017, ~\$4B+; FBI Ten Most Wanted June 2022; Greenwood guilty December 2022, 20-year sentence September 2023). The OneCoin case anchors the pre-2018-pig-butchering era and demonstrates that the fake-platform structural class has a continuous lineage from MLM-token Ponzi (2014–2017) through Telegram-recruited multi-asset wallet Ponzi (2018–2019) into pig-butchering fake-CEX (2020–2026).

## Observed indicators

- **Off-chain (operator-side):** no verifiable corporate registration in the claimed jurisdiction; no audit; no proof-of-reserves; high-yield "guaranteed return" claims; no public order book; no observable on-chain trading flow consistent with claimed AUM; brand-impersonation of a legitimate exchange or custodian; MLM-style referral commissions baked into the user-acquisition flow.
- **On-chain:** deposit-aggregation pattern into a single or small number of operator wallets without observable matched-trading outflow; large fan-in concentration consistent with custodial-Ponzi receivables rather than exchange operational flow.
- **User-side:** un-fillable withdrawal requests; "tax" / "verification fee" / "anti-money-laundering deposit" preconditions for withdrawal; communication-channel collapse after exit.
- **Receivables-fingerprint:** pre-2018 cases launder predominantly through correspondent banking (OneCoin's "Fenero Funds" Cayman vehicles; ~\$400M routed via BNY Mellon / Bank of Ireland) because receivables were fiat; 2020-onward cases launder predominantly on-chain (mixers, cross-chain bridges, Russian-CEX off-ramps, Huione Group laundering substrate) because receivables are crypto.

## Detection signals

- **Off-chain OSINT:** regulator-warning subscription (FCA, SEC, KNF, SFC, BaFin, ASIC published warnings on unlicensed platforms); investigative-journalism cross-reference (BehindMLM, ICAC, BBC *Missing Cryptoqueen*-class long-form investigative reporting); corporate-registry verification in the claimed-headquarters jurisdiction; absence of a public blockchain explorer when the platform claims to operate one.
- **On-chain forensics:** deposit-aggregation-without-trading-outflow signal; cross-cluster overlap with Huione Group / Cambodia-compound laundering substrate; aggregated victim-deposit fan-in to a small wallet set.
- **User-side defensive signal:** small test-withdrawal precedes any meaningful deposit. The "if you cannot withdraw a small test amount before depositing more, the platform is a fake-CEX" rule is the canonical user-side mitigation.
- **Retail-bank-side debit-card monitoring** for known scam-platform deposit destinations is the dominant *cohort-level* defender intervention. The FBI's Operation Level Up (initiated January 2024) had notified 5,831+ victims and prevented \$359M+ in losses by April 2025.

## Real-world examples

- **OneCoin — global — 2014–2017 (operating) / 2017-onward (federal action)** — Loss approximately \$4B+ alleged across approximately 3.5M investors per DOJ SDNY indictment / Greenwood plea. The canonical pre-pig-butchering-era anchor. SQL-database-backed centralised ledger marketed as a public blockchain-based cryptocurrency; no public blockchain ever existed. Greenwood guilty plea December 2022, 20-year sentence September 2023; Ignatova FBI Top Ten Most Wanted (June 2022) with \$5M State Department reward (June 2024); Mark Scott convicted November 2019 of laundering ~\$400M of OneCoin proceeds through "Fenero Funds" Cayman vehicles. DOJ opened OneCoin victim-claims process April 2026 (~\$40M restitution fund). See [`examples/2014-2017-onecoin-ignatova.md`](../examples/2014-2017-onecoin-ignatova.md).
- **CryptoFX — Mauricio Chavez — Texas / Latino community — 2020–2022 (operating) / 2022-onward (SEC / DOJ action)** — SEC charged ~\$300M Latino-community-targeted MLM fake-investment scheme. The canonical T11.005.002 fake-custodian / fake-asset-manager fraud worked example for the Spanish-language affinity-fraud sub-cohort. See [`examples/2022-09-cryptofx-chavez.md`](../examples/2022-09-cryptofx-chavez.md).
- **HyperVerse / HyperFund — Sam Lee — global — 2020–2022 (operating) / 2024-onward (DOJ + SEC action)** — \$1.7–1.9B alleged across the global cohort per DOJ + SEC January 2024 enforcement actions. Fake-asset-manager / "AI-trading" yield product structured as MLM Ponzi; canonical T11.005.002 worked example for the global multi-language cohort. See [`examples/2024-01-hyperverse-hyperfund.md`](../examples/2024-01-hyperverse-hyperfund.md).
- **Cohort context** — FBI IC3 2024: \$5.8B reported pig-butchering-class losses (U.S. only). Chainalysis 2024: pig-butchering revenue +40% YoY. The cohort aggregates as the single largest retail-attack class in crypto by aggregated loss; per-victim recovery rates are low; the dominant recovery surface is DOJ civil forfeiture at cohort scale (Chen Zhi / Prince Group \$15B Bitcoin seizure October 2025; Philippines compound \$225M USDT seizure June 2025; OneCoin \$40M victim-claim fund April 2026).

### Adjacent / forthcoming worked-example anchors (TAXONOMY-GAPS-tracked)

The following per-incident worked examples are referenced from the TAXONOMY-GAPS T11.005 candidate framing and are pipeline targets for v0.x. They are not yet committed to `examples/` at v0.1 freeze:

- BeurAx-class fake-trading-platform cohort (2020–2026) — T11.005.001.
- Philippines compound \$225M USDT seizure (DOJ June 2025) — T11.005.001 / T11.005.003.
- Chen Zhi / Prince Group \$15B Bitcoin forfeiture (DOJ October 2025; largest forfeiture in U.S. history) — T11.005.003.
- Forsage / Lado Okhotnikov (SEC August 2022, ~\$300–340M) — T11.005.002 smart-contract-MLM-Ponzi sub-shape.
- CoinDeal / Neil Chandran (SEC + DOJ January 2023, ~\$45M) — T11.005.002.
- PlusToken (2018–2019, ~\$2–5.7B; existing OAK example at [`examples/2019-06-plus-token.md`](../examples/2019-06-plus-token.md)) — T11.005.002 multi-asset-wallet Ponzi sub-shape.
- JPEX (Hong Kong, September 2023, ~\$200M) — borderline T11.005.001 / unlicensed-offshore-exchange.
- Southeast Asia compound cohort (Cambodia / Myanmar / Laos, 2020–2026) — T11.005.003.
- Huione Group / FinCEN section 311 final rule (October 2025) — T11.005.003 laundering-substrate anchor.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T11.005's primary detection surface is off-chain OSINT (regulator-registry monitoring, corporate-registry verification, brand-impersonation surveillance), not the on-chain runtime risk-engine surface MG covers.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: Chainalysis Crypto Crime Report cohort tracking; FBI IC3 PSA feed; FBI Operation Level Up retail-bank coordination; FinCEN advisories (2023 pig-butchering alert; 2025 Huione Group section 311); regulator-published licensed-VASP registries (KNF, SFC, SEC, FCA, FinCEN MSB registry); investigative-journalism cohorts (BehindMLM, ICAC, BBC); on-chain analytics providers (TRM, Elliptic, Chainalysis Reactor) for cluster-attribution forensics.

## Mitigations

- **Regulator-side:** publication and continuous maintenance of licensed-VASP registries; cross-jurisdiction warning-feed coordination (KNF / SFC / SEC / FCA / FinCEN MSB / BaFin / ASIC); civil-forfeiture infrastructure at the cohort-recovery scale.
- **Platform-side warnings:** FBI IC3 PSAs; FinCEN 2023 pig-butchering alert; FinCEN 2025 Huione Group section 311 designation; CFTC retail-customer advisories; CISA / Treasury cyber-fraud joint advisories.
- **User-side:** small-test-withdrawal-before-deposit invariant; refusal to deposit to platforms without verifiable corporate registration / audit / proof-of-reserves; refusal to send "tax" or "verification fee" preconditional to withdrawal; cross-reference against published licensed-VASP registries before depositing.
- **Retail-bank-side:** debit-card monitoring for known scam-platform deposit destinations (FBI Operation Level Up coordination model — 5,831+ victims notified, \$359M+ saved by April 2025).
- **Off-chain CTI integration:** subscription to regulator-warning feeds; investigative-journalism cross-reference; corporate-registry verification automation for emerging platforms.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1 are a **gap** — no existing OAK-M* entry directly maps to the regulator-publication / retail-bank-coordination / off-chain-OSINT mitigation surface that T11.005 most leverages. v0.x candidate Mitigation entries: "OAK-M-x licensed-VASP-registry monitoring", "OAK-M-x retail-bank deposit-destination blocklist coordination", "OAK-M-x small-test-withdrawal-before-deposit invariant".

## Citations

- See worked examples for primary citations: `[doj2017ignatovaindictment]`, `[doj2023greenwoodsentencing]`, `[fbi2022ignatovamostwanted]`, `[state2024ignatovareward]`, `[doj2022greenwoodguiltyplea]`, `[coindesk2026onecoinvictims]`, `[bbc2019cryptoqueenpodcast]`, `[behindmlmonecoin]`.
- HyperVerse / HyperFund and CryptoFX worked examples carry the SEC + DOJ enforcement-record citation set.
- Cohort context: `[chainalysis2025rug]` and Chainalysis annual Crypto Crime Report 2024–2026 series; FBI IC3 annual Internet Crime Reports.

## Discussion

T11.005 is the structurally largest **retail-aggregate-loss** Technique class in OAK at v0.1 and the most under-served by v0.1 Tactic / Technique mapping. The cohort aggregates ~\$5.8B in U.S.-reported 2024 losses alone (FBI IC3) plus the broader global multiple of that figure; for comparison, T11.001 (Bybit + DMM + WazirX-class) federal-record dollar totals are an order of magnitude smaller in aggregated retail-victim count even where individual-incident totals are larger.

The Technique was held in TAXONOMY-GAPS through v0.1 because:

1. The class is "off-chain" in the load-bearing sense — the platform's database is the substrate of fraud, not an on-chain protocol — and earlier OAK drafts treated it as out-of-scope for an *on-chain* attack-knowledge framework.
2. The detection-and-mitigation surface lives at the regulator / retail-bank / off-chain-OSINT layer rather than the on-chain forensics layer that anchors most other OAK Techniques.
3. The cohort attribution is largely confirmed at the federal / regulator-record level (DOJ, SEC, CFTC, FCA, FinCEN, OFAC) rather than at the cluster-forensic level that anchors OAK-G actor profiles.

The promotion to `emerging` reflects that:

- The class is now multi-source-attested (DOJ, SEC, FBI IC3, FinCEN, OFAC, Chainalysis Crypto Crime Report, FCA, KNF, SFC, BBC investigative reporting).
- Multiple worked examples now anchor the class across distinct sub-patterns (OneCoin = T11.005.002 historical; CryptoFX = T11.005.002 affinity-fraud Latino; HyperVerse = T11.005.002 global-MLM; with a pipeline of T11.005.001 fake-CEX and T11.005.003 compound-operated cases tracked in TAXONOMY-GAPS for v0.x).
- The structural distinctions vs. T11.001 / T11.002 / T11.003 / T5.005 / T4 / T2.x are clean enough to support sub-Technique granularity.

`stable` will be appropriate once the v0.x worked-example pipeline (BeurAx-class, Chen Zhi / Prince Group, Philippines compound, Forsage, CoinDeal, PlusToken-as-T11.005.002-anchor, JPEX-as-borderline, Southeast Asia compound cohort, Huione Group laundering substrate) is committed under `examples/`.

The receivables-fingerprint observation (pre-2018 cases launder through correspondent banking; 2020-onward cases launder on-chain) is structurally important and recurs across the cohort. Contributors writing future T11.005 worked examples should preserve the receivables-fiat-vs-crypto dimension as a first-class observable. The laundering-chain fingerprint correlates with the era of the case rather than the structural class.

Operator-mobility-and-disappearance is a recurring T11.005 feature (Ignatova October 2017; Schneider May 2023; multiple Southeast Asian compound operators). Contributors should record (a) operator nationality, (b) jurisdiction footprint, (c) extradition-cooperation status with U.S. / EU / OECD jurisdictions, (d) post-incident operator-mobility events. Federal-record-confirmed attribution does not require operator-in-custody.

The realised-loss-versus-aggregate-claimed-loss gap is structurally informative. Per-victim recovery rates are typically <5%; the dominant recovery surface is DOJ civil-forfeiture at cohort scale. Contributors should report (a) realised recovery, (b) recovery surface (restitution fund vs. civil forfeiture vs. unrecovered), (c) aggregate-claimed-loss-to-realised-recovery ratio explicitly.
docs.techniques/T11.006-cold-storage-seed-phr:
# OAK-T11.006 — Cold-storage Seed-phrase Exfiltration at Rest

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T11 (Custody and Signing Infrastructure)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** chain-agnostic (the substrate-of-extraction is the BIP39 seed phrase / private key material itself; downstream extraction occurs across whichever chains the affected wallet holds)
**First documented:** 2022-04 (iCloud-backup MetaMask / Iacovone case) for the implicit-cloud-custody sub-pattern; 2022-12 (LastPass encrypted-vault exfiltration) for the user-initiated plaintext-storage sub-pattern; cohort window remains open at v0.1 reporting horizon
**Aliases:** "seed-phrase at rest exfiltration", "password-manager seed-storage compromise", "iCloud-backup wallet drain", "third-party-storage seed-phrase compromise"

## Description

Compromise of seed-phrase / private-key material held in third-party storage services where the user did not realise they had created a custody surface. The structural feature is *seed-phrase material at rest in a third-party storage substrate* whose security posture is independent of the wallet vendor and the user's on-chain hygiene. Compromise of the third-party service produces seed-phrase material to attackers without any direct user-action signing or wallet-software compromise.

T11.006 has **two structurally-distinct sub-patterns**:

- **T11.006.001 — User-initiated plaintext-equivalent storage.** The user explicitly stores seed-phrase material in a third-party service (LastPass Secure Notes; 1Password / Bitwarden Secure-Notes-equivalent; password-protected notes in productivity tools; email drafts; cloud-synced text files), with vault encryption typically bound to a master password whose strength varies across users. Compromise path: the third-party service's encrypted vaults are exfiltrated; attackers brute-force weak master passwords offline; the resulting plaintext yields seed phrases that are used to drain on-chain wallets. The exfiltration-to-drain time window is unbounded — once the encrypted material is in attacker hands, the brute-force window cannot be closed. Canonical: 2022-12 LastPass encrypted-vault exfiltration → 2023–2025 multi-year crypto-drain cohort, ~$35M+ aggregated across ~150+ victims plus ~$150M Larsen heist (federal-record-attested via March 2025 NDCA forfeiture filing); see [`examples/2022-12-lastpass-vault-cohort.md`](../examples/2022-12-lastpass-vault-cohort.md).

- **T11.006.002 — Implicit cloud-custody via default-on cloud-backup.** The wallet vendor's mobile app does not opt out of OS-level cloud-backup defaults, auto-syncing the encrypted vault to a cloud surface tied to a separate-identity security posture (Apple ID for iOS / iCloud; Google account for Android; Microsoft account for Windows). Compromise path: the user's cloud-account credential is compromised via SIM-swap, Apple-ID phishing, social-engineering of Apple Support, or shared-device theft; the attacker accesses the cloud-backup containing the encrypted wallet vault; the vault password is brute-forced offline if weak. Canonical: 2022-04 iCloud-backup MetaMask seed-phrase cohort (Iacovone case ~$650K-$655K plus broader 2022–2024 cohort; MetaMask issued public iOS-backup-disable security guidance two days after Iacovone's public disclosure); see [`examples/2022-04-icloud-metamask-seed-phrase-cohort.md`](../examples/2022-04-icloud-metamask-seed-phrase-cohort.md).

T11.006 is **structurally distinct** from adjacent T11 sub-Techniques:

- **T11.001** (Third-Party Signing-Vendor Compromise) — vendor-side UI / signing-flow compromise with organisational-customer victim shape (Bybit / Safe{Wallet}). T11.006's failure surface is third-party storage at rest; victims are individual end-users.
- **T11.002** (Wallet-Software Distribution Compromise) — vendor-side build-pipeline / update-channel surface (Atomic Wallet). T11.006 does not require any wallet-software compromise; the wallet binary is genuine.
- **T11.003** (In-Use Multisig Smart-Contract Manipulation) — contract-modification of a real on-chain multisig. T11.006 operates entirely off-chain at the storage-service layer.
- **T4 phishing** — phishing acquires on-chain authority signing on a real platform. T11.006's substrate-of-extraction is the seed phrase itself, recovered offline from third-party storage; no on-chain authority signing is required and no user-action signing event correlates with the extraction.

The legitimate-use overlap is **partial**: password managers and cloud-backup are legitimate user-side tools for non-crypto credentials. The class-specific failure mode is the *combination* of (a) seed-phrase material stored in a third-party service and (b) the third-party service's compromise. The user-side mental model "I'm self-custodial because I hold my own keys" collapses when the seed-phrase material is held by a third-party service whose security posture is outside the user's control.

## Observed indicators

- **Service-side:** breach disclosure or exfiltration event affecting a third-party storage service with non-trivial crypto-holding user fraction (LastPass Dec 2022; Apple ID phishing campaigns; Google / Microsoft account compromise cohorts).
- **Per-victim cohort fingerprint:** victims with no other commonality (no SIM swap, no email compromise, no mobile-phone malware, no on-chain phishing) other than having stored seed-phrase material in the same third-party service. Process-of-elimination cohort tracking by ZachXBT, Taylor Monahan, and TRM Labs has been the canonical methodology for the LastPass cohort.
- **Time-shifted drain pattern:** attack-vector-time-shift between the exfiltration event and the realised drains is a structural feature — the LastPass cohort's drains span 2023–2025 from a December 2022 exfiltration because brute-forcing weak master passwords offline takes time.
- **Apple-ID / Google-account compromise antecedent:** for the implicit-cloud-custody sub-pattern, the proximate compromise is at the cloud-account level (SIM-swap, Apple-ID phishing, support-call social-engineering), which precedes the wallet-vault decryption.

## Detection signals

- **User-side audit:** enumerate where seed-phrase material has been stored — password manager Secure Notes, cloud-synced text files, email drafts, screenshots in cloud-synced photo galleries, browser-saved passwords.
- **Wallet-vendor audit:** verify whether the wallet's mobile app opts out of OS-level cloud-backup (iOS `UIDocument.disableBackup` / `NSURLIsExcludedFromBackupKey`; Android `allowBackup=false`). Wallet-vendor cloud-backup behaviour varies across vendors and OS versions.
- **Cohort-tracking (per-victim process-of-elimination):** ZachXBT / Taylor Monahan / TRM Labs methodology — interviewing affected victims about non-storage attack vectors and finding none, producing the storage-commonality observation independently of vendor-side disclosure.
- **Federal-record signals:** forfeiture filings naming a third-party-storage commonality (March 2025 NDCA Larsen filing) are the slow-but-strong attribution signal; industry-forensic attribution typically lands 12–18 months earlier.

## Real-world examples

- **LastPass encrypted-vault exfiltration → multi-year crypto-drain cohort — multi-chain — 2022-12 (breach) / 2023-2025 (drains) (canonical T11.006.001 case)** — At least ~$35M aggregated across ~150+ individual victims tracked by ZachXBT, Taylor Monahan (MetaMask), and TRM Labs through 2023–2025; the single largest documented victim is Ripple co-founder Chris Larsen, ~$150M (January 2024, federally tied to the LastPass breach in a March 2025 U.S. forfeiture filing). TRM Labs traced over ~$28M converted to BTC and laundered through Wasabi Wallet's CoinJoin in late-2024 / early-2025 plus a further ~$7M wave in September 2025. Cohort attribution: `inferred-strong` at the operator-cluster level (Russian-cybercriminal infrastructure, OAK-G03-adjacent; on-chain off-ramps include Cryptex which is OFAC-sanctioned for ransomware-related laundering); LastPass / GoTo continues to publicly dispute the causal link. See [`examples/2022-12-lastpass-vault-cohort.md`](../examples/2022-12-lastpass-vault-cohort.md).
- **iCloud-backup MetaMask seed-phrase cohort — multi-chain (EVM-dominant) — 2022-04 onward (canonical T11.006.002 case)** — Canonical anchor case ~$650K-$655K (Dominic Iacovone — NFT collector — April 15, 2022). Attack onset: Apple ID phishing (spoofed Apple Support call requesting a six-digit verification code); attacker accessed iCloud Backup containing the MetaMask vault; brute-forced the vault password offline; drained ~$650K-$655K in ETH, ERC-20 tokens, and NFTs. MetaMask issued public security guidance on April 17, 2022 advising iOS users to disable iCloud Backup for MetaMask app data. Broader cohort losses through 2022–2024 not centrally tabulated but documented in MetaMask security-team's public guidance and per-victim Reddit / Twitter testimonies. Attribution: `pseudonymous` at the per-incident operator-cluster level. See [`examples/2022-04-icloud-metamask-seed-phrase-cohort.md`](../examples/2022-04-icloud-metamask-seed-phrase-cohort.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T11.006's primary detection surface is off-chain at the storage-service / cloud-account layer, not the on-chain runtime risk-engine surface MG covers.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: industry-forensic per-victim cohort tracking (ZachXBT, Taylor Monahan / MetaMask security team, TRM Labs); breach-tracking and have-I-been-pwned-class services for storage-service compromise notification; Apple / Google account-security advisories; wallet-vendor security advisories on cloud-backup behaviour (MetaMask iOS-backup-disable guidance is the canonical example). No widely-deployed runtime detector observes the seed-phrase-at-rest surface as of v0.1.

## Mitigations

- **User-side seed-phrase storage hygiene (highest-leverage):** cold storage on physical media (paper, metal); no third-party-service storage of seed-phrase material; no auto-synced cloud-storage including iCloud / Google Drive / OneDrive / Dropbox; no browser-saved-passwords or password-manager Secure-Notes-equivalent for seed phrases.
- **Wallet-vendor responsibility:** opt out of OS-level cloud-backup at the app level (iOS `UIDocument.disableBackup` / `NSURLIsExcludedFromBackupKey`; Android `allowBackup=false`); document the cloud-backup posture in user-facing security guidance; issue immediate post-incident advisories when cloud-backup-as-implicit-custody surface is exploited (MetaMask's April 2022 response is the canonical pattern).
- **Seed-phrase rotation invariant:** if seed-phrase material was *ever* stored in a third-party storage service whose vaults have been exfiltrated, rotate the seed phrase regardless of how strong the master password / encryption was thought to be at the time of storage. The brute-force window is unbounded once the encrypted material is in attacker hands.
- **Cloud-account hygiene:** strong, unique cloud-account passwords; hardware-backed two-factor authentication (not SMS-based); awareness of Apple Support / Google Support social-engineering vectors that solicit verification codes via spoofed-caller-ID calls.
- **Per-victim cohort methodology adoption:** for industry forensics — process-of-elimination cohort analysis (ZachXBT / Taylor Monahan methodology) can establish attribution even when vendor-side disclosure is constrained or actively disputed.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1 are a **gap** — no existing OAK-M* entry directly maps to the seed-phrase-storage-hygiene mitigation surface that T11.006 most leverages. v0.x candidate Mitigation entries: "OAK-M-x cold-storage seed-phrase invariant"; "OAK-M-x wallet-app cloud-backup opt-out invariant"; "OAK-M-x seed-phrase rotation post-third-party-breach trigger".

## Citations

- See worked examples for primary citations.
- LastPass cohort: `[krebslastpass2023]`, `[krebslastpass2025]`, `[trmlabslastpass2025]`, `[zachxbtlastpass2023]`, `[theblocklastpass2023]`, `[bleepinglastpass2025]`, `[hackernewslastpass2025]`, `[infosecuritylastpass2023]`, `[cointelegraphlarsen2024]`, `[lastpassbreachdisclosure2022]`.
- iCloud cohort: see `examples/2022-04-icloud-metamask-seed-phrase-cohort.md` for the citation set covering MetaMask's April 2022 security advisory, Iacovone's public disclosure, and industry-press secondary coverage.

## Discussion

T11.006 closes a structural gap in OAK v0.1's T11 coverage. The dominant v0.1 T11 worked examples (Bybit / Safe{Wallet} for T11.001, Atomic Wallet for T11.002, WazirX for T11.003) all share an *organisational-customer-or-vendor-side compromise* shape; T11.006 introduces a **third-party storage-service-at-rest** failure surface that is structurally distinct and that the v0.1 T11.001 / T11.002 / T11.003 sub-Techniques do not cover.

The structural OAK lesson is that **a third-party service that stores plaintext-equivalent seed-phrase material at rest is a custody surface the user did not realise they had created**. The user's mental model of self-custody — "I hold my own keys" — collapses when the seed-phrase material is stored in a third-party service whose security posture is outside the user's control. The lesson generalises beyond LastPass / iCloud: any third-party service that stores plaintext-equivalent seed-phrase material at rest (LastPass, 1Password, Bitwarden if Secure-Notes-equivalent is used, iCloud Notes / Drive, Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive, browser-saved passwords, screenshots in cloud-synced gallery, email drafts) constrains the user's effective custody posture to the security posture of the third-party service.

**Attack-vector-time-shift** is a structural feature of T11.006.001 specifically. The LastPass exfiltration event was December 2022 but realised drains span 2023–2025 because brute-forcing weak master passwords offline takes time. Defender practice for any user whose seed-phrase material was ever stored in a third-party service whose vaults were exfiltrated should treat the seed phrase as **permanently compromised** regardless of how strong the master password / encryption was at the time of storage. Seed-phrase rotation is the only operational response.

**Per-victim cohort attribution methodology** is the canonical forensic surface for T11.006. ZachXBT and Taylor Monahan's methodology — interviewing each victim about non-storage attack vectors (SIM swap, email compromise, mobile-phone malware, phishing) and finding none — produced the LastPass-commonality observation independently of any vendor-side disclosure. The pattern recurs across the T11 family where vendor-side disclosure is constrained by litigation strategy or organisational-reputation considerations; the per-victim cohort methodology is the cleanest forensic surface in those cases.

**Vendor-side denial-of-causation** is a recurring pattern. LastPass / GoTo continues to publicly dispute the causal link from the December 2022 vault exfiltration to the multi-year crypto-drain cohort. The pattern recurs across the T11 family (Atomic Wallet's constrained post-mortem disclosure under litigation pressure). Contributors writing future T11.006 worked examples should treat vendor-side disclosure as a first-class observable and should not under-weight industry-forensic attribution at the `inferred-strong` level when the federal record has not yet caught up.

The two sub-patterns differ at the **per-incident shape** layer. T11.006.001 (user-initiated storage) is opt-in by user action — the user chose to put seed-phrase material into the storage service. T11.006.002 (default-on cloud-backup) is opt-in by inaction — the user did nothing to opt out of an OS-level default that the wallet vendor also did not opt out of at the app level. Wallet-vendor app-data backup behaviour varies across vendors, and defender practice for any user with non-trivial holdings should include auditing the wallet app's cloud-backup behaviour explicitly.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once the per-incident worked-example anchor set expands beyond the current two cohort files. Pipeline anchors for v0.x include additional per-incident cases within the LastPass cohort with quantified loss data, and the broader 2022–2024 iCloud-backup cohort with cumulative-loss figures beyond the Iacovone case.
docs.techniques/T11.007-hardware-wallet-suppl:
# OAK-T11.007 — Hardware-wallet Supply-chain / Physical-access Compromise

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T11 (Custody and Signing Infrastructure)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** chain-agnostic (the substrate-of-extraction is the BIP39 seed phrase / hardware-wallet-controlled private-key material; downstream extraction occurs across whichever chains the affected wallet supports)
**First documented:** 2017 (early Ledger Nano S inserts cohort) at the cohort-shape layer; 2020-01-31 (Trezor One / Model T RDP-downgrade Kraken disclosure) for the physical-access capability anchor; 2025 counterfeit Ledger Nano S Plus cohort for the deployed-attack anchor; 2023–2026 fake-firmware-update / recovery-app phishing cohort for the active-phishing sub-pattern
**Aliases:** "counterfeit hardware wallet", "fake Ledger / fake Trezor", "pre-seeded recovery card", "hardware-wallet phishing", "physical-access seed extraction"

## Description

Brand-impersonation-driven hardware substitution, pre-seeded-recovery-card scams, physical-access seed-extraction attacks against hardware-wallet microcontrollers, and brand-trust-leveraged phishing targeting hardware-wallet users for seed-phrase exfiltration. The structural feature is *brand-impersonation-driven trust transferred to a structurally-compromised hardware / channel surface* — the legitimate-vendor's brand provides the social-engineering substrate that produces user-side trust in a hardware unit, communication, or companion-app that the legitimate vendor did not produce.

T11.007 has **three structurally-distinct sub-patterns**:

- **T11.007.001 — Counterfeit-hardware substitution.** Counterfeit devices distributed through informal retail channels (Chinese marketplaces — AliExpress / Taobao third-party sellers; Telegram-based resellers; in-person counterfeit-product markets; second-hand purchases without packaging) that brand-impersonate legitimate Ledger / Trezor / similar products. The counterfeit unit either pre-seeds the recovery phrase (the user funds a wallet whose seed the operator already knows) or replaces the secure element with an attacker-controlled microcontroller storing seeds in plaintext flash with embedded radio for exfiltration. Canonical (deployed-attack anchor): 2025 counterfeit Ledger Nano S Plus cohort (~$9.5M+ across ~50+ victims, 20+ blockchain ecosystems; ESP32-S3 microcontroller substitution with chip markings physically scraped off; antenna trace on PCB for 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi / BLE radio exfiltration; legitimate Ledger Nano S Plus is air-gapped by design); see [`examples/2025-counterfeit-ledger-nano-s-plus-cohort.md`](../examples/2025-counterfeit-ledger-nano-s-plus-cohort.md). Adjacent: 2021 mailed-fake-hardware-wallet replacement campaign leveraging the December 2020 Ledger customer-data leak.

- **T11.007.002 — Physical-access hardware-side seed extraction.** Voltage-glitching, side-channel, or chip-tampering attacks against the wallet's microcontroller / secure element, requiring physical access to the device. Canonical (capability anchor): 2020-01-31 Trezor One / Model T RDP-downgrade voltage-glitch attack disclosed by Kraken Security Labs; the underlying STM32F205 / STM32F427 microcontroller flaw is structural rather than patchable; vendor-acknowledged structural vulnerability. The BIP39 passphrase feature is the defender-side mitigation primitive that bounds realised loss to passphrase strength rather than preventing the attack outright (an attacker who extracts the seed obtains a seed that, without the passphrase, opens an empty decoy wallet). Newer Trezor products (Trezor Safe 3 / Safe 5, 2023–2024) ship with a dedicated EAL6+ certified secure element addressing the structural vulnerability at the hardware layer. See [`examples/2020-01-trezor-kraken-rdp-downgrade.md`](../examples/2020-01-trezor-kraken-rdp-downgrade.md).

- **T11.007.003 — Brand-trust-leveraged active phishing for seed-phrase exfiltration.** Phishing campaigns impersonating legitimate hardware-wallet vendor communications via email, physical mail, in-app prompts (via trojanised companion apps), and unsolicited mailed-hardware-replacement letters. The December 2020 Ledger customer-data breach (which leaked email + physical-address + phone-number data of ~273,000 customers) provides the operational substrate for personalisation data, increasing per-recipient success rates across the broader cohort. Sub-campaigns at v0.1: Ledger-data-breach-leveraged email phishing (2020 onward); Kaspersky / ESET 2023 spring cohort (85,000+ scam emails detected in a single quarter); Trezor-impersonating physical-mail-letter campaign (2025–2026; QR codes leading to fake `trezor.authentication-check.io` / `ledger.setuptransactioncheck.com` domains); trojanised-Ledger-Live / trojanised-Trezor-Suite companion-app cohort (2023 onward, infostealer-driven). See [`examples/2023-2026-fake-firmware-update-phishing-cohort.md`](../examples/2023-2026-fake-firmware-update-phishing-cohort.md).

T11.007 is **structurally distinct** from adjacent T11 sub-Techniques:

- **T11.002** (Wallet-Software Distribution Compromise) — substitutes the *software* the user runs on a legitimate device. T11.007.001 substitutes the *hardware itself*; T11.007.003 substitutes the *vendor communication channel* or the *companion-app code path*.
- **T11.006** (Cold-storage Seed-phrase Exfiltration at Rest) — third-party storage-service-at-rest failure surface (LastPass / iCloud-MetaMask). T11.007.003 is *active phishing-driven* seed-phrase solicitation rather than passive storage compromise.
- **T4 phishing** — the substrate-of-extraction in T4 is an on-chain authority grant (Permit2, allowance, setApprovalForAll). T11.007.003's substrate-of-extraction is the seed phrase itself.

## Observed indicators

- **Purchase channel:** hardware purchased through informal channels (Chinese-marketplace third-party sellers, Telegram resellers, in-person counterfeit-product markets, second-hand without packaging) rather than the vendor's authorised retail channels.
- **Tamper-evidence:** packaging without the vendor's tamper-evident seal (Ledger's tamper-evident packaging; Trezor's holographic seal); device behaviour during initialisation that does not match the vendor's documented onboarding flow.
- **Hardware fingerprints:** chip markings physically scraped off; antenna traces on a PCB for a device whose legitimate variant is air-gapped; visible solder rework or piggyback components.
- **Vendor-impersonation communications:** unsolicited emails / physical mail / in-app prompts requesting the user to "verify" / "update firmware" / "complete security upgrade" / "replace compromised device" by entering their seed phrase; QR codes leading to typosquat / near-miss domains; phone calls or chat-support requests soliciting the seed phrase.
- **Companion-app behaviour:** trojanised Ledger Live / Trezor Suite UI fakes an error and initiates a "recovery process" prompting for the seed phrase; the legitimate companion app does *not* request the seed phrase.

## Detection signals

- **User-side purchase verification:** authorised-retailer-purchase verification; refusal of any informal-channel purchase regardless of how legitimate the device appears.
- **Vendor-side authenticity check:** vendor-side device-genuineness check (Ledger Live's authenticity verification; Trezor Suite's parallel check) executed on first connection of any new device.
- **Tamper-evident packaging inspection:** vendor-supplied tamper-evident seal verification at unboxing; refusal to use a device whose packaging shows tampering.
- **Vendor invariant enforcement:** *legitimate vendor never asks for the seed phrase across any communication channel* — invariant published by Ledger, Trezor, and the broader hardware-wallet category in writing through user-onboarding documentation, customer-support pages, and recurring security advisories. User-side enforcement of the invariant is the binding mitigation for T11.007.003.
- **Vendor-side continuous-update threat-intelligence:** Ledger's "Ongoing phishing campaigns" page (continuous-update tracking of active campaigns); SatoshiLabs / Trezor parallel security-advisory feed; Kaspersky / ESET / Microsoft / Group-IB threat-intelligence on hardware-wallet-targeting phishing cohorts.
- **BIP39 passphrase as physical-access mitigation:** for T11.007.002 specifically, the BIP39 passphrase is the structural defender-side primitive that bounds realised extraction outcome to passphrase strength rather than preventing the extraction itself.

## Real-world examples

- **Counterfeit Ledger Nano S Plus hardware-wallet supply-chain compromise cohort — multi-chain — 2025 (canonical T11.007.001 deployed-attack anchor)** — Publicly-reported confirmed losses ~$9.5M+ across ~50+ documented victims, spanning 20+ blockchain ecosystems, per the Brazilian-cybersecurity-researcher disclosure that surfaced the cohort in 2025. Counterfeit operation: ESP32-S3 microcontroller substitution with chip markings scraped off; plaintext seed-phrase storage in flash; antenna trace on PCB for 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi / BLE radio exfiltration; visual-fidelity brand impersonation of legitimate Ledger Nano S Plus. Distribution channel: AliExpress / Taobao third-party sellers, Telegram-based resellers, in-person counterfeit-product markets. Multi-platform malware infrastructure: trojanised companion apps for Android, Windows, macOS, and iOS (via Apple TestFlight). Attribution: `inferred-strong` at the cohort-operator-level (multi-platform / multi-vector / cross-blockchain coordination indicates a structured operator-cluster); `pseudonymous` at the per-incident named-individual level. See [`examples/2025-counterfeit-ledger-nano-s-plus-cohort.md`](../examples/2025-counterfeit-ledger-nano-s-plus-cohort.md).
- **Trezor One / Model T RDP-downgrade voltage-glitch seed extraction (Kraken Security Labs disclosure) — hardware wallets — 2020-01-31 (canonical T11.007.002 capability anchor)** — $0 realised on-chain loss; coordinated-vulnerability disclosure from Kraken Security Labs to SatoshiLabs (responsibly disclosed 2019-10-30; public disclosure 2020-01-31). The STM32 microcontroller's Read Protection feature could be downgraded from RDP2 to RDP1 by voltage-glitching during boot-up, exposing SRAM contents (including the encrypted seed) over the ARM SWD debugging interface. Approximately 15 minutes of physical access plus modest hardware-hacking equipment required. Vendor-acknowledged structural vulnerability; not patchable by firmware update. BIP39 passphrase published as the defender-side mitigation primitive. Trezor Safe 3 (2023) and Trezor Safe 5 (2024) ship with a dedicated EAL6+ secure element addressing the structural vulnerability. See [`examples/2020-01-trezor-kraken-rdp-downgrade.md`](../examples/2020-01-trezor-kraken-rdp-downgrade.md).
- **Fake hardware-wallet firmware-update / recovery-app phishing cohort — Ledger / Trezor user base — 2023 onward (canonical T11.007.003 active-phishing anchor)** — Cohort-level cumulative loss not centrally tabulated; documented per-incident cases through 2023–2026 in the high-five-figure to low-six-figure range. Kaspersky's spring 2023 cohort tabulation reported "over 85,000 scam emails" detected in a single quarter. Sub-campaigns: Ledger-data-breach-leveraged email phishing (using the December 2020 Ledger customer-data leak — ~273,000 customer email + physical-address + phone-number records — for personalisation); Trezor-impersonating physical-mail-letter campaign (2025–2026, QR codes to typosquat domains); trojanised companion-app cohort (Kaspersky 2023 disclosure: infostealers swap fragments of legitimate Ledger Live / Trezor Suite code with trojanised code that fakes an error and initiates a "recovery process"). Attribution: `inferred-strong` at the cohort-operator-level; `pseudonymous` at the per-incident named-individual level for most sub-campaigns. See [`examples/2023-2026-fake-firmware-update-phishing-cohort.md`](../examples/2023-2026-fake-firmware-update-phishing-cohort.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T11.007's primary detection surface is off-chain at the supply-chain / communication-channel / physical-access layer, not the on-chain runtime risk-engine surface MG covers.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: Ledger's "Ongoing phishing campaigns" page (continuous-update tracking); SatoshiLabs / Trezor security advisories; Kaspersky / ESET / Microsoft / Group-IB threat-intelligence on hardware-wallet-targeting phishing cohorts; Cyble Research and Intelligence Labs supply-chain cohort tracking; vendor-side device-genuineness check (Ledger Live; Trezor Suite); industry-press cohort framing (BleepingComputer, Decrypt, Cointelegraph, 9to5Mac).

## Mitigations

- **Authorised-retailer-purchase invariant (highest-leverage for T11.007.001):** any user with non-trivial holdings should purchase hardware exclusively through the vendor's authorised retail channels (vendor official store; named authorised retailers); informal-channel purchases (Chinese marketplaces, Telegram resellers, in-person counterfeit markets, second-hand without packaging) should be treated as **structurally compromised regardless of how legitimate the device appears**.
- **Tamper-evident packaging inspection:** vendor-supplied tamper-evident seal verification at unboxing; refusal to initialise a device whose packaging shows tampering.
- **Vendor-side device-genuineness check:** execute the vendor's authenticity verification (Ledger Live's check; Trezor Suite's parallel) on first connection of any new device. Reject devices that fail the check.
- **BIP39 passphrase as physical-access mitigation (T11.007.002):** for users with non-trivial holdings on Trezor One / Model T or any hardware whose underlying microcontroller is known-vulnerable to physical-access seed-extraction, BIP39 passphrase usage should be treated as **mandatory** (not optional). For new deployments where the physical-access threat model is non-trivial, prefer secure-element-equipped hardware (Trezor Safe series, Ledger Nano series, or equivalent with Common-Criteria-certified secure element).
- **Legitimate-vendor-never-asks-for-seed-phrase invariant (highest-leverage for T11.007.003):** user-side enforcement that the legitimate vendor *never* requests the seed phrase across email, physical mail, in-app prompts, phone calls, chat support, or firmware-update flows. Refuse to enter the seed phrase into any surface other than the legitimate device's own physical input mechanism.
- **Vendor-side continuous-update threat-intelligence subscription:** subscribe to vendor-side phishing-campaign tracking (Ledger's "Ongoing phishing campaigns" page; Trezor's parallel advisories) and treat any unsolicited communication purporting to be from the vendor as a phishing candidate until verified through the vendor's authenticated channels.
- **Companion-app integrity:** download Ledger Live / Trezor Suite exclusively from the vendor's official site or through an authenticated app-store entry; verify cryptographic checksums where the vendor publishes them; treat companion-app prompts soliciting the seed phrase as definitively malicious.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1 are a **gap** — no existing OAK-M* entry directly maps to the hardware-wallet supply-chain authenticity surface or the brand-impersonation phishing surface. v0.x candidate Mitigation entries: "OAK-M-x authorised-retailer-purchase invariant"; "OAK-M-x device-genuineness check execution"; "OAK-M-x BIP39 passphrase invariant for known-vulnerable hardware"; "OAK-M-x legitimate-vendor-never-asks-for-seed-phrase invariant".

## Citations

- See worked examples for primary citations.
- Counterfeit Ledger cohort: Brazilian-cybersecurity-researcher 2025 disclosure; industry-press secondary coverage.
- Trezor RDP-downgrade: Kraken Security Labs disclosure; Trezor "Our Response to the Read Protection Downgrade Attack" January 31, 2020 blog post.
- Phishing cohort: Kaspersky / ESET / Microsoft / Group-IB threat-intelligence advisories; Ledger "Ongoing phishing campaigns" page; SatoshiLabs / Trezor security advisories.

## Discussion

T11.007 closes a structural gap in OAK v0.1's T11 coverage. The dominant v0.1 T11 worked examples (Bybit / Safe{Wallet} for T11.001, Atomic Wallet for T11.002, WazirX for T11.003) all share an *organisational-customer-or-vendor-side compromise* shape; T11.007 introduces a **brand-impersonation-and-supply-chain** failure surface that is structurally distinct.

The structural OAK lesson is that **a hardware wallet purchased outside authorised retail channels is structurally indistinguishable from a deliberate seed-exfiltration device**, and the legitimate-vendor's brand impersonation is the social-engineering surface that produces user-side trust. The wallet's cryptographic correctness (secure element architecture, firmware integrity, BIP39 implementation) is meaningful only if the device the user holds is the device the legitimate vendor produced. Hardware-wallet purchase-channel verification is a first-class user-side T11 mitigation surface.

The three sub-patterns differ at the **substrate** layer:

- **T11.007.001** substitutes the hardware unit itself; the brand-impersonation provides social-engineering trust at purchase time.
- **T11.007.002** exploits a structural microcontroller vulnerability against a legitimate device; physical access is the binding constraint.
- **T11.007.003** exploits the vendor's communication channel as a brand-impersonation surface; the device is legitimate but the *channel* is counterfeit.

The 2020 Ledger customer-data leak is structural to the cohort: ~273,000 customer email + physical-address + phone-number records provide ongoing personalisation data that produces apparent legitimacy in T11.007.003 phishing campaigns. The user-side mental model "this email / letter / app is from Ledger because it has their branding, addresses me by name, and references my purchase history" is structurally false because the operational substrate is the leaked customer database.

The **vendor-side commitment + user-side enforcement** of the *legitimate-vendor-never-asks-for-seed-phrase* invariant is the load-bearing mitigation primitive for T11.007.003. The vendor commits in writing through user-onboarding documentation, customer-support pages, and recurring security advisories; the user enforces the invariant by refusing to enter the seed phrase into any surface other than the legitimate device's own physical input mechanism. The invariant is simple but operationally fragile because phishing campaigns continuously refine their personalisation surface.

For T11.007.002, the BIP39 passphrase converts physical-access seed-extraction capability from a "wallet drainable" outcome to a "wallet-revealing-empty-decoy" outcome. The structural OAK lesson is that **the physical-access threat model is operationally distinct from the software-attack threat model**, and hardware-wallet selection must be evaluated against both threat models independently.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once additional per-incident worked-example anchors land — additional counterfeit-hardware cohorts beyond the 2025 Ledger Nano S Plus cohort, additional physical-access deployed-attack cases beyond the Trezor RDP-downgrade capability anchor, and additional sub-campaign anchors within the active-phishing cohort.
docs.techniques/T11.008-embedded-wallet-ident:
# OAK-T11.008 — Embedded-Wallet Identity-Provider Compromise

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T11 (Custody and Signing Infrastructure)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (Polygon-resident Polymarket / Magic Labs canonical at v0.1; cross-chain analogues across Privy / Web3Auth / Dynamic deployments on Ethereum, Base, Arbitrum, Solana, others)
**First documented:** 2024-09 (Polymarket Magic-Labs takeover); cohort scale-out 2024–2026 across Polymarket-class platforms whose user onboarding runs through third-party email-auth / OAuth / MPC-social-login providers
**Aliases:** "Magic Labs takeover", "Privy / Web3Auth / Dynamic compromise", "embedded-wallet auth-provider compromise", "email-magic-link wallet hijack", "social-login wallet drain"

## Description

Compromise of the identity-provider layer of an *embedded wallet* — a wallet whose key derivation runs through a third-party authentication service (email magic-link, OAuth federation, MPC-backed social login). The on-chain wallet appears non-custodial from the protocol perspective; from the user's perspective, the access path is "log in with your email" or "log in with Google / Apple / X." The effective custody surface is the **auth provider's security posture**, not the user's hardware-wallet hygiene or the contract's correctness.

Vendor cohort at v0.1: Magic Labs, Privy, Web3Auth (now `tKey` / `Auth` / Torus), Dynamic, Particle Network, and adjacent embedded-wallet-as-a-service offerings. The on-chain transaction is correctly signed by the provider's key; the upstream compromise occurs at the email-auth, OAuth, or MPC-share layer.

T11.008 is **structurally distinct** from adjacent T11 sub-Techniques and access-acquisition Techniques:

- **T11.001** (Third-Party Signing-Vendor Compromise) — covers vendor-side UI / signing-flow compromise with organisational-customer victim shape (Bybit / Safe{Wallet}). T11.008's victims are *individual end-users* of the embedded-wallet platform, not the platform-operator.
- **T11.002** (Wallet-Software Distribution Compromise) — substitutes the wallet binary on a legitimate device. T11.008 does not require any wallet-software compromise; the wallet derivation runs entirely through the auth provider's hosted infrastructure.
- **T11.006** (Cold-storage Seed-phrase Exfiltration at Rest) — third-party storage-service-at-rest failure surface (LastPass / iCloud-MetaMask). T11.008 does not require a stored seed phrase; the user typically *has no seed phrase* in the embedded-wallet model.
- **T4 phishing** — phishing campaigns that exploit embedded-wallet auth flows are upstream of T11.008 (e.g., the [`examples/2025-11-polymarket-comment-section-phishing.md`](../examples/2025-11-polymarket-comment-section-phishing.md) campaign captures email-magic-link credentials to exploit the Magic Labs auth path). T11.008 is the *custody surface* the phishing chains into; the phishing itself is T4.

The structural feature is **the widespread "non-custodial" claim collapses back to "as custodial as the auth provider's security posture."** The user's mental model of self-custody is constructed around the on-chain wallet's contract-level non-custodial properties; the operational reality is that the access path is mediated through a third-party identity provider whose compromise produces wallet access without any on-chain action required.

## Observed indicators

- **User-onboarding flow:** the platform's signup is "log in with email" / "log in with Google / Apple / X" / "log in with phone number" rather than "connect wallet." A non-custodial wallet is auto-derived as part of the auth flow.
- **No user-controlled seed phrase:** the user is never shown a seed phrase / mnemonic / private key for the auto-derived wallet; key derivation runs through the auth provider's hosted infrastructure.
- **Auth-provider attribution:** post-incident, the platform attributes the compromise to "abuse of a third-party authentication tool" / "third-party login tool" / "third-party auth service" — the load-bearing platform-side framing.
- **On-chain transaction signature:** the on-chain action is a *legitimate signed transaction* by the auth-provider-managed key; on-chain forensics surface no signing-flow anomaly because the auth provider's signature is valid.
- **Cohort fingerprint:** affected users share the embedded-wallet platform but have no other commonality (no on-chain phishing signature, no compromised wallet binary, no SIM-swap chain).

## Detection signals

- **Auth-provider-side telemetry:** unusual login patterns at the email-magic-link / OAuth layer (geographic anomalies, device anomalies, rate-limit triggers); session-token anomalies; multi-device session reconciliation failures. The detection surface lives at the auth provider's infrastructure, not at the on-chain runtime.
- **Platform-side audit:** integration-time review of the auth provider's security posture (SOC-2 / equivalent attestations; hardware-backed multi-factor authentication offering; recovery-flow design that limits social-engineering surface).
- **Cross-platform wave detection:** when multiple embedded-wallet platforms share the same auth provider, a compromise wave at one platform is a leading indicator for the others. The Polymarket September 2024 + December 2025 incidents (both attributed to "third-party login tool") plus the broader 2024–2026 cohort of Magic Labs / Privy / Web3Auth-class compromises form the cohort-detection surface.
- **User-side awareness:** users with material holdings on embedded-wallet platforms should treat the email-auth credential as the load-bearing security primitive — strong unique passwords, hardware-backed two-factor authentication, awareness of phishing flows that solicit the magic-link verification code.

## Real-world examples

- **Polymarket account takeover via third-party auth — Polygon — 2024-09 (canonical T11.008 case)** — Loss undisclosed; Polymarket characterised the affected set as "a small number of users." Attack vector: abuse of Magic Labs (the third-party email-auth provider used to derive non-custodial wallets for Polymarket users). Funds held on-chain in a smart-contract wallet but access mediated through a Magic-Labs-managed key derived from the email-auth flow. Attacker called `proxy()` on the Magic-derived wallets, transferring USDC to attacker-controlled addresses. Polymarket publicly attributed the incident to abuse of a third-party authentication tool. Attribution: `inferred-strong`; specific operator attribution not published. The September 2024 incident shares structural pattern with the December 2025 Polymarket account-breach wave (also attributed to third-party auth) and with the broader 2024–2026 cohort of Magic Labs / Privy / Web3Auth-class compromises. See [`examples/2024-09-polymarket-magic-labs-takeover.md`](../examples/2024-09-polymarket-magic-labs-takeover.md).
- **Polymarket comment-section phishing campaign — Polygon — 2025-11 (T11.008-chained T4 case)** — ≥ $500,000 cumulative loss across affected users. The attack chain: T4 phishing (comment-pinning on engagement-weighted platform; cloned login page captures email + magic-link verification code) → T11.008 (attacker authenticates to Magic Labs as the user; gains access to the user's Polymarket-derived non-custodial wallet; transfers USDC).
