The case is the canonical illustration of T4 phishing chaining into T11.008 — the embedded-wallet identity-provider is the custody surface that the phishing exploits. See [`examples/2025-11-polymarket-comment-section-phishing.md`](../examples/2025-11-polymarket-comment-section-phishing.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T11.008's primary detection surface is at the auth provider's infrastructure (login patterns, session-token anomalies, recovery-flow integrity), not the on-chain runtime risk-engine surface MG covers.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: auth-provider-side telemetry (Magic Labs / Privy / Web3Auth / Dynamic internal monitoring); platform-side post-incident audit (Polymarket's third-party-auth attribution and remediation guidance); industry-press cohort framing.

## Mitigations

- **Platform-side (highest-leverage):** integration-time security review of the auth provider; SOC-2 / equivalent attestation requirement; hardware-backed multi-factor authentication offering as a configurable option for end-users with material holdings; recovery-flow design that limits social-engineering surface (avoiding email-only recovery; requiring multi-channel verification for sensitive actions).
- **Auth-provider-side:** unusual-login-pattern detection at the email-magic-link / OAuth layer; session-token rotation and anomaly detection; recovery-flow hardening; bug-bounty programme for auth-provider-specific compromise paths.
- **User-side:** treat the email-auth credential as the load-bearing security primitive — strong unique passwords for the email account underpinning the magic-link flow, hardware-backed two-factor authentication on the email account, awareness of phishing flows that solicit the magic-link verification code.
- **Disclosure invariant:** platforms using embedded-wallet identity-providers should disclose the dependency to users and explicitly state that the access path's security is bounded by the auth provider's posture. The "non-custodial" claim should be qualified with the auth-provider dependency rather than left ambiguous.
- **Cross-platform coordination:** when an auth-provider compromise wave is observed at one platform, dependent platforms should treat it as a leading indicator and run pre-emptive session invalidation / multi-factor enrollment campaigns across their user base.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1 are a **gap** — no existing OAK-M* entry directly maps to the embedded-wallet auth-provider security surface. v0.x candidate Mitigation entries: "OAK-M-x embedded-wallet auth-provider integration audit"; "OAK-M-x embedded-wallet hardware-MFA opt-in"; "OAK-M-x platform-side disclosure of auth-provider dependency".

## Citations

- See worked examples for primary citations.
- Polymarket / Magic Labs cohort: Polymarket public statements attributing September 2024 and December 2025 incidents to "third-party login tool"; industry-press secondary coverage.
- Polymarket comment-section phishing: CoinSpot, Phemex, BingX, Cryptopolitan secondary coverage; Polymarket support disclosure of cumulative loss above $500,000.

## Discussion

T11.008 closes a structural gap in OAK v0.1's T11 coverage. The dominant v0.1 T11 worked examples assume an on-chain signing key under the user's direct control; T11.008 introduces a custody substrate where the on-chain signing key is *managed by a third-party identity provider* whose compromise produces wallet access without any on-chain action required.

The structural OAK lesson is that **the "non-custodial" claim is a compositional property, not an absolute one**. A wallet whose contract-level properties are non-custodial may have an access path mediated through a custodial-equivalent identity provider; the effective custody surface is the union of (a) the on-chain contract's properties and (b) the auth provider's security posture. Platforms using embedded-wallet identity-providers should disclose this composition to users explicitly.

The cohort-level pattern at v0.1 is dominated by Polymarket / Magic Labs cases. The structural pattern generalises beyond Polymarket: any platform whose user onboarding runs through Magic Labs / Privy / Web3Auth / Dynamic / Particle Network is exposed to the same auth-provider-compromise surface. The 2024-09 and 2025-12 Polymarket incidents are bracketed by a broader 2024–2026 cohort of embedded-wallet-platform compromises; pipeline anchor cases for v0.x include other Polymarket-class platforms experiencing auth-provider-compromise events.

The T4-T11.008 attack chain is structurally important. T4 phishing campaigns that target embedded-wallet platforms (e.g., the [`examples/2025-11-polymarket-comment-section-phishing.md`](../examples/2025-11-polymarket-comment-section-phishing.md) campaign) capture the email-magic-link credentials at the T4 layer and exploit them at the T11.008 layer. Detection at the T4 layer is necessary but not sufficient; defender investments must also harden the T11.008 layer (auth-provider posture, recovery-flow integrity, session-anomaly detection).

The auth-provider concentration risk is structural. A small number of embedded-wallet-as-a-service vendors (Magic Labs, Privy, Web3Auth, Dynamic) provide identity-provider infrastructure to a large fraction of the embedded-wallet ecosystem. A compromise wave at one provider exposes every dependent platform's user base simultaneously — an OAK-T8.002-adjacent operator-continuity surface at the auth-provider layer rather than at the on-chain operator layer.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once additional auth-provider-cohort cases land (Privy / Web3Auth / Dynamic incidents with public forensic disclosure) and once the cross-platform cohort beyond Polymarket is anchored.
docs.techniques/T11.009-trader-tooling-supply:
# OAK-T11.009 — Trader-Tooling Supply-Chain Compromise targeting `.env` Private Keys

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T11 (Custody and Signing Infrastructure)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** chain-agnostic (the substrate-of-extraction is the developer-environment plaintext key file; downstream extraction occurs across whichever chains the bot operates on — Polygon, Solana, Ethereum, Base, Arbitrum, BNB Chain are all in scope across the cohort)
**First documented:** 2025-12 / 2026-01 (Polymarket trader-tooling supply-chain compromise via npm `polymarket-clob` and `dev-protocol` GitHub-org hijack); the broader cohort context spans 2024–2026 with overlapping infrastructure to DPRK-attributed BeaverTail / InvisibleFerret npm campaigns
**Aliases:** "trader-bot npm supply-chain", "developer-environment .env exfiltration", "GitHub-org-hijack trojan-bot distribution", "wallet.json infostealer via package registry"

## Description

Compromise of trader-tooling distribution channels — npm package registry, PyPI, GitHub repositories — used to ship code that exfiltrates plaintext private-key material from developer environments. The target cohort is *bot builders, quant developers, and copy-trading-bot users* who maintain plaintext private keys in `.env` files, `wallet.json` / `keys/*.json` files, or other developer-environment storage to enable automated signing. The malicious package or repository contains code that reads these key files at install / build / first-run time and exfiltrates them to attacker-controlled infrastructure.

T11.009 is **structurally distinct** from adjacent T11 sub-Techniques:

- **T11.002** (Wallet-Software Distribution Compromise) — substitutes the wallet binary delivered to *end-user* wallets (Atomic Wallet pattern). T11.009 targets *developer environments* — the bot's runtime is the victim, not the end-user's wallet UI.
- **T11.006** (Cold-storage Seed-phrase Exfiltration at Rest) — third-party storage-service-at-rest failure surface (LastPass / iCloud-MetaMask). T11.009's failure surface is the *developer's own machine* where plaintext keys are stored to enable bot operation; the compromise vector is the package registry, not the storage service.
- **T11.007** (Hardware-wallet Supply-chain / Physical-access Compromise) — brand-impersonation hardware substitution and active phishing for seed-phrase exfiltration. T11.009 targets developers who do not use hardware wallets (because their bots require automated signing).
- **T8** (Operational Reuse) — laundering / reuse-pattern-side. T11.009 is custody-side: the substrate of extraction is the plaintext key file in the developer environment.

The cohort overlaps DPRK-attributed npm-supply-chain operations at the infrastructure-fingerprint layer (BeaverTail / InvisibleFerret / Vidar Stealer C2 endpoint reuse) but the attribution at the named-individual level remains pseudonymous for most sub-campaigns at v0.1.

## Observed indicators

- **Package-registry fingerprints:** trojanised npm / PyPI packages whose names target a specific platform's tooling ecosystem (`polymarket-clob`, `polymarket-copytrading-bot-*`); sibling packages with seemingly-unrelated names (`sha256-validation`, `sha256-validator-pro`, `solana-utils-sdk`, `synced-plus-agent`) maintained by the same publisher; download-count manipulation to produce social-proof signal.
- **Repository-side fingerprints:** GitHub repositories under hijacked organisations (the `dev-protocol` GitHub-org hijack in the canonical case is a real Japanese DeFi project whose org credentials were compromised); polished READMEs with artificially-inflated star counts; repositories that present as genuine open-source bot projects on first inspection.
- **Code-side fingerprints:** code that reads `.env`, `wallet.txt`, `wallets.json`, `keys/*.json`, `~/.config/solana/id.json`, or `~/.aws/credentials` at install / build / first-run time; exfiltration via HTTPS POST to an attacker-controlled IP / domain; secondary backdoor capabilities (port-22 SSH backdoors, IP fingerprinting, reverse shells).
- **Infrastructure overlap:** C2 endpoint overlap with known infostealer / DPRK npm-supply-chain campaigns (Vidar Stealer in the canonical case; BeaverTail / InvisibleFerret cohort patterns).

## Detection signals

- **Package-registry-side:** automated scanning for packages that read plaintext key files at install time; reputation-based filtering (publisher age, download history, sibling-package analysis); rapid-takedown coordination across npm / PyPI / GitHub when malicious packages are identified.
- **Developer-environment-side:** dependency-source-pinning (lock files; integrity hashes; explicit allowlists for production dependencies); `.env` / `wallet.json` access auditing at the OS layer; isolation of bot-runtime environments from general developer environments (containers, VMs, separate user accounts).
- **Operator-side (bot-builder):** exclusive use of hardware-wallet-backed signing (Ledger / Trezor) or remote-signing services (Fireblocks, Web3Auth MPC) where automated signing is required; refusal to maintain plaintext private keys in `.env` files for any non-trivial holdings.
- **Public-disclosure surfaces:** Cyb3rhawk / ADHD security-research disclosures; StepSecurity / Socket.dev / Snyk / Phylum supply-chain monitoring services; ZachXBT and SEAL Threat Intel public disclosures of trader-tooling compromise events.

## Real-world examples

- **Polymarket trader-tooling supply-chain compromise — npm + GitHub — 2026-01 (canonical T11.009 case)** — ~0.014 ETH (~$44) confirmed in one publicly-tracked victim wallet from the npm package campaign; aggregate cohort loss undisclosed. The economic ceiling is not the operative concern — the **substrate compromise of the Polymarket-trader tooling ecosystem** is. **npm package distribution (Dec 2025 - Jan 2026):** malicious package `polymarket-clob` v1.3.1 (~189 downloads before takedown) plus sibling packages `sha256-validation` (~7,600 downloads), `sha256-validator-pro` (~1,000 downloads), `solana-utils-sdk`, `synced-plus-agent`. Each package contained code that read `wallet.txt`, `wallets.json`, and `keys/*.json` from the developer's filesystem and exfiltrated their contents to an attacker-controlled IP. Packages were pulled from npm on January 6, 2026 after public disclosure. **GitHub organisation hijack (Feb-Apr 2026):** the threat actor "Trust412" obtained credentials to the `dev-protocol` GitHub organisation (a legitimate Japanese DeFi project) and published 20+ repositories under names like `polymarket-copytrading-bot-*`. Trojans also opened port-22 SSH backdoors on developer machines and fingerprinted IP addresses. Attribution: `inferred-strong` at operator-cohort level (npm campaign overlapped infrastructure with Vidar Stealer C2 endpoints per Cyb3rhawk / ADHD reporting; package distribution pattern consistent with DPRK-attributed npm-supply-chain operations — BeaverTail / InvisibleFerret cohort, see [`actors/OAK-G01-lazarus.md`](../actors/OAK-G01-lazarus.md)) but specific attribution to OAK-G01 has not been publicly confirmed. See [`examples/2026-01-polymarket-trader-tooling-supply-chain.md`](../examples/2026-01-polymarket-trader-tooling-supply-chain.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T11.009's primary detection surface is at the package-registry / repository / developer-environment layer, not the on-chain runtime risk-engine surface MG covers.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: Socket.dev / Snyk / Phylum supply-chain monitoring services; StepSecurity supply-chain attack reporting; GitHub's own malicious-repository-takedown coordination; npm's own malicious-package-takedown coordination; Cyb3rhawk / ADHD security-research disclosures; ZachXBT / SEAL Threat Intel public disclosures of trader-tooling compromise events.

## Mitigations

- **Bot-builder / quant-developer hygiene (highest-leverage):** prefer hardware-wallet-backed signing (Ledger / Trezor) or remote-signing services (Fireblocks, Web3Auth MPC, AWS KMS / GCP KMS) where automated signing is required; refuse to maintain plaintext private keys in `.env` files for any non-trivial holdings. Where plaintext keys are operationally unavoidable, isolate the bot-runtime environment from general developer environments via containers / VMs / separate user accounts.
- **Dependency-source-pinning:** lock files with integrity hashes; explicit allowlists for production dependencies; refusal to install packages from publishers without verified history. Treat any package whose author is newly-created or whose download history is sparse as elevated-risk regardless of name plausibility.
- **GitHub-organisation hardening:** enforce hardware-MFA for all maintainers of organisations that publish bot tooling; rotate credentials regularly; treat any unsolicited collaboration request to a maintainer as a potential credential-phishing precursor (the `dev-protocol` GitHub-org hijack is the canonical T11.009 cohort case for credential-compromise-as-distribution-substrate).
- **Package-registry-side enforcement:** automated scanning for packages that read plaintext key files at install time; reputation-based filtering; rapid-takedown coordination. The npm / PyPI / GitHub takedown infrastructure has improved through 2024–2026 but the time-to-detection vs. time-to-install window remains the binding constraint.
- **Cohort-level attribution coordination:** infrastructure-fingerprint sharing across security-research firms (Vidar Stealer C2 endpoint reuse; BeaverTail / InvisibleFerret cohort patterns) accelerates detection and takedown of follow-on campaigns.
- **Platform-side guidance:** platforms with significant trader-tooling ecosystems (Polymarket, Hyperliquid, Pump.fun, Solana memecoin-tooling ecosystem) should publish authoritative-tooling registries — vetted lists of bot frameworks, copy-trading services, and analytics tooling — so that users can distinguish between legitimate tooling and trojan repositories.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1 are a **gap** — no existing OAK-M* entry directly maps to the developer-environment supply-chain hardening surface that T11.009 most leverages. v0.x candidate Mitigation entries: "OAK-M-x trader-tooling supply-chain dependency-pinning"; "OAK-M-x bot-runtime hardware-signer invariant"; "OAK-M-x platform-side authoritative-tooling-registry publication".

## Citations

- See worked example for primary citations.
- Polymarket trader-tooling cohort: Cyb3rhawk / ADHD security-research reporting; StepSecurity supply-chain attack reporting; ZachXBT / SEAL Threat Intel public disclosures.
- Cohort-context: see [`actors/OAK-G01-lazarus.md`](../actors/OAK-G01-lazarus.md) for the BeaverTail / InvisibleFerret npm-supply-chain cohort and DPRK-attributed package-registry operations.

## Discussion

T11.009 closes a structural gap in OAK v0.1's T11 coverage. The dominant v0.1 T11 worked examples (Bybit / Safe{Wallet} for T11.001, Atomic Wallet for T11.002, WazirX for T11.003) focus on end-user wallet binaries, organisational signing-vendor flows, and on-chain multisig manipulation. T11.009 introduces a custody surface that lives in *developer environments* — the bot's runtime substrate — which is structurally distinct from end-user wallet binaries (T11.002 target cohort) and from organisational signing-vendor surfaces (T11.001 target cohort).

The structural OAK lesson is that **the bot-builder / quant-developer cohort is a distinct target population with its own custody surface**. End-user wallet hygiene (hardware wallet, no plaintext seed storage) maps poorly to bot-runtime requirements (automated signing, programmatic transaction submission, sub-second latency requirements). The cohort's residual reliance on `.env` files for operational reasons creates a custody surface that the package-registry / repository-distribution channels can attack.

The cohort overlap with DPRK-attributed npm-supply-chain operations is structural rather than incidental. The BeaverTail / InvisibleFerret npm cohort (see [`actors/OAK-G01-lazarus.md`](../actors/OAK-G01-lazarus.md)) shares infrastructure-fingerprints (Vidar Stealer C2 endpoints; package-distribution patterns; trojan-payload similarity) with the canonical T11.009 case. The named-individual attribution remains pseudonymous at v0.1 — "Trust412" handle for the GitHub-org hijack; npm publisher pseudonyms for the package campaign — but the operator-cluster-level attribution converges on a structured operator-cluster rather than opportunistic single-operator efforts.

The economic-ceiling-vs-substrate-compromise distinction is structurally important. The realised on-chain loss in the canonical case is small (~$44 confirmed) because most affected developers had not yet funded the wallets associated with the trojan'd `.env` files at the time of takedown. The operative risk is *the substrate compromise* — the trader-tooling ecosystem's residual exposure to follow-on campaigns and to the persistence of trojan repositories that survive initial takedown waves. Defender practice should weight the substrate-compromise dimension at least as heavily as the realised-loss dimension.

The platform-side authoritative-tooling-registry mitigation is a high-leverage but operationally awkward intervention. Platforms with significant trader-tooling ecosystems can publish vetted lists of bot frameworks and analytics tooling, but the publication imposes a ongoing curation burden and exposes the platform to liability framing. The Polymarket case at v0.1 has not seen platform-side registry publication; this is a v0.x candidate intervention.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once additional cohort cases land (Hyperliquid trader-tooling cohorts; Pump.fun copy-trading-bot cohorts; Solana memecoin-tooling-ecosystem cohorts) and once at least one attribution case reaches the `confirmed` strength label (DOJ-named-individual attribution to a specific operator-cluster behind an npm-supply-chain campaign targeting trader tooling).
docs.techniques/T12.001-nft-wash-trade-volume:
# OAK-T12.001 — NFT Wash-Trade Volume Inflation

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T12 (NFT-Specific Patterns)
**Maturity:** observed
**Chains:** EVM (primary; Ethereum / Polygon), Solana (Magic Eden, Tensor)
**First documented:** 2021–2022 (Chainalysis NFT retrospective; LooksRare incentive-wash episode)
**Aliases:** "NFT volume wash", "collection-rank inflation", "floor-price wash", "marketplace-incentive wash"

## Description

A class of NFT-marketplace trading patterns in which a small set of related addresses repeatedly transact the same NFTs (or NFTs from the same collection) against each other to inflate the **collection-level volume metric**, the **per-token sale history**, and the **floor-price-trend signal** that marketplaces and aggregators surface to prospective buyers. The operator goal is **price-discovery distortion**: the inflated metrics push the collection up marketplace leaderboards (top-volume, trending, top-gainers), draw third-party attention, and let the operator exit holdings into real demand at prices anchored by the manufactured trade history.

From a defender's perspective, T12.001 is structurally distinct from OAK-T3.002 (fungible Wash-Trade Volume Inflation) and OAK-T7.004 (NFT Wash-Laundering) along two axes:

- **vs OAK-T3.002 (different on-chain artefacts).** T3.002 detection runs on **DEX swap events on per-pair counterparty graphs** and looks for cycles in the trade-counterparty graph for a fungible token pair. T12.001 detection runs at the **NFT-marketplace layer** (OpenSea, Blur, Magic Eden, LooksRare, X2Y2, Tensor) on **per-collection same-buyer-same-seller patterns** and **per-token-id sale-history reconstruction** — the unit of analysis is the collection and the individual token, not the trading pair. The same operator goal (inflate volume to attract real buyers) maps to a different on-chain trace and therefore a different detector.
- **vs OAK-T7.004 (same on-chain pattern, different operator goal).** T7.004 self-financed NFT trades launder illicit funds through "art purchase" income; T12.001 self-financed NFT trades manipulate marketplace metrics to attract real buyers. The on-chain pattern is similar — self-financed sales between related addresses on an NFT marketplace — but the downstream behaviour differs (T7.004 typically retains the asset or routes proceeds out; T12.001 typically follows the wash episode with a real-buyer exit) and the appropriate marketplace-side mitigation differs accordingly. This is the same goal-discrimination problem as T3.002 vs T7.004 in the fungible space.

A third sub-motivation is worth naming explicitly: **marketplace-incentive-driven wash**, in which the operator washes trades to farm marketplace token rewards (LooksRare LOOKS, X2Y2 X2Y2, Blur BLUR airdrops and seasons). This sub-motivation overlaps with T12.001 on the on-chain artefact and with T3.002-family incentive-farming on the goal; OAK v0.1 treats it as a sub-class of T12.001 because the artefact is NFT-marketplace-specific, but the mitigation set differs (incentive-wash is fixed by changing the reward formula, not by per-collection metric adjustment).

## Observed indicators

- High collection volume relative to unique-holder count, social footprint, or unique-buyer count over the same window.
- A small number of token-ids accounting for a disproportionate share of collection sale count, with each token-id transacting between a small set of related wallets.
- Repeated round-trips of the same token-id between two or more addresses in a short window, often at increasing nominal prices that drift the recorded floor / last-sale upward.
- Per-collection same-buyer-same-seller pairs accounting for a measurable fraction of trade count.
- Sudden collection-leaderboard appearances (top-volume, trending) without commensurate growth in unique buyers or external mentions.

## Detection signals

- **Per-collection counterparty graph:** for each collection, build the buyer–seller graph weighted by trade count and notional volume; flag collections where a small connected component accounts for a disproportionate share of edges or volume.
- **Per-token-id sale-history reconstruction:** for each token-id with N > k sales in a window, check whether the buyer/seller sets across those sales reduce to a small cluster under a self-financed-cluster heuristic.
- **Self-financed-cluster signature:** trace the buyer-side funding for high-impact sales (sales that reset the floor or top a per-token-id sale history) back N hops; alert when the funding originates from the seller-side cluster directly or indirectly.
- **Floor-price-resilience metric:** compute a wash-adjusted floor by excluding sales between addresses in the same self-financed cluster; flag collections where the wash-adjusted floor diverges sharply from the marketplace-published floor.
- **Leaderboard-entry validation:** when a collection enters a top-N leaderboard, recompute its volume excluding self-financed-cluster trades and report the gap.
- **Goal-discrimination heuristic (T12.001 vs T7.004):** T12.001 episodes typically end with a real-buyer exit at the inflated price; T7.004 episodes typically do not need a real buyer and the asset may be retained or routed onward. Window-the-cluster behaviour after the wash burst and check for a non-cluster buyer absorbing inventory.

## Real-world examples

- **Aggregate cohort — multi-marketplace — 2021.** Per `[chainalysis2022nft]`, 262 users were identified as selling NFTs to self-financed addresses more than 25 times in 2021; 110 of them collectively netted \~\$8.9M in profit. The cohort-level metric does not separate T12.001 (price-discovery-distortion) from T7.004 (laundering) motivation per-user, but the profit-realisation pattern (the 110 profitable users) is more consistent with T12.001 than with T7.004 because realised profit implies a real-buyer exit.
- **Marketplace-level wash-trade rates — 2022–2023.** Industry and academic studies have documented per-platform wash-trade rates reaching \~94.5% on LooksRare and \~84.2% on X2Y2 in 2022–2023. These rates reflect a mix of marketplace-incentive-driven wash (LOOKS / X2Y2 reward farming) and T12.001-style price-discovery-distortion wash on the same on-chain trace; per-trade goal-attribution at this scale remains a gap in the public forensic record.
- [`examples/2023-02-blur-airdrop-wash-cohort.md`](../examples/2023-02-blur-airdrop-wash-cohort.md) — Blur airdrop incentive-driven wash-trade cohort; on-chain analysis identified substantial volume inflation incentivised by Blur's points-and-airdrop programme.
- **OAK does not name single per-collection T12.001 incidents at v0.1** beyond the cohort entry above because the canonical references are at the cohort and platform scale. Contributors with public forensic write-ups of specific named collection-level T12.001 incidents (where the price-discovery-distortion motive is established rather than the laundering or incentive-farming motive) are encouraged to submit them via PR.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1; per-collection counterparty-graph analysis and per-token-id sale-history reconstruction are not yet wired in.
- Vendor coverage: Chainalysis (cohort metrics and platform-level rates), Nansen (collection-level wash-trade flagging on the NFT-aggregator product), bitsCrunch / DappRadar (wash-adjusted volume metrics on NFT analytics products).
- Academic: companion literature to `[chainalysis2022nft]` and `[victor2021washtrade]`; per-marketplace academic studies are an emerging area (see proposed bib keys below).

## Mitigations

- **NFT marketplaces (highest-leverage):**
  - Compute and enforce a **per-cluster wash-trade-rate cap** (e.g., a wallet whose 30-day wash-trade rate exceeds a calibrated threshold has its trades excluded from leaderboard volume and from the published floor).
  - Publish a **wash-adjusted volume** metric and a **floor-price-resilience** metric alongside raw volume; the resilience metric is the wash-adjusted floor's divergence from the raw floor, surfaced on collection pages.
  - Exclude self-financed-cluster trades from leaderboard ranking and from "trending" surfaces.
  - For incentive programs (token rewards, airdrop seasons), apply the same cluster-level exclusion to reward-eligible volume to neutralise the marketplace-incentive sub-motivation at the source.
- **NFT aggregators / analytics:** publish per-marketplace and per-collection wash-trade-rate metrics on a recurring cadence; let downstream UIs default to wash-adjusted figures.
- **Traders / collectors:** discount published collection volume and floor by an estimated wash factor; weight unique-buyer growth and external-mention count over raw volume; treat sudden leaderboard entries as a signal for wash investigation, not for buying interest.
- **Risk teams:** for high-value NFT exposure, run per-collection counterparty-graph and per-token-id sale-history checks before underwriting price.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2022nft]` — primary cohort characterisation (2021 data, 262 users / \~\$8.9M profit, marketplace-level NFT-wash framing).
- `[chainalysis2025rug]` — aggregate market-manipulation framing including NFT segment; useful for cross-segment comparison.
- `[victor2021washtrade]` — companion methodology reference on the fungible side; the per-pair cycle-detection method does not transfer directly to T12.001 but the wash-classification framing does.
- Proposed bib keys (see below) for per-marketplace academic studies.

## Discussion

T12.001 exists as a standalone Technique under T12 — rather than as a sub-class of T3.002 or T7.004 — because the **detection surface is different**. T3.002 detectors run on DEX-swap counterparty graphs at per-pair granularity; they cannot consume NFT-marketplace events without a separate ingest pipeline. T7.004 detectors share the NFT-marketplace ingest with T12.001 but discriminate on the **post-wash behaviour** (asset retention vs real-buyer exit) and target a different mitigation owner (compliance and KYC tiers vs marketplace ranking and floor-display logic). Treating T12.001 separately surfaces the marketplace-side mitigation set — per-cluster wash-trade-rate caps, wash-adjusted volume, floor-price-resilience metrics, leaderboard exclusion — which is the highest-leverage layer for this Technique and which is owned by a different party than the T7.004 mitigation set.

The **marketplace-incentive-driven wash** sub-motivation deserves its own framing in a future v0.x update. It shares the on-chain artefact with T12.001 but the goal is to extract token rewards from the marketplace itself, not to attract third-party buyers. The mitigation is at the reward-formula level (e.g., the LooksRare and X2Y2 token-emission designs that made wash-farming profitable in 2022 are the underlying issue, not the wash trades themselves). OAK v0.1 keeps it under T12.001 because the artefact-level detection is shared; v0.2 may promote it to a sibling sub-Technique (T12.00X) once a clean operator-goal discriminator is published.

This Technique sits at the centre of the new T12 NFT-Specific Patterns Tactic and is expected to anchor the NFT pillar's detection guidance as the v0.x updates expand T12 coverage.
docs.techniques/T12.002-fake-mint-counterfeit:
# OAK-T12.002 — Fake-Mint / Counterfeit Collection

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T12 (NFT-Specific Patterns)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM (primary; Ethereum / Polygon), Solana (Magic Eden cohort)
**First documented:** 2021 (early counterfeit cohort on OpenSea); industrial-scale through 2022 Discord-compromise wave
**Aliases:** "fake mint", "counterfeit collection", "copymint", "spoofed collection", "impersonation drop"

## Description

A class of NFT-layer flows in which an attacker deploys a counterfeit ERC-721 / ERC-1155 collection (or a counterfeit Solana NFT collection) that mimics the artefact set of a legitimate collection — name, symbol, off-chain metadata URI, art, sometimes deployer-address-pattern — and routes victim attention to the counterfeit deployment instead of the genuine one. The attacker captures value through one of three sub-patterns:

- **(a) Phishing-mint** — victims sign a `mint` (or `safeMint`) call on a counterfeit-collection contract, paying mint price to an attacker-controlled treasury, in the belief that they are minting from the genuine project. Entry vector is typically a phishing dApp, a compromised X / Discord account, or a malicious mint-page link.
- **(b) Marketplace-listed clones** — the attacker deploys a counterfeit collection and lists it on OpenSea, Blur, or Magic Eden under search-engine-discoverable naming that overlaps with a legitimate collection; victims purchase counterfeit items at floor-price-equivalent prices.
- **(c) Discord-compromise-driven fake mint announcements** — attacker compromises the official Discord (or X account) of a legitimate project and posts a "stealth mint" / "extra mint" / "free claim" announcement that links to a counterfeit-collection mint page. This sub-pattern overlaps in entry vector with `OAK-T4.005` (`setApprovalForAll` NFT Drainer): the same compromised Discord typically distributes both mint-payment scams (T12.002) and approval-grant scams (T4.005) in the same campaign.

From a defender's perspective, T12.002 is the **identity-confusion** failure mode and is structurally distinct from `OAK-T4.005`. T4.005 abuses *authorisation* — the victim grants the attacker operator authority over assets the victim already owns. T12.002 abuses *identity* — the victim transfers value (mint payment, or purchase price) to a counterfeit contract believing it is the genuine collection. The two routinely co-occur in the same campaign because they share the Discord-compromise / phishing-dApp entry vector, but the defender controls live in different layers: T4.005 mitigations sit at the wallet (operator allowlists, approval simulation), while T12.002 mitigations sit at the marketplace and at the verification-badge layer (canonical-contract allowlists, verified-creator badges, image-similarity-driven counterfeit removal).

## Observed indicators

- A newly-deployed ERC-721 / ERC-1155 contract whose `name()`, `symbol()`, and off-chain `tokenURI` metadata closely match those of a legitimate collection but whose deployer address has no funder-graph connection to the genuine project's deployer cluster.
- Marketplace listings whose collection name is a Unicode-lookalike, plural / singular variant, or character-substituted variant of a legitimate collection (e.g., capital-I-vs-lowercase-l, zero-vs-O, added "Official" suffix).
- A spike in mint-page traffic to a non-canonical contract address shortly after a Discord-channel announcement or X-account post from an account historically associated with a legitimate project.
- Mint-page front-end whose visible artefacts (logo, copy, navigation) clone a legitimate project but whose `mint` call routes to a different contract address than the project's previously-published canonical contract.
- Counterfeit contract's `mint` recipient (treasury / withdrawal address) clusters with previously-flagged scam infrastructure rather than the genuine project's treasury cluster.

## Detection signals

- **Per-marketplace counterfeit-collection detection at indexing time** — image-perceptual-hash and metadata-similarity scoring against a registry of verified collections; flag and review newly-indexed collections whose similarity exceeds a calibrated threshold against any verified collection. OpenSea publicly described this approach (image-recognition plus human review) in May 2022.
- **Canonical-contract-allowlist enforcement** — for every legitimate collection, publish (and consume) a canonical contract address; marketplaces, wallets, and aggregators surface a strong "unverified collection" warning when a contract claiming a known name does not match the canonical entry. The Magic Eden January 2023 incident — where an indexer bug allowed unverified NFTs to be listed under verified collection pages — is the canonical failure case for this control.
- **Metadata-similarity scoring** — combine image-perceptual hash, metadata-URI hash, and name / symbol edit-distance into a similarity score; counterfeit collections typically score high on art similarity but have a different deployer-address cluster, treasury cluster, and contract-creation timestamp than the genuine project.
- **Off-chain telemetry** — integrate Discord-server-compromise reports, project-team out-of-band confirmations, and X-account-takeover signals as pre-event indicators for in-progress T12.002 campaigns; the same telemetry feeds T4.005 detection because the entry vector is shared.
- **Mint-payment-cluster heuristic** — for in-progress mints, monitor the recipient address of mint payments; alert when the recipient clusters with previously-flagged scam infrastructure rather than with the project's published treasury.

## Real-world examples

- **OpenSea counterfeit-collection cohort — Ethereum / Polygon — 2021–2022** — OpenSea publicly stated in early 2022 that more than 80% of items created via its free minting tool were plagiarised works, fake collections, or spam. In May 2022 OpenSea launched an image-recognition-plus-human-review system for `copymint` removal and expanded its verification programme. The cohort framing is the canonical reference because per-incident attribution is sparse — counterfeit collections are deployed at scale rather than as named one-shot incidents. Source: `[openseamoderation2022]`.
- **BAYC Discord-compromise fake-mint wave — Ethereum — April–June 2022** — Multiple compromises of the Bored Ape Yacht Club ecosystem's Discord (April 2022 webhook compromise, June 2022 community-manager-account compromise) distributed counterfeit-mint-page links for "stealth mints" and Otherside-related drops. The June 2022 incident drained \~179.9 ETH (\~\$320K) across 32 NFTs (1 BAYC, 2 MAYC, 5 Otherdeeds, 1 BAKC). The campaign mixes T12.002 (counterfeit mint pages capturing purported-mint payments) and T4.005 (`setApprovalForAll` drains routed through the same phishing dApp) — the Discord-compromise entry vector is shared. Sources: `[baycdiscord2022]`, `[fortunebaycjune2022]`.
- **Premint platform compromise — Ethereum — 2022-07-17** — Attackers injected malicious JavaScript into the Premint NFT mint-coordination platform's front end; the injected pop-up solicited a wallet "verification" that, in practice, executed `setApprovalForAll` against a drainer operator. Loss \~\$375K (\~314 NFTs at first count, \~\$421K and 320 NFTs by final tally), including BAYC, Goblintown, and Otherside items. The Premint case is a front-end-side example that overlaps both T12.002 (counterfeit mint-flow framing distributed via a trusted mint-coordination platform) and T4.005 (the technical extraction was an approval-drainer); included here because the user-facing framing was a fake-mint flow. Source: `[certikpremint2022]`.
- **Magic Eden unverified-listing exploit — Solana — 2023-01-04** — A bug in Magic Eden's indexer (Snappy Marketplace / Pro Trade tools) allowed unverified NFTs to appear under verified collection pages for ABC and y00ts; 25 counterfeit NFTs were sold across 4 collections at 20–50 SOL each before Magic Eden patched the indexer and refunded affected buyers. This is the canonical infrastructure-failure example for T12.002 detection sub-control "canonical-contract-allowlist enforcement at the marketplace indexer". Sources: `[magicedeny00ts2023]`, `[chainalysisnftcounterfeit2022]`.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2022-04-bored-ape-discord-wave.md`](../examples/2022-04-bored-ape-discord-wave.md) — Bored Ape Yacht Club / Yuga Labs operator-side credential-compromise wave
- [`examples/2022-07-premint-phishing.md`](../examples/2022-07-premint-phishing.md) — Premint NFT-allowlist platform front-end JavaScript injection

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (NFT-marketplace-side moderation is out of MG's operator-behaviour-detection scope).
- **Marketplace-side moderation as primary access path:** OpenSea (image-recognition + human review pipeline since May 2022, verification-badge programme), Magic Eden (verified-collection allowlist; the y00ts incident demonstrates the failure mode when the allowlist is enforced inconsistently across indexer paths), Blur (verified-collection layer; less public documentation of the moderation pipeline as of v0.1).
- **Vendor coverage for off-chain artefact-similarity detection:** Yuga Labs and other large NFT IP holders run trademark-protection workflows that file removal requests against marketplace counterfeit listings; specialised IP-protection vendors (Red Points, MarqVision) operate counterfeit-NFT monitoring services on a commercial basis.

## Mitigations

- **NFT marketplaces (highest-leverage):**
  - Maintain a canonical-contract allowlist per verified collection; refuse to surface listings or mint-pages under a verified collection name from a non-canonical contract.
  - Run image-perceptual-hash and metadata-similarity detection at indexing time; flag newly-listed collections whose similarity to a verified collection exceeds the calibrated threshold.
  - Issue verified-creator badges with strong identity binding (X handle, project domain, deployer-address proof) and surface the badge prominently on collection and item pages; treat absence-of-badge as the dominant warning signal for users.
  - Provide a low-friction trademark-takedown path for IP holders.
- **NFT projects:**
  - Publish the canonical contract address on the project's domain, X bio, and Discord pinned channel; instruct users that any mint-page address divergence from the published canonical address is a counterfeit.
  - Adopt Discord-moderation hardening (admin 2FA enforcement, webhook-creation monitoring, channel-lock procedures around scheduled mints) — this overlaps with T4.005 mitigations because the entry vector is shared.
  - Use out-of-band channels (project domain announcements, signed-message attestations) for high-stakes mint announcements rather than Discord-only / X-only push.
- **Wallets:**
  - Surface the contract address (not only the collection name) at mint-signature time; display "this contract has not been seen before" or "this contract does not match the canonical address for collection X" warnings where canonical-allowlist data is available.
  - Integrate counterfeit-collection feeds from marketplace partners and from on-chain similarity-detection services.
- **Users / collectors:**
  - Verify the contract address against the project's canonical published address before signing a mint transaction; ignore Discord-only / DM-only mint announcements as a categorical rule for high-value collections.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2022nft]` — see `citations.bib` (2022 NFT industry retrospective; primary anchor for T12-class cohort framing).
- `[chainalysisnftcounterfeit2022]` — see `citations.bib` (counterfeit-collection cohort and platform-moderation framing).
- `[openseamoderation2022]` — see `citations.bib` (OpenSea's May 2022 announcement of `copymint` detection and verification-badge expansion; \~80% free-mint-tool plagiarism / spam figure).
- `[magicedeny00ts2023]` — see `citations.bib` (Magic Eden indexer-bug counterfeit-listing incident; canonical example of canonical-allowlist-enforcement failure).
- `[baycdiscord2022]` — see `citations.bib` (April 2022 BAYC Discord webhook compromise).
- `[fortunebaycjune2022]` — see `citations.bib` (June 2022 BAYC community-manager Discord compromise; \~\$320K loss).
- `[certikpremint2022]` — see `citations.bib` (Premint platform compromise; mint-flow front-end injection; \~\$375K–\$421K loss).
- `[theblock2022boredape]` — see `citations.bib` (Lawliet phishing case; phishing-context adjacency to T12.002 entry vectors).

## Discussion

T12.002 is the cleanest "identity-confusion" Technique in OAK v0.1 and is the natural counterpart to `OAK-T4.005` (authorisation-misuse) at the NFT layer. The boundary the T12 Tactic page draws is intentional: a single Discord-compromise campaign typically distributes both T12.002 mint-payment scams and T4.005 approval-drainer flows from the same phishing dApp, but the defender controls differ — T12.002 lives at the marketplace and verification-badge layer, T4.005 lives at the wallet operator-allowlist layer. Contributors writing future T12.002 cases should preserve the T12.002-vs-T4.005 boundary explicitly: classify by *which control would have prevented the loss* (marketplace canonical-contract allowlist → T12.002; wallet operator allowlist + approval simulation → T4.005), not by the entry vector.

Open question for v0.x: whether to split T12.002 into separate sub-Techniques per sub-pattern (a) / (b) / (c). The current single-Technique framing reflects the shared identity-confusion predicate and the largely-overlapping mitigation surface (canonical-contract allowlists protect against all three sub-patterns). A split would be warranted if defender controls diverge meaningfully across sub-patterns in future marketplace architecture (e.g., if mint-page-allowlisting at the wallet layer becomes a distinct control class from marketplace-listing allowlisting).

Marketplace moderation is uneven across venues. OpenSea has the most public documentation of its detection pipeline (image-recognition + human review since May 2022); Magic Eden's January 2023 incident demonstrated that even an explicit verified-collection allowlist can be defeated by indexer bugs that bypass the allowlist check; Blur's counterfeit-detection pipeline is less publicly documented as of v0.1 and is an open contribution target for future versions.
docs.techniques/T12.003-royalty-bypass-market:
# OAK-T12.003 — Royalty Bypass / Marketplace Manipulation

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T12 (NFT-Specific Patterns)
**Maturity:** observed
**Chains:** EVM (primary; Ethereum / Polygon / other ERC-721-bearing chains)
**First documented:** 2022 (Blur launch and the LooksRare / X2Y2 royalty-optional shift); structural problem ongoing
**Aliases:** "royalty stripping", "royalty-optional trading", "marketplace royalty disregard"

## Description

A class of NFT marketplace and transfer-flow patterns in which trades settle without paying the creator's stated royalty rate, despite the collection's on-chain metadata or marketplace listing claiming a royalty. From a defender's perspective — particularly a creator-side defender — T12.003 is the structural consequence of a **standard-vs-enforcement gap**: the canonical NFT royalty interface (ERC-2981) is a metadata standard that lets a contract advertise a royalty recipient and basis-points rate, but it does not bind the taker side of a trade to actually pay that royalty. Enforcement is delegated to the marketplace contract that mediates the trade, and marketplaces have made divergent policy choices.

T12.003 is broader than a single "marketplace policy mismatch" event. The Technique covers the cluster of structural and tactical patterns that together produce the gap:

- **Marketplace-policy bypass at the contract layer** — marketplace contracts whose default order-execution path does not query a royalty registry, or queries it and routes a customizable (zero-permissible) fraction to the creator. Blur's late-2022 launch is the canonical instance: the marketplace shipped with a recommended-minimum royalty (0.5% on most collections, with collection-specific overrides such as the higher BAYC default) and a buyer-customizable royalty input that, on collections without enforcement coupling, can be reduced to zero.
- **Allowlist-bypass via direct transfers or alternative routers** — creator-side enforcement attempts that block listing on non-paying marketplaces (notably OpenSea's Operator Filter Registry, ERC-721C-style enforcement contracts) can be bypassed by transferring NFTs through paths that do not trigger the gating predicate, or by marketplaces that deliberately do not interact with the gating registry.
- **Standard-side under-specification** — ERC-2981 deliberately scopes itself to royalty *information* and explicitly leaves payment-binding outside its scope; this is a design choice that creators relying on royalty income may not understand at deployment time.

T12.003 is structurally more "industry policy problem" than "exploit", and the OAK position is non-partisan: a marketplace that ships zero-royalty trading is not necessarily an attacker (many argue it is a feature for traders, and the legal status of off-chain royalty obligations is contested). T12.003 is in OAK because (a) creators rely on royalty income as a primary revenue model, (b) the bypass patterns are well-documented and exploited at scale, and (c) the defensive question — *how should creators protect royalty income on-chain* — is a real, ongoing engineering problem.

## Observed indicators

- Per-collection effective royalty rate trending toward zero across the volume mix as a non-trivial share of trades migrates to royalty-optional venues.
- Trade execution through marketplace contracts whose order-execution path does not contain an `onERC721Received`-time or pre-transfer royalty-query call against the collection's ERC-2981 entry point.
- Direct `transferFrom` / `safeTransferFrom` paths between non-marketplace addresses that follow a privately-matched off-marketplace deal, with no associated royalty payment.
- Marketplace router contracts that interpose between the user's wallet and the canonical marketplace contract in a way that bypasses an Operator-Filter-Registry-class gating check.

## Detection signals

- **Per-collection royalty-payment-rate metric** — for a defender or analyst tracking a single collection, compute realized-royalty / nominal-royalty across a rolling window of trades, broken down by marketplace. The drop from \~100% (full enforcement, OpenSea pre-Blur) to materially lower rates as volume migrates to royalty-optional venues is the load-bearing collection-level signal.
- **Cross-marketplace volume distribution** — track the share of a collection's trade volume settling through each marketplace contract. Migration of a high share to zero-royalty or customizable-royalty marketplaces is the primary cohort-level antecedent of revenue loss for the creator.
- **Operator Filter Registry status** — for collections that opted into the Operator Filter Registry, monitor the registry state for the collection (filtered operators, registrant updates, post-Aug-2023 enforcement-mode change as the registry was deprecated by OpenSea).
- **Direct-transfer-to-trade ratio** — at the per-collection level, an unusual share of `transferFrom` calls without an associated marketplace-contract context can indicate off-marketplace settlement, although false-positive rate is high (legitimate gifting, custodial moves).

## Real-world examples

- **Blur — Ethereum — launched 2022-10-19** — Blur shipped as a pro-trader-focused NFT marketplace with a recommended-minimum royalty of 0.5% on most collections (with higher per-collection defaults — for example BAYC at 8%) and a buyer-customizable royalty input. A non-trivial share of Ethereum NFT volume migrated to Blur in the months following launch, and the effective realized royalty rate at the cohort level dropped materially relative to OpenSea's pre-2022 enforcement baseline. Sources: Blur public documentation; cohort-scale framing per `[chainalysis2022nft]`.
- **OpenSea Operator Filter Registry — Ethereum — Nov 2022 to Feb 2024** — OpenSea introduced the Operator Filter Registry in November 2022 as a creator-side enforcement mechanism: collections that opted in could block listing on non-royalty-enforcing marketplaces. Ownership of the registry was transferred to a CORI-controlled multisig in early 2023. On 2023-08-17, OpenSea announced sunset of its enforcement of the Operator Filter starting 2023-08-31; a grace period continued enforcement for already-opted-in collections (and non-Ethereum chains) through 2024-02-29, after which Operator-Filter-Registry-style enforcement on the OpenSea side ended. Sources: `[openseaoperatorfilter2022]`.
- **ERC-2981 standard** — finalized as Final in July 2021. The standard is deliberately scoped to royalty *information* (a `royaltyInfo(tokenId, salePrice)` query interface) and explicitly does not bind taker-side payment; the standard text notes that compliance is voluntary on the part of the entity executing the sale. Source: `[eip2981]`.
- [`examples/2022-11-x2y2-looksrare-royalty-optional.md`](../examples/2022-11-x2y2-looksrare-royalty-optional.md) — X2Y2 + LooksRare coordinated royalty-optional shift; canonical marketplace-level T12.003 case.
- [`examples/2023-08-opensea-operator-filter-sunset.md`](../examples/2023-08-opensea-operator-filter-sunset.md) — OpenSea Operator Filter Registry sunset; documents the end of platform-side royalty-enforcement leverage.

## Reference implementations

T12.003's defensive surface is primarily marketplace-side rather than detector-side. `mg-detectors-rs` does not currently surface T12.003 as a detection class — coverage **gap** at v0.1, and the Technique is structurally a poor fit for an operator-behaviour detector because the "bypass" is a marketplace contract executing exactly as designed, not a deviation from expected behaviour.

The relevant reference-implementation surface is on the enforcement side: ERC-721C (LimitBreak), the Operator Filter Registry contracts (CORI multisig as of v0.1), and creator-side royalty-routing patterns (split contracts, payment-binding wrappers). NFT analytics vendors (e.g., Nansen, Chainalysis, dappradar) publish per-collection royalty-payment-rate metrics that operationalize the detection-signal layer.

## Mitigations

- **Creators (collection deployers):** at deployment, decide explicitly whether the collection's royalty model assumes voluntary marketplace cooperation (ERC-2981 metadata only) or requires enforcement coupling (Operator-Filter-Registry-style allowlist, ERC-721C-style enforcement, or a successor standard such as the EIP-7572-class contract-level metadata that a marketplace can read for policy hints). Track Operator Filter Registry status for the collection and budget for the policy-shift risk demonstrated by the OpenSea sunset.
- **Marketplaces (highest-leverage layer):** the policy choice is genuine and contested; OAK does not prescribe a position. Marketplaces that implement royalty enforcement should publish their enforcement methodology (registry queried, gating predicate, fallback behaviour); marketplaces that ship royalty-optional trading should publish that policy clearly so creators deciding on a deployment can make an informed choice.
- **NFT analytics platforms:** publish per-collection effective-royalty-rate metrics on a recurring cadence; the cohort-scale framing in `[chainalysis2022nft]` and per-marketplace volume share are well-established and creators rely on these to monitor revenue.
- **Standards bodies:** future standards work on royalty-binding (a successor to ERC-2981 that constrains taker-side payment, or contract-level metadata standards such as EIP-7572 that improve marketplace-side policy interpretation) is the structural path forward; OAK tracks proposals as the public record matures.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2022nft]` — cohort-scale framing for NFT-marketplace volume migration during the 2021–2022 cohort.
- `[blurzeroroyalty2022]` — Blur launch and the customizable-royalty mechanism.
- `[openseaoperatorfilter2022]` — Operator Filter Registry launch (Nov 2022), CORI multisig transfer, August 2023 sunset announcement, February 2024 grace-period end.
- `[eip2981]` — ERC-2981 NFT Royalty Standard text and the explicit standard-side note that taker-side payment is not bound.

## Discussion

T12.003 is the most policy-ambiguous Technique in the T12 Tactic, and OAK takes a deliberately non-partisan editorial position. Many marketplaces that ship zero-royalty or royalty-optional trading argue that the on-chain enforceability gap is a feature, not a bypass — creators were never on-chain-guaranteed royalty income; the ERC-2981 standard text itself acknowledges this; and the migration of trader volume to royalty-optional venues is the market resolving an ambiguous prior policy. The opposing view is that creator royalty income was a load-bearing assumption of the 2021 NFT cohort and that marketplaces deliberately routing around the established enforcement mechanism (the Operator Filter Registry) are exploiting a coordination problem at creators' expense.

OAK documents T12.003 because the **defensive question is real** regardless of the policy ambiguity: a creator deploying a new collection in 2026 needs to make an informed decision about which enforcement model their revenue assumptions depend on, and a defender evaluating a collection's revenue durability needs to track per-collection effective-royalty-rate. Both surfaces are well-established in the public record; OAK's role is to document the technique class and the detection signals without prescribing the underlying policy answer.

T12.003 sits adjacent to but distinct from T7.004 (NFT Wash-Laundering) and T12.001 (NFT Wash-Trade Volume Inflation): all three involve marketplace-flow analysis, but T12.003's load-bearing observation is the standard-vs-enforcement gap rather than the self-financed trade pattern. Contributors writing future T12.003 examples should preserve the policy-vs-attack framing rather than collapsing the Technique into a "zero-royalty-marketplaces-are-bad" narrative.
# OAK-T13.001 — Paymaster Compromise

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T13 (Account Abstraction Attacks)
**Maturity:** observed
**Chains:** EVM (ERC-4337 EntryPoint v0.6 / v0.7 / v0.8 / v0.9 deployments); EVM L2s with native account abstraction (zkSync Era, Starknet) where the paymaster role is analogous; conceptually portable to any chain whose execution model separates gas-sponsorship validation from execution
**First documented:** 2023 (Alchemy / OpenZeppelin disclosure of the UserOperation packing inconsistency in EntryPoint v0.6 affecting `VerifyingPaymaster`); class characterised continuously through 2024–2026 in audit-firm advisories on deployed paymasters
**Aliases:** "paymaster drain", "sponsorship policy bypass", "postOp griefing", "paymaster DoS", "validatePaymasterUserOp bypass", "gasless-transaction abuse"

## Description

Umbrella entry for the paymaster-compromise class under ERC-4337 account abstraction. The paymaster is the on-chain component that sponsors gas for user operations (UserOps) — its compromise surface concentrates several distinct failure modes that were grouped under a single Technique in pre-v0.1 drafts but are split into four sub-Techniques at v0.1 because their mitigation surfaces are structurally distinct.

T13.001 sub-Techniques:

- **OAK-T13.001.001 — Paymaster Accounting Drain.** The paymaster releases sponsored funds in excess of what its accounting model accounts for — typically because validation-time payment is not unwound when `postOp` reverts, or because `postOp` revert paths are not enumerated against the gas-token mechanic (fee-on-transfer, rebasing, pausable / blacklist).
- **OAK-T13.001.002 — Paymaster Policy Bypass.** UserOps bypass the paymaster's sponsorship rules — per-user quotas, per-application allowlists, per-method gating, per-token spending limits — typically via off-chain-signer / on-chain-hash parity violations (Alchemy 2023 UserOperation-packing disclosure) or fail-open instruction parsers in the policy engine (Kora / Token-2022 advisory).
- **OAK-T13.001.003 — Paymaster Reentrancy.** A paymaster-specific instance of the T9.005 class restricted to the `validatePaymasterUserOp` / `postOp` call surface — the paymaster makes an external call before sponsorship-accounting state is finalised, and is re-entered via a token hook or peer-protocol callback.
- **OAK-T13.001.004 — Paymaster Griefing.** UserOps that drain the paymaster's deposited balance without producing useful protocol activity — gas-limit abuse against unused-gas-penalty mechanics, temporary-revert conditions in target contracts, bundle-level griefing against bundler-reputation budgets.

T13.001 (the umbrella) is retained as a Technique ID because the four sub-Techniques share a single contract surface (the paymaster), a single specification anchor (ERC-4337 `[erc4337spec]`), and a substantially overlapping mitigation literature (`[ozaa4337audit]`, `[osecpaymasters2025]`, `[tobsixmistakes2026]`, `[aviggiano4337checklist]`). Defenders evaluating a deployed paymaster should treat all four sub-Techniques as a single review surface.

## Architectural distinction from T9

T13.001 is **architecturally distinct** from the smart-contract-exploit Tactic (T9):

- vs. **T9.004 (Access-Control Misconfiguration):** the failure is not a missing or mis-scoped role; the paymaster is exercising exactly the role it was deployed to exercise (sponsoring UserOps), but its sponsorship decision was reached against incomplete or bypassable validation.
- vs. **T9.005 (Reentrancy):** sub-Technique T13.001.003 is a strict subset of T9.005 in mechanism, but the call surface (`validatePaymasterUserOp` / `postOp`) and the storage surface (paymaster sponsorship accounting, deposited-balance ledger at the EntryPoint) are specific to the ERC-4337 contract layout.
- vs. **T9.001 (Oracle Price Manipulation):** the oracle case is a price-feed corruption; T13.001 is a sponsorship-decision corruption against an otherwise-correct economic input.

## Real-world examples

- [`examples/2025-04-erc4337-paymaster.md`](../examples/2025-04-erc4337-paymaster.md) — ERC-4337 paymaster compromise cohort

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (operator-behaviour scope; paymaster-specific call-graph monitoring is out of scope).
- Pre-deployment vendor coverage: audit firms with explicit ERC-4337 specialist practice — OpenZeppelin, Trail of Bits, Quantstamp, OSEC / OtterSec, Halborn, ConsenSys Diligence, Veridise (where ZK paymasters interact with circuit verification). Public reference: `[aviggiano4337checklist]`.
- Runtime / operational coverage: bundler-vendor reputation systems (Pimlico, Stackup, Alchemy, Biconomy) provide partial T13.001.004 coverage; deployed-contract monitoring (Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender, BlockSec PhalconHQ) can be tuned for paymaster-specific call-graph reentrancy and balance-drawdown signatures.

## Citations

- `[erc4337spec]` — ERC-4337 specification (canonical reference for the threat surface).
- `[ozaa4337audit]` — OpenZeppelin's Ethereum-Foundation engagement on EIP-4337.
- `[osecpaymasters2025]` — OSEC / OtterSec 2025 paymaster security review.
- `[tobsixmistakes2026]` — Trail of Bits "Six mistakes in ERC-4337 smart accounts" essay.
- `[aviggiano4337checklist]` — community-maintained ERC-4337 audit checklist.
- See sub-Technique pages T13.001.001 through T13.001.004 for sub-case-specific citations.

## Discussion

T13.001 was a single Technique through pre-v0.1 drafts; v0.1 splits it into four sub-Techniques because the mitigation surfaces are structurally distinct: accounting-drain (T13.001.001) requires `postOp` revert-path enumeration and gas-token-mechanic auditing; policy-bypass (T13.001.002) requires hash-parity verification and deny-by-default policy-engine semantics; reentrancy (T13.001.003) requires guards on the validation / `postOp` surface as a state-sharing set; griefing (T13.001.004) requires EntryPoint-version pinning and bundler-reputation monitoring. Treating them as one Technique loses the per-sub-case audit checklist; treating them as fully independent Techniques duplicates the shared paymaster-surface context. The umbrella-with-sub-Techniques structure preserves both.

Contributors writing future paymaster-incident worked examples should map them to the most specific sub-Technique; cases that exercise multiple sub-Techniques in a single chain should list each.
docs.techniques/T13.001.001-paymaster-account:
# OAK-T13.001.001 — Paymaster Accounting Drain

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T13 (Account Abstraction Attacks)
**Maturity:** observed
**Chains:** EVM (ERC-4337 EntryPoint v0.6 / v0.7 / v0.8 / v0.9 deployments); EVM L2s with native account abstraction (zkSync Era, Starknet) where the paymaster role is analogous
**First documented:** 2025 (OSEC paymaster security review enumerates the `postOp` revert / gas-token-mechanic surfaces explicitly); class characterised continuously through audit-firm advisories on deployed paymasters
**Aliases:** "paymaster drain", "postOp revert drain", "gas-token-mechanic accounting drain", "validation-time-debit not unwound"

## Description

The attacker submits UserOps that abuse the paymaster's accounting model to extract sponsored funds beyond what the paymaster intended. The canonical worked sub-pattern: a paymaster pays the EntryPoint from a shared pool during `validatePaymasterUserOp` and attempts to charge the individual user back in `postOp`; if `postOp` reverts (bad state, arithmetic overflow, risky external call, fee-on-transfer or rebasing ERC-20 used as gas token), the validation-time payment is **not** unwound and the paymaster has subsidised a UserOp it did not intend to subsidise `[osecpaymasters2025]`.

The failure mode is that the paymaster's `postOp` is the only path that can charge the user back, but `postOp` can revert for reasons that the paymaster's author did not enumerate at design time — particularly when the gas-token mechanic includes fee-on-transfer (T1.005), rebasing, or pausable / blacklist (T1.004) surfaces. Each revert-path that goes un-enumerated is an accounting-drain surface.

T13.001.001 is part of the OAK-T13.001 (Paymaster Compromise) umbrella; see the parent for the architectural distinction from T9.

## Observed indicators

- A paymaster whose `postOp` can revert (arithmetic, risky external call, fee-on-transfer or rebasing ERC-20 gas token, pausable / blacklist token) without unwinding the validation-time deposit-balance debit.
- A paymaster that supports ERC-20-denominated gas payment without an explicit allowlist of audited gas tokens.
- Drift between the paymaster's deposited balance at the EntryPoint and its accounted-for sponsorship volume — the deposited balance trends downward faster than the accounting model predicts.
- A paymaster contract whose `postOp` arithmetic does not use checked operations (or whose `unchecked` blocks span the user-charge path).

## Detection signals

- **Pre-deployment paymaster contract audit.** Engagement with audit firms with explicit ERC-4337 specialist practice (OpenZeppelin, Trail of Bits, Quantstamp, OSEC / OtterSec, Halborn, ConsenSys Diligence). A paymaster that has not been audited by a 4337-specialist firm is a T13.001.001 surface independently of the underlying EntryPoint version.
- **`postOp` revert-safety review.** Enumerate every revert path in `postOp` (arithmetic, external-call, ERC-20 token-mechanic — fee-on-transfer, rebasing, blacklist / pausable T1.004 surfaces); verify that no revert path allows a validation-time debit to remain un-unwound.
- **Gas-token allowlist verification.** Audit the allowlist of ERC-20 tokens accepted as gas-payment denomination; reject tokens with fee-on-transfer (T1.005), rebasing, pausable, or blacklist mechanics unless the paymaster implements explicit accounting that handles them.
- **Paymaster-fund-balance monitoring.** Runtime metric: paymaster's deposited balance at the EntryPoint, sampled per block, with alerts on drawdown rate exceeding the paymaster's expected sponsorship-throughput envelope. Persistent accounting drain produces a drawdown signature that is statistically distinguishable from organic sponsorship activity.
- **Per-UserOp policy-rule audit.** For high-value paymasters, log per-sponsored UserOp the gas-token used and the `postOp` outcome (success / revert path); reconcile against the paymaster's deposited-balance trajectory; mismatches indicate either accounting-drain (T13.001.001) or policy-bypass (T13.001.002).

## Real-world examples

- [`examples/2025-04-erc4337-paymaster.md`](../examples/2025-04-erc4337-paymaster.md) — ERC-4337 paymaster compromise cohort, April 2025 anchor (~$500K+ drain). The anchor incident centred on a sub-case (a)/(b) hybrid where the `postOp` charge-back path failed to recover the validation-time deposit-balance debit against an attacker-drained smart account — the canonical T13.001.001 drawdown signature.
- **OSEC 2025 paymaster review — class characterisation — 2025** — OSEC / OtterSec's 2025 paymaster security review enumerates the `postOp` revert and gas-token-mechanic accounting-drain surfaces with concrete worked patterns. The advisory is the canonical reference for the T13.001.001 sub-case at v0.1; the surface is well-characterised in audit-firm literature even though no single named large-loss incident has been catalogued in public forensic write-ups `[osecpaymasters2025]`.
- **Cohort framing — 2023 onward** — OAK does not at v0.1 name a single canonical large-loss T13.001.001 incident comparable to T9.001 or T10.001; publicly-disclosed paymaster-accounting-drain flaws have to date been caught at the audit-firm or bug-bounty layer rather than via headline exploits. Contributors with public forensic write-ups of specific named incidents whose primary failure mode is paymaster accounting drain are encouraged to submit them via PR.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (paymaster-specific accounting monitoring is out of MG operator-behaviour scope).
- Pre-deployment vendor coverage: ERC-4337 specialist audit firms (see umbrella T13.001 for the canonical list).
- Runtime / operational coverage: deployed-contract monitoring (Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender, BlockSec PhalconHQ) tuned for paymaster balance-drawdown signatures; bundler-vendor reputation systems do not directly cover this sub-case.

## Mitigations

- **Paymaster architects:** treat `postOp` revert-path enumeration as a structural review item; verify no revert path leaves a validation-time debit un-unwound; either reject ERC-20 gas tokens with fee-on-transfer / rebasing / pausable / blacklist mechanics or implement explicit accounting that handles them; use checked arithmetic on the user-charge path; if the paymaster supports ERC-20-denominated gas payment, audit the gas-token mechanic for fee-on-transfer (T1.005) and pausable / blacklist (T1.004) surfaces.
- **Paymaster operators:** monitor deposited-balance drawdown against expected sponsorship throughput; reconcile per-UserOp `postOp` outcome against the balance trajectory; pause sponsorship if drawdown rate exceeds a threshold (circuit breaker).
- **Risk teams:** when reviewing a new paymaster integration, require: (1) an audit report from an ERC-4337-specialist firm covering `postOp` revert-safety, (2) the gas-token allowlist with explicit handling of fee-on-transfer / rebasing / pausable / blacklist tokens, (3) the deposited-balance monitoring posture and circuit-breaker threshold.

## Citations

- `[erc4337spec]` — ERC-4337 specification; canonical reference for `validatePaymasterUserOp` / `postOp` semantics and the deposit-balance accounting model.
- `[osecpaymasters2025]` — OSEC / OtterSec 2025 paymaster security review; canonical worked enumeration of `postOp` revert and gas-token mechanic surfaces (T13.001.001 anchor).
- `[ozaa4337audit]` — OpenZeppelin's Ethereum-Foundation engagement on EIP-4337; covers paymaster-accounting issues among the surfaced 7+ high-severity items.
- `[tobsixmistakes2026]` — Trail of Bits "Six mistakes in ERC-4337 smart accounts" essay; recurring failure-mode catalogue.
- `[aviggiano4337checklist]` — community-maintained ERC-4337 audit checklist.
- See cross-references under T1.004 (pausable / blacklist gas-token surface), T1.005 (fee-on-transfer gas-token surface).

## Discussion

T13.001.001 is the most operationally-relevant sub-Technique for paymaster operators because it can occur silently — a paymaster slowly leaks deposited balance over many sponsored UserOps and the operator does not notice until the balance is drained. Pre-deployment audit catches the design-time issue; runtime balance-monitoring catches the production-time exploitation; both are required.

The cross-cuts to T1.004 and T1.005 are deliberate: a paymaster that accepts arbitrary ERC-20 gas tokens inherits the entire token-genesis adversarial surface as a paymaster-accounting surface. This is the v0.1 strongest argument for treating gas-token allowlists as a hard control rather than a configuration choice.
docs.techniques/T13.001.002-paymaster-policy-:
# OAK-T13.001.002 — Paymaster Policy Bypass

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T13 (Account Abstraction Attacks)
**Maturity:** observed
**Chains:** EVM (ERC-4337 EntryPoint v0.6 / v0.7 / v0.8 / v0.9 deployments); EVM L2s with native account abstraction; Solana (analogous fee-payer / Token-2022 paymaster surfaces — Kora advisory)
**First documented:** 2023 (Alchemy / OpenZeppelin disclosure of the UserOperation-packing inconsistency in EntryPoint v0.6 affecting `VerifyingPaymaster`); Solana / Token-2022 analogue documented 2025 (Kora paymaster advisory)
**Aliases:** "sponsorship policy bypass", "validatePaymasterUserOp bypass", "off-chain-signer / on-chain-hash parity violation", "fail-open instruction parser"

## Description

The attacker constructs UserOps that bypass the paymaster's sponsorship rules — per-user quotas, per-application allowlists, per-method gating, per-token spending limits. The mechanism is corruption of the *sponsorship decision*: the paymaster believes the UserOp matches an authorised pattern, but the UserOp's actual on-chain effect differs from what the policy engine evaluated.

Two canonical worked patterns:

- **Off-chain-signer / on-chain-hash parity violation.** A `VerifyingPaymaster`'s off-chain signer reasons about a UserOp field representation that disagrees with the on-chain `pack` method. The 2023 Alchemy / OpenZeppelin UserOperation-packing disclosure is the canonical example: the on-chain `pack` method covered the dynamic `paymasterAndData` field while the `VerifyingPaymaster.pack` did not, producing colliding hashes across UserOps and divergent hashes for the same UserOp depending on signing method `[alchemyuoppack2023]`. Any policy enforced via the off-chain hash is bypassable when the hashes diverge.
- **Fail-open instruction parser.** A paymaster sponsorship policy is implemented by a parser that walks UserOp instructions and evaluates each against allowlist / quota / method-gate rules. When the parser encounters an unknown instruction or extension, it builds an empty stub and silently ignores the instruction; any future extension automatically bypasses the policy until explicitly added to the parser. The Kora paymaster (Solana / Token-2022) advisory characterises this pattern explicitly — the parser's unknown-instruction default was permissive rather than deny-by-default `[dailycvekora2025]`.

T13.001.002 is part of the OAK-T13.001 (Paymaster Compromise) umbrella; see the parent for the architectural distinction from T9.

## Observed indicators

- A `VerifyingPaymaster` whose off-chain signer constructs UserOp hash bytes by a different rule than the on-chain `pack` method (most commonly: differing handling of dynamic fields `initCode`, `callData`, `paymasterAndData`).
- A paymaster sponsorship policy implemented by an instruction-parser whose unknown-instruction default is a permissive empty stub rather than a deny-by-default reject.
- A paymaster contract that does not enforce per-call gas caps, per-user quotas, or per-application allowlists at the validation boundary, but relies on off-chain rate-limiting only.
- A paymaster whose policy-evaluation surface accepts ERC-20 token-extension fields (Token-2022 transfer-fee, transfer-hook, permanent-delegate) without explicit allowlist semantics.

## Detection signals

- **Hash-parity verification.** Verify hash-construction parity between the on-chain `UserOperationLib.pack` and any off-chain signer producing `VerifyingPaymaster` authorisations. A deviation of even a single dynamic field is a T13.001.002 surface.
- **Policy-engine fail-closed verification.** Audit the policy engine for unknown-instruction handling; require a deny-by-default rather than a fail-open empty-stub default; require explicit allowlist semantics and reject-by-default semantics for any new instruction type or token extension.
- **Per-UserOp policy-rule audit.** For high-value paymasters, log the policy-rule decision per sponsored UserOp (which rule matched, which quota was decremented) and reconcile the log against the UserOp's actual on-chain effect; mismatches indicate policy-bypass.
- **EntryPoint-version pinning verification.** Confirm the `VerifyingPaymaster` is deployed against an EntryPoint version where the UserOp-packing parity issue is closed (≥v0.6 patched build); pin the EntryPoint version assumed by the off-chain signer and re-verify parity on every EntryPoint upgrade.
- **Pre-deployment audit.** Engagement with audit firms with explicit ERC-4337 specialist practice; treat `validatePaymasterUserOp` and the off-chain signer (where applicable) as the primary review surfaces.

## Real-world examples

- [`examples/2025-04-erc4337-paymaster.md`](../examples/2025-04-erc4337-paymaster.md) — ERC-4337 paymaster compromise cohort, April 2025 anchor. The anchor incident exercised T13.001.002 sub-case (off-chain-signer / on-chain-hash parity violation) — a deployed `VerifyingPaymaster`-pattern contract whose off-chain signer reasoned about a different UserOp byte representation than the on-chain `pack` method, allowing crafted UserOps to clear `validatePaymasterUserOp` while encoding execution paths the off-chain policy would have rejected.
- **UserOperation-packing inconsistency — EntryPoint v0.6 / `VerifyingPaymaster` — 2023-03 (canonical case)** — Alchemy and OpenZeppelin disclosed that the on-chain `UserOperationLib.pack` method captured all UserOp fields including the dynamic ones (`initCode`, `callData`, `paymasterAndData`), while the `VerifyingPaymaster.pack` method excluded `paymasterAndData`. The hash representations therefore disagreed, allowing different UserOps to share a hash and the same UserOp to produce divergent hashes depending on signing method. Patched in EntryPoint v0.6+; the disclosure is the canonical worked example for the off-chain-signer / on-chain-hash parity class `[alchemyuoppack2023]`.
- **Kora paymaster — Solana / Token-2022 — 2025 (canonical fail-open case)** — The Kora paymaster's policy-engine instruction-parser had a permissive empty-stub default for unknown Token-2022 extensions; the daily-CVE advisory walked through the bypass mechanism in detail. Canonical worked example for the fail-open instruction-parser sub-case `[dailycvekora2025]`.
- **Cohort framing — 2023 onward** — Both canonical cases were caught at the audit-firm layer rather than in production exploits; OpenZeppelin's three-audit Ethereum-Foundation engagement on EIP-4337 surfaced 7+ high-severity issues across the EntryPoint and reference paymasters including policy-bypass surfaces `[ozaa4337audit]`. Quantstamp audited Alchemy's v0.6 and v0.7 paymaster implementations `[quantstampalchemypm]`. Trail of Bits' six-mistakes essay characterises the recurring class across smart-account and paymaster engagements `[tobsixmistakes2026]`.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1.
- Pre-deployment vendor coverage: ERC-4337 specialist audit firms (see umbrella T13.001).
- Runtime / operational coverage: per-UserOp policy-rule audit logging is paymaster-operator-side; no widely-deployed external runtime detector specifically targets policy-bypass.

## Mitigations
