- **Curve Finance frontend DNS hijack — Ethereum — 2022-08** — Approximately \$575K drained from users who interacted with a cloned Curve UI served from compromised DNS (the underlying nameserver at registrar `iwantmyname` was the compromise vector, not the Curve registrar account). Users on the cloned UI were prompted to approve a malicious contract; proceeds were subsequently moved to centralized exchanges and Tornado Cash. Curve responded by redirecting `.fi` to neutral nameservers and operating from `curve.finance` until stabilised. Forensic write-up: `[rektcurve2022]`.
- **Balancer frontend BGP/DNS hijack — Ethereum — 2023-09-19/20** — A social-engineering attack on EuroDNS (the registrar for the `.fi` TLD) allowed nameserver records for `balancer.fi` to be updated; a forked phishing front-end then prompted users to approve token transfers via Angel Drainer phishing contracts. SlowMist also identified BGP hijacking against the upstream IP space as a parallel vector. Total loss approximately \$238K before Balancer regained domain control at 17:45 UTC on September 20. The Balancer DAO subsequently announced `.fi` domains as structurally unsafe given the EuroDNS exposure. <!-- TODO: cite https://medium.com/balancer-protocol/dns-security-incident-post-mortem-1b1feb735aca -->
- **Galxe DNS hijack — multi-chain — 2023-10** — A social-engineering attack on Galxe's DNS provider replaced the canonical front-end with a phishing surface; users were prompted to sign approvals/permits to attacker-controlled spenders. Per-incident on-chain trace and post-mortem: `[galxepostmortem2023]`, `[peckshieldgalxe2023]`, `[rektgalxe2023]`. Loss in the low single-digit millions; the incident is the canonical 2023 reference for "registrar account compromised, dApp domain repointed" and is structurally identical to the Curve and Balancer cases.
- **CoinStats Snap supply-chain — multi-chain — 2024-06** — `[coinstats20240622]`, `[slowmistcoinstats2024]` document a supply-chain compromise of the CoinStats portfolio-tracker mobile/desktop dApp surface; signing prompts originating from inside the trusted-app surface led approximately 1,590 wallets to sign malicious payloads. T4.002-adjacent: the compromise was at the dApp client distribution layer rather than DNS, but the structural signal (trusted-surface mismatch with canonical interaction pattern) is the same.
- **DEXX exchange-extension drainer — multi-chain — 2024-10/11** — `[dexxstatements2024]`, `[slowmistdexx2024]`, `[zachxbtdexx2024]` document the DEXX trading-extension compromise: signing prompts originating from inside the DEXX surface authorised attacker-controlled token transfers across the user base. Per `[peckshielddexx2024]`, the compromise sat at the operator-infrastructure layer rather than user-side phishing, making it a T4.002-class signal at the cohort-attribution level.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2021-12-badgerdao.md`](../examples/2021-12-badgerdao.md) — BadgerDAO frontend compromise via Cloudflare Workers
- [`examples/2022-07-premint-phishing.md`](../examples/2022-07-premint-phishing.md) — Premint NFT-allowlist platform front-end JavaScript injection
- [`examples/2022-08-curve-dns-hijack.md`](../examples/2022-08-curve-dns-hijack.md) — Curve Finance frontend DNS hijack
- [`examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md`](../examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md) — Inferno Drainer service ecosystem
- [`examples/2023-2026-fake-dex-clone-frontend-cohort.md`](../examples/2023-2026-fake-dex-clone-frontend-cohort.md) — Fake-DEX clone-frontend cohort (proposed T4.x candidate; structurally distinct from T4.002 — the legitimate platform is never compromised; the entire UI is hosted on a typosquatted domain and reached via paid Google / X ads or App Store wrappers).
- [`examples/2025-02-solana-x-account-compromise-cohort.md`](../examples/2025-02-solana-x-account-compromise-cohort.md) — Solana brand-X-account compromise cohort (Jupiter / Pump.fun / DogWifCoin). **Adjacent cohort, not T4.002 proper** — the trust-substrate compromised is the brand's social-media announcement channel rather than the protocol's own front-end / DNS / signing surface; proposed T4.x brand-X-account-compromise sub-class.

## Reference implementations

- MG `mg-detectors-rs` — partial coverage (link TBD): per-protocol grantee-baseline drift detection.
- Wallet vendors — partial coverage via grantee allowlists; OAK-M30 per-dApp-domain allowlists are the highest-leverage user-side intervention.
- DNS/BGP monitoring: Cloudflare Radar, RIPE NCC route monitors, Spaceshift / DNSSEC enforcement, and registry-lock at registrar level.

## Mitigations

- **OAK-M30 (Per-dApp Domain and App-Store-Package Allowlist):** highest-leverage user-side layer — pin canonical dApp domains and content hashes; warn on signing prompts originating from non-allowlisted origins.
- **OAK-M18 (Out-of-Band Destination Verification):** verify destination contract addresses against canonical protocol-published lists before signing any permit.
- **OAK-M31 (EIP-712 Permit Display and Signing Risk Heuristics):** surface signed-payload spender vs canonical-protocol-spender mismatch.
- **OAK-M19 (Air-Gap Cold Wallet Signing):** for treasury-class wallets, route signing through air-gapped device that displays full permit scope independently of the dApp surface.
- **OAK-M40 (Supply-Chain Package Integrity):** for the dApp-distribution-layer subclass (CoinStats, DEXX), package-integrity verification covers the same signal at the build-and-distribute layer.
- **Protocols:** publish canonical contract addresses in machine-readable form; integrate with wallet allowlists; apply registry-lock and DNSSEC on critical protocol domains; pin frontend via ENS+IPFS content hash with multi-sig update authority.

## Citations

- `[rektcurve2022]` — see `citations.bib` (Curve Finance DNS hijack, August 2022).
- `[galxepostmortem2023]`, `[peckshieldgalxe2023]`, `[rektgalxe2023]` — see `citations.bib` (Galxe DNS hijack, October 2023).
- `[coinstats20240622]`, `[slowmistcoinstats2024]`, `[zachxbtcoinstats2024]`, `[blocksec_coinstats2024]`, `[peckshieldcoinstats2024]` — see `citations.bib` (CoinStats compromise, June 2024).
- `[dexxstatements2024]`, `[slowmistdexx2024]`, `[zachxbtdexx2024]`, `[peckshielddexx2024]` — see `citations.bib` (DEXX extension compromise, late 2024).
- `[slowmist2024report]` — see `citations.bib` (frontend / phishing-domain ecosystem aggregate).

## Discussion

T4.002 sits at the intersection of on-chain telemetry and traditional infosec; coverage requires inputs from outside the on-chain surface. Coordination with traditional CTI feeds (DNS/BGP/registrar change feeds) is an open integration item.

False-positive reasoning: a single grantee anomaly is noisy because protocols routinely deploy new router contracts (e.g., Uniswap V4 deployment); the high-precision signal is grantee-anomaly + DNS/content-hash drift in the same window. Edge case: a protocol intentionally migrating its front-end during a planned redeploy will trigger the same DNS/hash-drift signal — defenders need an out-of-band confirmation channel (protocol's GitHub release tag, signed announcement) before alerting downstream consumers.

v0.x deferred items: cross-chain frontend-compromise observability (a single dApp's compromise affects users across all chains it supports — current detection is largely per-chain), and frontend-attestation standards (signed-frontend manifests, akin to subresource integrity but at the dApp-bundle level) — adoption is nascent and not yet standardised across the dApp ecosystem.
docs.techniques/T4.003-address-poisoning.md:
# OAK-T4.003 — Address Poisoning

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T4 (Access Acquisition), OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM (primary), Solana, Tron
**First documented:** 2022 (early reports); industrial-scale 2023 onward (Tsuchiya et al. 2025 USENIX Security cohort)
**Aliases:** "zero-value transfer scam", "lookalike-address phishing", "wallet-history poisoning"

## Description

A class of attacks in which an attacker injects transactions involving a lookalike address (visually similar to one of the victim's known counterparties) into the victim's transaction history, with the goal that the victim will subsequently copy-paste the lookalike address from their wallet history and send funds to the attacker rather than the intended counterparty. From a defender's perspective, T4.003 is the largest-volume class of T4-category attacks in production today: `[tsuchiya2025poisoning]` (USENIX Security 2025) characterises approximately 17.3M poisoning transfers observed on Ethereum since April 2023 across three sub-classes (tiny-value transfers, zero-value transfers, counterfeit-token transfers).

T4.003 is structurally distinct from the signature-based T4.001 Permit2 and the allowance-based T4.004 approve-pattern drainers because the attacker does **not** acquire any on-chain authority over the victim's wallet — the attacker only manipulates the victim's wallet history display, relying entirely on the victim's wallet UX (truncated address display, copy-paste from history) and the victim's own subsequent send action. This makes T4.003 a textbook OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion) modifier of T4: the victim authorises the transfer themselves and there is no on-chain authorisation event to detect at the moment of compromise.

## Observed indicators

- Sustained inflow of small-value or zero-value transfers from previously-unseen addresses into a high-balance wallet, often arriving in clusters that match the victim's recent legitimate counterparty pattern.
- Lookalike-address generation pattern: attacker addresses share the first 4–6 characters and last 4 characters with one of the victim's known counterparties (truncated-display attack surface). Vanity-address generators (`profanity`, `vanity-eth`) are the canonical operator tooling.
- Zero-value `Transfer(from, to, 0)` events emitted by ERC-20 contracts naming the victim's address as sender or receiver — used to plant fake history when zero-value direct transfers do not propagate to the wallet UI.
- "Counterfeit token" sub-class: ERC-20 contracts deploying a `Transfer` event with attacker-controlled `from`/`to` parameters that mimic a stable-token symbol (USDT, USDC) but originate from an unaffiliated contract.
- Persistent campaign-cluster funding: Tsuchiya 2025 documents that 17.3M poisoning transfers across the cohort cluster into a small number of operator-funder graphs traceable via OAK-T8.001 (Common-Funder Cluster Reuse) heuristics.

## Detection signals

- Wallet-side (highest leverage, OAK-M29): detect inbound transfers whose source address is structurally similar (first/last-N-character match) to addresses already in the user's send history; surface them as suspicious in the wallet UI rather than mixing them silently into history. Trezor Suite's address-poisoning filter and Rabby / MetaMask "address book mismatch" warnings are reference implementations.
- Per-wallet: monitor for the canonical "tiny-value-transfer / zero-value-transfer / counterfeit-token-transfer" pattern targeting active high-balance wallets over a rolling window.
- Pre-send (OAK-M18 / OAK-M29 intersection): wallet UX intervention — require explicit verification of the *full* destination address (not just the first/last six characters) for sends above a configurable threshold.
- Counterfeit-token detection: cross-reference token contract address against canonical token registries (CoinGecko, Etherscan token lists) at display time; any `Transfer` event from a token claiming a popular symbol but residing at a non-canonical contract is a high-precision flag.
- Post-event forensic: trace the lookalike address back to the campaign cluster and apply OAK-T8.001 (Common-Funder Cluster Reuse) heuristics to identify other potentially-targeted wallets.

## Real-world examples

- **\$68M WBTC poisoning case — Ethereum — 2024-05-03** — A single victim wallet (0x1E227…) initiated a legitimate transfer to counterparty 0xd9A1b at 09:14 UTC; later in the day the victim copy-pasted what they believed was the same address from their wallet history but was in fact a poisoned lookalike (0xd9A1c…), sending approximately \$68M in WBTC to the attacker. The attacker subsequently returned \$68M of the original transfer following a public bounty negotiation; the operator had nonetheless netted approximately \$1.49M from the broader 66-day campaign of which this victim was the most prominent target — a 1,147% ROI on campaign cost per Chainalysis's published forensic analysis. See worked example at `examples/2024-05-address-poisoning-68m.md`. Source: `[chainalysis2024poisoning]`.
- **Cohort context — Ethereum — April 2023 onward** — `[tsuchiya2025poisoning]` characterises approximately 17.3M poisoning transfers across the three sub-classes; the academic cohort is the canonical reference for class scale and per-sub-class detection-methodology calibration. Targeting profile: more active wallets with larger balances, longer transaction histories, and recurring counterparty patterns are disproportionately represented in victim selection.
- **Trezor hardware-wallet user advisory — multi-chain — 2024 onward** — Trezor published an explicit address-poisoning advisory and shipped a Suite-level filtering mechanism that blurs and marks-as-unverified inbound transactions matching the poisoning pattern. The advisory is the canonical hardware-wallet vendor T4.003 acknowledgement and reference implementation of the OAK-M29 mitigation. <!-- TODO: cite https://trezor.io/support/troubleshooting/coins-tokens/address-poisoning-attacks -->
- **ScamSniffer poisoning campaign tracking — Ethereum — 2024-25** — ScamSniffer's per-campaign poisoning trackers attribute mid-six-figure individual losses across 2024-2025 to operator clusters that overlap on funder-graph heuristics with the broader Tsuchiya cohort. <!-- TODO: cite https://drops.scamsniffer.io/ poisoning-campaign-specific tracker -->

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2025-03-solflare-base-x-homograph-cve.md`](../examples/2025-03-solflare-base-x-homograph-cve.md) — Solflare / `base-x` Unicode-homograph CVE-2025-27611. **Adjacent T4.003 sub-class** — library-level address-validation bypass that accepted lookalike (homograph) destination addresses as valid base58, structurally analogous to address-poisoning at the address-rendering / address-validation substrate rather than at the wallet-history-display substrate. Six years undetected in `base-x` (downstream `bs58`, `@solana/web3.js`).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T4.003 detection lives at the wallet-UX layer, not at the on-chain detection layer where MG operates).
- Vendor coverage: Trezor Suite address-poisoning filter (reference for hardware-wallet UX layer); Rabby and MetaMask "address book mismatch" warnings; Coinbase Wallet, Trust Wallet poisoning-pattern detection. Revoke.cash and similar tools are *not* relevant here because no allowance is granted.

## Mitigations

- **OAK-M29 (Full Address Verification and Lookalike Detection):** highest-leverage layer — wallet vendors flag inbound zero-value or near-zero-value transfers from previously-unseen senders whose addresses are structurally similar to addresses in the user's send history; require explicit full-address verification for sends above a configurable threshold; surface address-book labels prominently in send flows.
- **OAK-M18 (Out-of-Band Destination Verification):** verify the *full* destination address before sending, not the first/last six characters; maintain an address book of known counterparties and prefer the address-book entry over wallet-history lookup.
- **OAK-M19 (Air-Gap Cold Wallet Signing):** air-gapped signing devices that render the full destination address with checksum (rather than truncated) defeat the truncated-display attack surface for treasury-scale transactions.
- **OAK-M04 / OAK-M07 (Funder-Graph and Cross-Chain Attribution):** maintain campaign-cluster watchlists; cross-reference outbound transfers from organisational treasury wallets against known-poisoning-campaign attacker addresses before broadcast.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2024poisoning]` — see `citations.bib` (\$68M case study + campaign metrics).
- `[tsuchiya2025poisoning]` — see `citations.bib` (academic cohort characterisation, USENIX Security 2025).
- `[chainalysisnftcounterfeit2022]` — see `citations.bib` (early counterfeit-token sub-class precedent).

## Discussion

T4.003 is one of the highest-leverage Techniques in OAK for the wallet-UX-layer mitigation argument. Effectively all on-chain detection happens after the fact; the defender's actionable layer is the wallet UI showing the full address, the address-book pattern matching, and the user's habit of verifying the full address. Contributors writing future T4.003 examples should preserve the on-chain-vs-UX-layer distinction explicitly — it is the most actionable single takeaway from the Technique class, and it is what differentiates T4.003 from T4.001 (where on-chain detection of the permit-grantee cluster is meaningful).

False-positive reasoning: legitimate exchange-deposit address rotation can produce inbound transfers from previously-unseen addresses that share prefix characters with prior counterparties (CEX hot-wallet hashing patterns happen to collide); the precision-improving signal is the value-near-zero filter combined with the structural-similarity threshold. Edge case: airdrop campaigns intentionally seed many low-value transfers; legitimate airdrops typically originate from contracts with public registry entries, allowing the OAK-M29 implementation to whitelist them.

A separate consideration: the Bybit case (`examples/2025-02-bybit.md`) involved a *different* class of "destination address swap at sign time" — the attacker swapped the address inside the signing UI rather than poisoning the user's history. OAK does not classify Bybit as T4.003 because the entry vector is software supply-chain compromise of the signing infrastructure, not wallet-history poisoning. The structural similarity (victim signs / approves a transfer to an attacker address they did not intend) is a useful teaching point but the underlying defense lives in different places.

v0.x deferred items: cross-chain poisoning observability (some campaigns operate identical operator clusters across Ethereum, Tron, and Solana — current detection feeds are largely per-chain), and ERC-4337 / smart-account name-resolution semantics where the destination is resolved from a named identifier rather than a raw address (poisoning shifts to name-registration squatting and is structurally adjacent rather than identical).
docs.techniques/T4.004-allowance-approve-drai:
# OAK-T4.004 — Allowance / Approve-Pattern Drainer

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T4 (Access Acquisition)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM (primary)
**First documented:** widespread from approximately 2022; characterised in `[checkpoint2023drainers]`
**Aliases:** "approve drainer", "unlimited allowance phishing", "ERC-20 approve scam"

## Description

A class of phishing flows in which a victim is induced to submit an on-chain `approve(spender, value)` transaction granting an attacker-controlled address the standard ERC-20 allowance (typically `uint256.max` — effectively unlimited) over one or more of the victim's tokens. Once the allowance is granted on-chain, the attacker exercises it via `transferFrom(victim, recipient, value)` to drain the victim's balance of the approved tokens. From a defender's perspective, T4.004 is structurally distinct from T4.001 (Permit2 signature-based authority misuse) because the authority artefact lives **on-chain** as an `Allowance` record rather than as an off-chain signed payload — meaning per-token, per-spender allowance monitoring at the wallet or risk-team layer can detect the granted authority before the `transferFrom` is exercised.

T4.004 is one of the dominant flows in the modern wallet-drainer service ecosystem (`[slowmist2024report]`); per `[checkpoint2023drainers]`, recurring on-chain spender addresses tied to Angel Drainer, Inferno Drainer, and successor service families exhibit the canonical approve-then-transferFrom flow.

## Observed indicators

- ERC-20 `Approval(owner, spender, value)` event with `value = uint256.max` (`0xff…ff`) or large round numbers (`1e36`, `2^96`) naming an attacker-clustered spender, shortly preceded by victim interaction with a phishing site or compromised front-end.
- ABI signature `approve(address,uint256)` (selector `0x095ea7b3`) sent to an ERC-20 with the spender field pointing at a freshly deployed contract with no other inbound legitimate flow.
- Per-spender allowance distribution showing a single spender holding outsized aggregate allowance across many distinct token contracts or distinct victim wallets.
- Attacker-side `transferFrom` (selector `0x23b872dd`) calls executed shortly after the corresponding `Approval` event (single-block to single-day window typical).
- Drainer-router contract addresses on the receiving end matching `[checkpoint2023drainers]` / ScamSniffer / SlowMist threat-feed entries (Angel Drainer routers are publicly enumerated).
- Cohort-funder graph: drainer-router deployers cluster on the funder-graph heuristic (OAK-T8.001), enabling forward attribution of new operator addresses before victim flow accumulates.

## Detection signals

- Real-time on-chain: `Approval` event watcher with threshold rules — alert on `value >= 2^96` to a non-allowlisted spender; cross-reference spender against drainer-cluster allowlist before the attacker exercises.
- Per-EOA approval-velocity outlier detection: a high-balance EOA generating multiple distinct approve grants to non-allowlisted spenders within a short window indicates phishing-context interaction.
- Per-spender, per-chain heat map of held allowances — forward-looking indicator of which addresses are actively phishing.
- Mempool watcher (OAK-M06): pre-block telemetry on `transferFrom` calls targeting known drainer-router addresses provides intervention window for monitored treasury wallets.
- Wallet-side (OAK-M31): surface the user's outstanding allowances at sign time; warn on `approve(uint256.max)` to non-allowlisted spenders; simulate the approve transaction's downstream effect.
- Post-event forensic: cluster victim wallets by common-spender approval to identify the size of the campaign cohort.

## Real-world examples

- **Lawliet Bored Ape phishing — Ethereum — 2022-01-31** — Loss \~\$2.7M across 13 NFTs (7 BAYC, 5 MAYC, 1 Doodle). The collector engaged with a fake "extra mint" announcement posted in the compromised Moschi Mochi Discord, sent 0.49 ETH for 14 scam NFTs, then signed a sequence of `setApprovalForAll` (T4.005) and `approve` calls; the attacker exercised these via `transferFrom` to drain the collection. T4.004/T4.005 chained example. Source: `[theblock2022boredape]`.
- **CoinStats Snap supply-chain compromise — multi-chain — 2024-06** — `[coinstats20240622]`, `[slowmistcoinstats2024]`, `[blocksec_coinstats2024]`, `[peckshieldcoinstats2024]`, `[zachxbtcoinstats2024]` document compromise of CoinStats's portfolio-tracker dApp surface affecting approximately 1,590 wallets; signing prompts authorised attacker-controlled `approve` calls across multiple ERC-20 positions. The compromise sits at the dApp-distribution layer and chains into the canonical T4.004 extraction.
- **DEXX exchange-extension drainer — multi-chain — 2024-10/11** — `[dexxstatements2024]`, `[slowmistdexx2024]`, `[zachxbtdexx2024]`, `[peckshielddexx2024]` document the DEXX trading-extension compromise; signing prompts originating inside the trusted DEXX surface authorised `approve` and `transferFrom` flows across the user base. Cohort-attribution analysis attributes the compromise to operator-infrastructure rather than user-side phishing — same on-chain artefact (T4.004 `Approval` event) with distinct entry vector.
- **Pink Drainer extractions — multi-chain — 2024** — `[scamsniffer2024pink]` documents Pink Drainer's >\$3M cumulative extractions across 2024 including the OpenAI CTO and Orbiter Finance founder cases; `approve` and Permit2-signature flows were used in combination depending on token support. The cohort's October 2024 retirement transferred operations to Angel Drainer per `[scamsniffer2024lineage]`.
- **Inferno Drainer canonical victim cohort — multi-chain — 2023-2024** — Per `[scamsniffer2024lineage]` and `[checkpoint2023drainers]`, Inferno Drainer's recurring spender-address infrastructure produced a sustained T4.004 victim flow across 2023 (\~\$81M from 134K users over nine months per ScamSniffer's 2023 dataset) and continued through 2024 with \~22% market share Q1–Q2 and \~43% Q3 before the October 2024 transfer to Angel Drainer.
- **Wallet-drainer ecosystem aggregate — multi-chain — 2024** — `[slowmist2024report]` documents \~\$494M in user losses across 2024; \~56.7% used permit signatures (T4.001) and the remaining substantial fraction used the on-chain approve-pattern flow described here.
- **OAK-G02 attribution (Drainer-as-a-Service operator family)** — see `actors/OAK-G02-drainer-services.md`. T4.004 is the canonical service-layer Technique alongside T4.001.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2024-06-coinstats-snap.md`](../examples/2024-06-coinstats-snap.md) — CoinStats MetaMask-Snap-related compromise
- [`examples/2024-07-li-finance.md`](../examples/2024-07-li-finance.md) — Li.Fi cross-chain aggregator facet exploit
- [`examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md`](../examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md) — Inferno Drainer service ecosystem
- [`examples/2023-2026-fake-dex-clone-frontend-cohort.md`](../examples/2023-2026-fake-dex-clone-frontend-cohort.md) — Fake-DEX clone-frontend cohort (proposed T4.x distribution-surface variant chains into T4.004 `Approval` / allowance solicitation backed by Inferno / Angel / MS Drainer / PhishLab kits).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (wallet-layer phishing detection out of MG scope).
- Vendor coverage: Rabby, Pocket Universe extension, MetaMask transaction simulation, Coinbase Wallet warning system; revoke.cash and similar tooling for outstanding-allowance management.

## Mitigations

- **OAK-M08 (Per-Spender Approval Audit and Revocation):** revoke unused allowances on a recurring cadence using revoke.cash or comparable tools; never grant `uint256.max` allowance to an unfamiliar spender; treat any allowance grant from a phishing-context interaction as compromised even if no `transferFrom` has yet executed (revoke immediately).
- **OAK-M31 (EIP-712 Permit Display and Signing Risk Heuristics) — extends to plain `approve`:** simulate the approve transaction's downstream effect at sign time (will granting this allowance allow funds to be drained?); warn on `uint256.max` allowance to non-allowlisted spenders; surface outstanding allowances prominently in the wallet UI.
- **OAK-M30 (Per-dApp Domain and App-Store-Package Allowlist):** block `approve` signing prompts originating from non-allowlisted domains.
- **OAK-M19 (Air-Gap Cold Wallet Signing):** for treasury-class wallets, route approve signing through air-gapped device with explicit display of spender, value, and token contract.
- **OAK-M06 (Mempool Pre-Block Telemetry):** organisational watchers for known-drainer-router target addresses to intercept exercise of granted allowances.
- **Token contract designers:** the ERC-20 increase/decrease-allowance pattern reduces the consequence of a stale allowance; new token deployments should consider time-limited allowance designs.

## Citations

- `[checkpoint2023drainers]` — Check Point research on drainer service families and recurring spender addresses.
- `[slowmist2024report]` — 2024 ecosystem aggregate (loss volume, share of permit vs approve flows).
- `[scamsniffer2024lineage]` — drainer-cohort lineage and 2024 retrospective.
- `[scamsniffer2024pink]` — Pink Drainer high-value extractions including OpenAI CTO and Orbiter Finance founder.
- `[theblock2022boredape]` — Lawliet \$2.7M case (T4.004/T4.005 chained).
- `[coinstats20240622]`, `[slowmistcoinstats2024]`, `[blocksec_coinstats2024]`, `[peckshieldcoinstats2024]`, `[zachxbtcoinstats2024]` — CoinStats Snap supply-chain June 2024.
- `[dexxstatements2024]`, `[slowmistdexx2024]`, `[zachxbtdexx2024]`, `[peckshielddexx2024]` — DEXX extension compromise late 2024.

## Discussion

The T4.001 / T4.004 split is partially historical: Permit2 (and EIP-2612 Permit) became dominant precisely because they offered phishing-as-a-service operators a cleaner workflow (signed off-chain payload, no on-chain pre-event signal). T4.004 is the older, on-chain-visible flow. Both remain in active use; some incidents combine both. Contributors writing future T4.001/T4.004 examples should preserve the **on-chain-vs-off-chain authority artefact** distinction explicitly because the corresponding detection and mitigation surfaces differ.

False-positive reasoning: legitimate DEX/aggregator interaction routinely produces `approve(uint256.max)` to canonical router contracts (Uniswap V2 router, 0x ExchangeProxy, 1inch AggregationRouter, CowSwap settlement). The high-precision signal is grantee-clustering against a known-bad allowlist combined with originating-domain provenance. Edge case: legitimate user revocation followed by re-approval to the same legitimate router produces a benign `approve` event that may briefly cluster with phishing-context activity at the per-EOA velocity layer; cohort-funder graph attribution is the disambiguator.

v0.x deferred items: cross-chain approval observability (the same drainer operator deploys functionally identical router contracts on multiple EVM chains; current allowlists are mostly per-chain), and ERC-4337 approval semantics where the `approve` is invoked by the smart account's `executeBatch` — wallet-UX-layer interception requires parsing the inner UserOperation calldata rather than the outer transaction.
docs.techniques/T4.005-setapprovalforall-nft-:
# OAK-T4.005 — `setApprovalForAll` NFT Drainer

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T4 (Access Acquisition)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM (primary; Ethereum / Polygon / BNB Chain)
**First documented:** 2021 (early Bored Ape phishing wave); industrial-scale 2022+
**Aliases:** "NFT phishing drainer", "approval-for-all phishing", "BAYC phishing"

## Description

A class of phishing flows in which a victim is induced to submit an on-chain `setApprovalForAll(operator, true)` transaction granting an attacker-controlled operator address total transfer authority over one of the victim's ERC-721 (or ERC-1155) NFT collections. Once the operator authority is granted, the attacker can transfer any NFT in the approved collection from the victim's wallet via `transferFrom` / `safeTransferFrom` (ERC-721) or the ERC-1155 batch equivalent. From a defender's perspective, T4.005 is the NFT-collection-level analogue of T4.004's per-token approve flow — the failure mode is structurally the same (victim signs an authority grant they did not understand), but the granularity is the entire collection rather than a single token's allowance.

T4.005's entry vector is most often a compromised Discord server (with a fake "extra mint" or "claim airdrop" announcement) or a phishing dApp linked from social media. The victim's wallet-UX interpretation of `setApprovalForAll` calls is the cornerstone of the defender's signal: most legitimate NFT marketplace flows require this approval, so wallet warnings need to distinguish legitimate marketplace operators from attacker-controlled ones.

## Observed indicators

- ERC-721 / ERC-1155 `ApprovalForAll(owner, operator, approved=true)` event (event topic `0x17307eab39ab6107e8899845ad3d59bd9653f200f220920489ca2b5937696c31`) naming an attacker-clustered operator address shortly after victim engagement with a phishing context (compromised Discord channel, fake dApp).
- ABI signature `setApprovalForAll(address,bool)` (selector `0xa22cb465`) sent to an ERC-721 / ERC-1155 contract with operator pointing at a freshly deployed contract or an EOA with no other inbound legitimate flow.
- Operator addresses concentrating outsized aggregate authority across many distinct collections and many distinct victim wallets.
- Attacker-side `transferFrom` (ERC-721) or `safeBatchTransferFrom` (ERC-1155) calls executed shortly after the corresponding `ApprovalForAll` event, often draining multiple NFTs from a single victim in rapid succession (single-block sweep typical for high-value collections).
- Operator address NOT in canonical marketplace operator allowlist (OpenSea Seaport `0x00000000000000ADc04C56Bf30aC9d3c0aAF14dC`, Blur `0x000000000000Ad05Ccc4F10045630fb830B95127`, LooksRare, Magic Eden, Foundation, Rarible).

## Detection signals

- Real-time on-chain: `ApprovalForAll` event watcher; alert when operator clusters with previously-flagged drainer infrastructure or is non-allowlisted-and-newly-deployed.
- Per-wallet (OAK-M08 / OAK-M31): surface the user's outstanding `ApprovalForAll` grants at sign time and at periodic review intervals; warn on grants to non-allowlisted operators.
- Off-chain telemetry: integrate compromised-Discord-server reporting (community reports, Discord moderation tooling) as a pre-event indicator for in-progress NFT phishing campaigns.
- Per-operator NFT-velocity outlier detection: an operator producing many `transferFrom` calls within a short window across distinct victim wallets is a cohort-attribution signal.
- Mempool watcher for known drainer-operator target addresses (OAK-M06) provides intervention window between `ApprovalForAll` and the operator's first `transferFrom`.

## Real-world examples

- **Lawliet Bored Ape phishing — Ethereum — 2022-01-31** — Loss \~\$2.7M across 13 NFTs (7 BAYC, 5 MAYC, 1 Doodle). The attacker took control of the Discord server of NFT collection Moschi Mochi to post a fake announcement about an extra mint (1,000 NFTs for a chance to win a \$25,000 raffle). Lawliet engaged with the fake mint, sent 0.49 ETH for 14 scam NFTs, then signed a sequence of `setApprovalForAll` calls to attacker-controlled operators; the operator subsequently exercised these via `transferFrom` for each BAYC and derivative. Source: `[theblock2022boredape]`.
- **toddkramer.eth Bored Ape phishing — Ethereum — 2021-12** — Loss \~593 ETH (\~\$2.2M at the time) across 16 NFTs via an analogous `setApprovalForAll` flow; one of the earliest large-loss precedents and a structural template for the 2022 wave.
- **BAYC Discord webhook compromise — Ethereum — 2022-04-01** — Per `[baycdiscord2022]`, the BAYC Discord server's webhook integrations were briefly compromised; phishing announcements directed users to fake-mint dApps requesting `setApprovalForAll`. Yuga Labs publicly advised the community to avoid Discord-originated minting flows during the window.
- **BAYC community-manager Discord compromise — Ethereum — 2022-06-04** — Per `[fortunebaycjune2022]`, approximately \$360K in NFTs were drained via a Discord compromise of a BAYC community manager account; the attacker posted phishing announcements that drove users into `setApprovalForAll` flows.
- **Yuga Otherside Discord compromise — Ethereum — 2022-08** — Per `[yugaotherside2022]`, `[peckshieldyugaotherside2022]`, `[zachxbtyugaotherside2022]`, `[slowmistyugaotherside2022]`, `[theblockyugaotherside2022]`, the Otherside Discord was compromised; phishing announcements drove users into `setApprovalForAll` grants to attacker operators across BAYC, MAYC, and Otherdeed collections.
- **Discord-server-compromise vector — multi-incident — 2022 onward** — TRM Labs and others have documented a series of connected NFT Discord-server compromises distributing T4.005-class phishing dApps; Discord-compromise is the dominant entry path for high-value NFT collection phishing.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2022-04-bored-ape-discord-wave.md`](../examples/2022-04-bored-ape-discord-wave.md) — Bored Ape Yacht Club / Yuga Labs operator-side credential-compromise wave
- [`examples/2022-04-ronin-discord.md`](../examples/2022-04-ronin-discord.md) — Ronin Bridge Discord-impersonation followups
- [`examples/2022-07-premint-phishing.md`](../examples/2022-07-premint-phishing.md) — Premint NFT-allowlist platform front-end JavaScript injection
- [`examples/2022-08-yuga-otherside.md`](../examples/2022-08-yuga-otherside.md) — Yuga Labs Otherside Discord-phishing wave
- [`examples/2023-12-galxe-dns.md`](../examples/2023-12-galxe-dns.md) — Galxe frontend DNS hijack pair
- [`examples/2024-06-coinstats-snap.md`](../examples/2024-06-coinstats-snap.md) — CoinStats MetaMask-Snap-related compromise
- [`examples/2023-2026-fake-dex-clone-frontend-cohort.md`](../examples/2023-2026-fake-dex-clone-frontend-cohort.md) — Fake-DEX clone-frontend cohort (proposed T4.x distribution-surface variant chains into T4.005 `setApprovalForAll` solicitation for NFT drains where the kit substrate is fake-marketplace adjacent).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (wallet-layer phishing detection out of MG scope).
- Vendor coverage: wallet vendors with `setApprovalForAll` simulation and operator-allowlisting; NFT-marketplace-side integrations that label legitimate marketplace operator addresses (OpenSea registered operator filter `[openseaoperatorfilter2022]` is a precedent for marketplace-side enforcement); revoke.cash for outstanding `ApprovalForAll` revocation.

## Mitigations

- **OAK-M08 (Per-Spender Approval Audit and Revocation):** revoke unused `ApprovalForAll` grants on a recurring cadence using revoke.cash or comparable tools; never grant operator authority from an interaction initiated via a Discord announcement without out-of-band verification of the dApp URL.
- **OAK-M31 (Signing Risk Heuristics) — extended for NFT operator grants:** highest-leverage layer — maintain a marketplace-operator allowlist (OpenSea Seaport, Blur, LooksRare, Magic Eden, Foundation, Rarible); warn on `setApprovalForAll` to non-allowlisted operators; surface outstanding `ApprovalForAll` grants prominently and provide a one-click revoke flow.
- **OAK-M30 (Per-dApp Domain Allowlist):** block `setApprovalForAll` signing prompts originating from non-allowlisted domains, particularly social-media-shared "extra mint" dApps.
- **OAK-M19 (Air-Gap Cold Wallet Signing):** for high-value NFT vaults, route operator-grant signing through air-gapped device with explicit display of the operator address.
- **NFT communities:** Discord moderation policies that reduce the impact of compromised-server announcements (channel-lock procedures, mod 2FA enforcement, rate-limited announcement permissions, removal of webhook integrations from announcement channels); cross-channel verification for announcements about extra mints or airdrops.

## Citations

- `[theblock2022boredape]` — Lawliet \$2.7M case.
- `[baycdiscord2022]` — BAYC Discord webhook compromise April 2022.
- `[fortunebaycjune2022]` — BAYC community-manager Discord compromise June 2022.
- `[yugaotherside2022]`, `[peckshieldyugaotherside2022]`, `[zachxbtyugaotherside2022]`, `[slowmistyugaotherside2022]`, `[theblockyugaotherside2022]` — Yuga Otherside Discord compromise August 2022.
- `[openseamoderation2022]`, `[openseaoperatorfilter2022]` — OpenSea marketplace-side operator filter precedent.
- `[checkpoint2023drainers]` — drainer service family characterisation including setApprovalForAll flows.
- `[slowmist2024report]` — 2024 ecosystem aggregate.

## Discussion

T4.005 sits at the intersection of OAK's main scope and the broader NFT-specific pillar called out in `TAXONOMY-GAPS.md`. It is included in T4 at v0.1 because the failure mode is structurally identical to T4.004 (allowance grant exercised via subsequent transfer call) and because the attacker-side detection signals (operator-address clustering) parallel the spender-address clustering for T4.004. Other NFT-specific Techniques (wash-trade NFT volume inflation, fake-mint scams, royalty bypass) are deliberately deferred to a future v0.x update.

False-positive reasoning: legitimate marketplace listing flows require `setApprovalForAll` to the marketplace operator contract (OpenSea Seaport, Blur, LooksRare). The high-precision signal is operator non-membership in the canonical marketplace allowlist combined with originating-domain provenance. Edge case: a marketplace deploying a new operator contract version (e.g., OpenSea Seaport 1.5 → 1.6 upgrade) produces a transient surge of `ApprovalForAll` to a not-yet-allowlisted address that is functionally legitimate; allowlist updates need a defined SLA from marketplaces to wallet vendors.

The Discord-compromise entry vector is one of the most actionable defender lessons from the T4.005 cohort: defender controls live both at the wallet layer (allowlisting marketplace operators, surfacing outstanding `ApprovalForAll` grants) and at the community-moderation layer (Discord 2FA, channel-lock procedures, webhook-integration hygiene). Contributors writing future T4.005 examples should preserve the on-chain-and-off-chain bifurcation explicitly.

v0.x deferred items: cross-chain `ApprovalForAll` observability for collections deployed on multiple EVM chains (Polygon-based NFTs particularly); ERC-6551 token-bound-account semantics where the NFT itself is an authority over a wallet (T4.005 grant on a TBA-bearing collection compounds risk in ways not yet covered).
docs.techniques/T4.006-walletconnect-session-:
# OAK-T4.006 — WalletConnect Session Hijack

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T4 (Access Acquisition)
**Maturity:** observed
**Chains:** EVM (primary), Solana, multi-chain (any chain WalletConnect supports)
**First documented:** systematic 2023 onward; mobile-app-impersonation cases at scale 2024
**Aliases:** "WalletConnect phishing", "fake-dApp pairing", "QR-code wallet hijack"

## Description

A class of phishing flows in which a victim is induced to establish a WalletConnect session with a malicious dApp (or with software masquerading as a legitimate dApp), after which the attacker uses the session to solicit signing of malicious payloads. Distinct from OAK-T4.001 (Permit2 Signature-Based Authority Misuse) because the entry vector is the **session-establishment** step rather than the signing-flow step inside an already-trusted dApp. Distinct from OAK-T4.002 (Compromised Front-End) because the attacker controls a separate fake-dApp / malicious-app surface rather than compromising a legitimate dApp's distribution channel.

Real-world cases combine three sub-vectors: (a) malicious mobile apps masquerading as legitimate WalletConnect-using dApps (App Store / Google Play distribution), (b) QR-code phishing on social media or in physical-world contexts that establishes a WalletConnect session with attacker infrastructure, and (c) deep-link-based malicious dApp invocation that bypasses traditional URL inspection. The per-session payloads themselves typically chain into T4.001 or T4.004 — i.e., once the session is hijacked, the attacker uses standard signature-phishing or approve-pattern flows.

## Observed indicators

- Mobile-app distribution channels (App Store, Google Play) hosting apps with names mimicking WalletConnect-using dApps (e.g., "Mestox Calculator", "WalletConnect — DeFi & NFTs", "WalletConnect — Airdrop Wallet").
- Session-establishment events with peer dApps whose URL or deep-link is structurally inconsistent with the user's intended interaction.
- Per-session signing-payload patterns matching OAK-T4.001 or OAK-T4.004 signatures (the session is the entry vector; the actual extraction is a chained Technique).

## Detection signals

- Wallet-side (highest-leverage): integrate per-dApp domain / app-store-package allowlists; warn at session-establishment time when the peer dApp is not on the allowlist; surface session-history with operator-clustering alerts.
- App-store moderation: detect impersonation patterns at app-store level; remove malicious apps following user reports.
- On-chain: post-session, the resulting signing payloads chain into T4.001 / T4.004 — detection at the chained-Technique layer applies.

## Real-world examples

- **Fake "WalletConnect" mobile-app campaign — Google Play — 2024-09** — A malicious app distributed under multiple names ("Mestox Calculator", "WalletConnect — DeFi & NFTs", "WalletConnect — Airdrop Wallet") achieved over 10,000 downloads via Google Play, ranking via fake reviews and brand impersonation. Approximately \$70K stolen across over 150 victims. The malicious app used smart contracts and deep links to silently drain assets once users were tricked into using the app. Sources: industry reporting (The Hacker News) and follow-up CTI write-ups.
- **WalletConnect protocol-level vulnerability disclosure — 2022** — A Trail of Bits audit identified HTML5 local-storage usage for session data as a medium-severity vulnerability (CVE-2022-28843) due to susceptibility to cross-site scripting attacks. The vulnerability is at the protocol-implementation layer rather than at the user-interaction layer, but is documented for completeness.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T4.006 detection lives at the wallet-UX layer + app-store-moderation layer, not at the on-chain layer where MG operates).
- Vendor coverage: wallet vendors with rich session-history and peer-dApp warnings (Trust Wallet, Coinbase Wallet, Rabby); WalletConnect protocol-side telemetry where exposed.

## Mitigations

- **Wallets (highest-leverage):** maintain per-dApp domain / app-store-package allowlists; warn at session-establishment time on non-allowlisted peers; surface session-history with operator-clustering alerts; require explicit user confirmation for high-value signing payloads regardless of session trust state.
- **App stores:** detect impersonation patterns; require WalletConnect-using apps to verify domain / dApp affiliation as a publishing precondition.
- **End users:** establish WalletConnect sessions only from canonical dApp URLs (read directly, not via QR code from untrusted source); prefer session-by-session connections over persistent sessions for high-value wallets.

## Citations

- General industry reporting on the September 2024 Google Play "WalletConnect" impersonation campaign; specific bib entry deferred to a v0.x update where a single canonical authoritative source is identified.

## Discussion

T4.006 is the entry-vector-specific framing for what frequently chains into T4.001 / T4.004 / T4.005. The defender's analytical question is whether a session-establishment-time warning would have prevented the chained extraction; in essentially all documented cases, the answer is yes — the failure mode is consent-to-session, not consent-to-payload. Contributors writing future T4.006 examples should expect the chained-Technique pattern and link explicitly to whichever T4 sub-Technique handles the actual extraction.

The mobile-app-distribution sub-vector (Google Play / App Store impersonation) is the most operationally-scalable T4.006 entry vector at v0.1 and is expected to grow as mobile-wallet usage grows. Contributors should monitor app-store moderation policy changes as a defensive-baseline signal — improvements in app-store impersonation detection move the T4.006 attack surface across mobile platforms.
docs.techniques/T4.007-native-app-social-phis:
# OAK-T4.007 — Native-app Social Phishing on Engagement-Weighted Platforms

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T4 (Access Acquisition)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (Polygon-resident Polymarket canonical at v0.1; cross-platform analogues across Friend.tech, Pump.fun, Farcaster, and any platform with engagement-weighted in-platform distribution)
**First documented:** 2025-10/11 (Polymarket comment-section phishing campaign exploiting comment-pinning mechanic)
**Aliases:** "comment-section phishing", "engagement-weighted phishing", "in-platform paid-pinning phishing", "native-app social phishing"

## Description

Phishing campaigns whose distribution surface is a paid-for or engagement-weighted *in-platform mechanic* that the attacker manipulates to occupy visibility (e.g., comment-pinning, reply-ranking, engagement-weighted feed ranking). The platform's own mechanics fund or amplify the distribution surface; the attacker buys visibility through a legitimate platform mechanic rather than through external advertising or DNS-level compromise.

T4.007 is **structurally distinct** from sibling T4 Techniques:

- **T4.001 (Permit2 Authority Misuse)** — defines the on-chain extraction primitive. T4.007 is a *distribution-surface* sub-Technique; the on-chain extraction primitive may be Permit2 misuse (T4.001), allowance-drainer (T4.004), setApprovalForAll-drainer (T4.005), or — distinctively for the canonical case — a *non-on-chain* credential-capture (email-magic-link verification code captured by a fake login + fake captcha, exploiting embedded-wallet identity-provider auth flow).
- **T4.002 (Compromised Front-End Permit Solicitation)** — DNS-hijack / supply-chain JS / BGP / registrar compromise of a real platform's user-facing surface. T4.007 does not compromise the legitimate platform's surface; the attacker uses the platform's own legitimate engagement-weighted mechanic to anchor phishing visibility.
- **T4.008 (Fake-DEX Clone-Frontend Phishing)** — paid-for inventory on a *legitimate ad platform* (Google Search ads, X promoted posts, Telegram bot traffic, App Store) distributing counterfeit DEX UIs. T4.007 distributes phishing through the *target platform's own* engagement mechanic.

The structural feature is that the platform's own mechanics fund the distribution surface. The attacker's spending is denominated in the platform's native asset (Polymarket positions in the canonical case; potential extensions to Friend.tech keys, Pump.fun bonding-curve buys, Farcaster casts) and the distribution-surface visibility is the return on that spending — independent of any external ad-platform enforcement surface.

## Observed indicators

- **Platform mechanic exploited:** comment-pinning ranks comments by the commenter's combined position rather than by community signals; reply-ranking on engagement-weighted platforms; feed-ranking algorithms that reward in-platform spending.
- **Symmetric position-taking:** attacker buys both YES and NO shares (or equivalent symmetric exposure) on a high-volume market specifically to anchor pinning visibility; the position is not directional and the platform-native asset spend is the cost-of-distribution rather than a directional bet.
- **High-volume / high-engagement target markets:** attacker selects markets with high cumulative volume to maximise the eyeball-count downstream of the pinned comment slot.
- **Phishing-comment content pattern:** "private markets with better odds" / "exclusive trading group" / "claim airdrop here" framings that direct users to an obfuscated URL — typically a tracking / redirect domain that resolves to a cloned platform login page.
- **Credential-capture stage:** the credential-capture flow may exploit the platform's identity-provider integration directly (fake login + fake captcha capturing email-magic-link verification code in the canonical case) or chain into a standard T4 on-chain authority grant.

## Detection signals

- **Platform-side comment-ranking audit:** detect symmetric position-taking patterns (buying YES + NO at sizes calibrated to anchor pinning) as a discrete signal independent of comment content moderation; surface elevated-risk pinned-comment slots for human review.
- **Cross-vendor credential-monitoring:** when phishing campaigns target an embedded-wallet identity-provider (Magic Labs / Privy / Web3Auth), independent vendors (CoinSpot, Phemex, BingX, Cryptopolitan in the canonical case) often surface campaign existence and scale before platform-side moderation catches up.
- **User-side `we-will-never-DM-you-first` invariant:** platform-published invariants (the canonical Polymarket statement: "we will never DM you first; do not click external links from comments") form the user-side mitigation but the operational signal is *user-attention rather than detection automation*.
- **DaaS-kit-fingerprinting absence as a finding:** the canonical case did not exhibit a publicly-named DaaS-kit fingerprint (Inferno / Angel / Pink / Monkey / Venom / Vanilla / Chick); operator opsec consistent with DaaS affiliate behaviour but no kit confirmed. The absence-of-kit-fingerprint is itself a finding for cohort-level attribution analysis.

## Real-world examples

- **Polymarket comment-section phishing campaign — Polygon — 2025-11 (canonical T4.007 case)** — ≥ $500,000 cumulative loss across affected users; per-incident losses individually unlogged but disclosed in aggregate by Polymarket support and corroborated by independent vendors (CoinSpot, Phemex, BingX, Cryptopolitan). Through October-November 2025, attackers ran a coordinated phishing campaign exploiting Polymarket's native comment-section mechanic. The campaign worked as follows: (1) attacker selected high-volume, high-engagement Polymarket markets (election trackers, sports outcomes, geopolitical events); (2) attacker bought *both* YES and NO shares on the target market in size sufficient to anchor a "top comment" — Polymarket's comment-pinning mechanic ranks comments by the commenter's combined position, not by reaction count, so the phishing comment remained pinned regardless of community downvotes; (3) the pinned comment offered "private markets with better odds" and linked to an obfuscated URL — typically a tracking/redirect domain that resolved to a cloned Polymarket login page; (4) the cloned login page presented a fake Cloudflare turnstile challenge and a Polymarket-branded login form that captured the user's email and the email-magic-link verification code; (5) with email + code, the attacker authenticated to Magic Labs as the user, gained access to the user's Polymarket-derived non-custodial wallet, and transferred USDC to attacker-controlled addresses. Independent disclosure on November 11, 2025 (user handle "25usdc" on X) and follow-up reporting from CoinSpot, Phemex, and BingX placed cumulative losses above $500,000. Attribution: `inferred-strong`; Polymarket and independent vendors confirmed campaign existence and scale; specific operator-cluster attribution not published — operator pattern is consistent with OAK-G02 (Drainer-as-a-Service) but no specific DaaS-kit fingerprint has been confirmed in this campaign — itself a finding. The chain into T11.008 (Embedded-Wallet Identity-Provider Compromise) is the load-bearing custody-substrate axis: the phishing captures the email-magic-link credential at the T4.007 layer and exploits Polymarket's Magic Labs auth path at the T11.008 layer. See [`examples/2025-11-polymarket-comment-section-phishing.md`](../examples/2025-11-polymarket-comment-section-phishing.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T4.007's primary detection surface is at the platform-side comment-ranking and credential-capture-flow layer, not the on-chain runtime risk-engine surface MG covers.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: ScamSniffer (general drainer-cohort tracking); SEAL Threat Intel (high-volume cohort disclosure); platform-side surveillance partnerships (Polymarket–Chainalysis announced April 2026); independent vendors (CoinSpot, Phemex, BingX, Cryptopolitan) for cross-platform cohort framing.

## Mitigations

- **Platform-side mechanic redesign (highest-leverage):** comment-pinning and reply-ranking algorithms should incorporate community-signal weighting (downvotes, flag rates, account-age) alongside in-platform-spending signals so that paid pinning cannot persist against community downvotes. The canonical Polymarket case is structurally significant *because* the mechanic ranked solely by combined position with no community-signal override.
- **Platform-side phishing-content moderation:** automated detection of obfuscated URLs, tracking-redirect domains, and known typosquat / near-miss domains in pinned-comment slots; human review for pinned-comment content on high-volume markets.
- **Platform-side user-education invariants:** publish in-product warnings (the canonical Polymarket "we will never DM you first; do not click external links from comments" invariant) at the points of maximum user attention — login flow, deposit flow, market-detail page.
- **Embedded-wallet identity-provider hardening (T11.008 chain):** because T4.007 in the canonical case chains into T11.008 (email-magic-link credential capture exploiting Magic Labs auth), platforms should require hardware-backed multi-factor authentication for transaction-signing flows; rate-limit magic-link verification-code attempts; surface unusual-login alerts to users in real time.
- **Independent-vendor cohort-coordination:** the canonical case was first surfaced through independent-vendor disclosure (user handle "25usdc" on X) before platform-side acknowledgment. Industry-press cohort coordination accelerates platform-side response and informs the user base across competing platforms.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1 are a **gap** — no existing OAK-M* entry directly maps to engagement-weighted-platform mechanic redesign. v0.x candidate Mitigation entries: "OAK-M-x community-signal-weighted ranking on engagement mechanics"; "OAK-M-x platform-side phishing-content moderation in pinned slots"; "OAK-M-x platform-published user-education invariants at high-attention points".

## Citations

- See worked example for primary citations.
- Polymarket comment-section phishing: independent disclosure November 11, 2025 (user handle "25usdc" on X); CoinSpot, Phemex, BingX, Cryptopolitan secondary coverage; Polymarket support disclosure of cumulative loss.

## Discussion

T4.007 closes a structural gap in OAK v0.1's T4 coverage. The dominant v0.1 T4 sub-Techniques (T4.001-T4.006) cover specific on-chain extraction primitives (Permit2, allowance, setApprovalForAll, address-poisoning, WalletConnect-session-hijack) and the compromised-frontend distribution surface (T4.002). T4.007 introduces a **distribution-surface-by-platform-mechanic-exploitation** failure surface that is structurally distinct from compromised-frontend distribution and from external-ad-platform distribution (T4.008).

The structural OAK lesson is that **engagement-weighted platforms create paid-distribution surfaces denominated in the platform's native asset, with attacker-controllable visibility independent of any external ad-platform enforcement**. The platform's own mechanics fund the distribution surface; the attacker's spending is a *cost-of-distribution* rather than a directional bet.

The pattern generalises beyond Polymarket. Any platform where engagement-weighted ranking determines visibility — Friend.tech-class apps (key-purchase ranking), Pump.fun (bonding-curve buy ranking), Farcaster (cast engagement weighting), and any future platform with similar mechanics — is exposed to the same attack class. The canonical Polymarket case is structurally significant because the comment-pinning mechanic ranked solely by combined position with no community-signal override; platform-mechanic redesigns that incorporate community-signal weighting bound the attack surface but do not eliminate it.

The credential-capture stage in the canonical case is structurally distinctive within T4. Most T4 sub-Techniques target an on-chain authority grant (Permit2 signature, allowance, setApprovalForAll). The Polymarket case targets the *embedded-wallet identity-provider auth credential* (email-magic-link verification code), chaining into T11.008 (Embedded-Wallet Identity-Provider Compromise). The combined T4.007–T11.008 attack chain is the canonical illustration of how phishing distribution surfaces and embedded-wallet custody surfaces interact in the prediction-market and broader embedded-wallet ecosystem.

The DaaS-kit-fingerprinting absence is structurally informative. The canonical case did not exhibit a publicly-named DaaS-kit fingerprint despite operator opsec consistent with DaaS affiliate behaviour. The absence-of-kit-fingerprint pattern suggests either (a) custom kit development for the embedded-wallet credential-capture sub-pattern (which is structurally distinct from on-chain authority-grant kits), or (b) a kit that is not yet publicly named at v0.1 reporting horizon. Future T4.007 worked examples should preserve the kit-fingerprint dimension explicitly.

The platform-side surveillance-partnership response (Polymarket–Chainalysis announced April 2026) is the canonical platform-level mitigation deployment for the broader Polymarket cohort. T4.007 mitigation specifically benefits from comment-ranking algorithm redesign (community-signal weighting) and pinned-comment content moderation (URL / domain reputation analysis at the moderation layer).

Promotion to `stable` will follow once additional cohort cases land — Friend.tech-equivalent engagement-weighted-platform phishing cohorts; Pump.fun comment-section phishing cohorts; Farcaster engagement-weighted-distribution phishing cohorts. The class anchor at v0.1 (Polymarket comment-section campaign) is sufficient for `emerging` status but the multi-platform anchor pattern that warrants `stable` is still forming.
docs.techniques/T4.008-fake-dex-clone-fronten:
# OAK-T4.008 — Fake-DEX Clone-Frontend Phishing

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T4 (Access Acquisition)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM dominant (Uniswap / PancakeSwap / Lido / Curve / Stargate / Orbiter / Radiant / Zapper / DefiLlama clone-frontends); Solana (Raydium clone-frontends); cross-chain
**First documented:** 2023-03 (MS Drainer kit cohort initial deployment); class-level industrial cohort observable across 2023–2026 with continuing distribution surface
**Aliases:** "fake DEX phishing", "clone-frontend phishing", "typosquat DEX UI", "paid-ad fake-DEX cohort", "Inferno-Drainer fake-frontend"

## Description

Phishing campaigns whose distribution surface is **paid-for inventory on a legitimate ad platform** (Google Search ads, X / Twitter promoted posts, Telegram bot traffic, Apple App Store / Google Play Store listings) and whose lure is a **counterfeit deployment of a popular DEX UI** — Uniswap, PancakeSwap, Curve, Raydium, Lido, Stargate, Orbiter, DefiLlama, Zapper, Radiant. The attacker-controlled UI is hosted on a typosquatted / near-miss / homoglyph domain (e.g., `unlswap.org` vs `uniswap.org`, `pancake-swap.app` vs `pancakeswap.finance`) controlled by the attacker from the moment of deployment, and connects to a wallet-drainer backend (Inferno → Angel / AngelFerno; MS Drainer; PhishLab kits).

T4.008 is **structurally distinct** from sibling T4 Techniques at the *substrate* level:

- **T4.002 (Compromised Front-End Permit Solicitation)** — compromises a *real platform's* user-facing surface (DNS, registrar, supply-chain JS, BGP). T4.008's fake-DEX **never had a real platform to compromise**; the entire UI is a counterfeit deployment hosted on a typosquatted / near-miss domain.
- **T4.001 / T4.004 / T4.005** — define the on-chain extraction primitive (Permit2 misuse, allowance-drainer, setApprovalForAll-drainer). T4.008 is a *distribution-surface* sub-Technique; the on-chain extraction primitive chains into T4.001 / T4.004 / T4.005 via the same drainer-kit substrate (Inferno / Angel / AngelFerno; MS Drainer; PhishLab) that the legitimate-frontend-compromise class uses.
- **T4.007 (Native-app Social Phishing on Engagement-Weighted Platforms)** — distribution surface is a target platform's *own* engagement mechanic. T4.008's distribution surface is paid-for inventory on a *separate* legitimate ad platform (search engine, social media, app store, messaging platform).

The novel structural feature is the **distribution surface** (paid ad placement on a legitimate ad platform) — not the on-chain extraction primitive. The detection-and-mitigation surface lives at the *ad-platform-side enforcement layer* (Google Ads tracking-template-misuse review; X promoted-post review; App Store / Play Store binary review; Telegram bot / channel takedown) rather than at the legitimate-platform infrastructure-hardening layer that resolves T4.002.

Four distinguishable distribution sub-surfaces:

- **T4.008.001 — Google Search paid ads.** Highest-volume sub-class. Attacker buys keyword inventory matching the legitimate platform's name; the paid result ranks above the legitimate platform's organic result. Tracking-template misuse displays the legitimate domain to Google Ads' review surface while the click resolves to the phishing URL. Calibration anchor: MS Drainer December 2023 ($59M / 63,210 victims; 10,000+ phishing websites; targeting Zapper / Lido / Stargate / DefiLlama / Orbiter / Radiant).
- **T4.008.002 — X (Twitter) paid promoted posts.** Second-highest-volume sub-class. ScamSniffer December 2023 reports six of nine paid phishing ads on their feed in late 2023 were MS Drainer instances. Calibration anchor: 2025-07-21 Polymarket-using DeFi user $1.23M loss to fake Uniswap clone reached through paid X / Google ad.
- **T4.008.003 — Telegram bot-driven traffic.** Attacker operates a Telegram channel or bot ("trade $TOKEN now," "snipe new tokens," "verify your wallet") that funnels users to the fake-DEX clone-frontend at signing time. Lure is community-amplified rather than search-amplified. Calibration anchor: 2024–2025 Solidus Labs PumpCell (~$800K/month October 2025) and Kaspersky / Group-IB observations of 2,000% surge in Telegram phishing-bot volume late 2024.
- **T4.008.004 — App Store fake-DEX wrapper apps.** Counterfeit Android / iOS applications listed under previously-benign or compromised developer accounts that wrap a phishing UI inside a WebView. The fake apps prompt users to enter their 12-word mnemonic phrase under the guise of "importing your wallet to PancakeSwap / SushiSwap / Raydium / Hyperliquid." PancakeSwap notably has no legitimate mobile application (web-only at v0.1 cutoff), so any mobile app under that brand is counterfeit. Calibration anchor: Cyble Research and Intelligence Labs 2024 cohort (22+ apps, 50+ phishing-domain infrastructure, Median-framework WebView wrapping); 2025 Google Play removal sweep (22+ apps).

## Observed indicators

- **Domain-side fingerprints:** typosquat / near-miss / homoglyph domains (`unlswap.org` vs `uniswap.org`; `pancake-swap.app` vs `pancakeswap.finance`); recent registration dates relative to claim of legitimate-platform-affiliation; absence of ENS / handle-equivalent integration that legitimate platforms typically have.
- **Ad-platform-side fingerprints:** paid Google Search ad ranking above the organic result; tracking-template alias displaying the legitimate domain to ad-review while resolving the click to a phishing URL; X / Twitter promoted post with a brand-impersonation account.
- **Mobile-app-side fingerprints:** WebView-wrapped phishing UIs (Median framework usage in the Cyble cohort); mnemonic-phrase input prompts during "wallet import" flows; embedded malicious URLs in privacy-policy text; app published under a developer account with sparse history.
- **On-chain backend fingerprints:** drainer-kit substrate (Inferno / Angel / AngelFerno; MS Drainer; PhishLab) at the on-chain extraction layer; spender-contract addresses observable in the user's signed transaction.

## Detection signals

- **Ad-platform-side enforcement (highest-leverage):** Google Ads tracking-template-misuse review; X promoted-post brand-verification at the post-creation stage; App Store / Play Store binary review with WebView-wrapper detection; Telegram bot / channel takedown coordination.
- **Domain-reputation services:** ScamSniffer / Web3 Antivirus / SEAL ISAC drainer-website blocklists; community-curated phishing-domain feeds (PhishFort, Chainabuse).
- **Wallet-side pre-trade simulation:** MetaMask / Rabby / Phantom / OKX Wallet pre-trade simulation surfaces drainer-spender-contract patterns and known phishing-domain warnings inline with the swap-confirmation prompt.
- **Independent-vendor cohort-tracking:** ScamSniffer's drainer-campaign tracking and December 2023 MS Drainer publication; SlowMist's 2024 Mid-year and Annual Blockchain Security Report; BleepingComputer / Infosecurity Magazine reporting; Group-IB Inferno Drainer technical analysis; per-victim public reporting via ZachXBT, Lookonchain, and SEAL Threat Intel.

## Real-world examples

- **Fake DEX / clone-frontend distribution cohort — multi-chain — 2023 onward (canonical T4.008 multi-year cohort)** — ~$59M stolen by the **MS Drainer** kit alone across **63,210 victims** between March 2023 and December 2023, primarily distributed through Google Search ads and X (Twitter) ads spoofing fake-DEX clone-frontends of Zapper, Lido, Stargate, DefiLlama, Orbiter Finance, and Radiant (per ScamSniffer's December 2023 cohort write-up). ~$1.23M drained from a single Polymarket-using DeFi user on 2025-07-21 via a fake Uniswap clone reached through a Google Ads paid result; the bonded wallet-drainer used was the AngelFerno descendant of Inferno. ~$494M aggregate 2024 wallet-drainer ecosystem total per ScamSniffer (67% YoY increase, 332,000 unique victim addresses), of which fake-DEX-distribution-class campaigns are the dominant per-incident shape; the named-incident calibration is the calibration anchor. 22+ counterfeit Android applications impersonating PancakeSwap, SushiSwap, Raydium, Hyperliquid, and similar DEXes removed from the Google Play Store across 2024–2025 per Cyble Research and Intelligence Labs (CRIL); the campaign infrastructure spanned 50+ phishing domains and was embedded in WebView-wrapped phishing sites. Attribution: `pseudonymous` at cohort scale; partially clustered in named cases. MS Drainer is attributed to a Telegram-handle-vendor "Pakulichev" / "PhishLab" who sold the kit for $1,500 plus a 20% revenue share. Inferno Drainer (and its post-November-2023 affiliate-base continuity into Angel Drainer) is the dominant kit substrate behind 2024 cases. Per-victim attribution typically lands on the *affiliate*, not the kit-vendor. See [`examples/2023-2026-fake-dex-clone-frontend-cohort.md`](../examples/2023-2026-fake-dex-clone-frontend-cohort.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T4.008's primary detection surface is at the ad-platform / app-store / domain-reputation layer, not the on-chain runtime risk-engine surface MG covers. Drainer-spender-contract pattern detection is in scope for MG's authority-grant detection module but the distribution-surface dimension is out of scope.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: ScamSniffer (drainer-campaign tracking; December 2023 MS Drainer publication); Web3 Antivirus / SEAL ISAC drainer-website blocklists; PhishFort; Chainabuse; SlowMist 2024 Mid-year and Annual Blockchain Security Report; Group-IB Inferno Drainer technical analysis; wallet-side pre-trade simulation (MetaMask, Rabby, Phantom, OKX Wallet); Cyble Research and Intelligence Labs Play Store cohort coverage.

## Mitigations

- **Ad-platform-side enforcement (highest-leverage):** Google Ads should harden tracking-template-misuse review; X / Twitter promoted-post review should require brand-verification at the post-creation stage for promoted posts targeting trademark keywords; App Store / Play Store should require binary review with WebView-wrapper detection. The cohort persists not because detection is unsolved but because the *ad-platform-side enforcement* lags the per-campaign click-through window.
- **Domain-reputation feeds:** drainer-website blocklists (ScamSniffer, Web3 Antivirus, SEAL ISAC) should be integrated into wallet pre-trade simulation surfaces and into ad-platform review pipelines.
- **Wallet-side pre-trade simulation:** MetaMask / Rabby / Phantom / OKX Wallet pre-trade simulation should surface drainer-spender-contract patterns and known phishing-domain warnings inline with the swap-confirmation prompt, with high-friction confirmation flow when the simulation surface fails.
- **Legitimate-DEX-side defensive registration:** legitimate DEX operators should defensively register typosquat / near-miss / homoglyph domains; publish authoritative-frontend lists; integrate ENS / handle-equivalent identity systems that wallet UIs can verify.
- **App-store-side WebView-wrapper detection:** App Store / Play Store binary review should detect WebView-wrapped phishing UIs as a discrete risk class; require enhanced review for apps that embed external URLs in privacy-policy text or that brand-impersonate established DEX operators.
- **User-side hygiene:** prefer typing the legitimate-DEX URL directly into the address bar rather than clicking ad results; verify the domain matches the canonical legitimate domain before connecting a wallet; treat any unsolicited "verify your wallet" / "claim airdrop" prompt as suspicious by default.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1 are a **gap** — no existing OAK-M* entry directly maps to ad-platform-side enforcement. v0.x candidate Mitigation entries: "OAK-M-x ad-platform tracking-template-misuse hardening"; "OAK-M-x app-store WebView-wrapper detection"; "OAK-M-x wallet-side pre-trade phishing-domain simulation".

## Citations

- See worked example for primary citations.
- MS Drainer cohort: `[scamsniffermsdrainer2023]`, `[bleepingmsdrainer2023]`, `[cointelegraphmsdrainer2023]`.
- Uniswap-clone Polymarket-user case: `[cryptonewsuniswap12m2025]`, `[protosuniswap2025]`, `[gateuniswap2025]`.
- Telegram cohort: `[kasperskytelegram2025]`, `[bleepingtelegrambots2024]`.
- App-store cohort: `[cyblecryptophishingapps2024]`, `[hackreadgoogleplaypishing2024]`, `[techradarcryptoplaystore2024]`.

## Discussion

T4.008 is the **highest-volume paid-traffic-distributed phishing class in the wallet-drainer category 2023–2026**. The distribution surface is **legitimately purchased ad inventory** rather than compromised infrastructure. The cohort persists not because detection is unsolved but because the *ad-platform-side enforcement* lags the per-campaign click-through window.

The structural distinction from T4.002 (Compromised Front-End Permit Solicitation) is the *substrate* of the user-facing UI. T4.002 compromises a real platform's user-facing surface (DNS, registrar, supply-chain JS, BGP); the fake-DEX class **never had a real platform to compromise**. The structural OAK lesson is that the user-side mental model "I'm interacting with the legitimate Uniswap because the UI looks identical to Uniswap" is structurally false because the substrate of the UI is attacker-controlled from the moment of deployment.

The four distribution sub-surfaces operate at structurally different ad-platform enforcement layers:

- **T4.008.001 (Google Search paid ads)** — search-engine-ad-review enforcement; tracking-template-misuse is the canonical evasion vector.
- **T4.008.002 (X promoted posts)** — social-media-ad-review enforcement; brand-impersonation is the canonical evasion vector.
- **T4.008.003 (Telegram bot-driven traffic)** — messaging-platform takedown enforcement; community-amplification is the lure rather than search-engine ranking.
- **T4.008.004 (App Store fake-DEX apps)** — app-store binary-review enforcement; WebView-wrapper is the canonical evasion vector.

The on-chain extraction primitive is shared across all four sub-surfaces: drainer-kit substrate (Inferno / Angel / AngelFerno; MS Drainer; PhishLab) chains into T4.001 / T4.004 / T4.005 at the on-chain layer. The kit-vendor / affiliate operator-cluster split is structural — the kit-vendor (Pakulichev / PhishLab for MS Drainer; OAK-G02 cohort for Inferno / Angel) sells the kit for a fee + revenue share; the affiliate operator runs the per-campaign distribution and captures the bulk of the realised loss.

The 2024 wallet-drainer ecosystem aggregate (~$494M per ScamSniffer; 67% YoY increase; 332,000 unique victim addresses) anchors T4.008 as the dominant per-incident shape across the cohort. The class is included at v0.1 with `emerging` maturity rather than `stable` because (a) the multi-year cohort anchor is composite rather than per-incident, (b) the ad-platform-side enforcement maturation is ongoing, and (c) the kit-vendor-vs-affiliate operator-cluster attribution depth is partial across the cohort.

The structural cross-reference to T11.007.003 (Brand-trust-leveraged active phishing for seed-phrase exfiltration) is informative: T11.007.003 targets hardware-wallet vendor brand-trust to solicit *seed phrases* via email / physical mail / companion-app prompts; T4.008 targets DEX brand-trust to solicit *on-chain authority grants* via paid-ad-distributed clone frontends. Both classes exploit brand-impersonation; the substrate-of-extraction differs (seed phrase vs. on-chain authority grant). Future T4.008 worked examples should preserve the distinction and avoid conflating the two classes.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once per-incident worked-example anchors land beyond the multi-year cohort file (per-incident anchors for MS Drainer December 2023, the 2025-07-21 Uniswap-clone Polymarket-user case, the Cyble Play Store cohort, and Telegram-bot-traffic-specific anchors). The current cohort file is sufficient to anchor T4.008 at `emerging` per the v0.1 promotion criteria but is structurally a single composite anchor rather than the multi-anchor independent-confirmation pattern that warrants `stable`.
docs.techniques/T5.001-hard-lp-drain.md:
# OAK-T5.001 — Hard LP Drain

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T5 (Value Extraction)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana
**First documented:** 2020 (industry reports)
**Aliases:** "hard rug", "LP withdrawal event"

## Description

A single-event removal of a substantial fraction of a pool's quote-side liquidity by the LP-controlling party, after which the token's tradable depth collapses and remaining holders cannot exit at the prior market price. From a defender's perspective, T5.001 is the textbook "exit event" — fully observable on-chain, occurring in a single (or near-single) transaction, and trivially attributable in retrospect. T5.001 is the canonical extraction event chained downstream of OAK-T1 (transfer-restriction or mint-authority setup), OAK-T2 (single-sided or locked-liquidity spoof), and OAK-T3 (holder-capture pump). Its detection-at-the-moment is straightforward; the defensive value lies in **pre-event** classification via T1/T2/T3 signals so that holders never enter exposed positions.

T5.001 also covers the NFT analogue: a mint-and-abandon pattern where the operator removes the project's runway capital (mint proceeds wallet) in a single sweep after primary-mint sell-out, leaving holders with no road-map delivery. NFT cases are scoped under T5.001 because the failure mode (one-event extraction by deployer cluster) and detection signal (deployer-cluster outflow) match the fungible-LP case; the asset class differs but the Tactic-level taxonomy does not.

## Observed indicators

- Large LP-burn-or-withdraw event by an address that traces to the deployer cluster (OAK-M04 funder-graph).
- Simultaneous collapse of pool depth (>50% in a single block) and trading-pair price.
- Pre-event pattern often matches an OAK-T2.002 (Locked-Liquidity Spoof) setup: nominal lock with weak custodian, short lock duration, or delegate-callable unlock.
- NFT analogue: mint-proceeds wallet sweeps to mixers / CEX deposit addresses within hours of mint-out (OAK-T7.001 chain).

## Detection signals
