- On-chain real-time: LP token movements above a chain-specific threshold (e.g., >25% of pool LP supply within 1 block on EVM; equivalent on Raydium / Orca pool-state diff for Solana) from clusters previously flagged at T1/T2.
- Pre-event correlation: liquidity-removal pattern correlated with prior locked-liquidity-spoof signals (OAK-M05 authority-graph).
- Block-builder telemetry (OAK-M06): mempool view of large LP-burn calls broadcast by deployer-cluster addresses prior to inclusion.
- Cluster-level: same operator deploying repeated tokens with matching post-launch withdrawal signature (cohort-level attribution per `[chainalysis2025rug]`).

## Real-world examples

- **AnubisDAO (ANKH) — Ethereum — 2021-10-28 / 2021-10-29** — Loss \~13,556 ETH (\~\$60M at the time). Approximately 20 hours into the project's token sale, the LP was drained from one transaction and the proceeds laundered through Tornado Cash within 24 hours (chained as OAK-T7.001). Canonical hard-rug case study with extensive public attribution analysis. Forensic write-ups: `[decryptanubis2021]`, `[cointelegraphanubismixer2022]`; aggregate context: `[chainalysis2021scams]`.
- **SQUID (Squid Game token) — BNB Chain — 2021-11-01** — Loss \~\$3.38M. After the token's price spike, the operator cluster removed pool liquidity in a single event, dropping the price effectively to zero within minutes. Combined with the OAK-T1.001 transfer-restriction pattern (the contract blocked sells), SQUID is a textbook example of the T1 + T2 + T5 chain executing in the same operator's playbook. Forensic write-up: `[trmsquid2021]`.
- **Frosties (NFT) — Ethereum — 2022-01-09** — Loss \~\$1.1M (~\$1.3M including downstream laundering). Founders Ethan Nguyen and Andre Llacuna sold out the mint then within hours transferred the proceeds to wallets under their control and abandoned the project. First U.S. DOJ NFT-rug-pull prosecution; conspirators charged with wire fraud and money-laundering conspiracy. NFT-side T5.001 reference. <!-- TODO: cite https://www.justice.gov/usao-sdny/pr/two-defendants-charged-non-fungible-token-nft-fraud-and-money-laundering-scheme-0 -->
- **Pixelmon (NFT) — Ethereum — 2022-02-25 reveal** — Mint-side raise \~\$70M (\~25,000 ETH at \$2,800). The operator (pseud. "Syber") allocated mint-proceeds to non-roadmap blue-chip NFT purchases (\~400 ETH BAYC / CloneX / Azuki shopping spree from the project wallet) ahead of an artwork reveal that holders considered an effective rug. Disputed-classification: not a single-block LP drain but a treasury-side hard-extraction case routinely cited in the NFT-rug cohort. <!-- TODO: cite https://decrypt.co/93964/70-million-pixelmon-founder-calls-artwork-reveal-horrible-mistake -->
- **Memecoin hard-rug cohort — multi-chain — 2024–2025** — `[slowmist2024report]` and the public ZachXBT investigative thread cohort document hundreds of memecoin hard-rugs per quarter on Solana / Base / BSC. v0.1 names AnubisDAO / SQUID / Frosties / Pixelmon as the canonical individually-named cases; the 2024–2025 cohort is documented at the cohort level rather than per-incident because per-incident write-ups are sparse and operator clusters are repeat offenders. Cohort context: `[slowmist2024report]`, `[chainalysis2025rug]`.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2021-04-easyfi.md`](../examples/2021-04-easyfi.md) — EasyFi admin-key compromise
- [`examples/2021-05-pancake-bunny.md`](../examples/2021-05-pancake-bunny.md) — PancakeBunny yield-aggregator mint-pricing exploit
- [`examples/2021-10-anubisdao.md`](../examples/2021-10-anubisdao.md) — AnubisDAO (ANKH)
- [`examples/2021-11-squid.md`](../examples/2021-11-squid.md) — SQUID (Squid Game token)
- [`examples/2023-02-hopefinance.md`](../examples/2023-02-hopefinance.md) — Hope Finance router-rerouting rug pull
- [`examples/2024-06-holograph.md`](../examples/2024-06-holograph.md) — Holograph deployer-key compromise unlimited-mint-and-dump
- [`examples/2021-2026-influencer-amplified-non-memecoin-rug-cohort.md`](../examples/2021-2026-influencer-amplified-non-memecoin-rug-cohort.md) — Influencer-amplified non-memecoin promotion-and-dump cohort (Save the Kids / KIDS, June 2021 BNB Chain — anti-dump-mechanism bypass one day before launch produced the LP-drain extraction shape; broader cohort references T5.001 where applicable).

## Reference implementations

- MG `mg-detectors-rs` — D02 (link TBD)
- Chainalysis — coverage in research / compliance products
- SolRPDS — Solana-specific dataset (`[solrpds]`)

## Mitigations

- **OAK-M04 (funder-graph clustering):** identify deployer-cluster ahead of LP creation; flag pools whose deployer cluster touches known repeat-rug clusters.
- **OAK-M05 (authority-graph enumeration):** map LP-token custodian and unlock authority before listing; combine with OAK-M25 (listing-time gate).
- **OAK-M11 (rate limiting / per-block caps):** at the venue layer, cap LP-removal velocity per block to convert any T5.001 attempt into a T5.002 (slow trickle) which is harder to monetise before defender response.
- **Traders / risk teams:** combine pre-event T1/T2 signals into a composite risk score before entry; T5.001 itself is too late to mitigate in-trade.
- **Protocols / launchpads:** require listing-level verification of LP authority structure before listing (OAK-M25).

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2025rug]`, `[chainalysis2021scams]`, `[solrpds]` — see `citations.bib` (industry retrospectives + dataset)
- `[decryptanubis2021]`, `[cointelegraphanubismixer2022]` — AnubisDAO case study
- `[trmsquid2021]` — SQUID case study
- `[slowmist2024report]` — 2024 cohort context for memecoin hard-rugs
- <!-- TODO: cite Frosties DOJ indictment (justice.gov/usao-sdny) -->
- <!-- TODO: cite Pixelmon reveal-rug (decrypt.co/93964) -->

## Discussion

T5.001 is the most-studied extraction Technique. Per-incident attribution is straightforward post-hoc; the open detection problem is **pre-event** classification, where the signal lives in T1/T2/T3 features rather than T5.001 itself. v0.1 scopes T5.001 to deployer-cluster-attributable single-event extractions; the borderline cases are: (a) governance-authorised LP migrations later disputed by holders (e.g., DAO-vote-driven LP-redistribution that holders consider a rug — out-of-scope at v0.1, candidate for OAK-T5.005-style "treasury management exit"); (b) contested NFT roadmap-abandonment cases where the operator did not directly drain LP but failed to deliver against the mint commitment (Pixelmon-class — currently scoped to T5.001 but a future v0.x split into "NFT mint-proceeds extraction" is plausible). Calibration gap (v0.x): the per-chain LP-removal velocity threshold is currently hand-tuned per detector; a calibrated dataset of confirmed T5.001 events with pool-baseline normalisation would improve detector precision.
docs.techniques/T5.002-slow-lp-trickle-remova:
# OAK-T5.002 — Slow LP Trickle Removal

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T5 (Value Extraction), OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM, Solana
**First documented:** 2024–2026 (TM-RugPull research, 2026; Tran et al. 2025 Fragmented Rug Pull)
**Aliases:** "slow rug", "trickle drain", "patient drain", "fragmented rug pull (FRP)"

## Description

A pattern in which the LP-controlling party removes liquidity gradually over an extended window rather than in a single event, defeating threshold-based real-time detectors that fire only on large single-event withdrawals. From a defender's perspective, the Technique is a deliberate counter to T5.001-style detectors: each withdrawal individually falls below per-transaction-impact thresholds, but the cumulative outflow over a calibrated window is a substantial fraction of original LP. Detection therefore requires **windowed cumulative** analysis rather than per-transaction thresholds, and **multi-actor cluster aggregation** rather than single-address watching. T5.002 is the canonical T6 (Defense Evasion) modifier on T5 (Value Extraction): the underlying extraction motive is identical to T5.001, and the technique-shift exists only to evade per-transaction detectors.

The Tran et al. 2025 Fragmented Rug Pull formalisation describes the operator recipe as: (i) "keep the lid on" — preserve LP control so on-chain extraction remains feasible; (ii) "chop thin slices" — split total exit volume into many low-impact micro-trades each below detector thresholds; (iii) "pass the ladle" — delegate sells across multiple wallets so each participant takes a small share of the extraction. The paper labels 105,434 of 303,614 deployed pools (Nov 2018 – Mar 2025) as FRP, indicating the cohort is materially large and not a long-tail edge case.

## Observed indicators

- Sustained net-outflow from LP-control cluster over a window of hours to weeks, with each individual outflow below typical alerting thresholds.
- Cumulative outflow eventually reaching a substantial fraction (>20–40%) of original liquidity over a rolling multi-day window.
- Outflow pattern that alternates with small inflows, complicating naive net-flow tracking.
- Multi-wallet "ladle" pattern: extraction routed through many non-deployer wallets that share funder-cluster lineage (OAK-M04).
- Pool-depth time series shows monotonic-ish decline against a baseline volatility envelope, distinguishable from organic LP rebalance.

## Detection signals

- Windowed cumulative net-flow per LP-control cluster over rolling windows (e.g., 24h, 7d, 30d); threshold on cumulative fraction of original LP at launch baseline.
- Trend detection on pool-depth time series independent of per-transaction sizes — fit a linear / EWMA decay against baseline volatility.
- Cluster-aggregated extraction: union all wallets in the deployer's funder-graph (OAK-M04) and apply the windowed-cumulative threshold against the union, not per-wallet.
- Cross-correlate with off-chain operator behaviour signals (social media inactivity onset, domain expiry, governance-forum disengagement) as additional features per `[fullycryptosoftrug]`.

## Real-world examples

- **Fragmented Rug Pull (FRP) cohort — multi-chain — 2018–2025** — Tran et al. 2025 (`[frp2025]`) labels 105,434 pools across 303,614 candidates as FRP, involving 34.19M transactions, 401,838 inflated-seller wallets, and 1.50M unique interacting addresses. The paper's labels are the canonical reference cohort; per-incident named cases inside the cohort are not individually called out by the authors at the per-token level. OAK v0.1 cites the cohort as the canonical FRP source; contributors with public forensic write-ups of individual FRP-class tokens are encouraged to submit named-incident PRs for v0.2.
- **SafeMoon (V1 / V2 / V3 lineage) — BNB Chain — 2021–2023** — `[secsafemoon2023]` documents the SEC's case characterising the project's tokenomics (10% transfer tax, dev wallet allocation, V1→V2 1000:1 migration) as supporting sustained deployer-side extraction over a multi-quarter window. The "reflection tax" structure means each transfer constitutes a small extraction routed to dev/marketing wallets — structurally a slow-trickle pattern even where individual events are below T5.001-class thresholds. v0.1 treats SafeMoon as a transition case between T1.005 (hidden fee-on-transfer) and T5.002 (slow accumulation of extraction at the dev-wallet); the SEC complaint is the canonical primary source.
- **Polywhale Finance (KRILL) — Polygon — 2021-06** — Loss \~\$1M+; treasury-wallet drained from \~5M KRILL to \~1.6M KRILL over the run-up to the abandonment, with \~1.04M routed to the dev wallet, with KRILL trading from \~\$1.50 to \~\$0.13. Often cited as the canonical "soft rug" Polygon-side reference case, illustrating the multi-day extraction window with social-channel disengagement following the cumulative outflow. <!-- TODO: cite https://cointelegraph.com/news/polywhale-finance-founders-accused-of-rug-pull-amid-abrupt-shut-down -->
- **Adjacent — TM-RugPull cohort** — `[tmrugpull2026]` provides multi-chain temporal-hygiene-strict labelled data including slow-extraction sub-classes useful for retraining detectors against operator-cluster drift across BSC / Ethereum / Polygon.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2019-01-cryptopia.md`](../examples/2019-01-cryptopia.md) — Cryptopia exchange sustained drain
- [`examples/2021-10-anubisdao.md`](../examples/2021-10-anubisdao.md) — AnubisDAO (ANKH)

## Reference implementations

- MG `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage gap as of v0.1 (calibration in progress; documented in roadmap)
- Public detectors — limited; FRP-class detection requires windowed cumulative metrics that most v0.1 commercial vendors do not yet expose

## Mitigations

- **OAK-M11 (rate limiting / per-block caps):** at the venue layer, cap per-cluster LP-removal velocity such that even a "thin slice" strategy hits the cap once aggregated across the deployer cluster — converts T5.002 back into an observable T5.001-shaped event the detector can see.
- **OAK-M04 (funder-graph clustering):** mandatory for T5.002 detection — without cluster-level aggregation, the "pass the ladle" stage trivially evades single-address watchers.
- **OAK-M17 (time-locked governance and multi-block quorum):** for protocols with governance-controlled LP, time-lock all LP-removal proposals so any extraction is observable in advance of execution.
- **Traders / risk teams:** monitor cumulative pool-depth change relative to launch baseline rather than relying on transaction-level alerts.
- **Detection engineers:** implement windowed cumulative metrics in addition to per-transaction thresholds; aggregate at the deployer-cluster level not the address level.

## Citations

- `[frp2025]` — Tran et al. 2025 Fragmented Rug Pull formalisation and labelled cohort
- `[tmrugpull2026]` — multi-chain dataset with slow-extraction sub-classes
- `[secsafemoon2023]` — SafeMoon SEC complaint (transfer-tax + dev-wallet extraction structure)
- `[fullycryptosoftrug]` — soft-rug pattern off-chain signals reference
- <!-- TODO: cite Polywhale rug pull (cointelegraph.com) -->

## Discussion

T5.002 is documented as a **gap** in v0.1 reference implementations. Two open calibration problems remain at v0.x: (1) **window-size selection** — too short and organic LP rebalance triggers false positives; too long and the detector observes the rug only in retrospect. The Tran et al. 2025 cohort suggests 24h-to-7d windows are the most informative band, but per-chain calibration is required (memecoin Solana cycles are faster than BSC yield-farm cycles). (2) **cumulative-fraction threshold** — the natural threshold (>X% of original LP removed within window Y) is sensitive to launch-time LP baseline, which itself can be spoofed via T2.001 (single-sided liquidity plant) or T2.002 (locked-liquidity spoof). Robust calibration requires combining the cumulative-fraction signal with a baseline-quality score (i.e., is the "100% baseline" actually meaningful), which is why OAK-M04 funder-graph clustering and OAK-M05 authority-graph enumeration appear as upstream prerequisites in the mitigation stack rather than parallel options. The boundary with T1.005 (hidden fee-on-transfer) needs sharpening: tax-routed extraction (SafeMoon-style) is technically a continuous-trickle pattern but is currently scoped to T1.005 because the extraction primitive lives in transfer-mechanics rather than LP-removal-mechanics; v0.2 should clarify whether transfer-tax extraction is a third sub-Technique under T5 or remains an OAK-T1.005-with-T5-effect cross-reference.
docs.techniques/T5.003-hidden-mint-dilution.m:
# OAK-T5.003 — Hidden-Mint Dilution

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T5 (Value Extraction)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana
**First documented:** 2021 (Xia et al. canonical Uniswap scam-token cohort)
**Aliases:** "stealth mint", "hidden inflation", "post-launch mint"

## Description

A token whose contract retains an authorised minting role that is exercised after holders have entered, increasing the supply held by deployer-controlled addresses while diluting existing holders. The Technique decomposes into a T1-level pre-event signal (mint authority exists in the deployed contract) and a T5-level event (mint is actually exercised against deployer-cluster recipients) — Technique pages cover both legs because both are required for end-to-end detection. T5.003 is distinct from T1.005 (hidden fee-on-transfer) in that the extraction primitive lives in **supply expansion**, not in transfer mechanics; it is distinct from T5.001 / T5.002 because the extraction does not require LP removal — the operator's freshly-minted tokens are sold into the pool against existing holder demand, transferring value directly via dilution rather than via LP-side withdrawal.

T5.003 also captures the variant where a smart-contract-platform-level vulnerability (rather than an explicit mint-role) lets an attacker inflate a token balance — e.g., reentrancy-into-mint or balance-tracking bugs in lending markets where the protocol's bookkeeping treats borrow + collateral as independent of the underlying ledger. These cases are scoped here because the defender-observable effect (post-event total-supply / circulating-supply diverging from documented schedule) and the mitigation surface (continuous on-chain supply reconciliation against expected schedule) are identical to the explicit-mint-role case.

## Observed indicators

- Contract retains a privileged mint role at deployment without an immutable cap or governance gating.
- Post-launch mint events to deployer-clustered addresses (OAK-M04 funder-graph attribution).
- Total supply trajectory inconsistent with the published tokenomics — pre/post block-balance diff exceeds cumulative `Mint` event sum (suggesting a non-standard mint path).
- Solana-specific: SPL `MintTo` instructions emitted by a non-renounced mint authority post-listing; SPL Token-2022 `permanent-delegate` extension exercised to materialise tokens at deployer-controlled accounts (pairs with OAK-T1.002).
- Lending-market variant: pool-level totalSupply / totalBorrow diverges from underlying-asset reserve in a single block following a flagged transaction (reentrancy-into-mint signal).

## Detection signals

- Static (pre-event): presence of mint authority unbounded by hard cap or governance; non-zero `mintAuthority` on Solana SPL mints; ERC-20 `mint(address,uint256)` callable by a non-multisig EOA without time-lock (OAK-M02 static-analysis).
- Real-time: mint events to clusters flagged in earlier Tactics; alert on any `Transfer(0x0, deployer-cluster, X)` event where `X` is non-trivial relative to circulating supply.
- Reconciliation: compare on-chain `totalSupply` against published tokenomics on a recurring cadence (per-block on EVM, per-slot on Solana); divergence beyond declared emission schedule is the canonical T5.003 signal.
- Cross-event balance-diff: in a single block, sum of all `Transfer` `from=0x0` events against the change in `totalSupply` should match exactly; mismatches indicate a non-standard supply-mutation path (e.g., direct storage write, balance-tracking bug, reentrancy-into-mint).
- OAK-M03 continuous bytecode-diff monitor: if mint-authority assignment is patched in via proxy upgrade or storage write after deployment, the bytecode-diff (or storage-slot-diff) is the upstream signal.

## Real-world examples

- [`examples/2020-12-cover-protocol-blacksmith-mint.md`](../examples/2020-12-cover-protocol-blacksmith-mint.md) — Cover Protocol Blacksmith infinite-mint exploit (2020-12-28). Canonical 2020 worked example for the T5.003 *smart-contract-platform-level supply-expansion* variant: a cache-vs-storage staleness bug in the Blacksmith.sol staking contract materialised ~4×10^19 COVER tokens against a designed circulating supply of ~100K — a forty-six-order-of-magnitude `totalSupply` divergence in a single block. White-hat-return outcome (`grap.finance` returned 4,350 ETH); snapshot-based 1:1 migration to new contract was the load-bearing recovery. PeckShield + Mudit Gupta + ImmuneBytes triangulated forensic coverage.
- **Uniswap scam-token cohort (Xia et al. 2021)** — `[xia2021mintdump]` provides the canonical labelled cohort: a large-scale Uniswap scam-token study including hidden-mint cases as a documented sub-class with on-chain artefacts and per-token dilution traces. This is the academic-grade primary reference for T5.003.
- **Rari Capital / Fei Fuse pools — Ethereum — 2022-04-30** — Loss \~\$80M. The exploit was a reentrancy on the Fuse-pool `exitMarket()` path that let the attacker effectively duplicate borrow-side balances against single-side collateral — a balance-inflation primitive functionally equivalent to an unauthorised mint against the lending market's bookkeeping. Borderline classification: the primary attack surface is reentrancy (OAK-T9 family), but the defender-observable effect is unauthorised supply expansion of the pool's debt-token / cToken-equivalent, which is the T5.003 signature. Cited as a hybrid T9-with-T5.003-effect reference. <!-- TODO: cite https://www.coindesk.com/business/2022/04/30/defi-lender-rari-capitalfei-loses-80m-in-hack -->
- **BadgerDAO front-end injection — Ethereum — 2021-12-02** — Loss \~\$120M. Primary classification is OAK-T4 (front-end-injected approval-solicitation), but the attacker's outflow path involved exercising approvals against vault contracts in a pattern that downstream commentary frames as "hidden allowance harvest"-equivalent to T5.003 supply-side extraction at the vault-share-token level. Cited here as cohort context only — primary classification remains T4. Sources: `[halbornbadger2021]`, `[badgerpostmortem2021]`, `[chainalysisbadger2021]`.
- **TM-RugPull multi-chain extension** — `[tmrugpull2026]` provides a multi-chain dataset with strict temporal hygiene including hidden-mint sub-classes useful for retraining detectors against operator-cluster drift; complements `[xia2021mintdump]` with 2024–2026 data.
- **2024–2026 SPL Token-2022 cohort** — operator clusters using SPL Token-2022 `permanent-delegate` and unrestricted-mint-authority extensions to materialise post-launch supply at attacker-controlled accounts. Documented as a chain-shaped variant rather than per-incident; primary reference: `[neodyme2024token2022]`, `[solana2024permdelegate]`.

## Reference implementations

- MG `mg-detectors-rs` — D06 (link TBD)
- GoPlus — partial via mint-authority fields
- RugCheck — partial
- Solana-side: `[solrpds]` covers SPL mint-authority telemetry

## Mitigations

- **OAK-M02 (static-analysis pre-deployment):** flag retained mint authority on listing intake; require either renounced authority or governance-gated authority for clean-listing status.
- **OAK-M17 (time-locked governance and multi-block quorum):** if mint authority is governance-held, require time-lock on all mint-execution paths so any minting is observable in advance.
- **OAK-M03 (continuous bytecode-diff monitoring):** detect post-deployment introduction of mint-paths via proxy upgrade.
- **OAK-M25 (listing-time source-verification and audit-status gate):** venues require T1.002-equivalent SPL-permanent-delegate disclosure and ERC-20 mint-authority disclosure as pre-listing data.
- **Traders / risk teams:** filter out tokens with active mint authority unless authority is on a published trusted-authority allowlist; treat tokenomics-vs-on-chain-supply reconciliation as a recurring (not one-time) check.
- **Protocols / launchpads:** require either renounced mint authority or explicit governance-gated mint authority before listing.

## Citations

- `[xia2021mintdump]` — see `citations.bib` (Uniswap scam-token cohort, canonical primary)
- `[tmrugpull2026]` — see `citations.bib` (multi-chain dataset)
- `[neodyme2024token2022]`, `[solana2024permdelegate]` — Solana SPL Token-2022 mint-authority-class context
- `[halbornbadger2021]`, `[badgerpostmortem2021]`, `[chainalysisbadger2021]` — BadgerDAO cohort context (primary classification T4; T5.003 effect)
- <!-- TODO: cite Rari Capital / Fei Fuse exploit (coindesk.com / certik.com) -->

## Discussion

Token standards differ significantly in how mint authority is represented (EVM ERC-20 vs Solana SPL vs SPL Token-2022 with `permanent-delegate`). v0.2 should split T5.003 into chain-specific sub-techniques if the detection methodology diverges materially: the EVM signal is contract-source / role-grant-event analysis; the Solana SPL signal is mint-authority-account state plus extension flags; SPL Token-2022 adds permanent-delegate and confidential-transfer surfaces that have no EVM analogue. Calibration gap (v0.x): the lending-market / smart-contract-bug variant of T5.003 (Rari-class) is currently scoped here based on defender-observable effect (supply expansion), but the underlying primitive is distinct from "explicit retained mint role" cases. A v0.2 split could promote the lending-market variant to its own sub-Technique under T9 (smart-contract exploit) with T5.003 cross-reference, mirroring how T6.001 modifies T1 / T9 across Tactics. Attribution caveats: where the mint-recipient is a protocol-level address (e.g., a treasury or staking contract) rather than a deployer-cluster address, classification depends on whether the destination is a transparent governance-controlled venue or a deployer-aliased address; OAK-M05 authority-graph enumeration is the load-bearing distinguisher.
docs.techniques/T5.004-sandwich-mev-extractio:
# OAK-T5.004 — Sandwich / MEV Extraction

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T5 (Value Extraction)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM
**First documented:** 2019 (Daian et al., "Flash Boys 2.0")
**Aliases:** "sandwich", "MEV sandwich", "front-run + back-run"

## Description

A value-extraction pattern in which an actor with mempool or block-ordering visibility surrounds a victim's pending swap with their own ordering-adjacent transactions, capturing the price difference. From a defender's perspective, the pattern is well-characterized in the academic and public-research literature, and detection is concerned primarily with **post-hoc attribution** (who was sandwiched, by whom, for how much) rather than prevention at the protocol layer.

## Observed indicators

- Three-transaction patterns ordered such that two transactions from a related actor bracket a victim swap, producing a net favorable outcome for the bracketing actor.
- Concentration of such patterns at specific block builders / relayers.
- Stable per-block / per-slot extraction profile against a known set of pools.

## Detection signals

- Block-structure analysis: scan ordered transactions for the canonical three-transaction shape and compute realized extraction value per pattern.
- Counterparty: cluster brackets by submitting actor; flag actors with sustained extraction volume.

## Real-world examples

- **`jaredfromsubway.eth` — Ethereum — operating since 2023-02-27** — Industry-tracked sandwich-MEV operator. EigenPhi's 2023 appraisal characterised the bot's first ~3 months as ~254K transactions of which ~98.31% were identified as front/back-runs, with ~106K victim swaps, ~\$40.6M revenue, and ~\$6.3M profit; cumulative metrics later reported include ~82,679 ETH MEV revenue against ~76,850 ETH gas spent. The operator is repeatedly cited as a canonical reference for how sandwich extraction looks at scale on a public chain. Analytics source: `[eigenphijared2023]`.
- **MEV-Boost equivocation attack — Ethereum — 2023-04-02** — Two MIT-affiliated brothers, Anton and James Peraire-Bueno, exploited a flaw in MEV-Boost's relay protocol to extract ~\$25M from sandwich bots in a single ~12-second window. They deliberately broke the trust assumptions between relays and validators by submitting a malformed proposal that revealed the contents of a private MEV bundle, then front-ran the disclosed transactions. The DOJ unsealed indictments in May 2024 (5 counts including wire fraud and money laundering); the case is the first criminal prosecution of an MEV-boost-layer exploit. The attack inverts the canonical sandwich pattern — sandwich operators were themselves the victims. See worked example at `examples/2023-mev-boost-equivocation.md`. <!-- TODO: cite https://www.justice.gov/usao-sdny/pr/two-brothers-arrested-attacking-ethereum-blockchain-and-stealing-25-million -->
- **High-value single-block sandwich cohort — Ethereum — 2022-2024** — EigenPhi maintains rolling per-incident dashboards for high-value sandwiches; documented cases include multi-million-dollar sandwiches against retail swaps that lacked slippage-tolerance configuration. The 2022 \$24M Wintermute-related sandwich-class incidents (where the victim was an algorithmic market-maker, not retail) bracket the upper bound of public single-incident extraction. Analytics: `[eigenphijared2023]` plus per-incident EigenPhi / Flashbots dashboards.
- **Solana Jito-bundle MEV extraction cohort — Solana — 2023 onward** — Jito bundle infrastructure created a Solana-side MEV-extraction surface analogous to MEV-Boost but with different primitives (no public mempool; bundle-auction-priority-based ordering). Documented at the cohort level by Jito's own reporting and by independent dashboards (Helius, Triton). Specific high-value Solana sandwich incidents are routinely surfaced by ZachXBT and ScamSniffer on a rolling basis. v0.1 scopes T5.004 to EVM (see Solana scope note in Discussion); a Solana-MEV sub-Technique is a v0.x candidate.
- **Foundational academic characterisation — 2019** — Daian et al., "Flash Boys 2.0" (`[daian2019flashboys]`), is the foundational empirical and theoretical characterisation of priority-gas-auction-based MEV extraction on Ethereum, including sandwich attacks. The paper introduced the framing that subsequent industry analytics (EigenPhi, Flashbots) operationalised.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2023-03-paraspace.md`](../examples/2023-03-paraspace.md) — ParaSpace whitehat reentrancy rescue by BlockSec
- [`examples/2023-jaredfromsubway-mev.md`](../examples/2023-jaredfromsubway-mev.md) — `jaredfromsubway.eth` sandwich-MEV operator
- [`examples/2024-08-ronin-bridge-rescue.md`](../examples/2024-08-ronin-bridge-rescue.md) — Ronin Bridge whitehat MEV-bot rescue
- [`examples/2024-06-solana-validator-sandwich-mev-cohort.md`](../examples/2024-06-solana-validator-sandwich-mev-cohort.md) — Solana validator-coordinated sandwich-bot cohort and Foundation delegation-program removal. **Adjacent cohort, not T5.004 proper at v0.1** — Solana leader-schedule + private-mempool substrate; proposed Solana-MEV sub-Technique sibling per the Discussion section's Solana scope note.

## Reference implementations

- MG `mg-detectors-rs` — D13 (link TBD)
- Eigenphi, Flashbots Protect — public dashboards / mitigation services

## Mitigations

- **Traders / risk teams:** route swaps through MEV-protected venues; cap slippage tolerance.
- **Protocols / venues:** integrate MEV-aware order flow (private mempools, batch auctions, fair-ordering relays).

## Citations

- `[daian2019flashboys]` — see `citations.bib` (foundational MEV characterisation)
- `[eigenphijared2023]` — see `citations.bib` (`jaredfromsubway.eth` analytics)

## Discussion

T5.004 is the most cleanly characterized Technique in v0.1 on EVM — it has well-established literature, public datasets, and stable detection methodology. It serves as the "gold standard" template for how OAK Techniques should be written.

**Solana scope note (v0.1):** Solana sandwich and MEV extraction is a distinct surface (no global mempool; Jito bundle inspection, Helius / Triton MEV-tracking signature shapes, leader-schedule timing, validator-to-builder priority-auction fills are the relevant primitives). v0.1 indicators in this Technique are EVM-mempool-shaped and would not detect Solana sandwich behaviour cleanly; rather than carry an EVM-only signal under a "Solana" Chains label, T5.004 is scoped to EVM at v0.1. A separate Solana-MEV Technique (or a Solana sub-Technique under T5.004) is a v0.x candidate once a calibrated detector with Jito-bundle-aware signals has reference-implementation status.
docs.techniques/T5.005-treasury-management-ex:
# OAK-T5.005 — Treasury-Management Exit

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T5 (Value Extraction), OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana
**First documented:** 2021 (Polywhale-class incidents); regulatory record 2023 (SafeMoon federal complaint)
**Aliases:** "soft rug via treasury", "treasury draw exit", "team-unlock dump", "salary-rug"

## Description

A class of OAK-T5 patterns in which a team-controlled multisig or treasury wallet executes outflows to founder, team, or "investor" addresses that are framed publicly as legitimate operating expenditure — "treasury management", "salary draws", "marketing spend", "team token unlocks", "OTC investor distributions" — but whose downstream pattern (size, cadence, destination clustering, lack of corresponding off-chain deliverables) is inconsistent with the stated rationale and consistent with operator exit. From a defender's perspective, T5.005 is the canonical pattern of **substantive misuse of legitimate authority**: the team multisig has standing authority at the contract and governance layer, the transfers are not unauthorised at the smart-contract level, and surface-level static analysis returns clean. The failure mode is *substantive* — the outflows do not match the publicly-stated purpose of the treasury.

T5.005 is structurally distinct from OAK-T5.001 (Hard LP Drain) because the authority being exercised is *legitimately retained*: the team multisig is publicly disclosed as the treasury custodian, holders entered with notice that it would execute outflows, and no covert backdoor is involved. It is structurally distinct from OAK-T1.003 (Renounced-but-not-really) because the team is not falsely claiming to have given up authority — the question is whether the publicly-retained authority is being exercised in good faith. T5.005 is therefore not a defect in authority *structure* but a defect in authority *use*, which is why it is the hardest T5 sub-Technique for purely on-chain detection and the one that most often requires an off-chain "stated-purpose" reference to score.

T5.005 frequently chains downstream of OAK-T2.002 (Locked-Liquidity Spoof) when the off-chain claim about how treasury funds are stewarded is itself the misrepresentation, and upstream of OAK-T7.001 / T7.002 (Mixer-Routed Hop / CEX Deposit Layering) when the operator subsequently launders the proceeds.

## Observed indicators

- Team-controlled multisig executing recurring or large outflows to founder / team / "investor" cluster addresses, framed in announcements as treasury management, salary, marketing, or team-unlock distributions.
- Outflow magnitude or cadence that diverges materially from the project's published budget, tokenomics, or vesting schedule.
- Outflow destinations that route directly (or after one-hop) to centralised-exchange deposit addresses, mixer entry points, or freshly-funded laundering clusters rather than to vendor / payroll / operational counterparties.
- Public communications that justify outflows in vague aggregate terms ("operations", "growth", "strategic partners") without itemised expenditure reports or counterparty disclosure.
- Treasury-balance trajectory inconsistent with continued protocol operation — for example, sustained net-outflow at a rate that exhausts runway long before the project's own published roadmap.
- Multisig signer set concentrated within a single operator cluster (founder + close associates), with no independent stakeholder seat and no timelock between proposal and execution.

## Detection signals

- Treasury-balance time series per disclosed multisig, with cumulative net-outflow scored against the project's published budget where available.
- Destination-cluster classification on multisig outflows: flag outflows that terminate at CEX deposits, mixers, or operator-cluster wallets within N hops; flag outflows whose destinations match clusters previously surfaced under OAK-T8.001 (Common-Funder Cluster Reuse) or OAK-T8.002 (Cross-Chain Operator Continuity).
- Stated-purpose reconciliation: cross-reference treasury outflow destinations with the project's published vendor / counterparty list; flag outflows to undeclared destinations.
- Multisig governance posture: enumerate signers; flag signer sets that are operator-cluster-internal, lack timelock, or lack on-chain governance gating between proposal and execution.
- Cross-token cluster signal: deployer cluster previously operated treasuries that exhibited the same signature.

T5.005 detection is necessarily probabilistic — the on-chain artefact alone (a multisig outflow to a fresh address) is not conclusive without the off-chain stated-purpose reference. The Technique's detection methodology therefore lives at the junction of on-chain treasury-flow analysis and off-chain budget-document ingestion, and is the canonical case for OAK's "operator-behaviour-detector + governance-document-ingestion" composite-detector pattern.

## Real-world examples

- **SafeMoon (SFM) — BNB Chain — federal action 2023-11-01** — The U.S. SEC complaint specifically alleges that SafeMoon executives misappropriated approximately \$200M from purportedly-locked liquidity for personal expenditures, with the regulatory filing identifying purchases of luxury vehicles ("McLaren cars"), real estate, and travel as among the misappropriated uses. The case is OAK's canonical regulatory-record example of T5.005 because the federal complaint documents both (a) the public framing under which the funds were said to be stewarded ("locked liquidity" treasury) and (b) the substantively-divergent actual use of the funds — exactly the public-frame-vs-substantive-use mismatch that T5.005 names. The case is mapped primarily to OAK-T2.002 (Locked-Liquidity Spoof) under OAK's parsimony rule (the federal complaint's central allegation is the misrepresentation about locked liquidity) and is documented in `examples/2023-11-safemoon-charges.md`; SafeMoon is referenced here as the canonical T2.002 + T5.005 chained case for contributors writing future T5.005 examples. Source: `[secsafemoon2023]`.
- **Cohort context — soft-rug-via-treasury class** — The "soft rug" or "slow rug via team treasury" cohort is documented in industry retrospectives as a non-trivial fraction of overall rug-pull losses; the canonical worked example in the public record is the Polywhale Finance incident (2021), in which the team was found to have sold millions of tokens from the team-treasury wallet over a multi-week window while maintaining public communications consistent with continued protocol operation, sources: `[fullycryptosoftrug]`. Aggregate cohort context: `[chainalysis2025rug]`. As of v0.1, OAK does not name single incidents from this cohort beyond SafeMoon and the Polywhale reference because the public forensic record for individual T5.005 cases is thinner than for T5.001-class hard-rug cases — the substantive-misuse failure mode is harder to attribute publicly without either a regulatory filing or a community-led forensic investigation. Contributors with public forensic write-ups of named T5.005 incidents are encouraged to submit them via PR.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2019-06-plus-token.md`](../examples/2019-06-plus-token.md) — PlusToken (Plus Token) Ponzi
- [`examples/2022-05-pixelmon-reveal.md`](../examples/2022-05-pixelmon-reveal.md) — Pixelmon NFT reveal-rug
- [`examples/2024-05-cypher-hoak-insider-redemption-theft.md`](../examples/2024-05-cypher-hoak-insider-redemption-theft.md) — Cypher Protocol Hoak insider extraction from post-incident redemption fund (Solana, T5.005-class against the recovery surface of a prior incident)
- [`examples/2025-04-meteora-m3m3-class-action.md`](../examples/2025-04-meteora-m3m3-class-action.md) — Meteora / Kelsier M3M3 class-action (alleged 95% supply concentration via 150 insider wallets; treasury-management / launch-mechanic exit alleged in complaint)

## Reference implementations

- MG `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage gap as of v0.1 (treasury-balance + destination-clustering primitives exist; budget-document ingestion is an open work item).
- Public dashboards: DefiLlama treasury views and DeepDAO governance dashboards expose treasury-balance time series and signer-set composition that defenders can cross-reference manually.
- Forensic / compliance: Chainalysis, TRM Labs, and Elliptic provide post-event attribution where outflows route through traced clusters.

## Mitigations

The canonical defensive stack for T5.005 is **governance-gating + treasury-budget-publication + multi-stakeholder approval**, applied at the protocol-design layer rather than at the per-trade detector layer:

- **Protocols / DAOs (design-time):** require on-chain timelock between treasury-outflow proposal and execution; require multi-stakeholder signer set on the treasury multisig (independent seats from at least one non-founder party — auditor, foundation, large-holder representative); publish a quarterly treasury-budget report with itemised expenditure categories and counterparty disclosure; vest team-unlock allocations through publicly-verifiable on-chain vesting contracts rather than through ad-hoc multisig draws.
- **Risk teams / traders (pre-entry):** treat absence of any of the above (no timelock, operator-cluster-internal signer set, no published budget) as a T5.005 elevated-risk signal and a precondition for sizing-down or avoiding exposure.
- **Detection engineers:** maintain a per-protocol treasury-outflow ledger; alert on cumulative net-outflow above a fraction of the published budget, on outflows to undeclared destinations, and on outflows whose destinations terminate at CEX deposits / mixers within N hops.
- **Wallet-vendor / launchpad pre-trade UX:** surface treasury-multisig governance posture (signer set, timelock, budget-publication cadence) in pre-entry risk warnings.

T5.005 is not preventable by smart-contract-level controls alone — the failure mode is in the interpretation of authority, not in the authority's existence. The mitigation surface is therefore protocol-governance design and pre-entry diligence, not transaction-level static analysis.

## Citations

- `[secsafemoon2023]` — see `citations.bib` (U.S. SEC press release 2023-229; canonical regulatory-record T5.005 example via SafeMoon; primary reference in `examples/2023-11-safemoon-charges.md`).
- `[chainalysis2025rug]` — see `citations.bib` (industry retrospective; aggregate rug-pull cohort context).
- `[fullycryptosoftrug]` — see `citations.bib` (industry write-up documenting the Polywhale Finance team-treasury sell-off as the worked example of the soft-rug-via-treasury pattern).

## Discussion

T5.005 is the cleanest worked example in the OAK taxonomy of **substantive misuse of legitimate authority**, and is the canonical teaching point for distinguishing structural-defect Techniques (where the contract or authority *structure* itself is malicious — T1.001, T1.003, T2.002) from substantive-defect Techniques (where the *exercise* of an otherwise-legitimate authority is the failure). This distinction matters because the two classes require structurally different defensive stacks: structural-defect Techniques are addressable at the static-analysis-and-listing-precondition layer, whereas substantive-defect Techniques like T5.005 are addressable only at the protocol-governance-design and pre-entry-diligence layer, and require off-chain stated-purpose references to score reliably.

T5.005 is also the Technique most often confused with OAK-T5.001 (Hard LP Drain) by contributors writing their first OAK-T5 example: any case in which "team funds disappeared" superficially looks like both. The distinguishing question is whether the authority exercised was (a) the LP-control authority, executed in a single substantial event, against the public lock claim (T5.001), or (b) the publicly-disclosed treasury-management authority, executed in a stated-purpose-divergent manner (T5.005). SafeMoon is mapped primarily to T2.002 in `examples/2023-11-safemoon-charges.md` because the federal complaint's central allegation is the locked-liquidity misrepresentation rather than a stated-purpose-divergent treasury draw; the case is referenced here for the *secondary* T5.005 framing of the misappropriated proceeds, illustrating how a single incident can chain across multiple Techniques.

Future OAK versions are expected to split T5.005 into sub-Techniques along the stated-purpose-category axis (T5.005.NN — salary-draw, marketing-spend, team-unlock, OTC-investor-distribution), once the public forensic record is rich enough to characterise per-sub-pattern detection methodology distinctly. v0.1 publishes T5.005 as a single umbrella Technique and documents the substantive-misuse-of-legitimate-authority framing as the key teaching point.
docs.techniques/T5.006-vesting-cliff-dump.md:
# OAK-T5.006 — Vesting Cliff Dump

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T5 (Value Extraction)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM, Solana, Cosmos, BNB Chain (any chain hosting time-locked allocation contracts)
**First documented:** 2021–2024 (industry retrospectives at token-unlock-tracker level)
**Aliases:** "cliff dump", "unlock dump", "investor unlock sell-pressure"

## Description

A class of OAK-T5 (Value Extraction) patterns in which team / early-investor / advisor / KOL allocations become liquid at a scheduled vesting cliff event, and the recipients coordinate large sales into the public market in a short window — converting illiquid allocation into liquid extraction. The contractual basis for the unlock is fully legitimate: the tokens were granted under a published vesting schedule, the cliff is reached, and the recipient exercises a transfer right that is theirs to exercise.

T5.006 is therefore **not** a contract-correctness failure (the smart contract behaves exactly as designed) and **not** an operator rug pull (no one is breaking any agreement). It is included in OAK because the *defensive question* that holders, market-makers, and treasury risk teams face is real even when no contract is violated: are the cliff-unlock outflows consistent with publicly-stated investor / team intent (gradual disposal, market-making participation, OTC placement), or are they consistent with coordinated front-of-window dumping into the public order book?

T5.006 is distinct from:

- **OAK-T3.003 (Coordinated Pump-and-Dump)** — T3.003 participants are anonymous coordinated speculators with no contractual claim on the asset; T5.006 participants are identifiable team / investor recipients exercising contractually-legitimate authority.
- **OAK-T5.005 (Treasury-Management Exit)** — T5.005 assets are treasury-multisig-controlled and the contractual basis is what is under scrutiny; T5.006 assets are individually-allocated tokens whose contractual basis is intact, and the scrutiny is on coordination of disposal across recipients.
- **OAK-T5.001 / T5.002 (LP Drains)** — LP-drain patterns concern pool-side liquidity removal by an LP-control cluster; T5.006 concerns sell-side flow from non-LP recipient wallets into the public market.

## Observed indicators

- **Per-cliff outflow concentration** — a substantial fraction of recipient wallets (team, investor, advisor cohorts) move tokens off allocation contracts into exchange-deposit clusters within a short window of the cliff timestamp (hours to days).
- **Pre-cliff price action** — sustained price weakness in the days leading up to a publicly-known cliff date, consistent with informed market-makers / hedgers anticipating sell pressure; in some cases accompanied by perp-market open-interest skew or borrow-rate spikes for the asset.
- **Pre-cliff insider OTC placement** — a "selling-while-vesting" pattern in which recipients route allocations through OTC desks before the cliff (via assignable claims, structured forwards, or off-chain agreements settling on cliff), so that the on-cliff transfer is from recipient to OTC counterparty rather than to exchange. Visible on-chain only as unusual recipient-cluster outflow shapes.
- **Cohort coordination** — clustered destination addresses (same exchange, same OTC desk, same market-maker), or clustered timing (same block range), across nominally-independent investor cohorts.
- **Inconsistency with public commitments** — outflow profile materially diverges from what the project / lead investors stated publicly about disposal intent (e.g., "long-term aligned", "gradual disposal over 12+ months", "no sales planned at cliff").

## Detection signals

- **Per-token unlock-tracker integration** — ingest cliff calendars from token-unlock-trackers (TokenUnlocks / Tokenomist, CryptoRank, DefiLlama Unlocks, DropsTab) as scheduled-event input; pre-position monitoring on each known cliff for tokens in scope.
- **Per-recipient outflow monitoring** — for each known allocation contract, enumerate recipient cluster addresses; monitor outflow from those clusters within a window around the cliff (typically T-7d to T+30d) and label by destination class (CEX deposit, OTC desk, on-chain DEX swap, internal transfer).
- **Cliff-window concentration metric** — per cliff event, compute the share of unlocked supply that reaches exchange-deposit or DEX-swap destinations within the first N days; compare against the share that remains in recipient custody.
- **Pre-cliff price / derivatives signal** — track spot price drift, perp open-interest skew, and borrow-rate moves in the T-14d window before each scheduled cliff for tokens above a market-cap / liquidity threshold; persistent pre-cliff weakness across cliffs is itself a defensive signal.
- **Cohort clustering** — cluster recipient outflows by destination-address overlap, timing overlap, and routing-pattern overlap; flag clusters whose coordination shape exceeds what would be expected from independent disposal decisions.
- **Public-statement reconciliation** — text-extract investor / team public statements about disposal intent; flag cliff-window behaviour that materially diverges.

## Real-world examples

The pattern is well-characterised at the **token-unlock-tracker cohort level** rather than as named single incidents. Cohort framing is the canonical reference for v0.1: TokenUnlocks / Tokenomist, CryptoRank, DefiLlama Unlocks, and DropsTab each publish, for hundreds of post-2021-launch tokens, the scheduled cliff calendar plus retrospective price action around each cliff. Industry retrospectives at Chainalysis cover the broader market-manipulation context within which cliff-dump behaviour sits.

Many post-2021 token launches have documented vesting-cliff-dump patterns: investor-cliff unlocks correlated with sharp price drops, and pre-cliff insider OTC-sale ("selling-while-vesting") cases are visible in the cohort. As of v0.1, OAK does not name single incidents — the unlock-tracker datasets are the canonical reference, and contributors with public forensic write-ups of specific named cliff-dump cases are encouraged to submit them via PR. Aggregate / cohort sources: `[tokenunlocks2026platform]`, `[cryptorank2026unlock]`, `[defillama2026unlocks]`, `[chainalysis2025rug]`.

## Reference implementations

- **TokenUnlocks / Tokenomist** (`tokenunlocks.app` / `tokenomist.ai`) — canonical access path for scheduled cliff calendars, per-token allocation breakdowns, and retrospective unlock-vs-price overlays.
- **CryptoRank** (`cryptorank.io/token-unlock`) — per-token vesting schedule view with allocation-group filters (Team & Advisors, VC & Private Investors, Community, Treasury) directly aligned with T5.006's recipient-cohort framing.
- **DefiLlama Unlocks** (`defillama.com/unlocks`) — open-data feed; suitable as an integration source for detector pipelines.
- **DropsTab** — secondary tracker; useful for cross-validation of cliff dates.
- MG `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage gap as of v0.1 (T5.006 is new; recipient-cluster outflow monitoring around scheduled cliffs is a v0.x roadmap item).

## Mitigations

- **Project / token-issuer side:**
  - **Longer / smoother vesting curves** — replace large cliff unlocks with linear or near-linear release schedules that distribute sell-pressure across a wider time window.
  - **Gradual cliff release** — where a cliff is required (e.g., for tax / contractual reasons), split it into multiple smaller cliffs over weeks rather than a single large unlock event.
  - **Vesting-cliff disclosure standards** — publish, per cohort, the disposal intent at cliff (sell / hold / OTC); commit to disclosure of cliff-window net-flow ex-post. A clear stated intent is the precondition that lets defenders distinguish coordinated dumping from disclosed-and-expected disposal.
- **Listing-venue / launchpad side:**
  - Require vesting-cliff disclosure (full schedule, recipient cohorts, intent statement) as a listing precondition.
  - Require cliff-window post-disclosure (actual recipient-cluster outflow vs stated intent) as a listing-maintenance condition.
- **Trader / risk-team side:**
  - Pre-position around scheduled cliffs for any token in active book; size exposure relative to cliff calendar and historical cliff-window concentration metrics.
  - Treat materially-divergent cliff-window behaviour vs stated intent as a standalone risk signal independent of any single cliff's price outcome.
- **Detection-engineering side:**
  - Wire token-unlock-tracker calendars as scheduled-event input rather than treating each cliff as a surprise.
  - Build per-recipient-cluster outflow baselines so cliff-window concentration is measurable against the cohort's normal disposal cadence.

## Citations

- `[tokenunlocks2026platform]` — see `citations.bib` (TokenUnlocks / Tokenomist; canonical cliff-calendar access path)
- `[cryptorank2026unlock]` — see `citations.bib` (CryptoRank token-unlock dashboard; allocation-group framing)
- `[defillama2026unlocks]` — see `citations.bib` (DefiLlama Unlocks; open-data integration source)
- `[chainalysis2025rug]` — see `citations.bib` (broader market-manipulation context within which cliff-dump behaviour sits)

## Discussion

T5.006 is the first OAK Technique whose underlying activity is **fully contractually legitimate**. It is included because OAK is a defender-side framework, and the defender's question is empirical (what is the cliff-window outflow shape?) rather than normative (is the unlock allowed?). Holders, market-makers, and treasury risk teams who treat every scheduled cliff as a neutral event end up systematically on the wrong side of cliff-window flow; documenting the pattern lets defenders pre-position rather than react.

OAK takes no partisan position on whether a given recipient's disposal at a legitimate cliff is "wrong". The framework documents (a) the defensive question, (b) the indicators and signals that let it be answered for any given cliff, and (c) the disclosure-standard mitigations that let issuers and listing venues reduce ambiguity in advance. v0.x evolution should add per-recipient-cluster outflow detectors and a disclosure-standard schema (machine-readable cliff disclosure + post-cliff reconciliation) so that "stated disposal intent" can be checked against on-chain reality without per-token manual analysis.

A v0.x split is plausible into (a) **on-cliff dumping** (recipient → exchange / DEX in cliff window) and (b) **pre-cliff OTC pre-placement** ("selling-while-vesting" patterns visible only as recipient-side flow shape) — the two have meaningfully different detection methodologies and warrant separate sub-Techniques once the data supports calibration.
docs.techniques/T5.007-third-party-brand-impe:
# OAK-T5.007 — Third-party Brand-impersonation Custodial Soft-rug

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T5 (Value Extraction)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (Polygon-resident Polymarket-branded canonical case at v0.1; cross-platform analogues across any high-trust platform whose brand can be impersonated by off-platform services — Polymarket, Hyperliquid, dYdX, Pump.fun, GMX, prediction-market and trader-tooling ecosystems)
**First documented:** 2026-01 (Polycule trading bot — Polymarket-branded but unaffiliated, ~$230K, exit-via-"hack" announcement followed by communication blackout)
**Aliases:** "third-party brand-impersonation soft rug", "Polymarket-branded bot exit", "fake-affiliated trading-bot rug", "custodial soft-rug exit-as-hack"

## Description

An **off-platform service brands itself in a legitimate platform's name without contractual basis**, accumulates user deposits in operator-controlled custody, and exits citing an unverifiable "hack." The substrate of extraction is *user funds voluntarily deposited into operator-controlled custody* on the assumption of platform-affiliation that does not exist. The operator never had a fiduciary or contractual relationship with the platform whose brand they used.

T5.007 is **structurally distinct** from sibling T5 Value-Extraction Techniques and adjacent classes:

- **T5.001 (Hard LP Drain) / T5.002 (Slow LP Trickle Removal)** — token-level liquidity extraction via AMM pool manipulation. T5.007 has no token, no LP, no AMM pool; the substrate is a custodial deposit address.
- **T5.005 (Treasury-Management Exit)** — operator-side exit on a *real* DeFi platform where the operator had a fiduciary relationship with the protocol. T5.007's operator never had such a relationship; the brand is exploited rather than the contractual relationship.
- **T11.001 (Third-Party Signing-Vendor Compromise)** — third-party signing-vendor relationship at the organisational-customer layer. T5.007's "third party" is a brand-impersonating operator with no signing-vendor relationship; the operator *is* the custodian.
- **T11.005 (Operator-side Fake-Platform Fraud)** — closest adjacent class. T11.005 covers end-to-end operator-controlled platform fraud where the platform itself is the vehicle of theft (fake-CEX / pig-butchering / fake-custodian / fake-asset-manager). T5.007 differs in the brand-substrate dimension: T11.005's operator constructs an apparently-original platform brand (BeurAx-class fake exchanges; OneCoin / HyperVerse fake yield products); T5.007's operator brands itself in a *legitimate platform's* name without contractual basis, exploiting the legitimate platform's existing trust to accumulate deposits.
- **T4 phishing** — phishing campaigns acquire on-chain authority on a real platform without user-deposit. T5.007 users deposit *willingly* to the operator-controlled wallet on the assumption of platform-affiliation.

The structural feature is **brand-as-asset**: the operator extracts the legitimate platform's brand-trust without contractual basis, accumulates deposits on that exploited trust, runs custodial flow, and exits citing a "hack" that cannot be independently verified.

The exit-as-hack pattern is the **canonical T5.007 exit primitive**: the operator announces an external compromise (a "hack" of the operator-controlled wallet) which is structurally indistinguishable from an exit-scam without independent forensic verification. The on-chain outflow pattern often surfaces a single coordinated transfer (consistent with operator exit) rather than the dispersion pattern characteristic of an external attacker exfiltrating quickly under time pressure.

## Observed indicators

- **Off-platform service** branding itself with a legitimate platform's name without official partnership announcement; absence from the legitimate platform's authorised-third-party-service registry (where one exists).
- **Custodial deposit flow:** users deposit USDC / ETH / BTC into a service-controlled wallet rather than connecting their own wallet to a smart-contract integration.
- **"Hack" announcement on Telegram / Discord / X** characterising an external compromise of the operator-controlled wallet, typically without independent forensic disclosure or on-chain attacker-attribution analysis.
- **Communication blackout post-announcement:** absence of follow-up updates, no public operator identification, no observable recovery effort, no engagement with industry-press or community-forensic analysts.
- **On-chain exit-pattern fingerprint:** single coordinated outflow transfer pattern from the operator wallet (consistent with operator exit) rather than the dispersion / fragmented-extraction pattern characteristic of external attacker exfiltration.
- **Pre-event operating window:** the service operated publicly through some period (months to years) with no formal partnership announced; users assumed an affiliation that did not exist.

## Detection signals

- **Platform-side authoritative-third-party-service registry publication:** the legitimate platform publishes a vetted registry of authorised third-party services. Absence from the registry is the load-bearing user-side detection signal.
- **Brand-impersonation surveillance:** trademark monitoring; domain monitoring (typosquat / brand-association domain registration); Telegram-handle / X-handle brand-impersonation monitoring; legitimate-platform-side cease-and-desist outreach to clearly-impersonating services.
- **Industry-press cohort framing:** secondary-coverage of T5.007 cases (KuCoin and Cryptorank coverage of the Polycule case characterised it as fitting the soft-rug pattern) provides cohort-level detection at the framework layer.
- **On-chain exit-pattern analysis:** post-announcement on-chain analysis of the outflow pattern to distinguish operator-exit from external-attacker exfiltration; coordinated single-transfer pattern is the operator-exit fingerprint.
- **Operator-attribution surface:** post-announcement attempts to identify the operators publicly; communication blackout in response to attribution attempts is itself a T5.007 fingerprint.

## Real-world examples

- **Polycule trading bot — third-party brand-impersonation soft rug — 2026-01 (canonical T5.007 case)** — ~$230,000 in user funds frozen / withdrawn by operator. The Telegram-distributed "Polycule trading bot" branded itself as a Polymarket-integrated copy-trading service and operated a custodial wallet that users deposited USDC into. On January 13, 2026, the Polycule operators announced that the bot's wallet had been "hacked" and ~$230,000 in user funds had been drained. After the announcement, the operators went into communication blackout — no follow-up updates, no public operator identification, no observable recovery effort. The KuCoin and Cryptorank coverage characterised the event as fitting the soft-rug pattern: a custodial third-party tool brands itself with a legitimate platform's name, accumulates user deposits, and exits citing an external compromise that cannot be independently verified. Polycule occupies a category that v0.1 OAK does not cleanly cover at the existing T5 / T11 sub-Technique level: not a token-level rug (T5.001 / T2.002); not an operator-side treasury-management exit (T5.005 — the operator was never contractually associated with Polymarket); not phishing (T4 — users deposited willingly); not a custodial-vendor compromise (T11.001 — there was no third-party signing-vendor relationship; the Polycule operators *were* the custodian). Attribution: `inferred-strong`. The Polycule operators were never associated with Polymarket; the platform clarified it had no relationship with the bot. Operator identity was not publicly resolved as of v0.1; the disappearance pattern (communication blackout following the "hack" announcement, no recovery effort, no public operator identification) is consistent with an exit-scam rather than a third-party intrusion. See [`examples/2026-01-polymarket-polycule-bot.md`](../examples/2026-01-polymarket-polycule-bot.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T5.007's primary detection surface is at the brand-monitoring / authoritative-third-party-service-registry / off-chain operator-attribution layer, not the on-chain runtime risk-engine surface MG covers. On-chain exit-pattern analysis is in scope for MG's outflow-pattern detection but the brand-impersonation dimension is out of scope.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: industry-press cohort framing (KuCoin, Cryptorank, secondary-coverage outlets); ZachXBT and SEAL Threat Intel public disclosures of brand-impersonation events; legitimate-platform-side cease-and-desist publication where executed; trademark and domain-monitoring services.

## Mitigations

- **Platform-side authoritative-third-party-service registry (highest-leverage):** legitimate platforms with material third-party tooling ecosystems (Polymarket, Hyperliquid, Pump.fun, prediction-market and trader-tooling ecosystems generally) should publish authoritative registries of authorised partners. The registry is the load-bearing user-side detection primitive — users can verify a service's authorisation by registry lookup before depositing.
- **Platform-side brand-impersonation surveillance:** trademark / domain / Telegram-handle / X-handle monitoring + cease-and-desist outreach to clearly-impersonating services. The platform-side surveillance burden is non-trivial but the alternative (relying on user-side identification of impersonation) imposes the burden on individual users.
- **User-side custodial-deposit invariant:** treat any deposit to an off-platform service as a custodial deposit to the service operator, regardless of brand affiliation claimed; prefer self-custody-with-smart-contract-integration over operator-controlled-wallet deposit flows.
- **Industry-press cohort framing:** secondary-coverage outlets that recognise T5.007 cases as a structural class (KuCoin and Cryptorank coverage of the Polycule case) accelerate cohort-level user awareness across competing platforms.
- **On-chain exit-pattern analysis as evidence-of-exit-scam:** post-announcement on-chain forensic analysis (single coordinated transfer pattern vs. dispersion pattern) provides the technical evidence to distinguish operator-exit from external-attacker exfiltration. Public-record exit-pattern analysis disciplines future T5.007 attempts by raising attribution-resolution probability.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1 are a **gap** — no existing OAK-M* entry directly maps to platform-side brand-impersonation surveillance or authoritative-third-party-service-registry publication. v0.x candidate Mitigation entries: "OAK-M-x platform-side authoritative-third-party-service-registry"; "OAK-M-x platform-side brand-impersonation surveillance"; "OAK-M-x on-chain exit-pattern analysis as exit-scam evidence".

## Citations

- See worked example for primary citations.
- Polycule case: KuCoin and Cryptorank secondary coverage; Polymarket public clarification of no relationship with the bot; Telegram channel record of "hack" announcement and subsequent communication blackout.

## Discussion

T5.007 closes a structural gap in OAK v0.1's T5 coverage. The dominant v0.1 T5 sub-Techniques (T5.001-T5.006) all operate within a real platform's contractual / on-chain primitive surface — hard LP drain, slow LP trickle, hidden mint, sandwich MEV, treasury-management exit, vesting-cliff dump. T5.007 introduces an extraction class that operates *off-platform* with the legitimate platform's brand as the exploited asset.

The structural OAK lesson is that **brand-trust is itself an exploitable asset** for value-extraction operators, and platform-side authoritative-third-party-service registries are a load-bearing user-side mitigation primitive that legitimate platforms should publish for any tooling ecosystem with non-trivial deposit volume.

The T11.005-vs-T5.007 distinction is structurally important and worth preserving in future contributor guidance:

- **T11.005** covers end-to-end operator-controlled platform fraud where the platform itself is the vehicle of theft. The operator constructs an apparently-original platform brand (BeurAx-class fake exchanges; OneCoin / HyperVerse fake yield products) and the brand has no legitimate counterpart.
- **T5.007** covers off-platform service that brands itself in a *legitimate platform's* name without contractual basis. The brand has a legitimate counterpart whose trust is exploited; the legitimate platform is a third-party victim of the brand-impersonation alongside the depositing users.

The exit-as-hack pattern is the **canonical T5.007 exit primitive**. The operator announces an external compromise of the operator-controlled wallet — a claim that is structurally indistinguishable from an exit-scam without independent forensic verification. The on-chain outflow pattern often surfaces a single coordinated transfer (consistent with operator exit) rather than the dispersion pattern characteristic of an external attacker exfiltrating quickly under time pressure. Post-announcement communication blackout reinforces the operator-exit interpretation.

The structural similarity to the **Inferno Drainer / DaaS pattern** at the brand-as-exploited-asset layer is informative but not identical: DaaS phishing captures keys at signing time; T5.007 captures funds at *deposit* time. Users send funds voluntarily, expecting custodial-but-honest service. Defender practice should treat any deposit to an off-platform brand-impersonating service as a custodial deposit to the *service operator's identity* rather than to the impersonated platform's brand.

The Polymarket cohort is structurally significant for T5.007 because the platform's prediction-market tooling ecosystem has a high density of off-platform third-party services (copy-trading bots, analytics tools, trading agents) and the brand-impersonation surface is correspondingly broad. Pipeline anchor cases for v0.x include additional Polymarket-branded soft-rug events; Hyperliquid-branded soft-rug events; Pump.fun-branded soft-rug events; and trader-tooling-ecosystem-branded soft-rug events across the broader DeFi space.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once additional cohort cases land and once at least one case reaches `confirmed` attribution strength (named-individual operator attribution via DOJ / regulator action or via ZachXBT-style public-forensic identification with corroborating documentation). The class anchor at v0.1 (Polycule case) is sufficient for `emerging` status but the multi-anchor pattern that warrants `stable` is still forming.
docs.techniques/T6.001-source-verification-mi:
# OAK-T6.001 — Source-Verification Mismatch

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion); modifier on T1 (Token Genesis) and T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit) classes when paired
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM (primary)
**First documented:** widespread from approximately 2020 onward
**Aliases:** "verified-but-not-really", "fake source verification", "bytecode-source mismatch"

## Description

A class of evasion patterns in which a contract's deployed bytecode differs from the source code claimed at the block explorer or via off-chain marketing — typically because the verified source corresponds to an earlier, benign deployment that has since been replaced by a different bytecode at the same address (via proxy upgrade, `selfdestruct` + redeploy via CREATE2, or claim-only-no-actual-verification). From a defender's perspective, T6.001 defeats static analysis tools that read the verified source — the analyst sees one contract while the chain executes a different one — and is the canonical OAK-T6 modifier on T1 and T9 classes when the underlying primary Technique would otherwise be detectable via source-level review.

T6.001 is the first standalone OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion) Technique because the failure mode is *generic* (it modifies any source-readable Technique) and the detection signal is *unambiguous* (compare deployed bytecode against the bytecode produced by recompiling the verified source — they should match within compiler-deterministic noise).

## Observed indicators

- A contract whose verified-source compilation does not produce the deployed bytecode under reasonable compiler-version / optimisation-flag combinations.
- A contract whose verified-source claim has been updated multiple times against the same address (suggesting either legitimate proxy-pattern usage or T6.001 source-shuffling — the distinction requires deeper review).
- A contract whose source is "verified" only via partial-match (matching constructor arguments and metadata but with bytecode differences) — partial-match verification is a real block-explorer feature and is not in itself indicative of T6.001, but partial-match plus a marketing claim of full verification is.

## Detection signals

- Deterministic recompile: pull the verified source, recompile under the claimed compiler version + flags, compare the produced bytecode against the on-chain deployed bytecode. Non-match (beyond compiler-deterministic noise) is the canonical T6.001 signal.
- Proxy-pattern review: where the contract is a proxy, ensure the implementation address used at runtime corresponds to the verified-source claim; T6.001 frequently combines with proxy-upgrade patterns to point the proxy at unverified implementation contracts.
- CREATE2-replay detection: if a contract was deployed via CREATE2 and a `selfdestruct` event is observed, treat any subsequent redeploy at the same address as a candidate T6.001 vector.

## Real-world examples

- **Cohort scale — multi-chain — 2020 onward** — T6.001 is most visible at the cohort level rather than at the per-incident level: many memecoin and rug-pull tokens combine T1.001 / T1.002 with T6.001 to evade pre-trade static analysis. Industry retrospectives (`[chainalysis2025rug]`, `[slowmist2024report]`) cover the cohort-scale impact without naming individual T6.001 cases as canonical, because the underlying primary Technique (T1, T5, etc.) is typically the more publicly-reportable framing.
- **Per-incident named cases** — OAK does not at v0.1 name a single canonical T6.001 incident. Contributors with public forensic write-ups of specific named incidents whose primary failure mode is T6.001 (rather than a primary T1 / T9 incident with T6.001 as modifier) are encouraged to submit them via PR.

### Adjacent — Proxy-Upgrade Malicious Switching (T6.005 proposed)

- [`examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md`](../examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md) — Hyperbridge MMR-proof-forgery + counterfeit-DOT mint, 2026-04. Adjacent to T6.001: the bridge contract's verified bytecode matched its deployment, but the on-chain admin authority was reassigned via forged cross-chain message — a structurally distinct evasion at the *upgrade-authority* layer rather than the *source-bytecode* layer. Anchors the T6.005 (Proxy-Upgrade Malicious Switching) sub-Technique proposal in `TAXONOMY-GAPS.md`. The case also opens a T6.006 (Counterfeit Token Impersonation) surface via the bridge-internal-mint sub-class.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T6.001 detection is in scope for MG's static-analysis pipeline; calibration in progress).
- Vendor coverage: block-explorer "verified bytecode" checks (Etherscan, Sourcify); pre-deployment audit firms (Halborn, Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin); deployed-contract monitoring (Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender) for proxy-upgrade events tied to source-claim mismatch.

## Mitigations

- **Risk teams / static-analysis tooling:** treat `Etherscan-verified` claims as a starting point, not a conclusion; deterministic-recompile against the claimed source as a confirming step before downstream Technique-level static analysis.
- **Block explorers (highest-leverage):** continue to mature partial-match-vs-full-match labelling; surface the distinction prominently in the UI.
- **Standardisation:** Sourcify-style cryptographic source-verification (where the on-chain bytecode is verified against an off-chain source artefact via metadata hash matching) is a stronger signal than block-explorer-side verification and should be preferred where available.

## Citations

- See `[chainalysis2025rug]` for cohort-scale context on rug-pull retrospectives where T6.001 is a recurring modifier; the underlying primary citations live with the corresponding T1 / T5 Techniques.

## Discussion

T6.001 is OAK's first standalone T6 Technique. The choice to start with source-verification mismatch reflects three considerations: the failure mode is generic across Tactic classes; the detection signal is unambiguous (deterministic recompile); and the underlying tooling for detection is mature enough that contributors can implement detection without extensive new infrastructure. Future T6 Techniques in v0.x updates may include "audit-claim mismatch" (claimed audit by a firm that did not audit), "renouncement-but-not-really" (proxy-admin retains control after marketing-claimed renouncement), and "audit-pending marketing-claim" (token marketed as audit-pending where no audit is in progress) — each reflects a generic evasion pattern that modifies primary Techniques across multiple Tactics.
docs.techniques/T6.002-fake-audit-claim.md:
# OAK-T6.002 — Fake Audit-Claim

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion); modifier on T1 (Token Genesis), T2 (Liquidity Establishment), T3 (Holder Capture)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** chain-agnostic (the failure mode lives off-chain; the underlying Technique it modifies may be on any chain)
**First documented:** widespread from approximately 2021 onward
**Aliases:** "audit fraud", "fake CertiK audit", "audit-affiliation impersonation"

## Description

A class of OAK-T6 evasion patterns in which a token, protocol, or service falsely claims security-audit coverage by a recognised audit firm (CertiK, Halborn, Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, etc.) — either by fabricating an audit certificate that the firm did not issue, or by claiming audit affiliation with a firm that has not engaged with the project. The attack surface is the user's reasonable trust that an audit-claim implies professional security review; T6.002 defeats this trust by exploiting the lack of reliable verification infrastructure for audit claims at the user-decision layer.

T6.002 frequently combines with T1.001 / T1.003 / T1.004 (T1 underlying Techniques whose detection static analysis would otherwise surface) by lending the underlying scam contract a defensive-baseline-claim that delays user-side scepticism. The detection signal lives at the audit-firm-side verification layer: the firm itself can confirm or deny the audit claim; the user's burden is to perform that verification before treating the claim as load-bearing.

## Observed indicators

- A project's marketing or smart-contract front-end claims audit by a recognised firm without linking to a verifiable per-firm audit registry entry.
- The named firm publishes a public denial or non-affiliation statement regarding the project (e.g., CertiK's "not conducting an audit and are not affiliated with this project" pattern).
- Discrepancy between the project's claimed audit date / scope and any verifiable artefact at the firm's published audit registry.

## Detection signals

- Per-firm registry verification (highest-leverage): each major audit firm maintains a public registry of audited projects (CertiK Skynet, Halborn published audits, OpenZeppelin Defender audit-trail, Trail of Bits public-audits archive). User-side verification consists of looking up the project in the firm's registry and confirming the audit's existence and scope.
- Per-incident: monitor audit-firm public denial statements; treat any project that has been publicly disclaimed by a named audit firm as elevated risk for any underlying T1 / T2 / T3 Technique it might combine.
- Cross-cohort: per-project audit-claim cluster analysis — projects making audit claims that resolve via the same fabricated-certificate template are typically related at the operator level.

## Real-world examples
