- **ATMA case — 2024** — A project claimed it was undergoing CertiK audit; CertiK publicly clarified "not conducting an audit and are not affiliated with this project in any way." Following the public callout, the project deleted its social media presence. Source: `[certikfakeaudit]`.
- **Lymex exit scam — 2024** — Loss \~\$300K. The project falsely claimed CertiK audit coverage; per CertiK's public statement, no audit services were rendered (failed KYC verification at the engagement-attempt stage). The project subsequently exited. Source: `[certikfakeaudit]`.
- **Cohort context** — `[certikfakeaudit]` documents the broader pattern of CertiK-brand misuse including phishing sites with counterfeit security-audit certifications and direct impersonation of audit-firm employees on Telegram.
- **Adjacent — Swaprum, 2023** — \~\$3M rug pull on a CertiK-audited project. This is *not* a T6.002 case (the audit was real); it illustrates that real audit coverage does not bound post-launch operator behaviour. Cited as a defensive-baseline reference. Source: `[dlnewsswaprum2023]`.

### Adjacent T6 evasion classes (proposed sub-Techniques in v0.x)

- T6.005 (Proxy-Upgrade Malicious Switching, proposed) and T6.006 (Counterfeit Token Impersonation, proposed) cover defense-evasion failure modes that are structurally distinct from the audit-trust-failure triangle (T6.002 / T6.003 / T6.004). The canonical worked example for both proposed Techniques is [`examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md`](../examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md) — the Hyperbridge MMR-proof-forgery case demonstrates how upgrade-authority can be reached on-chain (not via stolen keys) and used to mint counterfeit tokens via legitimate bridge contract identity. See `TAXONOMY-GAPS.md` for the proposal scope.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T6.002 detection lives at the off-chain audit-registry verification layer; not in MG's on-chain scope).
- Vendor coverage: audit firms themselves (CertiK Skynet, Halborn published-audit registry, etc.); third-party audit-claim verification tooling is sparse at v0.1.

## Mitigations

- **Audit firms (highest-leverage):** maintain machine-readable public registries of audited projects, with cryptographically-verifiable certificates where possible; publish public denial statements promptly when brand misuse is detected; integrate registry-verification APIs that wallet vendors and risk teams can integrate.
- **Wallet vendors / risk teams:** integrate audit-firm registry-verification APIs at the user-warning layer; do not surface "audited" labels based on project-self-claim alone.
- **Standardisation:** machine-readable audit-attestation formats (similar to Sourcify-style cryptographic source-verification) would meaningfully reduce T6.002 attack surface.

## Citations

- `[certikfakeaudit]` — primary CertiK advisory on brand misuse (ATMA, Lymex cases).
- `[dlnewsswaprum2023]` — adjacent case (real audit, post-audit rug); illustrates the limits of audit-coverage-of-record.

## Discussion

T6.002 is OAK's first audit-related T6 Technique. The choice to scope it tightly around *fake* audit claims (rather than including weak / shallow / partial audits) reflects the cleanly-binary verification surface: the named firm can confirm or deny the audit claim, and that confirmation/denial is operationally actionable. Adjacent failure modes — real audit + post-audit operator action (Swaprum), shallow audit, audit of a contract version different from the deployed version — are documented as adjacent observations but are not classified as T6.002 because the failure mode and mitigation differ.

A live discussion in the audit-firm community concerns whether audit firms should bear more contractual or regulatory obligation to maintain machine-readable public registries; OAK does not opine but documents that the registry-verification gap is the primary defensive surface that T6.002 exploits.
docs.techniques/T6.003-audit-of-different-byt:
# OAK-T6.003 — Audit-of-Different-Bytecode-Version

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion); modifier on T1 (Token Genesis), T2 (Liquidity Establishment), T3 (Holder Capture), T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit) classes when paired
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** chain-agnostic (the failure mode is the audit-vs-deployed gap; the underlying Technique it modifies may be on any chain)
**First documented:** widespread from approximately 2021 onward
**Aliases:** "audit-deployed mismatch", "post-audit redeploy", "audit-version drift", "audit of fork"

## Description

A class of OAK-T6 evasion patterns in which a token, protocol, or service truthfully claims audit coverage by a recognised firm — but the audited bytecode differs from what is actually deployed at the cited contract address. The audit certificate is real and verifiable at the firm's registry; the audited code is not what is running on chain. From a defender's perspective, T6.003 defeats the user's reasonable trust that "audited by Firm X" implies professional review of the code that will execute on their funds, while leaving the audit-firm-side verification surface (registry lookup) intact and apparently confirmatory.

T6.003 is distinct from OAK-T6.001 (Source-Verification Mismatch) in that the *block-explorer source verification may match the deployed bytecode* (deterministic recompile of the verified Etherscan source produces the deployed bytecode within compiler-deterministic noise) — yet the *audit firm's audit report* references a different version of the code. T6.001 lives at the explorer-source-vs-deployed-bytecode layer; T6.003 lives at the audit-report-vs-deployed-bytecode layer. The two are independent: a contract can pass T6.001 detection (verified source matches deployed bytecode) and still fail T6.003 detection (audited bytecode does not match deployed bytecode).

T6.003 is distinct from OAK-T6.002 (Fake Audit-Claim) in that the audit firm did engage with the project and produced a real audit — it is published in the firm's registry, and the firm will confirm rather than deny the engagement on inquiry. The failure mode is not fabricated affiliation; it is *scope drift* between what was audited and what is deployed. T6.002 mitigations (per-firm registry verification) succeed against T6.003 only insofar as the registry exposes the audited bytecode hash; if the registry exposes only project name, scope, and date, T6.003 evades T6.002-style checks.

## Observed indicators

- A project that cites a real, registry-listed audit report whose audited contract version (commit hash, file set, or audited-bytecode hash) cannot be matched against the currently-deployed bytecode at the cited address.
- A project whose audit report covers a single contract but whose deployed system is multi-contract, with the unaudited contracts holding upgrade authority, treasury authority, or routing authority over the audited contract.
- A proxy contract that cites a prior implementation's audit while currently delegating to an unaudited or modified implementation.
- A redeploy event (CREATE2 redeploy after `selfdestruct`, or fresh deployment to a new address marketed under the same project name) post-dating the cited audit, with no follow-up audit on the redeployed bytecode.
- A forked / customised version of an audited reference codebase (e.g., a forked Uniswap V2 / Compound V2 / OpenZeppelin contract) deployed under marketing language that conflates the upstream audit with coverage of the local fork.

## Detection signals

- **Canonical signal (highest-leverage):** compare the audit-firm-published audited-bytecode hash (or audited source-commit hash recompiled deterministically) against the deployed bytecode at the cited contract address. Non-match (beyond compiler-deterministic noise) is the canonical T6.003 signal. This signal requires the audit firm to publish the audited-bytecode hash in machine-readable form; where the firm publishes only a PDF report referencing a commit hash, the user must recompile from that commit and compare to deployed bytecode.
- **Proxy-implementation review:** where the cited contract is a proxy, resolve the current implementation address and compare it to the implementation address audited in the cited report; T6.003 frequently presents as a proxy whose current implementation is not the audited implementation.
- **Multi-contract scope review:** map the deployed system's contract graph (factory, router, treasury, governor, upgrader) and confirm that each authority-holding contract is in scope of the cited audit; T6.003 frequently presents as a single-contract audit cited as coverage for a multi-contract system.
- **Redeploy detection:** monitor for redeploy events (CREATE2 redeploy, fresh deployment) post-dating the cited audit at the project's claimed address-of-record; treat any redeploy without a corresponding follow-up audit as a candidate T6.003 vector.
- **Fork-conflation detection:** where the project cites the audit of an upstream reference codebase (e.g., Uniswap V2 core audit), require deterministic recompile of the deployed bytecode from the upstream audited commit — any local-fork modifications surface as bytecode differences and resolve the conflation.

## Real-world examples

- **Cohort scale — multi-chain — 2021 onward** — T6.003 is most visible at the cohort level rather than at the per-incident level, because the underlying primary Technique (T1, T5, T9) is typically the more publicly-reportable framing once funds are lost. Industry retrospectives (`[chainalysis2025rug]`) cover the cohort-scale impact of audit-coverage-of-record claims that did not bound the deployed system; the audit-vs-deployed gap is a recurring modifier across rug-pull and exploit cohorts but is rarely the primary headline.
- **Adjacent — Swaprum, 2023** — \~\$3M rug pull on a CertiK-audited project. The audited contract was the deployed contract; the operator team performed a post-launch treasury-management exit (T5.005). This is *not* a T6.003 case — the audited bytecode and deployed bytecode matched — but it is cited as adjacent reference because public discourse around the case frequently conflates "audited contract was rugged" with "audit did not cover the deployed code", which is the T6.003 framing. Source: `[dlnewsswaprum2023]`.
- **Adjacent — fake-audit cohort, 2024** — `[certikfakeaudit]` documents the T6.002 (Fake Audit-Claim) cohort. T6.003 is the cleanly-distinct sibling failure mode: in T6.002 the audit does not exist; in T6.003 the audit exists but covers different bytecode.
- **Per-incident named cases** — OAK does not at v0.1 name a single canonical T6.003 incident with public forensic disclosure of the audit-vs-deployed bytecode gap. Audit firms have published advisories on the pattern at the class level (as part of brand-misuse and audit-coverage-scope discussions); per-incident write-ups that specifically document a bytecode-hash mismatch between audit report and deployed contract are encouraged via PR.

### Adjacent T6 evasion classes (proposed sub-Techniques in v0.x)

- T6.005 (Proxy-Upgrade Malicious Switching, proposed) is a closely-related failure mode at the *upgrade-authority* layer rather than the *audit-bytecode-attestation* layer: the audit may correctly cover the deployed bytecode at audit time, but the proxy is later upgraded to an unaudited (or malicious) implementation. The canonical worked example is [`examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md`](../examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md). See `TAXONOMY-GAPS.md` for the proposal scope.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T6.003 detection requires both a machine-readable audit-attestation source and a deployed-bytecode comparator; the audit-attestation side of the pipeline is the binding constraint).
- Vendor coverage: bytecode-comparison tooling at audit firms is **emerging** rather than mature at v0.1. Some firms publish audited-commit hashes in their PDF reports; few publish audited-bytecode hashes in machine-readable form. Sourcify-style cryptographic source-verification provides a partial substitute where the audited source is the same as the verified source, but does not generalise to the audit-attestation case.
- Adjacent: block-explorer "verified bytecode" infrastructure (Etherscan, Sourcify) provides the deployed-bytecode-hash side of the comparison; the audit-firm side is the gap.

## Mitigations

- **Audit firms (highest-leverage):** publish the audited-bytecode hash (or, where the audit is source-level, the deterministic-recompile bytecode hash from the audited source commit) in machine-readable form in the audit registry, alongside the project name, scope, and date. This converts T6.003 detection from a manual recompile-and-compare exercise into a registry-API lookup that wallet vendors and risk teams can integrate.
- **Standardisation (canonical solution):** machine-readable cryptographic audit attestations binding the audited bytecode hash to the audit certificate — analogous to Sourcify-style cryptographic source-verification but at the audit-attestation layer. An attestation format that wallets, explorers, and risk-team tooling can verify against the deployed bytecode would reduce T6.003 to a deterministic check rather than a manual review.
- **Wallet vendors / risk teams:** treat "audited by Firm X" project-self-claims as a starting point, not a conclusion; where the cited audit is registry-listed, perform the audited-bytecode-vs-deployed-bytecode comparison before surfacing an "audited" label at the user-warning layer; for proxy contracts, surface the audit-vs-current-implementation gap explicitly.
- **Block explorers:** where the verified-source claim and a cited audit reference different commits, surface the discrepancy in the contract page UI; the explorer is uniquely positioned to flag the audit-vs-source drift even before the audit-vs-bytecode comparison is automated.
- **Project teams:** publish the audited-commit hash and the deployed-bytecode hash side-by-side at the audit-claim surface (project documentation, marketing site, contract page); for multi-contract systems, publish the audited-scope graph (which contracts are in scope, which are not) explicitly rather than letting the user infer scope from the project name.

## Citations

- `[certikfakeaudit]` — adjacent, fake-audit class; cited to anchor the T6.003-vs-T6.002 distinction.
- `[dlnewsswaprum2023]` — adjacent, real-audit-then-rug; cited to anchor the T6.003-vs-T5.005 distinction (audit-vs-deployed gap is not the same as post-audit operator action on audited code).
- `[chainalysis2025rug]` — cohort-scale context for audit-coverage-of-record claims as a recurring modifier in rug-pull retrospectives.

## Discussion

T6.003 closes the third corner of the OAK-T6 audit-trust failure-mode triangle: T6.001 (verified source does not match deployed bytecode), T6.002 (claimed audit does not exist), T6.003 (audit exists but covers different bytecode). The three Techniques together describe the failure modes of the user-facing assertion "this contract has been audited" and bound the corresponding mitigation surfaces: explorer-side bytecode verification (T6.001), audit-firm-side registry verification (T6.002), and audit-firm-side bytecode attestation (T6.003).

The choice to scope T6.003 around *bytecode-version drift* (rather than including audit-quality, audit-scope-narrowness, or audit-recency concerns) reflects the same cleanly-binary-verification standard applied to T6.001 and T6.002: the audited-bytecode-hash-vs-deployed-bytecode-hash comparison is deterministic and operationally actionable, given the audit-attestation source. Adjacent failure modes — shallow audits, narrow-scope audits where the unaudited adjacent contracts are the failure surface but were never claimed in scope, audits that have aged past meaningful relevance — are documented as adjacent observations but are not classified as T6.003 because the failure mode is qualitative rather than binary and the mitigation is editorial rather than mechanical.

A live discussion in the audit-firm community concerns whether audit firms should standardise on publishing audited-bytecode hashes in machine-readable form as a default deliverable. OAK does not opine but documents that the audit-attestation gap — the absence of machine-readable bytecode-bound audit certificates — is the primary defensive surface that T6.003 exploits, and that closing this gap is the canonical mitigation. Until that infrastructure matures, T6.003 detection at scale remains a per-project manual exercise, and audit-claim assertions at the user-decision layer should be treated as load-bearing only after the audited-vs-deployed bytecode comparison has been performed.
docs.techniques/T6.004-audit-pending-marketin:
# OAK-T6.004 — Audit-Pending Marketing Claim

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion); modifier on T1 (Token Genesis), T2 (Liquidity Establishment), T3 (Holder Capture)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** chain-agnostic (the failure mode lives off-chain at the marketing-claim layer; the underlying Technique it modifies may be on any chain)
**First documented:** widespread from approximately 2021 onward
**Aliases:** "audit pending", "audit in progress", "audit forthcoming", "working with [firm]", "audit Q[X]"

## Description

A class of OAK-T6 evasion patterns in which a project markets itself as having an audit *in progress*, *forthcoming*, *pending*, or *being arranged with a named audit firm* — without an audit engagement actually being in progress, contracted, or planned at the named firm. The attack surface is the user's reasonable inference that "audit pending" implies an active or imminent professional security review; T6.004 defeats this inference by exploiting the asymmetry between forward-looking claims (which have no canonical verification surface) and backward-looking claims (which can be checked against the audit firm's published registry).

T6.004 is distinct from OAK-T6.002 (Fake Audit-Claim) along the temporal dimension. T6.002 falsely claims a *completed* audit and is defeated by per-firm registry verification: the audit either exists in the registry or it does not. T6.004 claims an audit *in progress* or *forthcoming* — a claim about future state, against which a registry-of-completed-audits lookup is structurally inconclusive (the audit not being in the registry yet is consistent with both an honest pending engagement and a fabricated one). The defender's analytical question shifts from "is this audit in the registry?" to "is the cited engagement actually in progress at the named firm?", and the verification surface shifts from the firm's audit-completed registry to the firm's engagement-tracking layer — which most firms do not expose publicly at v0.1.

T6.004 is distinct from OAK-T6.003 (Audit-of-Different-Bytecode-Version) in that T6.003 has a real, registry-listed audit whose audited bytecode does not match the deployed bytecode; T6.004 has no audit at all — only a forward-looking claim. T6.003's failure mode is scope drift between audited and deployed code; T6.004's failure mode is fabricated or never-materialising future engagement.

T6.004 is also distinct from "soft" project-side audit-language failures in which a project did engage a firm but missed its self-promised timeline — those cases have a real engagement contract or quote behind the marketing claim, even if the claim's timeline anchoring is unrealistic. T6.004 specifically covers cases where no engagement, no contract, and no quote exist behind the "pending" framing.

Sub-patterns observed in the wild:

- (a) Claim of pending audit that never materialises; project rugs / fails / disappears before any audit lands, with the "audit pending" claim having served only as a launch-window defensive-baseline assertion.
- (b) "We're working with [firm]" framing with no engagement contract, no quote, and no acknowledgement from the named firm — frequently combined with logo-borrowing on the project's marketing site.
- (c) Audit timeline pushed indefinitely as the project progresses, with user expectations anchored to a never-arriving artefact ("audit Q3" → "audit Q4" → "audit early next year" → silence).

## Observed indicators

- A project's marketing or smart-contract front-end claims an audit is "in progress", "pending", "forthcoming", "scheduled", or "being arranged" with a named audit firm — without linking to an engagement-tracking entry, public quote, or firm-side acknowledgement.
- A project displays an audit-firm logo or brand mark adjacent to "coming soon", "Q[X]", or similar language with no firm-side artefact backing the affiliation.
- The named firm publishes a public denial or non-engagement statement regarding the project (analogous to T6.002's denial pattern but applied to forward-looking rather than backward-looking claims).
- The project's stated audit timeline slips repeatedly across launch, liquidity establishment, and holder-capture phases with no public engagement artefact ever surfacing.
- The "audit pending" claim is removed from project marketing material around the same time the project rugs or stalls, leaving no archival trace of the original engagement assertion.

## Detection signals

- **Per-firm engagement-verification (highest-leverage):** for any "audit pending with [firm]" claim, query the named firm directly (or via its engagement-tracking surface, where one is published) to confirm that an engagement is in progress. Most major firms will respond to direct inquiries about whether they have engaged with a named project; absence of confirmation is the canonical T6.004 signal.
- **Per-firm registry-absence consistency check:** confirm the project does not appear in the firm's completed-audit registry. This is necessary but not sufficient — registry-absence is consistent with both an honest pending engagement and a fabricated one — but it bounds the question and rules out misclassification as T6.002 (claim-of-completed-audit) versus T6.004 (claim-of-pending-audit).
- **Per-incident:** monitor audit-firm public denial statements specifically about *engagement claims* (as distinct from completed-audit claims). Treat any project that has been publicly disclaimed by a named firm at the engagement layer as elevated risk for any underlying T1 / T2 / T3 Technique it might combine.
- **Timeline-slip analysis:** track project marketing claims over time; repeated audit-timeline slips with no public engagement artefact between slips is a strong T6.004 indicator. The pattern is most visible in archival snapshots (Wayback Machine, Discord logs) rather than at any single point in time.
- **Cross-cohort:** projects making "audit pending" claims that share fabricated-engagement-template language, identical firm-logo placements, or identical timeline-slip cadences are typically related at the operator level. Cohort-level pattern matching surfaces the class even where per-incident verification is lossy.

## Real-world examples

- **Cohort scale — multi-chain — 2021 onward** — T6.004 is most visible at the cohort level rather than the per-incident level, because "audit-pending" promises are typically made in marketing material (project websites, Discord pinned messages, Twitter threads) that disappears with the project. Industry retrospectives (`[chainalysis2025rug]`, `[slowmist2024report]`) document the pattern at cohort scale: forward-looking audit claims are a recurring component of commodity-scam-token launch playbooks, paired with T1 / T2 / T3 underlying Techniques to delay user-side scepticism through the holder-capture phase.
- **Audit-firm denial cohort — 2024** — `[certikfakeaudit]` documents the broader pattern of CertiK-brand misuse. While the primary cases cited (ATMA, Lymex) are T6.002 (completed-audit claims), the same cohort includes engagement-claim impersonation: projects framing themselves as "currently undergoing CertiK audit" or "in discussions with CertiK" without an engagement actually being in progress. CertiK's public-denial pattern ("not conducting an audit and are not affiliated with this project in any way") explicitly covers both backward-looking and forward-looking framings.
- **Per-incident named cases** — OAK does not at v0.1 name a single canonical T6.004 incident with public forensic disclosure of the engagement-claim-vs-engagement-record gap. The class is documented at industry-retrospective cohort scale; per-incident write-ups that specifically document a fabricated "audit pending" claim (with archival evidence of the original marketing material and firm-side denial of engagement) are encouraged via PR.

### Adjacent T6 evasion classes (proposed sub-Techniques in v0.x)

- T6.005 (Proxy-Upgrade Malicious Switching, proposed) and T6.006 (Counterfeit Token Impersonation, proposed) cover defense-evasion failure modes that are structurally distinct from the audit-trust-failure triangle (T6.002 / T6.003 / T6.004). The canonical worked example for both proposed Techniques is [`examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md`](../examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md). See `TAXONOMY-GAPS.md` for the proposal scope.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T6.004 detection lives at the off-chain engagement-verification layer; not in MG's on-chain scope).
- Vendor coverage: audit-firm engagement-tracking transparency is **emerging** rather than mature at v0.1. Most major firms publish completed-audit registries (the T6.002 verification surface) but do not publish in-progress-engagement registries (the T6.004 verification surface). Firms respond to direct inquiries about engagements but the response is not machine-readable and does not scale to cohort-level monitoring.
- Adjacent: project-marketing-claim archival infrastructure (Wayback Machine, Discord-log archives) provides the timeline-slip side of the detection signal; the firm-side engagement-acknowledgement surface is the gap.

## Mitigations

- **Audit firms (highest-leverage):** publish a machine-readable engagement-tracking surface — projects currently under engagement, with engagement-start date and expected-completion window — alongside the existing completed-audit registry. This converts T6.004 verification from a manual direct-inquiry exercise into a registry-API lookup that wallet vendors and risk teams can integrate. Where commercial confidentiality concerns prevent public engagement disclosure, an opt-in attestation API (firm confirms "yes, this project is under engagement" against a queried project identifier) provides a privacy-preserving alternative.
- **Standardisation:** machine-readable cryptographic engagement attestations binding the engagement to the firm — analogous to the audit-attestation format proposed for T6.003 but at the engagement layer rather than the completed-audit layer. An attestation a project can display on its marketing surface, that wallets and explorers can verify against the firm's public key, would reduce T6.004 to a deterministic signature check.
- **Marketplaces / launchpads / DEX aggregators:** require firm-side engagement attestation before surfacing any "audit-pending" label in the listing UI; treat unverified pending-audit claims as project-self-claim with no defensive weight, and surface this distinction explicitly to the user.
- **Wallet vendors / risk teams:** treat "audit pending" project-self-claims as a marketing assertion, not a defensive baseline; do not surface "audit-pending" labels at the user-warning layer based on project-self-claim alone; where a firm-side engagement attestation is available, surface it; where it is not, surface the absence explicitly rather than echoing the project's framing.
- **Project teams:** publish the engagement contract reference, firm-side quote artefact, or engagement-acknowledgement letter alongside any "audit pending" marketing claim; for engagements that have not yet been finalised, frame the claim as "exploring audit with [firm]" rather than "audit pending with [firm]" to preserve the distinction between active engagement and prospective engagement.
- **User-side:** treat any "audit pending" or "audit forthcoming" claim as carrying zero defensive weight until firm-side engagement confirmation is obtained; the asymmetry between fabrication-cost (a marketing-line assertion) and verification-cost (a direct firm inquiry) makes user-side scepticism the only viable defence at v0.1 absent engagement-tracking infrastructure.

## Citations

- `[certikfakeaudit]` — primary CertiK advisory on brand misuse; covers both T6.002 (completed-audit) and T6.004 (engagement-claim) framings within the same denial pattern.
- `[chainalysis2025rug]` — cohort-scale context for audit-related marketing claims as recurring components of rug-pull launch playbooks.
- `[slowmist2024report]` — cohort-scale context for forward-looking audit claims in commodity-scam-token deployment patterns.

## Discussion

T6.004 closes the fourth corner of the OAK-T6 audit-trust failure-mode space, completing the temporal axis of audit-claim evasion: T6.002 covers fabricated *backward-looking* claims (audit said to have happened, did not), T6.003 covers technically-true backward-looking claims that fail at the bytecode-scope layer (audit happened, but not on this code), and T6.004 covers fabricated *forward-looking* claims (audit said to be in progress, is not). The three audit-related Techniques together describe the failure modes of the user-facing assertion "this project has audit coverage" across both temporal directions and bound the corresponding mitigation surfaces: completed-audit registry verification (T6.002), audit-firm-side bytecode attestation (T6.003), and engagement-tracking transparency (T6.004).

The choice to scope T6.004 around *engagement-claim fabrication* (rather than including over-promised audit timelines on real engagements) reflects the same cleanly-binary-verification standard applied across T6.001–T6.003: the question "is there an engagement contract or firm-side acknowledgement behind this claim?" is determinate given the firm-side artefact. Adjacent failure modes — real engagement with slipped timeline, real engagement that the project later cancelled before audit completion, real engagement on a contract version that will not be the deployed contract — are documented as adjacent observations but are not classified as T6.004 because the failure mode is qualitative rather than binary and the mitigation is editorial rather than mechanical.

The forward-looking-vs-backward-looking asymmetry is the structural reason T6.004 is harder to defend against than T6.002. A backward-looking claim has a canonical verification surface (the completed-audit registry); a forward-looking claim has no such surface at v0.1 because audit firms generally do not publish in-progress-engagement registries. The asymmetry is not a property of the underlying engagements — both are facts the firm knows — but a property of the firm's published surfaces. Closing the asymmetry is a coordination problem at the audit-firm-industry layer rather than a technical problem at the protocol layer.

A live discussion in the audit-firm community concerns whether engagement-tracking transparency should be a default deliverable, balanced against commercial-confidentiality concerns of clients in early-engagement phases. OAK does not opine but documents that the engagement-tracking gap — the absence of a machine-readable in-progress-engagement registry — is the primary defensive surface that T6.004 exploits, and that closing this gap (whether via opt-in attestation API, public engagement registry, or cryptographic engagement-attestation standard) is the canonical mitigation. Until that infrastructure matures, T6.004 detection at scale remains a per-project manual exercise, and forward-looking audit claims at the user-decision layer should be treated as carrying zero defensive weight.
docs.techniques/T6.005-proxy-upgrade-maliciou:
# OAK-T6.005 — Proxy-Upgrade Malicious Switching

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion); modifier on OAK-T1 (Token Genesis), OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit), OAK-T10 (Bridge / Cross-Chain) where the upgrade itself is the attack vector
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (primary); cross-chain bridge variants (Polkadot ⇄ EVM canonical at v0.1)
**First documented:** structural class anchored from approximately 2022; canonical contract-layer-message-forgery 2026-04 (Hyperbridge)
**Aliases:** "verified-then-malicious upgrade", "proxy implementation switching", "upgrade-as-attack-vector", "post-verification implementation substitution"

## Description

Contracts verified at deployment with a benign implementation, then proxy-upgraded to a malicious implementation post-verification. The structural feature is that the *upgrade itself* is the attack vector — the protocol's standing surface (audit, source verification, defender-tool baseline) holds, but the implementation logic is substituted for attacker-controlled bytecode after the trust-substrate claims have been established.

T6.005 has **two structurally-distinct sub-classes**, each with its own detection-signal locus and mitigation surface:

- **(a) Operator-key-compromise sub-class** — the upgrade authority is reached via stolen / compromised operator keys. The on-chain extraction primitive is the upgrade; the proximate cause is off-chain key compromise. Detection-signal locus: off-chain key-handling-monitoring layer. Canonical: Steadefi August 2023 (~\$1.14M, internal-key compromise); Audius July 2022 storage-collision (~\$6M; see [`examples/2022-07-audius.md`](../examples/2022-07-audius.md)). The Magnate Finance August 2023 deployer-cluster case is an adjacent operator-side anchor at [`examples/2023-08-magnate-finance-base-deployer-cluster.md`](../examples/2023-08-magnate-finance-base-deployer-cluster.md).
- **(b) On-chain-message-forgery sub-class** — the upgrade authority is reached via forged cryptographic proof or message-verification flaw without any pre-stolen keys. The entire attack chain lives on-chain. Detection-signal locus: on-chain message-verification correctness layer. Canonical: Hyperbridge April 2026 (~\$2.5M realised / ~\$1.2B notional, MMR-proof-reuse forgery); Wormhole February 2022 white-hat-disclosed uninitialised-implementation pattern; AllianceBlock August 2024 re-initialisation flaw.

T6.005 is **structurally distinct** from adjacent defense-evasion-class Techniques:

- **T6.001 (Source-Verification Mismatch)** — covers verified-but-different-bytecode at deployment. T6.005's distinguishing feature is that the verification was correct *at the moment of deployment* and the divergence is introduced via a subsequent legitimate-mechanism upgrade event.
- **T6.002 (Fake Audit Claim)** — false audit attestation. T6.005's audit may have been entirely legitimate; the upgrade post-audit is what introduces the malicious implementation.
- **T9.004 (Access Control Misconfiguration)** — covers access-control flaws as the standing root cause. T6.005 is the *upgrade-as-vector* specialisation where the upgrade-authority access path is the attack chain's load-bearing primitive.
- **T11.001 (Third-Party Signing-Vendor Compromise)** — adjacent for the operator-key-compromise sub-class where the upgrade-authority key is reached via a signing-vendor compromise; the cases chain when the vendor surface is the upstream entry-vector.

T6.005 is the canonical generic modifier on T1 / T9 / T10 classes when the upgrade itself is the attack vector. The downstream extraction primitive can be a token mint (T6.005 + T6.006 chain in the bridge-internal-mint sub-pattern), a vault drain (T6.005 + T9.004 chain), or a counterfeit-token deployment (T6.005 + T6.006 chain in the deployer-cluster-rug sub-pattern).

## Observed indicators

- **Pre-event:** legitimate audited deployment with a non-trivial upgrade-authority surface (multisig, governance contract, cross-chain message handler, single deployer EOA). Standing T6.005 surface from the moment of deployment.
- **At-event (operator-key sub-class):** off-chain key-handling anomaly (workstation breach, internal-IR escalation, anomalous authentication on the upgrade-authority signer set); on-chain `upgradeTo` / `upgradeAndCall` / equivalent event emitted from a non-governance pathway.
- **At-event (on-chain-forgery sub-class):** on-chain admin-authority change event from a non-deployer / non-governance EOA following a cross-chain message receipt or proof-verification call; mint events / state-mutation events without corresponding upstream-source events (e.g., bridge mint without deposit-correlation).
- **Pattern continuity:** T6.005 typically chains downstream into T6.006 (counterfeit-token impersonation via the post-upgrade authority) or into T5 outflow (vault drain following implementation switch).

## Detection signals

- **Off-chain (operator-key sub-class):** key-handling-monitoring at the upgrade-authority signer set; vendor-disclosure feeds for internal-IR escalations; behavioural anomaly detection on signer-workstation telemetry.
- **On-chain (both sub-classes):** alert on `upgradeTo` / `upgradeAndCall` / proxy-implementation-change events emitted from a contract whose standing pattern is upgrades only via governance proposal; alert on admin-authority-change events without corresponding governance event; alert on cross-chain-message-receipt admin-authority changes.
- **Bridge-monitoring layer:** mint events without deposit-correlation; admin-rotation events triggered by cross-chain messages whose proof binding is below current-best-practice strength.
- **Cross-chain-proof-verification correctness:** auditor-side verification that cryptographic proofs commit *uniquely* to the message they authorise — not just that the proof is cryptographically valid. The Hyperbridge MMR-proof-reuse flaw is the canonical 2026 case for this defender check.
- **Contract-monitoring tooling:** Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender, BlockSec PhalconHQ runtime detectors for proxy-implementation-change events; Etherscan / chain-explorer "implementation changed" alerts.

## Real-world examples

- **Hyperbridge — Polkadot ⇄ Ethereum / Base / BNB / Arbitrum — 2026-04-13 (canonical on-chain-message-forgery sub-class)** — Loss approximately \$2.5M realised; \~\$1.2B notional in counterfeit bridged-DOT plus comparable counterfeit mints across ARGN / MANTA / CERE. The attacker exploited an MMR-proof-verification flaw that allowed an outdated valid proof from an earlier legitimate transaction to be accepted as authorisation for a different message. By pairing the proof with a forged admin-rotation message, the attacker re-assigned admin authority on the bridged-DOT token contract and used the acquired authority to mint counterfeit tokens without depositing backing collateral. The case is the canonical contract-layer-message-forgery T6.005 worked example — no operator keys were stolen, no pre-deployment social engineering occurred, the bridge contract was the legitimate audited deployment up to the moment the forged proof was accepted. See [`examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md`](../examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md).
- **Magnate Finance / Base deployer-cluster — Base — 2023-08** — Deployer-cluster pattern adjacent to the operator-key-compromise sub-class; the deployer's pre-existing pattern of deploy-then-rug informed the post-deployment upgrade trajectory. See [`examples/2023-08-magnate-finance-base-deployer-cluster.md`](../examples/2023-08-magnate-finance-base-deployer-cluster.md).
- **Audius — Ethereum — 2022-07** — Storage-collision in the proxy-implementation pattern; the upgrade-pathway flaw produced effective implementation-substitution capability. EVM-internal analogue of the Hyperbridge bridge-internal flaw. See [`examples/2022-07-audius.md`](../examples/2022-07-audius.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T6.005 is a runtime-monitoring + audit-control surface; the bridge-monitoring sub-pattern (admin-authority-change without governance event; mint without deposit-correlation) is closer to MG's runtime risk-engine scope than the audit / static-analysis sub-pattern. v0.x integration target.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: pre-deployment auditors (Halborn, Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, ConsenSys Diligence) for upgrade-pathway review; runtime monitoring (Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender, BlockSec PhalconHQ) for proxy-implementation-change events; bridge-side telemetry (varies by bridge; Hyperbridge did not have such monitoring at the time of the April 2026 incident).

## Mitigations

- **Pre-deployment auditors:** verify upgrade-authority pathway as a first-class review item; flag single-EOA upgrade authorities; flag absence of timelock between upgrade proposal and execution; verify cross-chain-message proof-binding strength (per-message commitment, not just proof validity).
- **Protocols:** apply timelock between upgrade proposal and execution to expose proposed implementation bytecode to a defender-review window; restrict upgrade authority to multisig / governance contract; for bridges, bind cryptographic proofs uniquely to the message they authorise via per-message commitments.
- **Risk teams:** maintain per-protocol inventories of (a) upgrade-authority signer set, (b) timelock duration if any, (c) cross-chain-message-driven admin-authority change pathways. Treat protocols whose admin-authority on destination-chain token contracts is gated by cross-chain-message verification as elevated T6.005 risk if the message-verification primitive admits replay, forgery, or signature-aliasing.
- **Bridge-side telemetry:** real-time monitoring of admin-authority-change events without governance correlation; mint events without deposit-correlation; cross-chain-message-receipt admin events.
- **Operator-side (operator-key sub-class):** key-handling-monitoring at the upgrade-authority signer set as a first-class operational-security control; cross-reference T11.001 mitigations for vendor-side build-pipeline integrity.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1: gap. v0.x candidates: "OAK-M-x upgrade-authority timelock-and-multisig discipline"; "OAK-M-x cross-chain-proof per-message commitment binding"; "OAK-M-x bridge admin-authority-change real-time monitoring".

## Citations

- See worked examples for primary citations. Hyperbridge: `[thedefianthyperbridge2026]`, `[cryptobriefinghyperbridge2026]`, `[cryptobriefinghyperbridgejump2026]`, `[theblockhyperbridge2026]`, `[dlnewshyperbridge2026]`, `[cointelegraphhyperbridge2026]`, `[ambcryptohyperbridge2026]`, `[coindeskhyperbridge2026]`, `[cmcacademyhyperbridge2026]`, `[polkadotforumhyperbridge2026]`, `[autheobridgesecurity2026]`. Audius: `[halbornaudius2022]`, `[peckshieldaudius2022]`, `[audiuspostmortem2022]`, `[theblockaudius2022]`. Magnate: see worked example for the deployer-cluster citation set.

## Discussion

T6.005 sits at an unusual intersection of OAK's Tactic structure: it is filed under T6 (Defense Evasion) because the structural feature is the *evasion of the audit / source-verification / standing-implementation trust-substrate*, but it is operationally a generic modifier on T1 / T9 / T10 attacks where the upgrade is the attack vector. Future contributors should preserve this dual framing — T6.005 is the parent for the upgrade-itself-as-attack-vector primitive, and the downstream extraction primitive determines whether the case chains into T1 (token genesis), T9 (smart-contract exploit), or T10 (bridge / cross-chain).

The two-sub-class split (operator-key vs. on-chain-forgery) is the analytically distinctive feature of the Technique. The detection-signal locus differs entirely between the sub-classes:

- **Operator-key sub-class** lives at the off-chain key-handling-monitoring layer. Mitigation surface is at the operator's signing-infrastructure security posture. Steadefi (August 2023) is the canonical compact reference; the case is structurally adjacent to T11.001-class signing-vendor compromise but with the customer-organisation-as-victim shape inverted to operator-as-target.
- **On-chain-forgery sub-class** lives at the on-chain message-verification correctness layer. Mitigation surface is at the cryptographic-proof-binding strength. Hyperbridge (April 2026) is the canonical clean reference because no off-chain operator key was compromised — the entire attack chain lives on-chain.

The case generalises beyond bridges. Any contract whose upgrade authority is gated by message verification (cross-chain bridge admin authority; oracle-driven upgrade pathway; governance-driven upgrade pathway with off-chain vote aggregation) is exposed to the on-chain-forgery sub-class if the verification primitive admits replay, forgery, or signature-aliasing. Wormhole's 2022 uninitialised-implementation bug is the EVM-internal analogue (different mechanism, same downstream); Audius 2022 is the storage-collision EVM-internal analogue.

The notional-vs-realised loss gap (Hyperbridge: \~\$1.2B notional vs. \~\$2.5M realised) reflects destination-pool-liquidity constraints when the downstream chain is T6.006 counterfeit-mint plus DEX cash-out. T6.005 contributors should record both notional and realised loss when the chain includes T6.006 because the ratio is a useful structural signal across the cohort. Forensic coverage that reports only the realised cash-out figure underestimates the *attempted* extraction scale by 1–3 orders of magnitude.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once the worked-example anchor set expands to multiple per-incident files for each sub-class. Currently the operator-key sub-class is anchored at Audius and Magnate (with Steadefi as the canonical pipeline target for v0.x) and the on-chain-forgery sub-class is anchored at Hyperbridge alone (with Wormhole 2022 and AllianceBlock 2024 as pipeline targets). The single-anchor-per-sub-class state is sufficient for `emerging` but warrants additional anchoring before `stable`.
docs.techniques/T6.006-counterfeit-token-impe:
# OAK-T6.006 — Counterfeit Token Impersonation

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion); chains downstream into OAK-T4 (Access Acquisition) for dust-attack lures and OAK-T5 (Value Extraction) for fake-LP-deposit drains
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (primary); cross-chain bridge variants (Polkadot ⇄ EVM canonical at v0.1)
**First documented:** dust-attack-lure / fake-LP cohort widespread from approximately 2022; canonical bridge-internal-mint sub-class 2026-04 (Hyperbridge)
**Aliases:** "fake-symbol-matching token", "bridge-token impersonation", "counterfeit-mint", "dust-attack lure", "fake LP-token"

## Description

Contracts that share the symbol (or other surface-identifier) of a legitimate token (USDC / USDT / WETH / WBTC / DOT) but are unrelated at the contract identity layer. Used in dust-attack lures, fake LP-token deposit drains, bridge-token impersonation on non-mainnet chains, and counterfeit-mint via compromised bridge admin authority.

T6.006 has **three structurally-distinct sub-classes**, ranked by severity and identity-layer-overlap with the legitimate token:

- **(a) Fake-symbol-matching deployment** — separate, unrelated contract sharing the symbol. Canonical for dust-attack lures (small-amount transfers of fake-token to a victim address with the goal of inducing the victim to investigate / interact) and fake LP-token deposit drains (a fake LP token of a popular pool is offered to vault / yield protocols that accept LP collateral by symbol). ScamSniffer 2023–2024 cohort coverage tracks the dust-attack lure variant at scale.
- **(b) Bridge-impersonation via fake-bridge-token deployment** — counterfeit deployment masquerading as a legitimate bridged token. The counterfeit is a separate contract from the legitimate bridge's token but is named / branded identically and is offered as the "bridged DOT" / "bridged BTC" / "bridged ETH" on a non-mainnet chain.
- **(c) Bridge-internal-mint** — the *legitimate* bridge contract mints counterfeit tokens via attacker-acquired admin authority, indistinguishable from legitimate at the contract identity layer. **Worst-case shape**; canonical Hyperbridge April 2026.

T6.006 is **structurally distinct** from adjacent Techniques:

- **T1.006 (Honeypot-by-Design)** — covers immutable-bytecode-at-deployment hostile-transfer-logic. T6.006's transfer-logic may be entirely benign; the attack is at the *identity* layer (symbol / branding) rather than the transfer-logic layer.
- **T6.005 (Proxy-Upgrade Malicious Switching)** — chains with T6.006 in the bridge-internal-mint sub-class where the upgrade-authority transfer is the mechanism by which the impersonation surface is opened.
- **T4.003 (Address Poisoning)** — adjacent attack class targeting transaction-history UX confusion. T6.006 targets symbol / branding confusion at the token identity layer.
- **T2.x rug-pull tokens** — token-side liquidity-establishment fraud where the *token itself* is the asset of fraud. T6.006's failure mode is impersonation of a *legitimate* asset, not the launch of a new fraudulent asset.

The notional-vs-realised loss gap is structurally informative across the cohort and should be recorded explicitly. Counterfeit mints scale infinitely on the supply side but are bounded on the cash-out side by destination-pool liquidity (in the bridge-internal-mint sub-class) or by victim-discovery latency (in the dust-attack-lure sub-class).

## Observed indicators

- **Sub-class (a) — fake-symbol-matching:** new token deployment with symbol matching a major asset (USDC / USDT / WETH / WBTC) but contract address divergent from the canonical deployment; small-amount inbound transfers to victim addresses; presence in fake LP-pool deployments offered to yield / vault protocols.
- **Sub-class (b) — fake-bridge-token:** branding consistent with a legitimate bridge (Wormhole / Hyperbridge / LayerZero / Synapse) but contract address not in the legitimate bridge's official deployment registry; offered for trading / bridging on chain-explorer surfaces but with no upstream reservation in the legitimate bridge's source-chain.
- **Sub-class (c) — bridge-internal-mint:** legitimate bridge contract emits mint events without corresponding deposit-correlation events on the source chain; admin-authority change events on the destination-chain token contract from a non-deployer / non-governance EOA. Chains with T6.005 — the upgrade itself is the precondition for the counterfeit-mint.
- **Pattern continuity:** T6.006 typically chains downstream into T5 outflow (fake-LP-token deposit drain; bridge-internal-mint cash-out via DEX into ETH / stablecoins) or T4 (dust-attack lure as prelude to wallet-drainer phishing).

## Detection signals

- **Pre-event (sub-classes a + b):** wallet / aggregator / venue-side token-list curation; refusal to honour symbol-only token resolution where contract-address verification against canonical registries fails; SAFE-list / known-good-token registry for asset-matching by contract address rather than symbol.
- **At-event (sub-class c):** real-time bridge monitoring for mint-without-deposit-correlation; bridge admin-authority-change events without governance correlation. Same detection-signal locus as T6.005's on-chain-forgery sub-class because the cases chain.
- **Cohort-level:** ScamSniffer / Etherscan / BscScan token-list curation; chain-analytics provider tagged-token registries; centralised-venue listing review.
- **Forensic:** post-event cluster-attribution of dust-attack-lure deployments to commodity drainer-kit infrastructure (Inferno / Angel / AngelFerno; MS Drainer; PhishLab) — the deployment-substrate is shared with broader T4 phishing-kit infrastructure.

## Real-world examples

- **Hyperbridge — Polkadot ⇄ Ethereum / Base / BNB / Arbitrum — 2026-04-13 (canonical bridge-internal-mint sub-class)** — Loss ~\$2.5M realised / ~\$1.2B notional. The counterfeit bridged-DOT was minted by *the legitimate bridge contract* via attacker-acquired admin authority, making it indistinguishable from legitimate bridged-DOT at the contract identity layer. Uniswap pools quoted the counterfeit identically to legitimate bridged-DOT — the impersonation succeeded at the protocol-identity layer, not just the symbol layer. Worst-case shape of T6.006, chained with T6.005's on-chain-forgery sub-class. See [`examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md`](../examples/2026-04-hyperbridge-merkle-proof-counterfeit-mint.md).

### Adjacent / forthcoming worked-example anchors (TAXONOMY-GAPS-tracked)

The fake-symbol-matching (sub-class a) and fake-bridge-token (sub-class b) sub-patterns are tracked in TAXONOMY-GAPS as v0.x worked-example pipeline targets. ScamSniffer 2023–2024 cohort coverage is the cohort-level anchor pending per-incident write-ups.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. The sub-class (c) bridge-internal-mint detection (mint-without-deposit-correlation) is closest to MG's runtime risk-engine scope; sub-class (a) and (b) detection lives at the wallet / aggregator / venue token-list-curation layer outside MG's substrate. v0.x integration target.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: ScamSniffer (dust-attack-lure tagging); Etherscan / BscScan token-list curation; chain-analytics providers (Chainalysis, TRM, Elliptic) for cluster-attribution forensics; centralised-venue listing-review for sub-class (b).

## Mitigations

- **Wallet UX:** asset resolution by contract address rather than symbol; SAFE-list of known-good token contract addresses; explicit warning when a token symbol resolves to multiple contract deployments.
- **Aggregator / venue:** token-list curation against canonical registries; refusal to route through sub-class-(a) / sub-class-(b) deployments; centralised-venue listing-review for bridge-token claims.
- **Bridge designers:** per-message commitment binding for cryptographic proofs that authorise admin-authority changes; mint-cap-and-deposit-correlation enforcement at the destination-chain token contract; bridge-side telemetry for mint-without-deposit-correlation events.
- **Risk teams:** maintain per-asset inventories of (a) canonical contract addresses across chains, (b) legitimate bridge-token contract addresses, (c) admin-authority pathway for each bridge integration. Treat tokens whose symbol matches a major asset but whose contract address is not in the canonical registry as elevated risk.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1: gap. v0.x candidates: "OAK-M-x asset-resolution by contract address not symbol"; "OAK-M-x bridge mint-without-deposit-correlation real-time monitoring"; "OAK-M-x token-list curation against canonical registries".

## Citations

- See worked example for primary citations. Hyperbridge: `[thedefianthyperbridge2026]`, `[cryptobriefinghyperbridge2026]`, `[cryptobriefinghyperbridgejump2026]`, `[theblockhyperbridge2026]`, `[dlnewshyperbridge2026]`, `[cointelegraphhyperbridge2026]`, `[ambcryptohyperbridge2026]`, `[coindeskhyperbridge2026]`, `[cmcacademyhyperbridge2026]`, `[polkadotforumhyperbridge2026]`, `[autheobridgesecurity2026]`.
- Sub-class (a) cohort: ScamSniffer 2023–2024 reports; `[chainalysis2025rug]` cohort framing; centralised-venue listing-review advisory cohort.

## Discussion

T6.006 chains naturally with T6.005 in the bridge-internal-mint sub-class (sub-class c), where the upgrade-authority transfer is the mechanism by which the impersonation surface is opened. The Hyperbridge case is the canonical chained-Technique anchor for both — and is the worst-case shape because the counterfeit is minted by the legitimate bridge contract, making it indistinguishable from legitimate bridged-DOT at the contract identity layer. Uniswap pools quoting bridged-DOT priced the counterfeit identically; the impersonation succeeded at the protocol-identity layer, not just the symbol layer.

The three-sub-class split (fake-symbol-matching / fake-bridge-token / bridge-internal-mint) is the analytically distinctive feature of the Technique. Severity scales with identity-layer-overlap: sub-class (a) is detectable at the wallet / aggregator / venue token-list-curation layer; sub-class (b) is detectable at the centralised-venue listing-review layer; sub-class (c) is detectable only at the bridge-side telemetry layer because the contract identity *is* the legitimate one.

The notional-vs-realised loss gap is structurally informative. Counterfeit mints scale infinitely on the supply side; the operator can only convert as much to ETH / stablecoins as the destination pools can absorb without crashing the price. T6.006 contributors should record both notional and realised loss; the ratio reflects destination-pool-liquidity constraints (for sub-class c) or victim-discovery-latency constraints (for sub-classes a / b).

The sub-class (a) cohort is detectable at scale by commodity scanners (ScamSniffer, Etherscan token-list curation) — the failure mode is the wallet / aggregator UX layer rather than detection-signal availability. Cohort-level mitigation lives at the asset-resolution-by-contract-address-not-symbol invariant; flagged contracts are blocked at the token-list-curation layer.

The sub-class (c) cohort is the structurally novel surface at v0.1 and the dominant `emerging` framing for T6.006. The Hyperbridge case is the canonical 2026 anchor; pipeline targets for v0.x include any future cross-chain-proof-verification incident where the bridge mints counterfeit-but-protocol-identity-legitimate tokens. The detection surface is *bridge-side telemetry* (admin-authority-change events without governance correlation; mint events without deposit-correlation) rather than contract-layer rewrite.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once the worked-example anchor set expands to per-incident files for each sub-class. Currently sub-class (c) is anchored at Hyperbridge alone; sub-classes (a) and (b) are anchored only at the cohort level via ScamSniffer 2023–2024 coverage. Per-incident anchors for each sub-class are pipeline targets for v0.x.
docs.techniques/T6.007-trust-substrate-shift-:
# OAK-T6.007 — Trust-substrate Shift / Vendor-side Promise Revocation

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T6 (Defense Evasion)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** chain-agnostic (the substrate-of-revocation is the vendor / regulator / infrastructure-policy claim; the realised effect is on user-side threat-model construction across whichever chains the affected product operates)
**First documented:** 2023-05-16 (Ledger Recover seed-recovery service announcement collapsing the "seed never leaves the device" trust-substrate claim that had informed Ledger users' threat-model construction since 2016)
**Aliases:** "vendor-policy promise revocation", "trust-substrate revocation event", "non-attack defender-credibility event", "vendor-policy-as-defense-evasion"

## Description

A **non-attack defender-credibility event** in which a vendor-policy decision (or, in adjacent variants, a regulatory action, infrastructure-level policy change, or oracle-resolution-policy change) revokes a load-bearing trust-substrate claim that previously informed user-side threat-model construction. The realised loss is **non-financial** (trust-substrate revocation; cohort-level effect on the broader category's trust posture; competing-vendor user-acquisition opportunities) but structurally significant — the user's effective security posture depends on both (a) the absence of attacks and (b) the persistence of vendor-side trust-substrate claims.

T6.007 is **structurally distinct** from sibling Defense Evasion Techniques:

- **T6.001 (Source-Verification Mismatch)** — defense-evasion-by-attacker-action; the attacker constructs a verifiable-source surface that does not match deployed bytecode. T6.007 is defense-evasion-by-vendor-policy-change; no attacker is involved.
- **T6.002 (Fake-Audit Claim)** — attacker-action; false-audit-claim surface. T6.007's structural feature is *legitimate vendor* policy change that revokes a previously-load-bearing trust-substrate claim.
- **T6.003 (Audit of Different Bytecode Version)** — attacker-action; audit pertains to a different bytecode than what is deployed. T6.007 has no audit-mismatch dimension.
- **T6.004 (Audit-Pending Marketing Claim)** — attacker-action / operator-action; marketing claim of pending audit that does not materialise. T6.007's vendor announcement is *executed* — the policy change is real, not marketing.
- **T6.005 (Proxy-Upgrade Malicious Switching)** — attacker-action / operator-action; mutable-implementation surface used to switch to malicious bytecode. T6.007 is the *non-malicious* analogue: legitimate vendor-policy change that nonetheless revokes user trust.
- **T6.006 (Counterfeit Token Impersonation)** — attacker-action; counterfeit token branded as legitimate. T6.007's vendor is the legitimate vendor performing legitimate corporate action.

The structural feature is **defense-evasion-by-vendor-policy-change** rather than defense-evasion-by-attacker-action. "Evasion" is reframed at the framework level: not the attacker evading the defender's controls, but the **vendor policy change evading the defender's mental model of what the product does**. The framework's credibility depends on documenting how user trust gets revoked even in the absence of a deployed attack.

The class structurally generalises beyond hardware-wallet vendor-policy:

- **Vendor-policy decisions** that revoke trust-substrate claims (Ledger Recover at v0.1; potential future custodial-services policy changes; potential future wallet-as-a-service policy changes).
- **Regulatory / legal-process events** that change the operational threat model (sanctions enforcement against mixers; KYC / AML rule changes affecting custody surface).
- **Infrastructure-level changes** that reshape the defender's mental model (L2 sequencer policy changes; oracle-resolution-policy changes — adjacent to T9.006.004's platform-override sub-pattern at [`examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md`](../examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md) and adjacent Polymarket cases).

## Observed indicators

- **Vendor-policy announcement:** the vendor publicly announces a policy decision (firmware update, terms-of-service change, custodial-flow introduction) that materially changes the product's trust-model surface.
- **Public-controversy magnitude:** unusually-high public response from the affected user base — security researchers, competing vendors, crypto-press outlets, and security-conscious users producing a coordinated critical response within days of the announcement.
- **Vendor-side communication mismanagement:** deleted-and-clarified social-media posts; CEO-level walk-back statements; emergency CTO interviews. The Ledger May 18, 2023 deleted tweet ("Technically speaking it is and always has been possible to write firmware that facilitates key extraction") is the canonical example.
- **Competing-vendor user-acquisition campaigns:** Trezor, GridPlus, Cypherock, Tangem, Coldcard ran user-acquisition campaigns capitalising on the Ledger Recover controversy — a category-level secondary indicator that the trust-substrate shift is structurally significant.
- **User-side threat-model invalidation:** users publicly articulating that the previously-load-bearing trust-substrate claim is now operationally false — "the seed-never-leaves-the-device claim is invalidated"; "the non-custodial claim is qualified by the auth-provider dependency"; "the censorship-resistance claim is qualified by the sequencer-policy".

## Detection signals

- **Vendor-policy-change-monitoring as part of operational-security workflow:** users with material holdings on a vendor's product should subscribe to the vendor's official communications and monitor for trust-substrate-relevant policy announcements. The signal is qualitatively different from per-incident attack-detection signals — it is a corporate-policy-tracking surface.
- **Industry-press cohort tracking:** crypto-press outlets (CoinDesk, The Block, Decrypt, Wired) typically surface trust-substrate-relevant policy changes within 24–72 hours of the vendor announcement; cohort-level coverage is the operational signal for users without direct vendor-communication-feed subscription.
- **Competing-vendor messaging:** when a vendor in a category announces a trust-substrate shift, competing-vendor messaging often surfaces the structural-significance dimension before the affected vendor's own communication catches up.
- **Vendor-side communication-quality anomaly:** deleted-and-reposted communications; ambiguous CEO statements; rapid-fire clarification cycles. These are signals that the policy change is being received as a trust-substrate revocation rather than as a routine product update.

## Real-world examples

- **Ledger Recover seed-recovery service trust-substrate-shift event — Ledger hardware-wallet ecosystem — 2023-05-16 (canonical T6.007 case)** — $0 realised on-chain loss; non-financial trust-substrate-revocation event. Ledger's announcement of an opt-in seed-recovery service that would split user seed phrases (encrypted, sharded via Pedersen Verifiable Secret Sharing across a 2-of-3 custodian set: Ledger / Coincover / EscrowTech) collapsed the public-facing mental model that "the seed never leaves the device" which had been one of Ledger's load-bearing trust-substrate claims since the Nano S launched in 2016. Realised effect: meaningful fraction of the security-conscious user base lost confidence in Ledger's trust posture; multiple competing hardware-wallet vendors ran user-acquisition campaigns capitalising on the controversy; the broader hardware-wallet category's "key never leaves device" trust substrate was structurally weakened across the cohort. Vendor response: Pascal Gauthier (Ledger CEO) publicly delayed the launch on May 23, 2023 and committed to releasing source code for the relevant firmware components prior to launch; service launched in October 2023 as Ledger Recover by Coincover with the 2-of-3 custodian shape. Ledger's deleted-and-clarified tweet on May 18, 2023 ("Technically speaking it is and always has been possible to write firmware that facilitates key extraction. You have always trusted Ledger not to deploy such firmware whether you knew it or not") caused a secondary controversy. Trust-substrate damage is **partially recovered but not fully restored**: the "seed never leaves device" claim is now operationally false for users who opt in; the user-side burden has shifted from "trust the device" to "trust the device + read the firmware update changelog every release for any change to the seed-handling surface." Attribution: `confirmed` at the vendor-corporate-decision layer. See [`examples/2023-05-ledger-recover-trust-substrate-shift.md`](../examples/2023-05-ledger-recover-trust-substrate-shift.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T6.007's primary detection surface is at the vendor-communications / corporate-policy layer, not the on-chain runtime risk-engine surface MG covers.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: industry-press cohort tracking (CoinDesk, The Block, Decrypt, Wired); competing-vendor messaging surfaces; user-community discussion forums (r/Ledger, r/CryptoCurrency, hardware-wallet-specific Twitter/X cohort); vendor-side official communications and developer-documentation feeds.

## Mitigations

- **User-side vendor-policy-change-monitoring (highest-leverage):** subscribe to the vendor's official communications feed for any product whose trust-substrate claim is load-bearing in your threat-model construction; treat trust-substrate-relevant policy announcements as first-class operational-security events.
- **Vendor-side commitment-stability:** vendors should treat trust-substrate claims as load-bearing operational commitments, not marketing language. Material changes to the trust-substrate surface should be communicated with the same care as security-incident disclosures, including public-comment windows for the affected user base before final policy adoption.
- **Vendor-side communication discipline:** during trust-substrate-shift events, vendor-side communications should avoid ambiguous claims about prior product capabilities (the Ledger May 18, 2023 deleted tweet is the canonical anti-pattern); all communications should be prepared in advance and reviewed for trust-substrate-implications.
- **Conditional-trust framing in user education:** vendor trust-substrate claims should be framed as **conditional on the vendor's continued policy adherence** in user-onboarding documentation, not as absolute properties. Users with non-trivial holdings should include vendor-policy-change-monitoring as part of their operational-security workflow.
- **Source-code release as trust-substrate-restoration primitive:** Ledger's commitment to release the relevant firmware-components source code prior to Ledger Recover launch is the canonical recovery-side primitive for vendor-side trust-substrate revocation events. Source-code release does not fully restore the prior trust substrate but it is the closest operational analogue for partial restoration.
- **Competing-vendor accountability:** the category-level competitive surface (Trezor, GridPlus, Cypherock, Tangem, Coldcard messaging during the Ledger Recover controversy) is a market-driven accountability primitive that disciplines vendor-side trust-substrate changes through user-acquisition opportunity-cost.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1 are a **gap** — no existing OAK-M* entry directly maps to vendor-policy-change-monitoring. v0.x candidate Mitigation entries: "OAK-M-x vendor-policy-change-monitoring"; "OAK-M-x conditional-trust framing in user education"; "OAK-M-x source-code release as trust-substrate-restoration primitive".

## Citations

- See worked example for primary citations.
- Ledger Recover: Ledger SAS official announcement May 16, 2023; Ledger Reddit AMA May 2023; CoinDesk / The Block / Decrypt / Wired secondary coverage; competing-vendor messaging archives.

## Discussion

T6.007 closes a structural gap in OAK v0.1's T6 coverage. The dominant v0.1 T6 sub-Techniques (T6.001-T6.006) all share a *defense-evasion-by-attacker-action* shape — the attacker constructs a surface that defeats the defender's controls. T6.007 introduces a **defense-evasion-by-vendor-policy-change** surface where no attacker is involved but the user's effective security posture is nonetheless reduced.

The framework's credibility depends on documenting how user trust gets revoked even in the absence of a deployed attack. A defender vocabulary that only catalogues attacker-action events misses the structural fact that vendor-policy changes can revoke load-bearing trust-substrate claims unilaterally, with realised non-financial effects on the affected user cohort.

The structural OAK lesson is that **vendor trust-substrate claims are first-class defender-side mitigation surfaces** that can be invalidated by vendor-policy changes without any adversarial action. The defender's mental model is constructed partly from the vendor's commitments; revocation of those commitments is a defender-mental-model invalidation event regardless of whether on-chain extraction occurred.

The Ledger Recover case is the canonical anchor at v0.1. Pre-Recover, Ledger's "seed never leaves the device" claim was a load-bearing user-side mental-model anchor that informed Ledger users' threat-model construction for nearly a decade (2016 Nano S launch to 2023 Recover announcement). Post-Recover, the claim is operationally false for users who opt into the service; users who do not opt in are still exposed to the *capability* surface that the firmware update introduced (the device can extract the seed if instructed; trust now depends on the firmware-release change-control process).

The Ledger CEO's framing — "the trade-off, I think, is acceptable" — did not restore the prior trust substrate. The structural lesson is that vendor-side trust-substrate claims, once revoked, cannot be fully restored through assurance language; partial restoration through source-code release is the closest operational analogue.

The class generalises beyond hardware-wallet vendor-policy. The Polymarket / UMA platform-override sub-pattern at [`examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md`](../examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md) (Polymarket public characterisation of the resolution as "unprecedented governance attack" is itself a defender-credibility-event signal at the platform layer) is adjacent to T6.007. Future T6.007 worked examples should preserve:

- The non-attack structure (no on-chain extraction)
- The realised-non-financial-loss dimension (trust-substrate revocation; cohort-level effect on category trust posture; competing-vendor user-acquisition opportunities)
- The vendor-communications-during-trust-substrate-shift dimension (the deleted-tweet pattern)
- The custodian-set jurisdiction footprint dimension where third-party-custodian shapes are introduced
- The cohort-level effect on the broader category's trust posture

Promotion to `stable` will follow once additional worked-example anchors land — additional vendor-policy-change events with material trust-substrate revocation implications; regulatory / legal-process events that change the operational threat model; infrastructure-level changes (L2 sequencer policy, oracle-resolution-policy) that reshape defender mental models. The class anchor at v0.1 (Ledger Recover) is sufficient for `emerging` status but the multi-anchor pattern that warrants `stable` is still forming.
docs.techniques/T7.001-mixer-routed-hop.md:
# OAK-T7.001 — Mixer-Routed Hop

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T7 (Laundering)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Bitcoin, cross-chain
**First documented:** 2017 (academic interest), enforcement attention 2022+
**Aliases:** "mixer hop", "obfuscation hop", "anonymity-set hop"

## Description

The routing of post-extraction assets through a privacy-oriented service whose explicit purpose is to break the linkage between deposit and withdrawal addresses. From a defender's perspective, T7.001 is identified less by the deposit event itself (which is publicly observable) and more by the **inferential linkage** between deposit and withdrawal sides via timing, amount, and counterparty heuristics — and by the residual on-chain artifacts on either side of the hop.

## Observed indicators

- Deposit transactions from previously T5-flagged clusters into known privacy-service deposit addresses (Tornado Cash 0.1 / 1 / 10 / 100 ETH pool contracts on Ethereum mainnet are the canonical EVM-side reference list; Tornado-Nova on Gnosis Chain and the Tornado Cash router contracts are documented variants).
- Withdrawal transactions from the same service whose timing, amount, and downstream-counterparty patterns are statistically associable with the upstream deposits.
- Continuation of activity on the withdrawal side that resumes patterns characteristic of the upstream operator (for example, T1 deployment-funding patterns).
- Anomalous use of post-OFAC-designated mixer infrastructure (Sinbad bitcoin deposit addresses pre-Nov-2023 seizure; eXch deposit addresses pre-2025 shutdown) by clusters that previously routed through Tornado Cash.

## Detection signals

- Deposit-side: per-cluster deposit volume into known privacy-service contracts; live-updated against the OFAC SDN list rather than frozen at a single 2022 snapshot.
- Linkage heuristics (probabilistic): timing-window, amount-class, and counterparty-graph heuristics that produce candidate withdrawal-side matches with stated confidence.
- Cluster continuation: T8.001-style operator-cluster continuity across the hop.
- Mitigation cross-reference: see OAK-M07 (cross-chain attribution graph) and OAK-M27 (Travel Rule / KYC at the privacy-chain boundary) for the operational mitigation surface.

## Real-world examples

- **AnubisDAO post-rug laundering — Ethereum -> Tornado Cash — 2021-10-29** — Following the OAK-T5.001 hard rug of ~13,556 ETH (see the T5.001 page), the drainer routed funds through Tornado Cash in four portions over the following 24 hours; an intermediary address subsequently laundered ~1,018 ETH through the same service. This is a clean public example of the T5 -> T7 chain on which most early laundering-route detection methodology was developed. Forensic write-up: `[cointelegraphanubismixer2022]`.
- **Tornado Cash sanctions designation — Ethereum — 2022-08-08** — The U.S. Treasury (OFAC) added Tornado Cash to the SDN list under Executive Order 13694, citing approximately $7B in alleged laundering volume routed through the protocol. The designation was a regulatory artefact rather than an incident; for OAK's purposes it bounds the time period during which "Tornado Cash deposit" became a per-transaction sanctions consideration for downstream venues. Source: `[ofac2022tornado]`.
- **Ronin Bridge -> Tornado Cash — Ethereum — 2022-04 onwards** — Following the March 2022 Ronin Bridge theft (~$625M, OAK-G01 / Lazarus attribution per `[ellipticronin2022]`), the operator routed extracted ETH through Tornado Cash in the months preceding the August 2022 sanctions designation; this is the canonical "pre-OFAC Lazarus -> Tornado" reference flow that the post-2022 substrate substituted away from.
- **Harmony Horizon -> Railgun continuation — Ethereum — 2023-01-13** — Per the FBI public statement of Jan 23 2023 (`[fbiharmony2023]`), the June 2022 Harmony Horizon theft proceeds (>$60M ETH) were moved through Railgun in January 2023 as continuation laundering. This is the canonical post-Tornado-Cash-sanctions privacy-substrate substitution case in the public record.
- **Sinbad mixer designation — Bitcoin — 2023-11-29** — OFAC designated Sinbad.io as a key Lazarus laundering tool that had processed flows from the Horizon Bridge and Axie Infinity / Ronin heists; the FBI / Dutch FIOD / Finnish KRP simultaneously seized the Sinbad infrastructure. Treasury and Elliptic characterised Sinbad as a successor to the May-2022-sanctioned Blender.io. Source: `<!-- TODO: cite URL -->` (Treasury press release JY1933).
- **eXch shutdown post-Bybit — multi-chain — 2025-05-01** — Per `[coindeskthorchainlazarus2025]` and Elliptic / TRM coverage, ~$200M of the $1.46B Bybit-extracted funds (Feb 2025, OAK-G01) were routed through the no-KYC eXch service before eXch announced May 1 2025 shutdown under DOJ / international scrutiny; eXch resumed limited API operations subsequently per TRM. Source: `<!-- TODO: cite URL -->` (Elliptic eXch retrospective; TRM eXch ongoing-operations post).
- **YoMix mixer — Bitcoin — 2023-2024** — Per Chainalysis, YoMix absorbed a substantial share of the Sinbad-equivalent flow after the Sinbad seizure; cited under `[chainalysis2024laundering]` as part of the post-Sinbad mixer-substrate evolution.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2016-08-bitfinex.md`](../examples/2016-08-bitfinex.md) — Bitfinex exchange theft
- [`examples/2019-06-plus-token.md`](../examples/2019-06-plus-token.md) — PlusToken (Plus Token) Ponzi
- [`examples/2019-11-upbit.md`](../examples/2019-11-upbit.md) — Upbit exchange hot-wallet drain
