- [`examples/2021-10-anubisdao.md`](../examples/2021-10-anubisdao.md) — AnubisDAO (ANKH)
- [`examples/2022-03-ronin-bridge.md`](../examples/2022-03-ronin-bridge.md) — Ronin Bridge
- [`examples/2022-04-beanstalk.md`](../examples/2022-04-beanstalk.md) — Beanstalk Farms governance attack
- [`examples/2022-06-harmony-horizon.md`](../examples/2022-06-harmony-horizon.md) — Harmony Horizon Bridge

- [`examples/2022-10-mango-markets.md`](../examples/2022-10-mango-markets.md) — Mango Markets oracle manipulation
- [`examples/2023-02-hopefinance.md`](../examples/2023-02-hopefinance.md) — Hope Finance router-rerouting rug pull
- [`examples/2023-03-euler-finance.md`](../examples/2023-03-euler-finance.md) — Euler Finance lending exploit
- [`examples/2023-06-atomic-wallet.md`](../examples/2023-06-atomic-wallet.md) — Atomic Wallet
- [`examples/2023-09-coinex.md`](../examples/2023-09-coinex.md) — CoinEx hot-wallet theft

- [`examples/2023-09-stake-com.md`](../examples/2023-09-stake-com.md) — Stake.com hot-wallet theft
- [`examples/2024-02-bitforex.md`](../examples/2024-02-bitforex.md) — BitForex hot-wallet drain (operator-driven exit-scam pattern)
- [`examples/2024-02-fixedfloat.md`](../examples/2024-02-fixedfloat.md) — FixedFloat instant-swap hot-wallet drain
- [`examples/2024-05-dmm-bitcoin.md`](../examples/2024-05-dmm-bitcoin.md) — DMM Bitcoin hot-wallet theft
- [`examples/2024-06-btcturk.md`](../examples/2024-06-btcturk.md) — BtcTurk hot-wallet drain (first incident)
- [`examples/2024-06-uwu-bittensor.md`](../examples/2024-06-uwu-bittensor.md) — Bittensor `bittensor` PyPI supply-chain coldkey-exfiltration compromise
- [`examples/2024-08-bittensor-coldkey-cohort.md`](../examples/2024-08-bittensor-coldkey-cohort.md) — Bittensor PyPI compromise
- [`examples/2024-09-bingx.md`](../examples/2024-09-bingx.md) — BingX hot-wallet drain
- [`examples/2024-09-indodax.md`](../examples/2024-09-indodax.md) — Indodax hot-wallet drain
- [`examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md`](../examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md) — Inferno Drainer service ecosystem
- [`examples/2025-01-phemex.md`](../examples/2025-01-phemex.md) — Phemex hot-wallet theft
- [`examples/2025-02-bybit.md`](../examples/2025-02-bybit.md) — Bybit cold-wallet theft
- [`examples/2025-02-bybit-thorchain-laundering.md`](../examples/2025-02-bybit-thorchain-laundering.md) — Bybit aftermath
- [`examples/2025-04-erc4337-paymaster.md`](../examples/2025-04-erc4337-paymaster.md) — ERC-4337 paymaster compromise cohort
- [`examples/2025-04-exch-shutdown.md`](../examples/2025-04-exch-shutdown.md) — eXch instant-exchange shutdown
- [`examples/2025-07-coindcx.md`](../examples/2025-07-coindcx.md) — CoinDCX operational-wallet drain
- [`examples/2025-08-btcturk.md`](../examples/2025-08-btcturk.md) — BtcTurk hot-wallet drain (recurrence)
- [`examples/2025-09-sbi-crypto.md`](../examples/2025-09-sbi-crypto.md) — SBI Crypto mining-pool drain

## Reference implementations

- MG `mg-detectors-rs` — graph-only / inferential coverage; documented as partial gap in v0.1.
- Chainalysis Reactor, TRM Forensics, Elliptic Investigator — coverage in compliance products.
- Academic detectors — see citations.

## Mitigations

- **Risk teams / venues:** screen incoming deposits against post-mix-withdrawal flags published by major compliance providers; subscribe to a live-updated SDN-anchored mixer watchlist rather than a frozen 2022 snapshot.
- **Protocols / venues:** integrate compliance-provider screening at deposit time. See OAK-M27 (Travel Rule + KYC at the privacy-chain boundary) and OAK-M07 (cross-chain attribution graph) for the canonical mitigation surfaces; OAK-M22 (rotate-on-disclosure) applies for venues that discover historical exposure to a now-designated mixer counterparty.

## Citations

- `[ofac2022tornado]` — Tornado Cash SDN designation, Aug 2022.
- `[cointelegraphanubismixer2022]` — AnubisDAO laundering route.
- `[ellipticronin2022]` — Ronin Bridge / Lazarus attribution.
- `[fbiharmony2023]` — FBI Harmony / Railgun continuation statement.
- `[coindeskthorchainlazarus2025]` — Bybit / eXch / THORChain post-extraction routing.
- `[chainalysis2024laundering]` — aggregate post-OFAC mixer-substrate evolution including YoMix.

## Discussion

T7.001 detection is fundamentally probabilistic and jurisdiction-sensitive. v0.1 documents the Technique; vendor-specific linkage methodologies are out of scope for the open framework but are linked from Reference implementations.

**Post-OFAC substrate (2022 -> present).** The Tornado Cash sanctions designation (Aug 2022) materially changed the mixer-routing landscape. Pre-OFAC: a small number of high-volume EVM-native mixers (Tornado Cash dominant; Blender.io for BTC, sanctioned May 2022) drew the bulk of post-extraction routing; cohort detection was near-binary (deposit-address match against a short SDN-anchored list). Post-OFAC: the substrate fragmented across (a) **privacy-preserving substrates** with broader feature surfaces — most prominently Railgun, used at scale in 2023+ DPRK laundering chains (FBI Jan 2023 PSA flagged the >$60M Harmony continuation through Railgun); (b) **successor mixers** that absorbed Tornado-class flow before themselves being designated — Sinbad (sanctioned and seized Nov 29 2023 as a Blender successor and OAK-G01 staple), eXch (post-Bybit-2025 shutdown after ~$200M routed; years of explicit no-KYC operation), YoMix (post-Sinbad flow absorption documented by Chainalysis); (c) **cross-chain mixer-equivalents** — THORChain in particular acted as a high-volume privacy-equivalent for the post-Bybit-2025 laundering chain (~$1.46B routed in ~10 days, classified under T7.003 but exercising mixer-equivalent obfuscation properties).

**Privacy-chain substitute risk.** Beyond mixers, sustained laundering pressure on transparent-chain substrates is pushing flows toward natively-private chains (Monero, Zcash shielded pool, Penumbra, Aztec Connect successors, Namada). Per `[chainalysisprivacychain2024]`, instant-exchanger-mediated XMR conversion is the documented bridge from EVM-extracted assets into Monero. The compliance posture of major venues (per `[binancexmrdelist2024]` Monero delistings) constrains the off-ramp for privacy-chain laundering, but the mid-flow obfuscation surface remains.

**False-positive reasoning.** Privacy-service deposits by individual users acting for legitimate confidentiality reasons are non-zero and not by themselves attributable; the false-positive cost of treating mixer-deposit alone as evidence is high enough that defender methodology consistently requires additional cluster-attribution signal (T5 origin, OAK-G* group attribution) before treating a mixer-routed flow as illicit. Detection consequence: a defender's mixer-routing watchlist must (i) be live-updated against the SDN list, (ii) include privacy-preserving substrates as candidate hops even where unsanctioned, (iii) treat cross-chain native-asset swap protocols (THORChain, Maya) as mixer-equivalents for cohort-tracking. The pre/post-OFAC routing-pattern divergence is now itself a forensic signal: a 2024+ extraction-to-laundering chain that routes only through Tornado Cash is anomalous, whereas a chain that fans out across Railgun / eXch / Sinbad-or-YoMix / THORChain matches the modern OAK-G01 cohort tradecraft. v0.x deferred work: a per-mixer cohort-attribution table linking each post-2022 sanctioned mixer to its dominant operator clusters.
docs.techniques/T7.002-cex-deposit-layering.m:
# OAK-T7.002 — CEX Deposit-Address Layering

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T7 (Laundering)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana, Bitcoin, cross-chain
**First documented:** systematic from 2020 onward; characterised at scale in `[chainalysis2024laundering]`
**Aliases:** "deposit layering", "diversified-deposit laundering", "structured CEX off-ramp"

## Description

A class of laundering flows in which proceeds are routed into a centralised exchange (CEX) through many distinct deposit addresses — often nested through intermediary personal wallets in a "hop" pattern — with per-address values calibrated to keep individual deposit amounts below the freeze and reporting thresholds the receiving exchange's compliance posture is calibrated against. From a defender's perspective, T7.002 is a *post-extraction* Technique: the proceeds are already attacker-controlled, and the question is whether the cumulative inflow into one or more CEXes can be detected and frozen before the operator successfully off-ramps to fiat.

T7.002 is structurally distinct from T7.001 (Mixer-Routed Hop) because the compliance surface is different: T7.001 obscures the on-chain trail before reaching any KYCing venue; T7.002 reaches a KYCing venue directly but distributes the inflow to defeat per-deposit-address thresholds. Sophisticated operators frequently chain both — T7.001 for early-stage obfuscation, then T7.002 for the off-ramp.

## Observed indicators

- A single exchange with many distinct deposit addresses receiving inflows whose collective source-cluster is consistent (suggesting a single beneficial owner across many KYC-distinct customer accounts at the exchange).
- Per-deposit values calibrated below typical reporting / freeze thresholds ($1K, $3K, $10K bands documented in `[chainalysis2024laundering]`); spike density just below and just above these thresholds.
- "Hop" pattern: funds enter the exchange via intermediary personal wallets that have no other apparent purpose; the intermediary wallet is funded once, deposits to the exchange, and is then dormant.
- Deposit-address-cluster size growth: a step-function increase in the count of distinct deposit addresses receiving inflows from a single source cluster within a short window.
- Customer-account-creation cohort: time-correlated spike in newly-KYCed customer accounts at the receiving exchange whose first deposit comes from a common source-cluster.

## Detection signals

- Exchange-side compliance: cluster deposit addresses by upstream funding source within a rolling window; aggregate nominally-distinct customer deposits whose upstream source-clusters overlap.
- On-chain (vendor-side, e.g., Chainalysis Reactor, TRM Forensics, Elliptic Investigator): per-cluster aggregate inflow tracking against known illicit-cluster watchlists; alert when a watchlisted cluster reaches a threshold-multiple of cumulative deposit volume across one or more exchanges.
- Threshold-structuring: distribution-shape detection on per-deposit values; alert on bands just below $1K / $3K / $10K reporting thresholds.
- Per-cluster cardinality: alert on source-clusters whose distinct-deposit-address count over rolling windows exceeds a calibrated threshold (the canonical 2023 cohort metric in `[chainalysis2024laundering]` was 109 deposit addresses each receiving >$10M in illicit funds).

## Real-world examples

- **Tian Yinyin / Li Jiadong OFAC case — multi-exchange — 2020-03-02** — Treasury OFAC and DOJ jointly designated and indicted two PRC nationals for laundering ~$100M+ of DPRK-attributed cryptocurrency-exchange-heist proceeds (originating from a 2018 ~$250M exchange compromise) through structured deposits across multiple exchanges. Tian moved >$34M through a newly-added bank account linked to his exchange account, and converted ~$1.4M into prepaid Apple iTunes gift cards usable for further BTC purchase at certain venues. This is the canonical CEX-deposit-layering attribution case in the public record and the reference precedent that subsequent OAK-G01 layering operations are measured against. Sources: `<!-- TODO: cite URL -->` (Treasury press release SM924; DOJ press release).
- **Aggregate scale — multi-chain — 2023** — Per `[chainalysis2024laundering]`, in 2023 alone 109 exchange deposit addresses received over $10M each in illicit cryptocurrency for a collective $3.4B, while 1,425 addresses received over $1M each for a collective $6.7B (~46% of all illicit value received by exchanges that year). The diversification trend (more addresses receiving smaller per-address amounts) is the canonical T7.002 signature at scale.
- **Threshold structuring — 2023** — `[chainalysis2024laundering]` documents distribution-shape spikes just below the $1K, $3K, and $10K reporting thresholds — a textbook T7.002 indicator that compliance teams can use as a calibration target. The TradFi structured-deposit doctrine maps directly.
- **Garantex deposit-pattern enforcement — 2022 -> 2025** — Per `[ofac2022garantex]`, `[doj2025garantex]`, `[treasury2025garantexnetwork]`, `[chainalysis2025garantex]`, and `[trmlabs2025grinex]`, Garantex (sanctioned April 2022, dismantled by international action 2025) was a recurrent T7.002 off-ramp for OAK-G01 and ransomware-attributed flows; the post-2025 Grinex successor pattern continues to exhibit T7.002-style deposit clustering. The Garantex enforcement arc is the canonical regulator-in-the-loop T7.002 case.
- **Atomic Wallet -> exchange off-ramps — multi-chain — 2023-06** — Per `[ellipticatomic2023]`, the ~$100M+ Atomic Wallet extraction (T11.002, OAK-G01) was laundered partially through Garantex via T7.002-style structured deposits; subsequent industry reporting tracked recurring 2024-2025 OAK-G01 deposit-cluster patterns into multiple unnamed exchanges with insufficient compliance posture.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2023-06-atomic-wallet.md`](../examples/2023-06-atomic-wallet.md) — Atomic Wallet
- [`examples/2024-03-munchables.md`](../examples/2024-03-munchables.md) — Munchables

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T7.002 is exchange-compliance-side; out of MG scope).
- Vendor coverage: Chainalysis Reactor, TRM Forensics, Elliptic Investigator (per-cluster aggregate inflow tracking against watchlists); exchange-side compliance teams (per-deposit-address structured-deposit detection).

## Mitigations

- **Exchanges (highest-leverage):** integrate per-cluster aggregate inflow tracking from compliance providers; aggregate nominally-distinct customer deposits whose upstream source-clusters overlap; threshold-structuring detection on the deposit-amount distribution; per-customer KYC posture review when aggregate-cluster inflows exceed configured thresholds. See OAK-M27 (Travel Rule + KYC at the privacy-chain boundary) for the canonical mitigation reference.
- **Risk teams (organisational):** maintain illicit-cluster watchlists; cross-reference outbound transfers from organisational treasury wallets against destinations whose downstream cluster shows T7.002 signatures. See OAK-M07 (cross-chain attribution graph) for the upstream attribution surface.
- **Regulators:** ensure structuring-evasion thresholds in cryptocurrency align with established TradFi standards; the documented spikes around $1K / $3K / $10K mirror TradFi structured-deposit doctrine.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2024laundering]` — 2024 Chainalysis Money Laundering report (canonical scale and pattern characterisation).
- `[ellipticatomic2023]` — Atomic Wallet -> Garantex laundering pathway.
- `[ofac2022garantex]`, `[doj2025garantex]`, `[treasury2025garantexnetwork]`, `[chainalysis2025garantex]`, `[trmlabs2025grinex]` — Garantex enforcement arc and Grinex successor.

## Discussion

T7.002 is OAK's reframing of "exchange off-ramp laundering" as a Technique rather than as an undifferentiated risk class. Per `[chainalysis2024laundering]`, the deposit-address diversification trend has accelerated through 2022-2024 specifically as compliance teams have improved per-deposit-address detection — a defender's improvement at the per-address level induced an operator response at the across-address level. Contributors writing future T7.002 examples should preserve this defender-operator co-evolution dynamic; T7.002 mitigation is not a single intervention but a continuous arms race calibrated against evolving operator distribution-shapes.

**False-positive reasoning.** Per-cluster aggregate-inflow alerts at the exchange compliance layer carry meaningful false-positive cost: institutional liquidity providers, OTC desks, and high-frequency arbitrage clusters legitimately route through many deposit addresses. Effective T7.002 detection couples cluster-cardinality with upstream watchlist attribution rather than treating cardinality alone as evidence. The 2024 Chainalysis cohort metrics (109 addresses each >$10M; 1,425 each >$1M) anchor the *illicit* sub-population once watchlist filtering has been applied.

**Post-OFAC substrate evolution.** As individual deposit-pattern detection has improved, sophisticated cohorts have shifted toward (a) routing through privacy substrates first (T7.001) before T7.002 entry into a less-scrutinised venue, (b) using non-KYC or weak-KYC venues (eXch pre-2025, Garantex pre-dismantling, the Grinex successor pattern), and (c) chaining through cross-chain bridges (T7.003) before settlement. v0.x deferred work: a per-venue compliance-posture scoring rubric that links observed T7.002 cohort cardinality to the venue's documented KYC and Travel Rule posture, calibrated against the `[chainalysis2024laundering]` cohort metrics.
docs.techniques/T7.003-cross-chain-bridge-lau:
# OAK-T7.003 — Cross-Chain Bridge Laundering

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T7 (Laundering)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana, Bitcoin, cross-chain
**First documented:** systematic from approximately 2022 onward; emerged as the dominant Lazarus laundering rail post Tornado Cash sanctions
**Aliases:** "chain hopping", "bridge laundering", "THORChain laundering" (the dominant rail)

## Description

A class of laundering flows in which proceeds are routed across multiple chains via cross-chain swap and bridge protocols, breaking the single-chain trail that traditional forensic methodology relies on. The typical pattern is: extract on chain A, swap to a bridgeable asset on chain A (e.g., ETH), bridge to chain B (often Bitcoin), swap to a settlement asset on chain B (often DAI or USDC re-minted on the destination chain), then exit via T7.002 CEX off-ramp or further chain hops. From a defender's perspective, T7.003 is the post-Tornado-Cash-sanctions evolution of `[ofac2022tornado]`-era laundering: when single-chain mixers became regulatorily costly, operators shifted to cross-chain swap infrastructure that achieves comparable obfuscation through chain-jumping rather than through mixing on a single chain.

T7.003 has become the dominant Lazarus Group / OAK-G01 laundering rail through 2024-2025. Per `[coindeskthorchainlazarus2025]`, THORChain emerged as the central protocol post Tornado Cash sanctions. After the February 2025 Bybit hack, Lazarus laundered the full $1.4B in approximately 10 days through THORChain — and THORChain node operators collectively earned at least $12M in fees from the Bybit heist alone. The fee accrual to the bridge operator is itself a T7.003 detection signal at the protocol-level: a sudden spike in fees against an active illicit-cluster-attributed flow is a structural indicator.

## Observed indicators

- Source-side: large outflow from a known illicit cluster into a cross-chain swap or bridge protocol within hours of an extraction event.
- Destination-side: large inflow to a "settlement asset" (DAI, USDC, BTC) on the destination chain consistent with the source-side timing and amount.
- Bridge-protocol-side: per-protocol fee spike correlated with an active illicit-cluster attribution (e.g., the ~$12M THORChain fee accrual post-Bybit).
- "Chain-hop" pattern: assets move ETH -> BTC -> DAI (or similar canonical paths) within a short window through multiple cross-chain protocols rather than a single direct swap.
- Liquidity-pool flow correlation: a step-function increase in inbound liquidity to a specific cross-chain swap pool (e.g., THORChain ETH.ETH / BTC.BTC pools) within hours of an attributed extraction event.
- Native-asset preference: the modern T7.003 rail uses native-asset cross-chain swaps (THORChain, Maya) rather than wrapped-asset bridges to avoid the canonical "lock-and-mint" attribution that Chainalysis / TRM / Elliptic graph-traversal heuristics most reliably catch.

## Detection signals

- Per-protocol attribution: monitor cross-chain swap and bridge protocols (THORChain, Maya, LI.FI, Across, Stargate, Synapse, etc.) for inflows from known illicit-cluster watchlists; produce per-protocol illicit-cluster inflow metrics analogous to T7.001 mixer-flow metrics.
- Per-incident speed: alert on the speed-from-extraction-to-bridge-deposit metric; the canonical Lazarus pattern is hours-to-days, not weeks.
- Fee-accrual-correlated-with-attribution: at the protocol-economics layer, sudden fee spikes that correlate with illicit-cluster inflow are a structural indicator that the protocol is being used as a laundering rail at scale.
- Cross-chain attribution graphs (OAK-M07): forensic providers (Chainalysis Reactor, TRM Forensics, Elliptic Investigator) maintain cross-chain attribution graphs that follow funds across hops; alert on flows that traverse N>=3 chain hops within a short window.
- Bridge-side runtime: cross-chain protocol watcher networks (related to OAK-M39) that publish per-protocol illicit-inflow metrics on a recurring cadence.

## Real-world examples

- **Bybit aftermath — Ethereum -> THORChain -> Bitcoin / DAI — 2025-02-21 onward** — Per `[coindeskthorchainlazarus2025]` and `[chainalysis2024laundering]`, the OAK-G01 Lazarus Group laundered ~$1.4B in Bybit-extracted ETH through THORChain in approximately 10 days post-extraction. THORChain node operators collectively earned at least $12M in fees connected to this single laundering operation. The case is the canonical T7.003 + G01 example at the headline-magnitude scale; the speed (10 days for the entire $1.4B) demonstrates the operational maturity of the modern Lazarus laundering rail. See worked example at `examples/2025-02-bybit-thorchain-laundering.md`.
- **Ronin Bridge -> cross-chain hops — Ethereum / Bitcoin — 2022-04 onwards** — Per `[ellipticronin2022]`, the post-extraction laundering chain for the March 2022 Ronin Bridge theft (~$625M, OAK-G01) included Tornado Cash (T7.001) for the initial obfuscation followed by progressive chain-hopping through bridges and cross-chain swap services as the Tornado-era substrate became regulatorily costly. The Ronin case is the canonical pre-THORChain-dominance T7.003 reference flow.
- **Harmony Horizon -> Railgun -> cross-chain — Ethereum / Bitcoin — 2022-2023** — Per `[fbiharmony2023]` and `[ellipticharmony2022]`, the June 2022 Harmony Horizon theft (~$100M, OAK-G01) saw multi-stage post-extraction routing including the documented January 2023 >$60M Railgun continuation and subsequent chain-hopping activity. Harmony is one of the cleanest public examples of the T7.001 -> T7.003 chain.
- **Multichain anySwap T7.003 surface — multi-chain — 2023-07** — Per `[chainalysismultichain2023]` and `[halbornmultichain2023]`, the July 2023 Multichain incident (`[dlnewsmultichain2023]`) created post-extraction T7.003 surface as the operator distributed assets across the Multichain-supported chain set; the case illustrates that T7.003 is not exclusively a Lazarus pattern but a generic post-extraction substrate available to any operator with cross-chain extraction footprint.
- **General Lazarus laundering profile — 2022-2025** — Per `[chainalysis2024laundering]`, the volume of stolen-funds inflows to cross-chain bridges increased substantially in 2024 vs prior years, primarily attributed to Lazarus-cluster activity. The shift mirrors the post-Tornado-Cash-sanctions repositioning catalogued in industry reporting.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2023-09-coinex.md`](../examples/2023-09-coinex.md) — CoinEx hot-wallet theft
- [`examples/2023-09-stake-com.md`](../examples/2023-09-stake-com.md) — Stake.com hot-wallet theft
- [`examples/2024-02-bitforex.md`](../examples/2024-02-bitforex.md) — BitForex hot-wallet drain (operator-driven exit-scam pattern)
- [`examples/2024-07-wazirx.md`](../examples/2024-07-wazirx.md) — WazirX
- [`examples/2024-09-indodax.md`](../examples/2024-09-indodax.md) — Indodax hot-wallet drain
- [`examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md`](../examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md) — Inferno Drainer service ecosystem
- [`examples/2024-10-radiant-capital.md`](../examples/2024-10-radiant-capital.md) — Radiant Capital cross-chain lending compromise
- [`examples/2025-01-phemex.md`](../examples/2025-01-phemex.md) — Phemex hot-wallet theft
- [`examples/2025-02-bybit.md`](../examples/2025-02-bybit.md) — Bybit cold-wallet theft
- [`examples/2025-04-exch-shutdown.md`](../examples/2025-04-exch-shutdown.md) — eXch instant-exchange shutdown
- [`examples/2025-05-cetus.md`](../examples/2025-05-cetus.md) — Cetus Protocol concentrated-liquidity overflow exploit
- [`examples/2025-07-coindcx.md`](../examples/2025-07-coindcx.md) — CoinDCX operational-wallet drain

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T7.003 is cross-chain forensic layer; requires multi-chain attribution graph that is out of MG scope).
- Vendor coverage: Chainalysis Reactor (cross-chain attribution), TRM Forensics (cross-chain tracing), Elliptic Investigator (cross-chain attribution); bridge-protocol-side compliance integrations are sparse at v0.1.

## Mitigations

- **Cross-chain bridge protocols (highest-leverage):** integrate compliance-provider screening at deposit time; pause or freeze deposits from sanctioned addresses; publish per-protocol illicit-cluster inflow metrics on a recurring cadence. See OAK-M07 (cross-chain attribution graph) and OAK-M39 (cross-protocol watcher network).
- **Risk teams:** maintain cross-chain attribution graphs; treat post-incident-time-window cross-chain flows as elevated risk for inbound deposits at downstream venues. See OAK-M22 (rotate-on-disclosure) for venues that discover historical exposure to a now-attributed cross-chain laundering counterparty.
- **Regulators / law enforcement:** the cross-chain attribution methodology is mature enough at v0.1 that asset-freeze actions can be coordinated across multiple chains within days of a major extraction event; this requires policy infrastructure beyond OAK's scope.

## Citations

- `[coindeskthorchainlazarus2025]` — primary citation for the THORChain-as-Lazarus-laundering-rail framing and the Bybit aftermath metrics.
- `[chainalysis2024laundering]` — aggregate evolution of cross-chain laundering through 2023-2024.
- `[ellipticronin2022]`, `[ellipticharmony2022]`, `[fbiharmony2023]`, `[halbornharmony2022]` — pre-THORChain-dominance Lazarus T7.003 reference flows.
- `[chainalysismultichain2023]`, `[halbornmultichain2023]`, `[dlnewsmultichain2023]` — Multichain anySwap incident as non-Lazarus T7.003 surface.
- `[ofac2022tornado]` — pre-OFAC substrate baseline for the post-2022 substitution.

## Discussion

T7.003 is structurally important to OAK because it operationalises the post-Tornado-Cash-sanctions reality: single-chain mixers are no longer the dominant laundering rail for headline-scale extractions; cross-chain swap and bridge protocols have replaced them. The OAK-T7.001 (Mixer-Routed Hop) Technique remains relevant but has shifted from "the dominant Lazarus laundering rail" to "one of multiple available rails", with T7.003 increasingly dominant for the headline-scale cases.

**False-positive reasoning.** Cross-chain swap protocols have substantial legitimate volume — DeFi-native portfolio rebalancing, cross-chain yield farming, and arbitrage flows. Per-protocol inflow alerts must be coupled with upstream illicit-cluster attribution rather than treating cross-chain inflow as evidence on its own. The fee-accrual-correlated-with-attribution signal (e.g., the $12M THORChain fee figure during the Bybit window) is most useful as an *aggregate-level* indicator that a protocol has become a laundering rail at scale, not as a per-transaction screening criterion.

**Privacy-chain substitute risk.** As cross-chain bridge laundering attracts compliance-provider attention, operators have begun to combine T7.003 with privacy-chain detours (Monero, shielded Zcash, Penumbra) at the mid-flow point, defeating the wrapped-asset attribution graph entirely between hops. Per `[chainalysisprivacychain2024]` and `[binancexmrdelist2024]`, the privacy-chain off-ramp is constrained by venue-side compliance posture but the mid-flow obfuscation remains.

A live policy question is whether the T7.003 mitigation responsibility lives at the cross-chain bridge protocol layer or at the receiving-venue layer. OAK does not opine; it documents that both layers offer real defensive surface and that the dollar-loss-prevented metric is most sensitive to whichever layer moves first. v0.x deferred work: a per-protocol T7.003 cohort-attribution table linking each major cross-chain swap protocol to its dominant illicit-flow share, calibrated against the `[chainalysis2024laundering]` 2024 metrics.
docs.techniques/T7.004-nft-wash-laundering.md:
# OAK-T7.004 — NFT Wash-Laundering

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T7 (Laundering)
**Maturity:** observed
**Chains:** EVM (primary; Ethereum / Polygon)
**First documented:** 2021 (early NFT-marketplace surge); systematic 2022 onward
**Aliases:** "NFT money laundering", "self-financed NFT trade", "wash-laundering through art"

## Description

A class of laundering flows in which an attacker uses self-financed NFT trades to convert proceeds into nominally-legitimate "art purchase" or "art sale" income at controlled valuations. The pattern is: attacker purchases an NFT from a self-controlled wallet at the desired-laundered value; the NFT may then be sold onward to a third party (rare) or retained as a "legitimately-acquired asset" (more common); the funds delivered to the seller side are now nominally-legitimate sales proceeds. From a defender's perspective, T7.004 is structurally distinct from OAK-T3.002 (Wash-Trade Volume Inflation) — even though the on-chain trade pattern is similar — because the *goal* differs: T3.002 inflates apparent volume to manipulate price discovery for downstream marks; T7.004 launders illicit proceeds by routing them through a self-financed art sale.

T7.004 is one of the lower-volume Techniques in the T7 family by aggregate dollar value: per `[chainalysis2022nft]`, NFT-specific money laundering was less than 1% of total crypto-wide money laundering in 2021, against ~$8.6B total crypto-wide money laundering monitored that year. However, the per-transaction value can be high (high-end NFT sales reach hundreds of thousands of dollars) and the social-legitimacy framing (an "art collector" rather than a "deposit address holder") creates compliance complexity that downstream venues have not consistently solved.

## Observed indicators

- A single user account selling NFTs to self-financed addresses many times (`[chainalysis2022nft]` documented 262 such users in 2021 with >25 self-financed sales each; 110 of them netted ~$8.9M in profit from the pattern).
- Per-marketplace wash-trade volume share: studies have documented platform-level wash-trade rates of ~94.5% on LooksRare and ~84.2% on X2Y2 in 2022-2023 — high rates that mix T3.002-style (platform-incentive-driven wash) and T7.004-style (laundering) patterns.
- Self-financed-cluster signature: source-of-funds analysis for the buyer-side wallet in an NFT trade, with attention to flows that originate from the seller-side cluster directly or indirectly.
- Outlier-valuation-against-collection-floor: NFT trades whose price is materially decoupled from contemporaneous floor-price for the same collection, executed between cluster-overlapping wallets.
- Counterfeit-or-low-liquidity collection signature: per `[chainalysisnftcounterfeit2022]`, T7.004 increasingly intersects with fake-mint / counterfeit collections (T12.002) where the laundering wallet self-mints and self-trades within a controlled collection, defeating floor-price comparison entirely.

## Detection signals

- Per-trade source-of-funds analysis: for high-value NFT trades, trace the buyer-side funding back N hops; alert on trades where the buyer-side funding originates from a cluster overlapping with the seller-side cluster.
- Per-cluster wash-trade rate: per the academic and industry methodology, compute the fraction of a user's NFT trades that involve a self-financed counterparty across a window. See OAK-M26 (wash-trade-rate metrics at marketplace layer).
- Goal-discrimination heuristic: distinguish T3.002 (price-discovery-targeting wash) from T7.004 (laundering wash) by the downstream behaviour — T3.002 typically aims to attract third-party buyers via inflated apparent activity; T7.004 typically does not need third-party buyers and the asset may be retained or further routed.
- Cross-reference with T7.001 / T7.002 / T7.003 cohorts: a wallet that exhibits T7.004 patterns *and* receives inflows from previously-mixer-routed clusters is a higher-confidence T7.004 candidate than one that exhibits the on-chain trade pattern alone.

## Real-world examples

- [`examples/2023-02-blur-airdrop-wash-cohort.md`](../examples/2023-02-blur-airdrop-wash-cohort.md) — Blur airdrop incentive-wash cohort (2022-10-19 through 2024). Anchors T7.004 in its **motive-ambiguous** framing: the on-chain trade pattern is structurally indistinguishable from laundering-motive wash-trades at the trace level even though the dominant cohort motive was airdrop-farming. The case is the canonical T3.002 / T7.004 motive-ambiguity reference and the cleanest illustration that marketplace-layer detection requires goal-discrimination heuristics rather than trade-pattern alone — primary classification T12.001, T7.004 referenced as adjacent.
- **Aggregate cohort — multi-marketplace — 2021** — Per `[chainalysis2022nft]`, 262 users were identified as selling NFTs to self-financed addresses more than 25 times in 2021; 110 of them collectively netted ~$8.9M in profit. The remaining 152 users in the cohort did not net profit on the wash-trades themselves, indicating the cohort spans both profit-seeking and pure-laundering motives. NFT-specific money laundering remained <1% of total crypto-wide laundering in 2021.
- **Platform-level wash-trade rates — LooksRare / X2Y2 — 2022-2023** — Industry research has documented platform-level wash-trade rates of ~94.5% (LooksRare) and ~84.2% (X2Y2) at peak — driven primarily by token-emission-farming incentive structures (T3.002 motive) but providing on-chain cover for T7.004 motive flows that mix into the same trace. Both marketplaces subsequently adjusted incentive structures.
- **CryptoPunks-class high-value wash trades — Ethereum — 2022** — Per `[theblock2022boredape]` and contemporaneous industry coverage, several high-value Bored Ape Yacht Club / CryptoPunks-class NFT trades during the 2022 NFT-bull-market peak exhibited self-financed counterparty signatures (cluster-overlap between buyer-side and seller-side wallets) at headline-grabbing valuations. The cohort-level evidence is documented; per-incident attribution is harder to establish than the price-discovery motive.
- **Blur farming as wash-trade-equivalent — Ethereum — 2023** — Per `[blurzeroroyalty2022]` and follow-up industry coverage, the 2023 Blur point-farming incentive structure produced on-chain trade patterns indistinguishable at the trace level from T7.004 wash-trades, even though the dominant motive was Blur-token-airdrop farming rather than laundering. The case is the canonical example of the T3.002 / T7.004 motive-ambiguity problem and the reason marketplace-layer detection requires goal-discrimination heuristics rather than trade-pattern alone.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1.
- Vendor coverage: Chainalysis (the original source for the cohort metrics), TRM Labs, Elliptic; academic detectors documented in `[chainalysis2022nft]` companion literature and `[victor2021washtrade]`.

## Mitigations

- **NFT marketplaces (highest-leverage):** integrate per-trade source-of-funds analysis at the marketplace layer; flag trades where buyer-side funding originates from the seller-side cluster; consider per-cluster wash-trade-rate caps as a marketplace-level policy. See OAK-M26 (wash-trade-rate metrics at marketplace layer).
- **Risk teams / regulated venues:** treat NFT-sale proceeds as elevated-risk for KYC-tier review, particularly for sellers whose wash-trade-rate exceeds a calibrated threshold. See OAK-M27 for the Travel Rule mapping.
- **Compliance providers:** publish per-marketplace wash-trade-rate metrics on a recurring cadence; the Chainalysis 2021 retrospective methodology is well-established and has been extended in subsequent industry research.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2022nft]` — primary cohort characterisation (2021 data, 262 users / $8.9M profit metric, NFT laundering as <1% of crypto-wide laundering).
- `[chainalysisnftcounterfeit2022]` — counterfeit-collection intersection with T7.004.
- `[theblock2022boredape]` — high-value-NFT cohort coverage.
- `[blurzeroroyalty2022]` — Blur farming as wash-trade-equivalent.
- `[victor2021washtrade]` — academic wash-trade detection methodology applicable to NFT marketplaces.

## Discussion

T7.004 is included in v0.1 partly as a **completeness move** for the T7 Tactic and partly because the T3.002-vs-T7.004 distinction is genuinely useful for defenders evaluating NFT marketplaces — the same on-chain pattern can serve two different operator goals, and the appropriate mitigation differs. As of v0.1, the cohort-scale metrics in `[chainalysis2022nft]` remain the canonical reference; per-incident named cases are sparse in the public forensic record because the laundering motive is harder to establish than the price-discovery-targeting motive.

**False-positive reasoning.** The on-chain trade pattern alone does not distinguish T7.004 from incentive-driven T3.002 farming or from legitimate same-collector intra-portfolio rebalancing. Effective T7.004 detection requires (i) source-of-funds attribution for the buyer-side wallet, (ii) goal-discrimination by downstream behaviour, and (iii) cross-reference with upstream T7.001 / T7.002 / T7.003 cohort attribution. Single-signal detection produces unworkable false-positive rates given the legitimate intra-collector trade volume.

**Post-OFAC substrate evolution.** As the T7.001 / T7.002 / T7.003 surfaces have attracted increasing compliance-provider attention, T7.004 has retained appeal precisely because the social-legitimacy framing of "art" creates KYC-tier ambiguity at downstream venues. v0.x deferred work: a per-incident named-case catalogue where the laundering motive is publicly established (rather than inferred), and a structured T3.002 / T7.004 motive-discrimination rubric usable at marketplace-layer screening.

This Technique sits at the intersection of OAK's main scope and the NFT-specific pillar called out in `TAXONOMY-GAPS.md`. As the NFT pillar expands in a v0.x update, T7.004 may be reframed alongside other NFT-specific Techniques; v0.1 includes it under T7 because the laundering frame is more operationally salient than the NFT-specific frame for this particular pattern.
docs.techniques/T7.005-privacy-chain-hops.md:
# OAK-T7.005 — Privacy-Chain Hops

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T7 (Laundering)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Bitcoin, cross-chain, Monero, Zcash
**First documented:** systematic from approximately 2017 onward (Monero gained darknet-market dominance in 2016–2017); enforcement-relevant attention 2020+
**Aliases:** "privacy-coin hop", "XMR hop", "Monero off-ramp", "shielded-pool hop"

## Description

A class of laundering flows in which proceeds are routed through a privacy-oriented blockchain (Monero, Zcash, Dash, Pirate Chain, etc.) — typically by depositing into a centralised exchange that supports both the source chain and the privacy chain and withdrawing on the privacy-chain side, or by using an instant-swap service that performs the same conversion without a custodial account. The on-chain attribution trail does not just become harder to follow on the privacy-chain side; at the chain-architecture level it is broken entirely. Monero's combination of ring signatures, RingCT confidential transactions, and stealch-address-derived one-time outputs means that the sender, recipient, and amount of every transaction are concealed by construction; Zcash shielded (z-) addresses provide comparable guarantees within the shielded pool, although the majority of historical ZEC volume has settled on its transparent (t-) side. From a defender's perspective T7.005 is a *terminal* obfuscation rail: once funds enter the privacy chain, defender capability is reduced to off-chain attribution (CEX deposit-side records, withdrawal-side patterns on the source chain, and post-privacy-chain re-emergence inference) rather than on-chain forensics.

T7.005 is structurally distinct from T7.001 (Mixer-Routed Hop): T7.001 obfuscates within a transparent chain via a smart-contract or service-level mixer whose deposit and withdrawal events remain publicly observable and statistically associable; T7.005 obfuscates by jumping to a chain whose architecture itself defeats on-chain attribution, so the equivalent statistical-association attack does not apply on the privacy-chain leg. T7.005 is also distinct from T7.003 (Cross-Chain Bridge Laundering): T7.003 adds attribution-graph hops between transparent chains and is defeated (probabilistically) by cross-chain forensic graphs; T7.005's destination chain is itself opaque, so adding more cross-chain forensic capacity does not close the gap. T7.005 is the third orthogonal laundering rail alongside T7.001 and T7.003.

The T7.005 venue surface has narrowed since 2023. Most major Western-regulated CEXes have delisted Monero or scheduled it for delisting; per `[binancexmrdelist2024]`, Binance announced XMR delisting on 2024-02-06 (effective 2024-02-20), continuing a multi-year delisting trend (Bittrex, OKX, others). The off-ramp surface for the source-chain → CEX → XMR pattern has consequently shifted toward a smaller set of remaining exchanges and toward instant-swap services that do not require account-level KYC. Per `[chainalysisprivacychain2024]` and the Chainalysis 2025 Crypto Crime Report cohort findings, vendor solicitation of Monero and instant-exchanger-mediated XMR conversion have continued to grow even as exchange-mediated XMR access has contracted.

## Observed indicators

- Source-chain side: outflow from a known illicit cluster into deposit addresses associated with a CEX that supports both the source asset and a privacy chain; per-deposit values may or may not be threshold-structured per T7.002.
- Source-chain side: outflow into instant-swap services (e.g., FixedFloat, ChangeNOW-type services) followed by no observable downstream on-chain trail consistent with the deposited amount.
- Withdrawal-side (source chain): no further movement of the deposited funds on the source chain in a pattern consistent with the operator's prior on-chain footprint — the trail terminates at the venue.
- Re-emergence side (post-XMR / post-ZEC): inflows on a *different* transparent chain at a later date, consistent in amount-class and timing with the original deposit, into addresses whose subsequent behavioural pattern matches the upstream operator (T8.001-style cluster-continuation across the opaque hop).
- Venue-side: a CEX with both source-chain deposit support and privacy-chain withdrawal support shows recurring same-customer deposit-then-privacy-chain-withdraw flows whose source-side cluster aggregates into a known illicit-cluster watchlist.

## Detection signals

- Per-CEX deposit-address attribution: forensic providers (Chainalysis, TRM, Elliptic) maintain CEX deposit-address-cluster attribution; the source-chain leg is detectable using the same machinery as T7.002 even if the privacy-chain leg is not.
- Instant-swap-service inflow tracking: inflows into known instant-swap-service deposit addresses from illicit-cluster watchlists, with no proportionate downstream source-chain trail, are an inferential T7.005 indicator (the funds have most likely exited the transparent ledger).
- Post-privacy-chain re-emergence heuristics: probabilistic linkage between source-side terminal-deposit events and later same-amount-class re-emergence on a different transparent chain, scored at stated confidence with explicit acknowledgement that on-chain certainty is unattainable across the opaque hop.
- Volume-shape signals: per-venue ratio of privacy-chain withdrawals to non-privacy-chain withdrawals over a rolling window, segmented by deposit-source-cluster risk score, as a venue-compliance-posture metric.
- Cluster continuation: T8.001-style operator-cluster continuity inferred across the privacy-chain hop from re-emergence patterns rather than direct on-chain linkage.

## Real-world examples

- [`examples/2025-04-exch-shutdown.md`](../examples/2025-04-exch-shutdown.md) — eXch instant-exchange shutdown (April–May 2025). eXch was a non-KYC instant cryptocurrency exchange that supported XMR conversion alongside BTC / ETH / USDT / USDC; its 2014–2025 operating window straddles the post-Binance-XMR-delisting (2024-02-20) venue-surface contraction that drove laundering-cohort migration toward instant-swap services. The case is the cleanest 2024–2025 anchor for the **instant-swap-service-mediated T7.005** sub-pattern: source-chain → instant-swap-service → privacy-chain conversion without account-level KYC, with a confirmed-strength sovereign-side enforcement closure (German BKA / Frankfurt Public Prosecutor's Office seizure 2025-04-30, U.S. DOJ whistleblower track) plus residual-infrastructure persistence (TRM Labs follow-up reporting). T7.005 chains alongside T7.001 (mixer-equivalent role) and T7.003 (cross-chain-swap role) at the same venue.
- **Aggregate cohort context — multi-chain — 2024** — Per `[chainalysis2024laundering]` and `[chainalysisprivacychain2024]`, privacy-chain conversion (predominantly XMR) is the dominant rail for a specific subclass of illicit cohorts (notably darknet-market vendors and CSAM vendors), with vendor solicitation of Monero and instant-exchanger-mediated XMR conversion continuing to grow through 2023–2024. The headline-magnitude theft cohort (Lazarus-class extractions) is *not* the primary T7.005 user-base at v0.1 cadence — that cohort is dominantly T7.003 (cross-chain bridge laundering); T7.005 dominates lower-magnitude high-volume vendor-class flows.
- **Lazarus historical XMR usage** — DPRK-attributed laundering has historically included Monero conversion at smaller-value layers (per `[chainalysis2024dprk]` and industry retrospectives), but at v0.1 the documented dominant Lazarus laundering rail post Tornado Cash sanctions is T7.003 cross-chain swap (THORChain etc.), not T7.005. Specific incident-level XMR attribution to Lazarus is sparse in the public forensic record precisely because the privacy-chain leg defeats on-chain confirmation; OAK does not assert specific incident attributions it cannot verify.
- **Binance XMR delisting — 2024-02-20** — Per `[binancexmrdelist2024]`, Binance's delisting of XMR (announced 2024-02-06, effective 2024-02-20) is the single largest venue-surface contraction event for source-chain → CEX → XMR off-ramping in the OAK-tracked period. The delisting is a regulatory artefact rather than an incident; for OAK's purposes it bounds the time period during which Binance was a viable XMR off-ramp venue and provides a stable reference for the venue-surface-shrinkage trend that has shaped T7.005 operator behaviour through 2024–2025.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T7.005 is fundamentally cross-chain forensic + venue-compliance-side; the privacy-chain leg is out of reach for any on-chain detector regardless of implementation effort, and the source-chain leg is covered by the T7.002 surface rather than a dedicated T7.005 detector).
- Vendor coverage: Chainalysis Reactor, TRM Forensics, Elliptic Investigator — source-chain deposit-side attribution into privacy-chain-supporting venues; instant-swap-service inflow tracking. The privacy-chain leg itself is not on-chain-traceable; vendor coverage is therefore inferential / re-emergence-based rather than direct.
- Venue-side compliance: per-CEX privacy-chain-withdrawal-support policy and per-CEX KYC posture on privacy-chain-paired customer flows.

## Mitigations

- **CEX compliance (highest-leverage):** integrate compliance-provider screening at deposit time on the source-chain side; apply elevated review for customer accounts whose deposit-then-privacy-chain-withdraw flow pattern correlates with watchlisted source-clusters; venues that retain XMR/ZEC support carry disproportionate T7.005 mitigation responsibility.
- **CEX delisting policy:** delisting privacy-chain pairs (the documented industry trend through 2020–2024) is a structural T7.005 mitigation. OAK documents the trend without taking a position on whether further delisting is the correct policy response — the policy debate is out of OAK's scope (see Discussion).
- **Travel-rule enforcement:** FATF Travel Rule-style cross-venue-message infrastructure improves the off-chain attribution layer that T7.005 forces defenders into; jurisdictions with stronger Travel Rule enforcement narrow the off-ramp surface for the post-privacy-chain re-emergence leg.
- **Instant-swap service compliance:** integrate compliance-provider screening on inflows; instant-swap services are at v0.1 a comparatively under-mitigated surface relative to CEXes and represent a high-leverage future mitigation target.
- **Risk teams (organisational):** treat post-incident-time-window flows into privacy-chain-supporting venues and instant-swap services as elevated risk; maintain re-emergence watchlists for amount-class-matching inflows on alternative transparent chains following an unresolved source-chain terminal-deposit event.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2024laundering]` — 2024 Chainalysis Money Laundering Report (privacy-chain laundering volume context within broader cross-chain trends).
- `[chainalysis2024dprk]` — DPRK-attributed cryptocurrency-theft retrospective (Lazarus historical privacy-chain usage context).
- `[chainalysisprivacychain2024]` — Chainalysis CSAM-vendor / instant-exchanger Monero-conversion analysis (vendor-cohort T7.005 usage pattern).
- `[binancexmrdelist2024]` — Binance XMR delisting announcement (venue-surface-contraction reference event).
- `[ofac2022tornado]` — Tornado Cash SDN designation (referenced for the post-2022 laundering-rail repositioning context that T7.005 sits within alongside T7.001 and T7.003).

## Discussion

T7.005 sits at the intersection of a documentable laundering technique and a contested public-policy question. The OAK position is that the framework documents the laundering use of privacy chains at the technique level — observed indicators, detection signals, and mitigations — without opining on the broader privacy-versus-surveillance policy debate. Privacy chains have legitimate non-laundering use-cases (financial privacy as an end in itself, protection of commercial counterparties, protection from chain-analytic targeting of ordinary users); OAK does not characterise privacy-chain use as inherently illicit and does not advocate for a particular regulatory outcome. The documented venue-surface contraction (delistings) is reported as an empirical fact that shapes operator behaviour, not as an OAK policy endorsement.

A live structural observation, separate from the policy debate: T7.005 is the laundering rail with the *highest defender asymmetry* of the T7 sub-techniques. T7.001 and T7.003 are probabilistically attackable through sufficient forensic investment; T7.005 is not — the chain-architecture-level opacity is by design and is not closable through better tooling on the privacy-chain side. Defender capability against T7.005 is therefore concentrated entirely in the off-chain (venue compliance, Travel Rule, instant-swap-service compliance) and inferential (re-emergence heuristics) layers. Contributors writing future T7.005 examples should preserve this asymmetry and resist the temptation to overclaim on-chain attribution against the privacy-chain leg.
docs.techniques/T7.006-defi-yield-strategy-la:
# OAK-T7.006 — DeFi Yield-Strategy Laundering

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T7 (Laundering)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana, cross-chain (within-chain laundering rail per chain)
**First documented:** systematic from approximately 2021 onward (DeFi-summer-and-after) as DeFi liquidity-and-yield surface grew large enough to absorb laundering flows; cohort-scale industry attention 2023+
**Aliases:** "yield-farm laundering", "LP-cover laundering", "staking-derivative laundering", "yield-user-persona laundering"

## Description

A class of laundering flows in which proceeds are deposited into legitimate DeFi yield-farming, liquidity-provision, lending, or liquid-staking protocols and withdrawn after a delay — using the "yield user" / "liquidity provider" persona as the cover story. The protocol itself is not designed to obscure flows; on the contrary, deposits and withdrawals are publicly observable and per-position-attributable on the chain. The laundering effect comes from the *crowd* the launderer joins: legitimate yield-seeking users produce a high-volume baseline of deposit-then-withdraw flows, and a laundering deposit-then-withdraw event is statistically indistinguishable from a yield-user event unless the defender resolves the per-position behavioural pattern. The defender's analytical question is whether a given deposit-and-withdraw trajectory matches a real yield-seeking user — long deposit duration, claim of accrued yield, rebalancing across pools or strategies, sensitivity to APY changes — or a laundering pattern — deposit immediately followed by withdrawal at the lockup minimum, no yield-claim, no rebalancing, no APY-sensitivity, withdrawal-recipient differs from deposit-source where the protocol allows it.

T7.006 is structurally distinct from T7.001 (Mixer-Routed Hop): T7.001 uses a service whose explicit purpose is obfuscation, so the mere fact of interaction with the service is itself a defender signal; T7.006 uses protocols whose explicit purpose is yield, so interaction with the protocol carries no inherent illicit-signal weight and the per-position behavioural pattern is the only defender surface. T7.006 is also distinct from T7.003 (Cross-Chain Bridge Laundering): T7.003 breaks the single-chain trail by jumping across chains; T7.006 stays within a single chain and obfuscates *temporally and persona-wise* through legitimate-yield-infrastructure cover. The two are frequently chained — T7.006 within chain A as a holding-and-cover stage, then T7.003 to chain B for the off-ramp leg — but each rail addresses a different defender surface.

Sub-patterns observed in practice:

- **Deposit-and-withdraw via different recipient address.** Where the protocol allows the depositor to specify a non-self withdrawal recipient (some lending markets, some LST contracts, some vault standards), the deposit-side cluster and withdrawal-side cluster differ by construction; the protocol acts as a same-asset-class bridge between two operator-controlled addresses without requiring a cross-chain hop.
- **Multi-protocol rotation.** Funds rotate through multiple yield protocols in sequence (e.g., lending market → LP pool → vault aggregator → staking derivative), each step adding a fresh per-position attribution surface and fragmenting the on-chain trail across protocols whose forensic-provider coverage and compliance posture vary widely.
- **Liquid-staking-derivative conversion.** Source asset is converted to a derivative token (stETH, jitoSOL, mSOL, etc.) held for a delay then redeemed; the derivative-token leg sits in a deeply liquid market whose on-chain footprint is dominated by legitimate stakers, providing strong cover-crowd density.
- **Single-sided LP-pool laundering.** Single-sided deposit into an LP pool followed by asymmetric withdrawal of a different pool-asset composition; the pool itself laundromats the asset-mix because the launderer's withdrawal need not match the deposit-side asset proportion.

## Observed indicators

- **Per-position duration distribution:** the per-position deposit-to-withdrawal duration for a flagged source-cluster sits at the lockup minimum or just above it, in contrast with the legitimate-user duration distribution which has a long tail (weeks-to-months for the dominant yield cohort).
- **No yield-claim event:** the position is withdrawn without ever calling the protocol's yield-claim / harvest entrypoint, despite the protocol exposing one. Legitimate yield-seeking users claim or compound; a laundering position ignores accrued yield because the carrying cost is not the laundering operator's optimization target.
- **No rebalancing / no APY-sensitivity:** the position does not move when APYs across competing protocols shift; legitimate yield-seekers (especially the high-volume vault-aggregator cohort) rebalance.
- **Deposit-source and withdrawal-recipient mismatch:** where the protocol permits, the withdrawal recipient differs from the deposit source — a structural T7.006 indicator that has no good legitimate-user explanation in most yield-protocol contexts.
- **Aggregate cluster-side flow shape:** for a deposit-cluster attributed to a known illicit source, the *aggregate* inflow into a given yield protocol over a window matches in amount-class and timing the prior extraction event — consistent with the protocol being used as a holding-and-cover venue rather than an end-state custody venue.

## Detection signals

- **Per-deposit duration analysis:** for each deposit attributed to a watchlisted source-cluster, compute the deposit-to-withdrawal duration and compare against the per-protocol legitimate-user duration distribution; alert on duration sitting at the lockup minimum with no yield-claim event in between.
- **Per-deposit-cluster aggregate flow analysis:** for each source-cluster attributed to a known illicit watchlist, compute the aggregate deposit-then-withdrawal flow into and out of each yield protocol over rolling windows; alert on patterns where aggregate-in matches aggregate-out at high-correlation within a short delay envelope and where the withdrawal-recipient cluster differs from the deposit-source cluster.
- **Yield-claim-rate signal:** per-protocol per-source-cluster ratio of yield-claim events to deposit events, normalised against the legitimate-user baseline; a near-zero claim rate against an active deposit volume is a structural T7.006 signal.
- **Withdrawal-recipient-divergence signal:** per-protocol fraction of positions where the withdrawal recipient differs from the deposit source, segmented by source-cluster risk score; an elevated divergence rate against a watchlisted source-cluster is a structural signal.
- **Cross-protocol rotation graph:** treat the yield-protocol surface as a graph with protocols as nodes and same-cluster sequential deposit-then-withdraw flows as edges; alert on watchlisted-source-cluster trajectories that traverse N≥3 yield protocols within a short window without yield-claim events.

## Real-world examples

- **Aggregate cohort context — multi-chain — 2023–2024** — Per `[chainalysis2024laundering]`, sophisticated laundering operators increasingly route proceeds through DeFi infrastructure (lending markets, LP pools, vault aggregators, liquid-staking protocols) as substrate, both for cover and for fragmenting the on-chain trail. Specific named T7.006 cases at the headline-magnitude scale are typically embedded inside larger laundering chains rather than reported as standalone T7.006 incidents; the cohort framing is therefore aggregate rather than incident-anchored at v0.1 cadence. The DeFi share of total laundering throughput has grown materially over 2022–2024 even as the share of *DeFi-stolen* funds laundered has shifted (see `[chainalysis2024laundering]` for the underlying time series).
- **Lazarus / OAK-G01 yield-protocol-rail usage — 2023–2025** — Per `[chainalysis2024dprk]`, DPRK-attributed laundering chains during 2023–2025 have included DeFi yield, lending, and LST stages alongside the dominant T7.003 cross-chain-bridge rail; the yield-stage typically sits as a pre-bridge or post-bridge cover-crowd stage rather than as a terminal laundering rail. OAK does not assert specific incident-level T7.006 attribution that has not been publicly forensically documented.
- **Cohort-scale framing caveat** — At v0.1, T7.006 is most usefully framed at the cohort scale rather than the incident scale because the laundering-pattern-versus-yield-user-pattern distinction is a per-position behavioural inference rather than a public-reporting-friendly transactional artefact. Forensic providers maintain the per-position attribution internally; OAK documents the technique surface without overclaiming public incident attribution.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T7.006 is per-position behavioural-pattern + per-cluster aggregate-flow forensic layer; the per-protocol legitimate-user duration distributions and yield-claim-rate baselines required for clean detection are out of MG scope).
- Vendor coverage: Chainalysis Reactor (per-cluster DeFi-protocol attribution), TRM Forensics (per-cluster DeFi-flow tracing), Elliptic Investigator (per-cluster DeFi-laundering-pattern attribution); cross-chain forensic providers are the canonical T7.006 detection access surface.
- Protocol-side compliance: per-protocol compliance integrations (deposit-time screening against illicit-cluster watchlists, withdrawal-recipient screening where permitted by the protocol's design, T7.006-pattern-aware position-level monitoring) are sparse at v0.1 and represent the highest-leverage future mitigation surface.

## Mitigations

- **DeFi protocol-side compliance (highest-leverage):** integrate compliance-provider screening at deposit time and at withdrawal-recipient-specification time; expose per-protocol illicit-cluster inflow and outflow metrics on a recurring cadence; treat positions with deposit-source / withdrawal-recipient divergence and zero-yield-claim trajectories as elevated-risk positions where the protocol governance permits action.
- **Travel-Rule extension to DeFi where regulatorily applicable:** in jurisdictions where DeFi protocols are within Travel-Rule scope (the regulatory boundary varies materially by jurisdiction at v0.1), Travel-Rule-style cross-venue-message infrastructure narrows the T7.006 off-ramp surface at the post-yield-rail stage; OAK documents the mitigation surface without taking a position on the regulatory boundary.
- **Per-deposit source-of-funds attribution at major venues:** downstream venues (CEXes, fiat off-ramps) that receive post-T7.006 outflows are the canonical attribution surface; per-deposit source-of-funds attribution at these venues, supported by forensic-provider cross-protocol attribution graphs, is where the T7.006 trail is typically closed in practice.
- **Risk teams (organisational):** treat post-incident-time-window inflows into yield-protocol deposit addresses as elevated risk, particularly where the inflow source-cluster is watchlisted; maintain per-protocol per-cluster monitoring across the deposit, hold, and withdraw legs rather than monitoring only the deposit edge.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2024laundering]` — 2024 Chainalysis Money Laundering Report (DeFi-as-laundering-substrate aggregate cohort framing).
- `[chainalysis2024dprk]` — DPRK-attributed cryptocurrency-theft retrospective (Lazarus / OAK-G01 yield-protocol-rail usage context inside larger laundering chains).
- See also `[ofac2022tornado]` (referenced for the post-2022 laundering-rail repositioning context that T7.006 sits within alongside T7.001, T7.003, and T7.005); citation is owned by the T7.001 / T7.003 surface and not re-introduced here.

## Discussion

T7.006 is one of the harder T7 sub-techniques to detect cleanly because legitimate yield-seeking-user behaviour overlaps heavily with the laundering-pattern surface at the per-deposit edge. A short-duration deposit with no yield-claim is consistent with both a laundering operation and a legitimate user who decided not to keep the position; a withdrawal-recipient-differs-from-deposit-source flow is consistent with both a laundering operation and a legitimate user moving to a fresh address for non-laundering reasons (treasury operation, multisig migration, custody change). The defender signal is consequently structural-and-aggregate (per-cluster, per-protocol, per-window flow shape) rather than per-event; per-event T7.006 alerts have a much higher false-positive rate than the equivalent T7.001 or T7.003 alert, and OAK contributors writing future T7.006 examples should preserve this asymmetry rather than overclaim per-event attribution.

A separate live observation: T7.006 grows in importance proportionally to the size of the legitimate yield-and-LP cover crowd. The technique is more effective on protocols and chains where the legitimate yield-user baseline is large (mature lending markets, deep LST markets, large LP pools); it is correspondingly weaker on protocols where the legitimate user-base is small enough that any specific position stands out. This shapes operator behaviour toward the deepest-liquidity protocols on each chain — which is also where the protocol-side-compliance-integration mitigation has the highest dollar-loss-prevented leverage. Contributors writing future T7.006 examples should preserve the cover-crowd-density framing; the technique is not a property of an individual protocol design but of the liquidity-baseline against which the laundering position is hidden.
docs.techniques/T8.001-cluster-reuse.md:
# OAK-T8.001 — Common-Funder Cluster Reuse

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T8 (Operational Reuse)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana
**First documented:** 2021 (TRM SQUID cross-incident attribution); systematic 2024–2025
**Aliases:** "operator cluster", "funder reuse", "deployer fingerprint", "operator-graph reuse"

## Description

The reuse of common funding-source addresses across the launch of multiple adversarial tokens or operations, allowing investigators to attribute distinct incidents to a single operator cluster. From a defender's perspective, T8.001 is what turns one-off alerts into longitudinal operator profiles — the Technique itself is structural, not behavioral, and is identified through cross-incident graph analysis. T8.001 covers the *single-chain* case; OAK-T8.002 covers the cross-chain case where per-chain graphs become jointly attributable only via cross-chain analysis.

## Observed indicators

- Single funding source appears upstream of deployment-related addresses across multiple tokens (depth-2 same-funder-ancestor ratio above threshold across N flagged tokens).
- Shared deployment patterns: identical compiler metadata, constructor parameter shapes, role mappings, salt patterns for CREATE2 deployment.
- Tooling fingerprints: identical bundler-tool signatures (Jito-bundle structure on Solana; private-mempool batching on EVM), identical multisend / disperse contract usage signatures.
- Persistent counterparty pattern: downstream beneficiaries (deposit addresses, off-ramp clusters) continue receiving from successor operations after a brand-or-domain rotation (Garantex → Grinex; Inferno → Angel).
- Off-chain reuse signals (where available): shared landing-page templates, shared social handles, shared hosting-fingerprints across nominally-unrelated tokens.

## Detection signals

- **Cross-token funder graph:** build a graph rooted at deployer-and-early-buyer addresses across all flagged tokens in a window; identify connected components with multiple distinct token deployments (Dune-style: `select component_id, count(distinct token) as tokens from cluster_components group by component_id having tokens > 3`).
- **Deployment-fingerprint clustering:** index contracts by `(creation-bytecode-hash, constructor-arg-shape, deployer-EOA, compiler-metadata)`; flag fingerprints recurring across multiple incident-flagged tokens.
- **Persistent-counterparty propagation:** when an operator brand is sanctioned or shut down, monitor downstream deposit clusters for resumption-of-flow from a new upstream cluster — the resumption signature is the highest-signal indicator of cluster continuity (Garantex → Grinex worked example per `[treasury2025garantexnetwork]` / `[trmlabs2025grinex]`).
- **Forta / on-chain-trace queries:** subscribe to deployment events; on emission, compute deployment fingerprint and funder-ancestor signature, match against known operator-cluster index.
- **Off-chain CTI integration:** cross-reference shared landing-page hashes / social-handle reuse against on-chain attribution clusters.

## Real-world examples

- **TRM Labs SQUID-cluster attribution — BNB Chain — 2021.** TRM's investigation of the SQUID hard-rug operator (see OAK-T1.001 / T5.001 example) traced the same operator cluster across two earlier scams, with three-incident cumulative recovery estimated at ~35,025 BNB (~$19.3M at the time). One of the cleanest public examples of T8.001 because the cross-incident funder-graph and deployment-fingerprint linkage was published with the original forensic write-up rather than reconstructed years later. Source: `[trmsquid2021]`.
- **Drainer-as-a-Service operator-graph handover — multi-chain — 2024.** Per `[slowmist2024report]`, SlowMist's tracking of Inferno Drainer through Q1–Q3 2024 (rising market share to ~43%) followed by the publicly-announced October 2024 handover of operations to Angel Drainer is T8.001 at the *service* layer — same operational infrastructure persists across operators. Defenders attributing post-Oct-2024 drainer activity should treat continuity-of-infrastructure as a stronger signal than continuity-of-actor. The lineage continues through Pink / Vanilla / Chick per `[scamsniffer2024lineage]`.
- **Garantex → Grinex laundering-cluster reuse — 2025.** Per `[treasury2025garantexnetwork]` and `[trmlabs2025grinex]`, the March 2025 Garantex takedown was followed within days by Grinex emerging as a successor exchange under the same operator network, with persistent counterparty patterns linking pre-takedown and post-takedown deposit clusters. The August 14, 2025 OFAC designation of Grinex and the A7A5 ruble-stablecoin network treats the brand discontinuity as operator continuity. The canonical T8.001 case at the laundering-infrastructure layer.
- **Lazarus / TraderTraitor cluster fingerprints — multi-incident — 2017 onward.** Per `[chainalysis2024dprk]` and `[chainalysisdprktradertraitor]`, OAK-G01 wallet clusters are tracked across the Ronin (Mar 2022), Harmony Horizon (Jun 2022), Atomic Wallet (Jun 2023), WazirX (Jul 2024), and Bybit (Feb 2025) incidents through funder-graph and deployment-fingerprint reuse; the cross-incident T8.001 coverage is the load-bearing attribution surface for Lazarus at modern multi-chain scale. The single-chain framing here covers the per-chain instances; OAK-T8.002 covers the cross-chain joint attribution.
- **Pink Drainer cluster reuse — 2024.** Per `[scamsniffer2024pink]`, Pink Drainer's $3M+ extractions (including the OpenAI CTO and Orbiter Finance founder cases) are attributable through funder-graph reuse across nominally-independent victim incidents — a per-affiliate T8.001 instance within the broader G02 service lineage.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2020-09-kucoin.md`](../examples/2020-09-kucoin.md) — KuCoin exchange hot-wallet theft
- [`examples/2021-11-squid.md`](../examples/2021-11-squid.md) — SQUID (Squid Game token)
- [`examples/2022-03-ronin-bridge.md`](../examples/2022-03-ronin-bridge.md) — Ronin Bridge
- [`examples/2023-09-coinex.md`](../examples/2023-09-coinex.md) — CoinEx hot-wallet theft
- [`examples/2023-09-stake-com.md`](../examples/2023-09-stake-com.md) — Stake.com hot-wallet theft
- [`examples/2024-02-fixedfloat.md`](../examples/2024-02-fixedfloat.md) — FixedFloat instant-swap hot-wallet drain
