- [`examples/2024-06-solana-validator-sandwich-mev-cohort.md`](../examples/2024-06-solana-validator-sandwich-mev-cohort.md) — Solana validator-coordinated sandwich-bot cohort (DeezNode \textasciitilde50% of cohort extraction; stable program-cluster fingerprint)

## Reference implementations

- MG `mg-detectors-rs` — D08 funder-graph subsystem (link TBD); applies depth-N ancestor enumeration across token deployments and maintains per-cluster watchlists.
- Chainalysis — coverage in compliance products (Reactor; cluster-attribution layer).
- TRM Labs — coverage in attribution products (Forensics; SQUID worked example is the public reference instance).
- Elliptic — Investigator product, cross-incident wallet-cluster identification.
- Bubblemaps — visual cross-token cluster surface for retail-side investigation.

## Mitigations

- **Risk teams / investigators:** maintain operator-cluster watchlists and re-flag any new token whose genesis maps into a known cluster. See OAK-M04 (Funder-Graph Clustering), OAK-M07 (Cross-Chain Attribution Graph).
- **Launchpads:** screen new deployments against publicly known operator-cluster lists before listing. See OAK-M25 (Listing-Time Source Verification and Audit-Status Gate).
- **Receiving venues (CEXes, bridges):** integrate cluster-attribution-provider screening at deposit time; treat persistent-counterparty propagation post-rotation as elevated-risk signal.

## Citations

- `[liu2025sybil]` — subgraph-based attribution methodology (academic foundation).
- `[trmsquid2021]` — SQUID-cluster cross-incident attribution.
- `[slowmist2024report]` — drainer-family handover example (Inferno → Angel).
- `[scamsniffer2024pink]`, `[scamsniffer2024lineage]` — drainer-cohort lineage tracking.
- `[treasury2025garantexnetwork]`, `[trmlabs2025grinex]` — laundering-cluster brand-rotation.
- `[chainalysis2024dprk]`, `[chainalysisdprktradertraitor]` — Lazarus cross-incident cluster.

## Discussion

Operator-cluster definitions decay over time as operators rotate funding paths; v0.2 should publish a freshness/decay convention for cluster watchlists (recommended: 90-day rolling re-validation of cluster boundaries against new deployments). False-positive sources: shared CEX hot wallets, shared bridge-deposit wallets, and shared MEV-bot funding wallets all produce common-funder signatures across unrelated operators — depth-2 ancestor matching alone is insufficient. Discriminators include intra-cluster funding-pattern variance (true operator clusters fund within tight time windows; coincidental shared-CEX-funded clusters do not) and downstream behaviour correlation.

The infrastructure-vs-actor framing matters operationally: in the Inferno → Angel and Garantex → Grinex cases, brand discontinuity under operator continuity is the persistent pattern, and watchlists keyed on brand or specific named-actor degrade rapidly. T8.001-aware controls should target persistent-counterparty propagation and deployment-fingerprint reuse rather than named-actor labels.

Cross-chain extension is OAK-T8.002. The single-chain case here is the more mature detection surface at v0.1; the cross-chain case requires additional graph-merging methodology and is currently coverage-gap territory for single-chain detectors.
docs.techniques/T8.002-cross-chain-operator-c:
# OAK-T8.002 — Cross-Chain Operator Continuity

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T8 (Operational Reuse)
**Maturity:** observed
**Chains:** cross-chain
**First documented:** systematic 2023 onward as multi-chain operator activity has scaled
**Aliases:** "multi-chain operator profile", "cross-chain attribution", "chain-hop operator"

## Description

A class of OAK-T8 patterns in which a single operator's footprint is observable across multiple chains — same funding-source patterns, same deployment fingerprints, same off-chain artefacts (social handles, hosting infrastructure, code-style fingerprints), or same downstream laundering destinations. Distinct from OAK-T8.001 (Common-Funder Cluster Reuse) because T8.001's canonical attribution surface is a *single-chain* funder graph; T8.002 covers the case where the operator's per-chain funder graphs are *individually* coherent but become *jointly* attributable only via cross-chain analysis.

T8.002 is structurally important for OAK-Gnn (Threat Actors) attribution at the modern multi-chain scale. OAK-G01 Lazarus Group activity is increasingly *only* attributable via T8.002 methodology — the same operator deploys across Ethereum, Solana, Bitcoin, Tron, and various L2 environments, and per-chain attribution alone undersells the operator profile.

## Observed indicators

- Cross-chain funding-source overlap: the same upstream addresses fund operator-controlled deployments on multiple chains, often via a centralised exchange withdrawal or a bridge-deposit hop.
- Cross-chain deployment-fingerprint reuse: identical compiler metadata, constructor parameter shapes, role mappings, salt patterns, or tooling-fingerprints across chains.
- Cross-chain laundering-destination overlap: proceeds from operator-controlled extractions on multiple chains converge at the same off-ramp infrastructure (CEX deposit clusters, mixer services, cross-chain swap protocols — THORChain, LI.FI, Across).
- Off-chain operator continuity: shared social handles, shared hosting infrastructure, shared code-style fingerprints across per-chain operator deployments.
- Cross-chain victim-profiling reuse: drainer-kit operators reuse victim-classification logic and target-list infrastructure across per-chain affiliate streams.

## Detection signals

- **Cross-chain funder-graph extension:** extend per-chain funder-graph computation to a cross-chain graph by ingesting bridge-deposit / CEX-withdrawal events as cross-chain edges; identify connected components whose addresses span multiple chains.
- **Per-fingerprint cross-chain match:** maintain per-deployment-fingerprint indexes across chains; alert on fingerprint matches across operator-clustered addresses on different chains (Dune-style: `select fingerprint, count(distinct chain) as chains from deployments group by fingerprint having chains > 1`).
- **Laundering-destination convergence:** per-cross-chain-laundering-destination flow analysis; identify destinations receiving inflows attributed to multiple per-chain operator clusters within a window.
- **Off-chain CTI integration:** cross-reference off-chain operator profiles (social handles, hosting fingerprints) against on-chain attribution clusters.
- **Cross-chain swap-protocol monitor:** subscribe to THORChain / LI.FI / Across transaction streams; on inflow from a known per-chain operator cluster, flag the outbound chain-and-address for downstream propagation.

## Real-world examples

- **OAK-G01 Lazarus / Bybit → THORChain → multi-chain — Feb–Mar 2025.** Per `[chainalysisbybitthorchain]`, `[coindeskthorchainlazarus2025]`, and `[chainalysis2024laundering]`, Lazarus laundered ~$1.4B of Bybit-extracted ETH through THORChain in approximately 10 days post-extraction; ~83% of stolen ETH was converted to BTC, with ~72% of those funds moving through THORChain; node operators earned ~$12M in fees from the operation. Subsequent industry tracking (Elliptic flagged 11,000+ wallets across chains potentially connected to the heist; FBI's sanctioned-wallet list held only 51) is the canonical T8.002 worked case at v0.1 — per-chain analysis alone substantially undersold the operator profile, and cross-chain joint attribution was load-bearing for the public attribution outcome. <!-- TODO: cite https://www.bitdefender.com/en-us/blog/hotforsecurity/lazarus-group-shows-no-signs-of-stopping-after-moving-almost-three-quarters-of-bybit-stolen-funds -->
- **OAK-G01 multi-chain operations — 2022 onward.** The post-2022 OAK-G01 operational pattern is canonically multi-chain: extractions on Ethereum (Ronin, Harmony, WazirX), Solana, Bitcoin, and various L2s; laundering via cross-chain swap protocols. Per `[chainalysis2024dprk]`, the cross-chain attribution methodology is the dominant approach for modern OAK-G01 attribution. The trilateral U.S.-Japan-ROK statement of January 14, 2025 (`[trilateraldprkstatement2025]`) attributed 2024 thefts including DMM Bitcoin (~$308M), Upbit (~$50M), Rain Management (~$16.13M), WazirX (~$235M), and Radiant Capital (~$50M) to the DPRK — a multi-chain incident set whose joint attribution surface is the T8.002 instance.
- **OAK-G02 Drainer-as-a-Service multi-chain operations — 2023 onward.** Per `[slowmist2024report]` and `[scamsniffer2024lineage]`, the named drainer-service families (Inferno → Angel handover Oct 2024; Pink, Vanilla, Chick successors) operate across multiple chains and across multiple wallet-vendor surfaces. Cross-chain victim-profiling reuse (target-list infrastructure shared across per-chain affiliate streams) is the cleanest non-Lazarus T8.002 case in the public record. T8.002 attribution at the service-infrastructure level is more stable than per-named-operator attribution.
- **OAK-G10 ALPHV/BlackCat ransom-payment cross-chain laundering — Feb–Mar 2024.** The Change Healthcare ~$22M Bitcoin payment and the operator-side exit-scam laundering exhibit cross-chain conversion (BTC → stablecoin / Monero) at downstream venues; the cross-asset laundering profile is the relevant T8.002 instance at the RaaS-payment layer per `[chainalysis2025ransomware]`.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md`](../examples/2024-10-inferno-drainer-handover.md) — Inferno Drainer service ecosystem
- [`examples/2025-04-exch-shutdown.md`](../examples/2025-04-exch-shutdown.md) — eXch instant-exchange shutdown
- [`examples/2026-04-drift-protocol-durable-nonces-dprk.md`](../examples/2026-04-drift-protocol-durable-nonces-dprk.md) — Drift Protocol durable-nonces DPRK exploit (\$285M); Solana extraction with cross-chain laundering trail (USDC, SOL, ETH) consistent with the post-2022 OAK-G01 multi-chain operational pattern.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (T8.002 detection requires cross-chain forensic graph that is out of MG single-chain scope).
- Vendor coverage: cross-chain attribution providers (Chainalysis Reactor, TRM Forensics, Elliptic Investigator) are the canonical access path.
- Open-data: bridge-aggregator transaction streams (THORChain explorer; LI.FI API; Across explorer) provide the ingest surface for community-built cross-chain graphs.

## Mitigations

- **Risk teams / cross-chain forensic providers:** maintain cross-chain attribution graphs with per-chain funder-graph extensions; integrate off-chain operator profile data into the attribution layer; publish per-operator-cluster watchlists at the cross-chain level. See OAK-M07 (Cross-Chain Attribution Graph).
- **Receiving venues (CEXes, bridges):** integrate cross-chain attribution-provider screening at deposit time; treat downstream off-ramp inflows from cross-chain-attributed operator clusters as elevated risk.
- **Cross-chain swap protocols (THORChain, LI.FI, Across):** the Bybit / THORChain case demonstrates that swap-protocol-level pre-execution screening of inflow addresses is the highest-leverage layer; protocol-design discussions on cross-chain compliance gates are an open v0.x area.
- **Standardisation:** cross-chain attribution-graph formats are not standardised at v0.1; vendor-specific access methodology is the canonical path. v0.x considerations include whether OAK should publish a normalised cross-chain attribution schema.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2024dprk]` — OAK-G01 multi-chain operations and cross-chain attribution methodology.
- `[chainalysisbybitthorchain]` — Bybit / THORChain laundering-window operator-fee figure.
- `[coindeskthorchainlazarus2025]` — narrative coverage of THORChain as Lazarus laundering rail.
- `[chainalysis2024laundering]` — broader cross-chain bridge-laundering pattern context.
- `[trilateraldprkstatement2025]` — government-level multi-incident attribution.
- `[slowmist2024report]` — OAK-G02 service-family multi-chain operations.
- `[scamsniffer2024lineage]` — drainer-cohort cross-chain lineage.
- `[chainalysis2025ransomware]` — ransomware-payment cross-asset laundering context.
- `[liu2025sybil]` — academic foundation for the subgraph-based attribution methodology.

## Discussion

T8.002 is structurally important to OAK because it operationalises the modern multi-chain attribution reality: per-chain analysis alone undersells operator profiles for the largest categories (notably OAK-G01). Contributors writing future T8.002 examples should make the **single-chain-coherent + cross-chain-jointly-attributable** distinction explicit — T8.002 is what makes a multi-chain operator profile readable as a single Group, even when no individual chain's evidence is conclusive.

A live design question is whether T8.002 should split into per-axis sub-Techniques (cross-chain funder graph; cross-chain deployment fingerprint; cross-chain laundering convergence; off-chain operator-profile continuity). v0.x updates may revisit the granularity if specific sub-axes reach independent characterisation maturity. The Bybit / THORChain case is the most-defender-relevant calibration anchor at v0.1 because the cross-chain laundering-convergence axis is the most-load-bearing for that incident.

False-positive sources at the cross-chain layer are different from the single-chain layer: shared cross-chain swap-protocol routers, shared bridge-deposit clusters, and shared CEX hot-wallet infrastructure all produce cross-chain edges that are not operator-attributable. Discriminators include matched-amount / matched-timing across the cross-chain edge (operator-controlled flows preserve amount and tight timing; protocol-mediated flows do not) and persistent-counterparty propagation at downstream venues.
docs.techniques/T9.001-oracle-price-manipulat:
# OAK-T9.001 — Oracle Price Manipulation

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana
**First documented:** 2020 (early cases on Compound and bZx); systematic 2022+
**Aliases:** "oracle attack", "price-feed manipulation", "single-block oracle exploit"

## Description

A class of exploits in which an attacker drives the price reported by a protocol's price oracle to an artificial value, then uses that artificial value as collateral or as a settlement reference in the same protocol. From a defender's perspective, T9.001 is identifiable through two complementary signals: the *oracle-side* signal (sudden price moves on the oracle's input venues that are not reflected in deeper-liquidity venues elsewhere) and the *protocol-side* signal (a single account taking actions whose outcomes depend on the manipulated price).

T9.001 is frequently chained with T9.002 (flash loans provide the capital required to move thin-liquidity oracle inputs); the two are documented separately because the underlying defensive control is different — T9.001 is mitigated at the oracle-resilience layer (TWAP windows, multi-venue feeds, deviation circuit breakers — see OAK-M09), while T9.002 is mitigated at the contract-design layer (flash-loan-resistant accounting, single-block-action prohibitions).

Sub-patterns observed in the wild:

- **Spot-feed manipulation** — single-venue spot price (often a thin AMM pool) drives a protocol's collateral or settlement valuation; canonical pattern in Mango, BonqDAO, Vee Finance.
- **Book-value / yield-bearing-token manipulation** — the oracle reads the share price of a yield vault rather than the underlying spot price; a single donation to the vault's reserves mints book-value upward (Cream Oct 2021, Rari/Fuse Apr 2022 cohort).
- **Reporter-pull oracle manipulation** — pull-based oracles (Tellor, UMA-style) where attackers post a manipulated value and ride out the dispute window with stake (BonqDAO).

## Observed indicators

- Sudden oracle price movement on a token whose primary trading venue is the oracle's only input.
- Same-block sequence of "manipulate price on input venue → consume manipulated price in target protocol".
- Disproportionate borrow / settlement / liquidation outcome relative to the protocol's normal activity profile in the surrounding window.
- Per-vault `pricePerShare` or `getPricePerFullShare` jump within a single block of >1% on a vault whose underlying assets did not move comparably on reference venues.
- Pull-oracle `submitValue` from a newly-funded reporter address whose stake barely exceeds the dispute-bond minimum.
- Oracle-deviation thresholds: spot-vs-TWAP > 3-5% over a 30-block window, or oracle-vs-CEX-mid > 2% sustained for ≥1 block on assets with deep CEX liquidity.

## Detection signals

- **Oracle-deviation circuit-breaker (OAK-M09):** compare each oracle's reported price against a wider-liquidity reference (CEX mid, multi-venue TWAP) and pause consumption beyond a calibrated deviation; standard mitigation is multi-venue TWAP plus deviation halt.
- **Cross-action correlation:** detect single-block sequences in which the same actor (or a tightly-related cluster — see OAK-M04 funder-graph clustering) both moves price on an oracle input venue and consumes the resulting price in the target protocol.
- **Vault-share invariant monitor:** alert on `pricePerShare` movements that exceed the underlying asset's reference-price movement by a calibrated margin.
- **Pull-oracle dispute-window monitor:** for Tellor / UMA-style oracles consumed by lending protocols, alert on `submitValue` reports whose deviation from a multi-venue reference price exceeds a threshold within the dispute window.
- **Post-event:** per-protocol replay of the exploit transaction against a price feed that includes a circuit-breaker; quantifies the loss avoided.

## Real-world examples

- **Mango Markets (MNGO) — Solana — 2022-10-11** — Loss \~\$110M. Avraham Eisenberg created offsetting long and short MNGO perpetuals positions, then purchased large quantities of MNGO on the venues feeding Mango's oracle (FTX, AscendEX, Serum). The reported MNGO price moved approximately 13× over a 30-minute window, inflating his collateral position, against which he then borrowed approximately \$110M. The CFTC charged Eisenberg with manipulative and deceptive scheme; criminal convictions were vacated in 2025 on venue / materiality grounds. See worked example at `examples/2022-10-mango-markets.md`. Source: `[cftcmango2023]`.
- **Cream Finance — Ethereum — 2021-10-27** — Loss \~\$130M. The attacker manipulated the price of `yUSDVault` (a Yearn vault share token) used by Cream as collateral by donating ~\$1B in yUSD-DAI to the vault via a flash-loan-funded sequence; the vault's `getPricePerFullShare` consequently doubled, and the attacker borrowed against the inflated collateral and repeated the cycle. The case is the canonical "book-value / share-price oracle" sub-pattern. See worked example at `examples/2021-10-cream-finance.md`. Sources: `[creamfinance2021postmortem]`, `[halborncream2021oct]`, `[muditgupta2021cream]`, `[immunefikream2021]`.
- **BonqDAO — Polygon — 2023-02-01** — Loss \~\$120M (notional; actual liquid recovery far smaller due to market depth on the protocol's stablecoin). The attacker exploited BonqDAO's reliance on Tellor as a pull-oracle without sufficient dispute-window safeguards: a Tellor reporter (the attacker) submitted a manipulated WALBT price, the value propagated to BonqDAO before the dispute window expired, and the attacker minted BEUR against artificially-revalued collateral and triggered liquidations of others. See worked example at `examples/2023-02-bonqdao.md`. Sources: `[bonqpostmortem2023]`, `[blocksecbonq2023]`, `[halbornbonq2023]`, `[peckshieldbonq2023]`, `[tellorbonq2023]`.
- **Vee Finance — Avalanche — 2021-09-21** — Loss \~\$35M. Vee Finance used Pangolin AMM spot prices for leveraged-trading settlement; the attacker manipulated thin-liquidity Pangolin pools (notably for ETH/USDT and BTC.b/USDT) to open and immediately close highly-leveraged positions at an artificial reference price, extracting protocol reserves. Sources: `[veefinancepostmortem2021]`, `[halbornveefinance2021]`, `[blocksecveefinance2021]`, `[slowmistveefinance2021]`, `[rektveefinance2021]`.
- **Rari Capital / Fuse Pool — Ethereum — 2022-04-30** — Loss \~\$80M across multiple Fuse pools. The attacker chained reentrancy in Rari's Fuse implementation with manipulation of pool-level price feeds to drain assets from several Rari Fuse pools simultaneously. The case is included as a cohort example of the "oracle as one of multiple chained flaws" pattern; the primary failure mode in some forensic write-ups is classified as reentrancy / T9.005, with oracle-feed manipulation as a contributing factor. <!-- TODO: cite URL — Rari Fuse Apr 2022 forensic write-up -->

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2020-02-bzx.md`](../examples/2020-02-bzx.md) — bZx Protocol flash-loan-funded exploits
- [`examples/2020-10-harvest-finance.md`](../examples/2020-10-harvest-finance.md) — Harvest Finance flash-loan-funded oracle manipulation
- [`examples/2021-05-pancake-bunny.md`](../examples/2021-05-pancake-bunny.md) — PancakeBunny yield-aggregator mint-pricing exploit
- [`examples/2021-05-spartan-protocol.md`](../examples/2021-05-spartan-protocol.md) — Spartan Protocol AMM-LP-pricing exploit
- [`examples/2021-09-vee-finance.md`](../examples/2021-09-vee-finance.md) — Vee Finance oracle manipulation
- [`examples/2022-04-inverse-finance.md`](../examples/2022-04-inverse-finance.md) — Inverse Finance recurring oracle exploits
- [`examples/2022-04-saddle-finance.md`](../examples/2022-04-saddle-finance.md) — Saddle Finance virtual-price manipulation
- [`examples/2022-07-nirvana-finance.md`](../examples/2022-07-nirvana-finance.md) — Nirvana Finance flash-loan-driven price manipulation
- [`examples/2023-04-allbridge.md`](../examples/2023-04-allbridge.md) — Allbridge stable-pool virtual-price manipulation
- [`examples/2024-03-woofi.md`](../examples/2024-03-woofi.md) — WOOFi Pro sPMM curve flash-loan manipulation
- [`examples/2024-06-uwu-lend.md`](../examples/2024-06-uwu-lend.md) — UwU Lend sUSDe oracle thin-input manipulation
- [`examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md`](../examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md) — Polymarket UMA governance attack
- [`examples/2025-04-kiloex.md`](../examples/2025-04-kiloex.md) — KiloEx oracle-feed manipulation
- [`examples/2025-04-loopscale.md`](../examples/2025-04-loopscale.md) — Loopscale RateX-token-pricing exploit
- [`examples/2025-07-gmx-v1.md`](../examples/2025-07-gmx-v1.md) — GMX V1 GLP global-short-tracking exploit
- [`examples/2025-07-polymarket-zelenskyy-suit.md`](../examples/2025-07-polymarket-zelenskyy-suit.md) — Polymarket Zelenskyy-suit market
- [`examples/2026-03-polymarket-iran-strike-journalist-coercion.md`](../examples/2026-03-polymarket-iran-strike-journalist-coercion.md) — Polymarket Iran-strike market
- [`examples/2022-06-lido-steth-depeg.md`](../examples/2022-06-lido-steth-depeg.md) — Lido stETH depeg cascade (May–June 2022); contributing T9.001 mode where DEX-spot-anchored Aave/Compound oracles propagated the secondary-market discount into liquidation triggers without redemption-rate buffer.
- [`examples/2024-04-renzo-ezeth-depeg.md`](../examples/2024-04-renzo-ezeth-depeg.md) — Renzo ezETH cascade; contributing T9.001 mode where lending-market DEX-spot ezETH oracles propagated the depeg amplitude into liquidation triggers without redemption-rate / slashing-event-delay buffer.
- [`examples/2025-07-lido-steth-aave-cascade.md`](../examples/2025-07-lido-steth-aave-cascade.md) — Lido stETH / Aave queue-saturation cascade; contributing T9.001 mode where Aave's wETH-pool steep utilisation-rate slope amplified the carry-flip channel and lending-market oracles propagated the discount without queue-depth-aware buffer.
- [`examples/2025-01-mango-markets-shutdown.md`](../examples/2025-01-mango-markets-shutdown.md) — Mango Markets January 2025 shutdown following the 2024 SEC settlement (regulatory / operational follow-on to the canonical 2022 Mango oracle-manipulation case at `examples/2022-10-mango-markets.md`; included for cross-reference of the protocol-lifecycle outcome).
- [`examples/2026-04-drift-protocol-durable-nonces-dprk.md`](../examples/2026-04-drift-protocol-durable-nonces-dprk.md) — Drift Protocol durable-nonces DPRK exploit. **Adjacent T9.001 mode** — once admin authority was compromised, the attacker whitelisted a worthless fake token (CVT) as collateral at an artificial reference price and borrowed against it. The collateral-pricing manipulation is T9.001-class; the entry vector is T11.001 / T11.003.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1 (see `COVERAGE.md`); T9 detection is protocol-layer and not in MG's current scope.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: Chainalysis, TRM Labs, and protocol-specific monitoring services (Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender, BlockSec PhalconHQ) provide detection in this class with varying methodologies; pre-deployment audit firms (Halborn, Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin) catch most missing-TWAP / single-venue patterns at review.

## Mitigations

- **Protocols (OAK-M09):** use TWAP-windowed oracles with deviation circuit-breakers; require multi-venue inputs with quorum; block in-block consumption of newly-updated oracle prices for sensitive actions; for pull oracles, enforce dispute-window respecting consumption (do not act on values within the active dispute interval).
- **Protocols (OAK-M16):** pre-deployment audit + formal verification of any oracle-consumption surface; explicit invariant that protocol actions cannot be solvent against a single-venue manipulated price.
- **Protocols (OAK-M11):** rate-limiting and per-block caps on actions whose outcome depends on the oracle (borrow caps per block, liquidation throughput limits) to bound the exploit payoff even when manipulation succeeds.
- **Risk teams:** maintain per-protocol oracle-architecture inventories; treat protocols whose oracle has a single input venue or no TWAP window as elevated risk; flag yield-vault-share oracles as a distinct risk sub-class from spot-price oracles.

## Citations

- `[cftcmango2023]` — Mango Markets enforcement
- `[chainalysis2025rug]` — market-manipulation aggregate
- `[owaspscstop10]` — Price Oracle Manipulation category in OWASP SC Top 10
- `[zhou2023sok]` — academic taxonomy
- `[creamfinance2021postmortem]`, `[halborncream2021oct]`, `[muditgupta2021cream]`, `[immunefikream2021]` — Cream Oct 2021
- `[bonqpostmortem2023]`, `[blocksecbonq2023]`, `[halbornbonq2023]`, `[peckshieldbonq2023]`, `[tellorbonq2023]` — BonqDAO Feb 2023
- `[veefinancepostmortem2021]`, `[halbornveefinance2021]`, `[blocksecveefinance2021]`, `[slowmistveefinance2021]`, `[rektveefinance2021]` — Vee Finance Sep 2021

## Discussion

The Mango Markets case is unusual in that the attacker publicly identified and litigated; most T9.001 incidents are pseudonymous. The 2025 vacatur of the criminal convictions on venue and materiality grounds left the underlying facts undisputed but opened a regulatory question about how "code is law" arguments interact with traditional market-manipulation statutes — a question OAK does not opine on, but contributors writing T9.001 examples should note where regulatory or judicial findings are still in flux.

**Sub-class structure (deferred to v0.x).** The five examples cohort splits cleanly into three sub-Techniques that may be promoted in a future revision:

- T9.001.001 — Spot-AMM-feed manipulation (Mango, Vee Finance) — single-venue thin-liquidity move.
- T9.001.002 — Yield-vault-share / book-value manipulation (Cream Oct 2021, partial Rari) — donation-based `pricePerShare` inflation.
- T9.001.003 — Pull-reporter-oracle manipulation (BonqDAO) — Tellor / UMA-style stake-and-report dispute-window bypass.

Each sub-class has a distinct mitigation surface (TWAP+multi-venue for .001; vault-share invariant for .002; dispute-window-respecting consumption for .003); v0.1 keeps them under the umbrella because the dollar-loss attribution and worked-example cohort are still building. Attribution-strength notes: Mango (high — public actor, court record), Cream / Vee / BonqDAO (medium — pseudonymous attackers, well-documented mechanics), Rari / Fuse (medium — mechanics public, primary-vs-contributing-factor classification disputed across forensic vendors).
docs.techniques/T9.002-flash-loan-enabled-exp:
# OAK-T9.002 — Flash-Loan-Enabled Exploit

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana, cross-chain
**First documented:** 2020 (early bZx cases)
**Aliases:** "flash-loan attack", "atomic-borrow exploit"

## Description

A class of exploits whose feasibility depends on the attacker temporarily holding an outsized capital position via a flash loan — funds borrowed and repaid within a single transaction. T9.002 is rarely the *only* relevant Technique in an incident; it is almost always the *enabling* Technique that makes a T9.001 (oracle manipulation), T9.003 (governance attack), or T9.004 (access-control / logic flaw) exploit profitable. OAK documents flash loans as a separate Technique because the defender-side signal and the contract-design mitigation are distinct from the underlying logic flaw.

The defender's question is: which protocol behaviours are sensitive to a same-transaction outsized-balance precondition, and have they been designed with flash-loan-resistance in mind? Protocols whose voting power, collateral valuation, or settlement logic can be influenced by an in-transaction balance are the persistent T9.002 surface.

## Observed indicators

- Single transaction containing a flash-loan-borrow → action(s) → flash-loan-repay sequence.
- Flash-loan ABI signatures from major providers in the call trace: `flashLoan(address,address[],uint256[],uint256[],address,bytes,uint16)` (Aave V2/V3), `flash(address,uint256,uint256,bytes)` (Uniswap V3), `flashLoan(address,address,uint256,bytes)` (Balancer V2), `flashLoan(address,uint256)` (dYdX SoloMargin / Maker DSS Flash).
- The actions inside the transaction touch a protocol whose state is sensitive to balance at a single block (governance vote, oracle consumption, vault `pricePerShare`, lending solvency check).
- Net economic outcome of the transaction is large relative to typical activity at the touched protocol (borrowed-vs-deposited delta exceeds the protocol's median user position).
- Borrowed amount on the flash loan is large relative to the manipulated venue's depth (e.g., flash-borrow ≥ 5× the target AMM pool's reserves).

## Detection signals

- **Per-transaction flash-loan-pattern detector:** scan for the flash-loan call signature pattern at any major flash-loan provider (Aave, dYdX, Balancer, Uniswap V3, Maker DSS Flash) followed by interactions with sensitive protocol functions (governance vote, oracle update, lending action, donation/reserve functions) in the same transaction.
- **Reentrancy guard / CEI compliance (OAK-M10):** at consuming-protocol layer, mandatory `ReentrancyGuard` plus checks-effects-interactions ordering reduces the surface where a flash-loaned balance can be re-entered into a state-changing call.
- **Per-protocol surface inventory:** catalogue protocols whose accounting is *not* flash-loan-resistant (governance from spot balances, oracles consumed in-block, donation/reserve functions without solvency checks); treat them as persistent T9.002 surface.
- **Pre-event:** monitor recently-deployed contracts for governance / lending / oracle-consumer code paths that take per-block snapshots of balances or voting power without flash-loan-resistance.
- **Block-level anomaly:** flash-loan provider's outflow spike correlated with sensitive-function call at a target protocol within the same block; useful as a coarse low-cost signal upstream of per-protocol detectors.

## Real-world examples

- **Beanstalk Farms — Ethereum — 2022-04-17** — Loss \~\$182M (\~\$76M attacker net profit). The attacker took flash loans totalling approximately \$1B in DAI, USDC, and USDT from Aave, used the funds to acquire approximately 67% of Beanstalk's STALK governance token voting power, and executed a malicious governance proposal (BIP-18) via the protocol's `emergencyCommit` function — all within a single transaction. The chain is canonically T9.002 (flash loan as precondition) → T9.003 (governance vote acquisition) → T7.001 (Tornado Cash laundering). See worked example at `examples/2022-04-beanstalk.md`.
- **Euler Finance — Ethereum — 2023-03-13** — Loss \~\$197M. A flash loan precondition combined with a missing-solvency-check in Euler's `donateToReserves` function let the attacker create an artificial-debt scenario that paid out on liquidation. The attacker subsequently returned the funds following negotiations. The chain is canonically T9.002 (flash loan as precondition) → T9.004 (access-control / logic flaw on `donateToReserves`). See worked example at `examples/2023-03-euler-finance.md`. Sources: `[chainalysiseuler2023]`, `[halborneuler2023]`, `[blocksec2023euler]`, `[elliptipeuler2023]`, `[eulerlabs2023statement]`.
- **PancakeBunny — BSC — 2021-05-19** — Loss \~\$45M (\~\$2.4M attacker net profit; the rest in BUNNY-token devaluation). The attacker flash-borrowed ~\$700M in BNB from PancakeSwap, used the borrowed BNB to manipulate the BNB/USDT and BUNNY/BNB pool reserves that Bunny's reward calculation read, triggered an outsized BUNNY mint as "yield reward", and dumped the freshly-minted BUNNY into the manipulated pool. T9.002 → T9.001 (oracle / pool-reserve manipulation) chain. See worked example at `examples/2021-05-pancake-bunny.md`. Sources: `[bunnypostmortem2021]`, `[peckshieldbunny2021]`, `[blocksecbunny2021]`, `[slowmistbunny2021]`, `[halbornbunny2021]`, `[rektbunny2021]`.
- **Cream Finance — Ethereum — 2021-10-27** — Loss \~\$130M. Flash-loan-funded ~\$1B donation cycle that inflated `yUSDVault` `getPricePerFullShare`, against which the attacker borrowed; the canonical T9.002 → T9.001 (book-value oracle manipulation) chain. See worked example at `examples/2021-10-cream-finance.md`. Sources: `[creamfinance2021postmortem]`, `[halborncream2021oct]`, `[muditgupta2021cream]`, `[immunefikream2021]`.
- **Saddle Finance — Ethereum — 2022-04-30** — Loss \~\$11M. Flash-loan-funded manipulation of a metapool's virtual price calculation in a Saddle stableswap pool that had not been migrated after a known vulnerability disclosure; the attacker drained metapool LP reserves. T9.002 → T9.004 (logic flaw in pool maths) chain. Sources: `[saddleincident2022]`, `[saddleincidentreport2022]`, `[peckshieldsaddle2022]`, `[blocksecsaddle2022]`, `[halbornsaddle2022]`, `[theblocksaddle2022]`.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2020-10-harvest-finance.md`](../examples/2020-10-harvest-finance.md) — Harvest Finance flash-loan-funded oracle manipulation
- [`examples/2020-11-akropolis.md`](../examples/2020-11-akropolis.md) — Akropolis Delphi flash-loan-funded reentrancy
- [`examples/2020-11-origin-dollar.md`](../examples/2020-11-origin-dollar.md) — Origin Dollar (OUSD) flash-loan-funded reentrancy
- [`examples/2021-05-spartan-protocol.md`](../examples/2021-05-spartan-protocol.md) — Spartan Protocol AMM-LP-pricing exploit
- [`examples/2021-09-vee-finance.md`](../examples/2021-09-vee-finance.md) — Vee Finance oracle manipulation
- [`examples/2022-04-inverse-finance.md`](../examples/2022-04-inverse-finance.md) — Inverse Finance recurring oracle exploits
- [`examples/2022-07-crema-finance.md`](../examples/2022-07-crema-finance.md) — Crema Finance fake-tick-array exploit
- [`examples/2022-07-nirvana-finance.md`](../examples/2022-07-nirvana-finance.md) — Nirvana Finance flash-loan-driven price manipulation
- [`examples/2023-02-bonqdao.md`](../examples/2023-02-bonqdao.md) — BonqDAO Tellor oracle manipulation
- [`examples/2023-02-platypus.md`](../examples/2023-02-platypus.md) — Platypus Finance emergency-withdrawal logic flaw
- [`examples/2023-02-yearn-v2.md`](../examples/2023-02-yearn-v2.md) — Yearn iearnUSDT v1 deprecated-vault misconfiguration

- [`examples/2023-08-balancer-v2.md`](../examples/2023-08-balancer-v2.md) — Balancer V2 Boosted Pools exploitation

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1; T9 is protocol-layer.
- Vendor coverage: Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender, BlockSec PhalconHQ (per-transaction monitoring with flash-loan-pattern detectors); pre-deployment audit firms with explicit flash-loan-resistance review (Halborn, Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, ConsenSys Diligence).

## Mitigations

- **Protocols (OAK-M11 + OAK-M10):** design accounting and governance to be flash-loan-resistant — snapshot balances over multi-block windows for sensitive votes; reject same-transaction price oracle consumption following price-moving inputs in the same block; enforce solvency checks at end-of-transaction rather than mid-transaction. Pair with mandatory CEI ordering and `ReentrancyGuard` on every state-changing external-call surface.
- **Protocols (OAK-M16):** pre-deployment audit + formal verification of accounting invariants under a "single-block outsized-balance attacker" threat model; require auditors to demonstrate at least one flash-loan adversary scenario in their report.
- **Protocols (OAK-M17):** time-locked governance + multi-block quorum, eliminates the entire T9.002 → T9.003 chain.
- **Risk teams:** maintain a per-protocol "flash-loan-sensitive surface" inventory.
- **Flash-loan providers:** policy-level mitigations (rate limits, KYC-tiered access) are widely debated and rarely deployed; the OAK position is that mitigation belongs at the consuming-protocol layer, not at the lending-protocol layer.

## Citations

- See worked examples for primary citations on Beanstalk (`examples/2022-04-beanstalk.md`), Euler, Cream, PancakeBunny, Saddle.
- `[zhou2023sok]` — academic taxonomy classifies flash-loan-enabled attacks as a meta-category.
- `[owaspscstop10]` — Flash-Loan Attacks category.

## Discussion

A persistent question in T9.002 framing is whether to treat the flash loan as the Technique or as a precondition that enables other Techniques. OAK chooses the former — making T9.002 a first-class Technique — because the defender-side detection signal is real (the flash-loan-pattern detector is a deployable signal) and because the protocol-side mitigation is real (flash-loan-resistance is a designable property). This framing diverges slightly from `[zhou2023sok]`, which classifies flash loans as a precondition class; both framings are valid and contributors may map between them as needed.

**Cohort observations.** Of the five canonical examples, four are T9.002 → T9.001 / T9.003 / T9.004 chains (Beanstalk → governance, Euler → access-control, Cream / PancakeBunny → oracle, Saddle → logic-flaw); none is "flash-loan-only" because flash loans are a delivery mechanism, not a value-extraction mechanism. Attribution-strength is generally high in this class (the flash-loan call is on-chain, transaction sequence is reproducible, and most cohort attackers are pseudonymous but mechanically traced). v0.x deferred work: an analysis surface around "which flash-loan provider was used" — Aave dominates the cohort, but cross-chain providers (Equalizer, dYdX) appear in smaller incidents and a per-provider breakdown may be worth a sub-page.
docs.techniques/T9.003-governance-attack.md:
# OAK-T9.003 — Governance Attack

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, cross-chain
**First documented:** 2022 (Beanstalk canonical case)
**Aliases:** "DAO governance exploit", "voting-power attack", "BIP attack", "malicious proposal"

## Description

A class of exploits in which an attacker acquires sufficient voting power in a protocol's on-chain governance — typically via a flash loan or via direct token accumulation against a thin governance token — to pass a malicious proposal that drains the protocol's treasury or otherwise alters protocol state in the attacker's favour. From a defender's perspective, T9.003 attacks the *governance design* — specifically, the assumption that voting-power distribution at proposal time reflects long-term stakeholder consent.

T9.003 is the canonical example of a Technique that is "operationally one transaction long" but "design-wise the protocol's entire governance lifetime": every governance proposal that does not enforce flash-loan-resistance, voting-power timelock, or quorum sufficiency is a standing T9.003 surface from the day the governance contract is deployed.

Sub-patterns observed in the wild:

- **Same-block flash-borrow vote** — flash-loan funds a vote on a single proposal that has no snapshot or timelock (Beanstalk).
- **Malicious proposal with hidden delegatecall** — proposal calldata is published, but a `delegatecall` deep in the execution payload silently grants control of governance state to the attacker (Tornado Cash governance May 2023).
- **Vote-buying / delegate-takeover** — attacker accumulates spot voting power by purchasing or borrowing the governance token via DEX or lending market, then submits a treasury-affecting proposal (Compound proposal-289 cohort).

## Observed indicators

- Sudden acquisition of governance-token voting power by a single address or tightly-related cluster, often funded by a flash loan.
- Voting-power-vs-circulating-supply ratio at vote-submission exceeds typical participation ratio (e.g., a single voter holds >30% of voted weight on a proposal whose median voter holds <1%).
- Submission and rapid execution of a governance proposal without the typical multi-day discussion / voting-period cadence.
- Use of "emergency" or expedited-execution functions in the governance contract that bypass normal proposal lifecycle (`emergencyCommit`, expedited-quorum branches).
- Treasury / privileged-action calls in the executed proposal payload directing funds to attacker-controlled addresses.
- Proposal calldata containing `delegatecall` to an address that has not been a governance target in the protocol's history.

## Detection signals

- **Real-time governance-quorum monitor:** monitor governance-token transfer events for sudden large acquisitions correlated with active proposals; alert on flash-loan-funded acquisitions specifically; cross-reference voted weight against a snapshotted distribution from N blocks before proposal creation.
- **Proposal-payload static analysis (OAK-M02):** before any governance proposal reaches its execution window, decode and statically analyse the calldata; alert on payloads that touch treasury, mint privileged roles, or perform `delegatecall` to non-canonical targets.
- **Pre-deployment governance-design review:** governance-contract audit for emergency-execution paths, quorum-without-timelock voting, and voting power computed from spot balances rather than from time-averaged or snapshotted balances.
- **Per-proposal simulation:** replay-style simulation of any proposal whose execution payload touches treasury or privileged-action surfaces; for cross-chain governance, replay against each target chain's state.

## Real-world examples

- **Beanstalk Farms — Ethereum — 2022-04-17** — Loss \~\$182M. Canonical T9.003 case. The attacker borrowed approximately \$1B via Aave flash loans, used the borrowed stablecoins to acquire approximately 67% of STALK voting power, and used the protocol's `emergencyCommit` function to bypass the normal proposal lifecycle and immediately execute a malicious BIP-18 that drained treasury funds to attacker-controlled addresses. The proceeds were laundered through Tornado Cash. See worked example at `examples/2022-04-beanstalk.md`. The case is the textbook T9.002 (flash loan) + T9.003 (governance attack) chain.
- **Tornado Cash governance — Ethereum — 2023-05-20** — Loss \~\$1.1M (TORN token + governance-control implications outsized relative to dollar loss). The attacker submitted a benign-looking proposal (Proposal 20) but the deployed implementation contained a `selfdestruct` that re-initialised state with attacker-controlled logic on execution; once executed, the attacker held effective unilateral control of the governance contract and minted TORN to themselves. The case is the canonical "malicious proposal with hidden delegatecall / state-replacement" sub-pattern; the attacker subsequently restored the prior implementation via a follow-up proposal, complicating attribution. See worked example at `examples/2023-05-tornado-cash-governance.md`. Sources: `[tornadocomm2023]`, `[peckshieldtornado2023]`, `[blocksectornadogov2023]`, `[slowmisttornadogov2023]`.
- **Compound — Ethereum — 2024-07-29 (Proposal 289)** — Loss: contested; the proposal would have transferred ~5% of Compound's COMP treasury (~\$24M) to a multisig controlled by a delegate consortium ("Golden Boys"). The proposal passed by a narrow margin via accumulated delegated voting power; community pushback led to a follow-up proposal that effectively reverted the transfer. The case demonstrates the **non-flash-loan T9.003 sub-pattern**: spot vote-accumulation by a delegate consortium without governance-timelock-resistance against well-organised whale coordination. Sources: `[compoundforumproposal289_2024]`, `[compoundproposal289_2024]`, `[goldenboyscompound2024]`, `[tallygovernancecompound2024]`.
- **Cohort context** — `[zhou2023sok]` documents governance-attack incidents as a recurring class through 2018-2022; smaller-scale governance-snapshot manipulation events through 2023-2025 are catalogued in industry retrospectives. MakerDAO and Gnosis-related governance-acquisition attempts surfaced in community forum discussions through 2022-2023 but did not result in completed exploits at the scale of Beanstalk; OAK does not treat them as canonical incidents but flags them as evidence the surface is actively probed. <!-- TODO: cite URL — MakerDAO governance attempt 2022 forum discussion -->

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2021-09-compound.md`](../examples/2021-09-compound.md) — Compound Comptroller distribution bug
- [`examples/2022-07-audius.md`](../examples/2022-07-audius.md) — Audius governance storage-collision attack
- [`examples/2024-03-curio.md`](../examples/2024-03-curio.md) — Curio DAO governance attack
- [`examples/2024-07-compound-vote-takeover.md`](../examples/2024-07-compound-vote-takeover.md) — Compound DAO Proposal 289 attempted governance takeover

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1.
- Vendor coverage: Forta governance-monitoring agents, Tally / Boardroom analytics dashboards (governance-side observation), Snapshot-based off-chain voting platforms (mitigation rather than detection); audit firms with explicit governance-design review practice (Halborn, Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, ConsenSys Diligence).

## Mitigations

- **Protocols (OAK-M17):** require flash-loan-resistant voting-power computation (snapshot taken at proposal-creation time, multi-block window); enforce minimum delay between proposal submission and execution (timelock); quorum + timelock; remove emergency-execution paths that bypass the normal lifecycle.
- **Protocols (OAK-M02):** static analysis of proposal calldata before execution-window opens; explicit denylist of `delegatecall` to non-canonical targets in proposal payloads.
- **Protocols (OAK-M16):** pre-deployment audit + formal verification of governance contract; specific attention to upgrade paths and emergency branches.
- **Risk teams:** for governance-token holdings, monitor proposal queues for any proposal whose execution payload affects treasury; maintain per-protocol governance-design inventories.

## Citations

- `[tornadocomm2023]`, `[peckshieldtornado2023]`, `[blocksectornadogov2023]`, `[slowmisttornadogov2023]` — Tornado Cash governance May 2023
- `[compoundforumproposal289_2024]`, `[compoundproposal289_2024]`, `[goldenboyscompound2024]`, `[tallygovernancecompound2024]` — Compound proposal 289
- See worked example for Beanstalk primary citations.
- `[zhou2023sok]` — academic taxonomy.
- `[owaspscstop10]` — adjacent categories under access control and protocol logic.

## Discussion

A defensive design observation: many post-Beanstalk governance contracts adopted snapshot-based voting power (e.g., Compound Governor Bravo's checkpoint pattern) and minimum proposal delays as standard. As of 2024-2025, large-scale flash-loan-T9.003 incidents are rarer at the top tier of DeFi protocols precisely because of this design shift; smaller-protocol governance attacks remain common and are typically not published forensically.

**Sub-class structure (deferred).** The three canonical examples partition cleanly:

- T9.003.001 — Flash-loan vote acquisition (Beanstalk) — defended by snapshot-and-timelock.
- T9.003.002 — Malicious proposal with hidden execution-payload semantics (Tornado Cash 2023) — defended by proposal-calldata static analysis (OAK-M02) and execution-target allowlists.
- T9.003.003 — Spot-vote delegate-coordination (Compound proposal 289) — *not* a security flaw in the technical sense; mitigation belongs at the governance-process layer, not the contract layer. Including it under T9.003 acknowledges that "governance attack" has a social dimension that contract-level mitigations alone do not address.

Attribution-strength notes: Beanstalk (high — fully on-chain, attacker known to forensic vendors); Tornado Cash 2023 (medium — restoration proposal complicates intent attribution); Compound 289 (high but contested — participants self-identified, but framing as "attack" vs "legitimate-but-contested governance" is community-dependent). v0.x deferred work: a structured rubric for distinguishing technical-T9.003 from social-T9.003 cases.
docs.techniques/T9.004-access-control-misconf:
# OAK-T9.004 — Access-Control Misconfiguration

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM, Solana, cross-chain
**First documented:** widespread; canonical modern cases 2022+
**Aliases:** "broken access control", "missing authorization check", "guardian-bypass", "logic-flaw extraction", "privilege-boundary violation"

## Description

A class of exploits in which a protocol contract permits an action that should have been gated by a privileged role, by a solvency check, or by another authorisation predicate — but the predicate is missing, evaluated incorrectly, or evaluated against the wrong subject. T9.004 is the broadest single class of smart-contract exploits by incident count: `[owaspscstop10]` (2025/2026) places access-control vulnerabilities at the top of the OWASP SC Top 10. From a defender's perspective, T9.004 is the residual category — the cases where audit, formal verification, or rigorous code review failed to catch a logic flaw before deployment.

OAK groups several sub-patterns under T9.004:

- **Missing authorisation modifier** — the canonical case, where a function lacks an `onlyOwner` / role-gate it should have (KiloEx 2025: external-callable `_setPrices`).
- **Misvalidated authorisation** — the function checks the *wrong* subject (e.g., compares against the caller address rather than the expected counterparty), or fails to validate a critical input (Wormhole 2022: missing guardian-account validation).
- **Inter-contract privilege-boundary violation** — privileged calls between contracts where the calling contract's identity is impersonable (Poly Network 2021: cross-chain manager could be re-targeted via crafted calldata).
- **Missing solvency / invariant check** — the contract permits an action that violates a stated invariant because the check was omitted at the call path that the attacker found (Euler 2023: `donateToReserves`).

## Observed indicators

- Single-transaction extraction events whose mechanism cannot be explained by oracle manipulation, governance acquisition, or operator-controlled outflow — i.e., the contract is being told to do something it should not have permitted.
- Use of an unusual call path or a deprecated function that bypasses the protocol's normal entry points.
- Bytecode patterns: function selectors corresponding to privileged operations (mint, withdraw, setPrice, setOwner, upgradeTo) whose dispatch path lacks a `CALLER` comparison or `SLOAD` of an access-control role mapping.
- Missing-modifier patterns in deployed bytecode: privileged function selector with no `JUMPI` branch on `caller == role-mapping[key]` near function entry.
- Misvalidated-input patterns: external-callable function whose payload encodes a contract / account address that is then passed to a privileged sub-call without validation.
- Post-event: a clean reproducible test case that demonstrates the missing check (audit-firm replay).

## Detection signals

- **Pre-deployment static analysis (OAK-M02):** unprotected privileged functions, missing `require` statements on critical inputs, deprecated function references, function selectors in deployed bytecode that lack expected role-gate dispatch sequences.
- **Symbolic execution / formal verification (OAK-M16):** stated invariants under all reachable call paths; particular attention to inter-contract privilege boundaries and upgradeable-contract initialisation paths.
- **Per-transaction monitoring** at deployed protocols: alert on calls to deprecated or rarely-used functions that are not part of normal user flows; alert on privileged-action calls (mint, withdraw, role-assignment) from EOAs or contracts not in the protocol's published authority set.
- **Post-upgrade smoke-test:** simulate the privileged function call from an unprivileged caller after every contract upgrade; expect failure, alert if the call succeeds.

## Real-world examples

- **Wormhole bridge — Ethereum ↔ Solana — 2022-02-02** — Loss \~\$325M (~120,000 wETH). The attacker exploited a deprecated signature-verification function on the Solana side of the bridge that did not validate guardian-account addresses, allowing the attacker to forge a validator action approval (VAA) calling `complete_wrapped` to mint approximately 120,000 wETH "out of thin air" on the Ethereum side. The protocol's missing-authorisation-check is the canonical T9.004 mechanistically; operationally the case is also a canonical OAK-T10.002 (Message-Verification Bypass) bridge incident and is cross-referenced there. Funds were replaced by Jump Crypto. See worked example at `examples/2022-02-wormhole.md`.
- **Poly Network — Ethereum / BSC / Polygon — 2021-08-10** — Loss \~\$611M (largely returned by the attacker post-event). The attacker exploited an inter-contract privilege-boundary violation: Poly Network's `EthCrossChainManager` contract had authority to invoke `EthCrossChainData.putCurEpochConPubKeyBytes()`, which set the public-key bytes of the cross-chain validator. By crafting a cross-chain message whose `_method` field re-targeted `EthCrossChainData` itself with the attacker's chosen pubkey, the attacker rotated the cross-chain validator's authority to themselves and authorised arbitrary withdrawals. The case is the textbook **inter-contract privilege-boundary** sub-pattern. Sources: `[chainalysispoly2021]`, `[kudelskipoly2021]`, `[elliptipoly2021]`.
- **Multichain — Multi-chain — 2023-07-06** — Loss \~\$126M. While the proximate cause was MPC-key loss (T10.001-shaped), the on-chain extraction surface was an access-control surface: the bridge's withdrawal function authorised any caller in possession of the threshold-signed message, and the off-chain MPC ceremony was the only access-control gate. The case is included as a **boundary case** between T10.001 (off-chain key compromise) and T9.004 (the on-chain consumption surface); OAK classifies it primarily under T10.001 with a T9.004 cross-reference. Sources: `[chainalysismultichain2023]`, `[halbornmultichain2023]`, `[dlnewsmultichain2023]`.
- **KiloEx — BSC / opBNB / Base — 2025-04-15** — Loss \~\$7M. The attacker called the externally-exposed `_setPrices()` function on KiloEx's `MinimalForwarder` contract directly; the function should have been internal or gated by a `onlyKeeper` modifier, but was reachable from any caller. The attacker set a manipulated oracle price, then opened and closed a leveraged position at the manipulated price for a clean extraction. The canonical **missing-modifier** sub-pattern. See worked example at `examples/2025-04-kiloex.md`. Sources: `[kiloexpostmortem2025]`, `[peckshieldkiloex2025]`, `[slowmistkiloex2025]`, `[blocksecsuikiloex2025]`.
- **Euler Finance — Ethereum — 2023-03-13** — Loss \~\$197M. The attacker exploited a missing solvency check in Euler's `donateToReserves` function: the function permitted donating collateral to the protocol's reserves without verifying that the donor's position remained solvent post-donation. The attacker used a flash loan (T9.002) to set up an artificial-debt scenario that became liquidatable after the donation, paying out at liquidation. The attacker subsequently returned the funds. See worked example at `examples/2023-03-euler-finance.md`. Sources: `[chainalysiseuler2023]`, `[halborneuler2023]`, `[blocksec2023euler]`, `[elliptipeuler2023]`, `[eulerlabs2023statement]`.

### Additional incidents

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1.
- Vendor coverage: pre-deployment audit firms (Halborn, Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, Certora for formal verification); deployed-contract monitoring services (Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender, BlockSec PhalconHQ).

## Mitigations

- **Protocols (OAK-M16):** require pre-deployment audit by an independent firm; consider formal verification for critical invariants; remove deprecated functions; instrument runtime invariants that pause the contract on violation.
- **Protocols (OAK-M02):** static-analysis gate at CI for unprotected privileged functions, missing `require` on critical inputs, and inter-contract privilege-boundary patterns; require zero new findings before deploying upgrades.
- **Protocols (OAK-M11):** rate-limiting and per-block caps on privileged actions to bound exploit payoff even when authorisation is bypassed (defence-in-depth).
- **Risk teams:** treat audit-pending and audit-not-public protocols as elevated risk; check that audited contract bytecode matches deployed bytecode (OAK-M01); prefer protocols with active bug-bounty programs.

## Citations

- `[zhou2023sok]` — academic taxonomy.
- `[owaspscstop10]` — Access Control Vulnerabilities (top category).
- `[chainalysispoly2021]`, `[kudelskipoly2021]`, `[elliptipoly2021]` — Poly Network Aug 2021.
- `[chainalysismultichain2023]`, `[halbornmultichain2023]`, `[dlnewsmultichain2023]` — Multichain Jul 2023.
- `[kiloexpostmortem2025]`, `[peckshieldkiloex2025]`, `[slowmistkiloex2025]`, `[blocksecsuikiloex2025]` — KiloEx Apr 2025.
- `[chainalysiseuler2023]`, `[halborneuler2023]`, `[blocksec2023euler]`, `[elliptipeuler2023]`, `[eulerlabs2023statement]` — Euler Mar 2023.
- See worked examples for Wormhole.

## Discussion

T9.004 is intentionally a broad category because the failure modes vary widely (missing role-gate vs missing input validation vs missing invariant). Sub-Techniques may be split out in v0.x updates as specific sub-patterns reach independent characterisation maturity:

- T9.004.001 — Missing-modifier (KiloEx) — single-line code defect, found by static analysis.
- T9.004.002 — Misvalidated-authorisation / guardian-bypass (Wormhole) — boundary with T10.002 for bridge cases.
- T9.004.003 — Inter-contract privilege-boundary violation (Poly Network) — design-level flaw, found by formal verification.
- T9.004.004 — Missing-invariant / solvency-check (Euler) — pair with T9.002 in chained exploits.

Attribution-strength notes: KiloEx and Euler (high — full forensic write-ups, reproducible test cases); Wormhole (high — Halborn / Chainalysis post-mortem, Jump remediation public); Poly Network (medium-high — attacker engaged publicly post-event, mechanics widely documented but attacker identity remains pseudonymous); Multichain (low — internal-vs-external attribution disputed).

Wormhole is included as the v0.1 canonical example because the missing-authorisation-check is unambiguous from public technical write-ups, the dollar loss is large, and the post-mortem published by Halborn and Chainalysis is sufficient for contributors to understand the failure mode without us reproducing exploit specifics here. The KiloEx case is included specifically because it post-dates the broad adoption of static-analysis CI in DeFi and demonstrates the surface persists when the static-analysis pass is run on the wrong artifact (the contract was deployed but the analysis gate did not block it).
docs.techniques/T9.005-reentrancy.md:
# OAK-T9.005 — Reentrancy

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit)
**Maturity:** stable
**Chains:** EVM (primary); EVM-compatible L2s; conceptually applicable to any chain whose execution model allows external calls before state finalisation
**First documented:** 2016 (The DAO); modern hook-based and cross-protocol variants 2020+
**Aliases:** "recursive call attack", "callback reentry", "cross-function reentrancy", "cross-protocol reentrancy", "read-only reentrancy", "ERC-777 hook reentrancy", "ERC-721/1155 receive-hook reentrancy"

## Description

A class of smart-contract exploits in which the target contract performs an external call to an attacker-controlled address before its own internal state has been brought into a consistent post-action state, and the attacker's contract uses control of the execution flow during that external call to re-enter the target — calling back into the same function, a sibling function on the same contract, or a related function on a peer protocol — while the target's storage still reflects the pre-action snapshot. The attacker thereby exercises a state transition (typically a withdrawal, a debt-accounting update, or a balance read) against stale state, repeatedly, and extracts more value than the protocol intends to release. From a defender's perspective, T9.005 is identifiable through the call-graph shape: a single external transaction whose internal call trace contains repeated re-entries into the same function selector (or into a tightly-coupled set of selectors on the same or related contracts) before the originating call returns. `[zhou2023sok]` and `[owaspscstop10]` both place reentrancy among the canonical smart-contract vulnerability classes; OWASP SC Top 10 lists Reentrancy explicitly as a top-tier category through 2025/2026.

OAK groups several sub-patterns under T9.005:

- **Single-function (canonical) reentrancy** — the function makes an external call before updating its own storage; the attacker re-enters the same function. This is the historical DAO pattern and is largely closed by the now-standard checks-effects-interactions discipline and OpenZeppelin's `ReentrancyGuard`.
- **Cross-function reentrancy** — function A makes the external call; the attacker re-enters function B, which reads or mutates state that A had not yet finalised. A per-function `nonReentrant` modifier does not block this if B is not gated by the same lock.
- **Cross-protocol (cross-contract) reentrancy** — the attacker re-enters a *different* protocol that reads stale state from the target (e.g., reads a balance, share-price, or LP-token accounting that the target has not yet updated). This is the dominant modern variant and is frequently chained with T9.002 (Flash-Loan-Enabled Exploit) to acquire the capital required to make the inconsistent intermediate state economically meaningful.
- **Hook-based reentrancy** — the external call is implicit in a token-standard hook: ERC-777 `tokensReceived`, ERC-721 `onERC721Received`, ERC-1155 `onERC1155Received` / `onERC1155BatchReceived`. The target may not realise it has surrendered execution flow, because the call site looks like a token transfer rather than an explicit `call`.
- **Read-only reentrancy** — the attacker re-enters a `view` function on the target during a state-mid-update window; a third protocol that reads that view-function output makes a decision against stale data. No state on the target is mutated by the re-entry itself; the loss is realised on the third protocol.

## Observed indicators

- Single-transaction extraction whose internal call trace shows repeated invocations of the same function selector on the same contract, or a fan-out of calls into related selectors on the same contract before the outer call returns.
- Token-transfer call sites (ERC-777, ERC-721, ERC-1155) that hand control to a user-supplied receiver address before the sender contract has finalised its accounting.
- Third-protocol settlement, liquidation, or pricing decision taken against a view-function reading from a contract that is mid-execution in the same transaction.
- Post-event: a clean reproducible test case that demonstrates the reentry path (audit-firm replay against a forked block).

## Detection signals

- Pre-deployment: static analysis for external calls preceding storage writes (Slither's `reentrancy-eth`, `reentrancy-no-eth`, `reentrancy-benign` detectors; MythX, Securify); flagging of any `call` / `transfer` / token-hook-triggering operation that is not preceded by the relevant state update.
- Pre-deployment: explicit per-function review of cross-function and cross-contract state dependencies; absence of `ReentrancyGuard` on functions that share a state surface.
- Pre-deployment: formal verification of "no external call observes pre-finalised state" as a stated invariant under all reachable call paths.
- Per-transaction monitoring at deployed protocols: alert on transactions whose internal call trace shows the target contract being re-entered (same `to` address appearing multiple times within a single outer call) before the outer call returns.
- Cross-protocol monitoring: alert on view-function reads from a contract whose storage is mid-update in the same transaction (read-only reentrancy signature).
- **Read-only reentrancy runtime signal:** cross-contract `view` / `staticcall` reads against a contract that is currently mid-execution (the call frame for that contract has not yet returned), where the read returns data inconsistent with the post-write state the contract will commit on frame exit. Canonical worked example: the Curve July 2023 incident family and the Market.xyz consumer that priced LP-token reads off Curve pools whose storage was mid-update during the malformed-`@nonreentrant`-lock window — the `view` function returned a transient inconsistent value to a third protocol that took an economic action against it. Detector implementation: per-transaction call-trace monitoring that flags `staticcall` to address X while X's outermost frame in the same transaction has not yet returned. <!-- TODO: a dedicated curve2023readonlyreentrancy citation key does not yet exist in citations.bib; the upstream reentrancy bug is covered by `[vyperpostmortem2023]`, `[curvepostmortem2023]`, `[halborncurvevyper2023]`, `[chaofantvyper2023]`, `[chainalysiscurvevyper2023]`, but the downstream Market.xyz read-only-reentrancy consumer surface needs its own primary source; add the dedicated key once selected. -->

## Real-world examples

- **The DAO — Ethereum — 2016-06-17** — Loss \~\$60M (~3.6M ETH at then-prevailing prices). The DAO's `splitDAO` / withdrawal path performed an external call to the recipient before zeroing the recipient's internal balance; the recipient contract re-entered the same path, draining the pool against repeatedly-stale state. The incident is the historical origin of the reentrancy class and prompted the contentious Ethereum hard fork that produced the Ethereum / Ethereum Classic split. Cited as the canonical reference incident for T9.005.
- **Lendf.Me — Ethereum — 2020-04-19** — Loss \~\$25M. The protocol accepted imBTC, an ERC-777-style token whose `tokensReceived` hook handed execution to the depositor before Lendf.Me finalised its internal accounting; the attacker used the hook to re-enter the deposit / borrow path against stale balance state. The case is the canonical hook-based reentrancy reference and is widely cited as the first large-loss ERC-777-hook incident.
- **Cream Finance (Iron Bank) — Ethereum — 2021-10-27** — Loss \~\$130M+. Cross-protocol reentrancy combined with a flash loan (T9.002): the attacker used flash-loaned capital to interact with Cream's collateral / borrow accounting through an external integration whose callback re-entered Cream while its internal state was mid-update. The case is the canonical modern reference for cross-protocol reentrancy chained with flash loans, and is the principal reason simple per-function `ReentrancyGuard` is no longer considered sufficient on its own.
- **Cohort context** — `[owaspscstop10]` (Reentrancy category) and `[zhou2023sok]` taxonomy both document that reentrancy continues to surface as an incident class through 2022–2024, predominantly in cross-function, cross-protocol, and hook-based forms rather than the canonical 2016-style single-function pattern.

### Additional incidents

- [`examples/2016-06-the-dao.md`](../examples/2016-06-the-dao.md) — The DAO reentrancy exploit
- [`examples/2020-04-lendf-me.md`](../examples/2020-04-lendf-me.md) — Lendf.me reentrancy via ERC-777 callback chain
- [`examples/2020-11-akropolis.md`](../examples/2020-11-akropolis.md) — Akropolis Delphi flash-loan-funded reentrancy
- [`examples/2020-11-origin-dollar.md`](../examples/2020-11-origin-dollar.md) — Origin Dollar (OUSD) flash-loan-funded reentrancy
- [`examples/2023-03-paraspace.md`](../examples/2023-03-paraspace.md) — ParaSpace whitehat reentrancy rescue by BlockSec
- [`examples/2023-04-hundred-finance.md`](../examples/2023-04-hundred-finance.md) — Hundred Finance empty-market rounding-error exploit
- [`examples/2023-04-sentiment.md`](../examples/2023-04-sentiment.md) — Sentiment Protocol read-only reentrancy via Balancer LP integration
- [`examples/2023-07-curve-vyper.md`](../examples/2023-07-curve-vyper.md) — Curve Finance Vyper compiler-level reentrancy
- [`examples/2023-08-balancer-v2.md`](../examples/2023-08-balancer-v2.md) — Balancer V2 Boosted Pools exploitation
- [`examples/2023-11-kyberswap.md`](../examples/2023-11-kyberswap.md) — KyberSwap Elastic tick-state-manipulation exploit
- [`examples/2024-05-sonne-finance.md`](../examples/2024-05-sonne-finance.md) — Sonne Finance empty-market rounding-error exploit
- [`examples/2024-06-velocore.md`](../examples/2024-06-velocore.md) — Velocore CPMM fee-rate underflow exploit
- [`examples/2024-09-onyx.md`](../examples/2024-09-onyx.md) — Onyx Protocol empty-market rounding exploit
- [`examples/2024-09-penpie.md`](../examples/2024-09-penpie.md) — Penpie Finance reward-claim exploit
- [`examples/2025-02-zklend.md`](../examples/2025-02-zklend.md) — zkLend empty-market rounding-error exploit
- [`examples/2025-03-1inch-resolver.md`](../examples/2025-03-1inch-resolver.md) — 1inch resolver-contract patch-not-propagated exploit
- [`examples/2025-05-cetus.md`](../examples/2025-05-cetus.md) — Cetus Protocol concentrated-liquidity overflow exploit
- [`examples/2025-06-resupply-finance.md`](../examples/2025-06-resupply-finance.md) — Resupply Finance wstUSR empty-market donation exploit
- [`examples/2025-07-gmx-v1.md`](../examples/2025-07-gmx-v1.md) — GMX V1 GLP global-short-tracking exploit
- [`examples/2025-11-balancer-v2.md`](../examples/2025-11-balancer-v2.md) — Balancer V2 ComposableStablePool rounding-error exploit

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. T9.005 is a pre-deployment / contract-design control surface and is outside MG's runtime risk-engine scope; OAK lists it for completeness of the T9 taxonomy.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: pre-deployment static analysers (Slither, MythX, Securify, Mythril); audit firms (Halborn, Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, ConsenSys Diligence); formal verification (Certora, Runtime Verification); deployed-contract monitoring (Forta, OpenZeppelin Defender, BlockSec PhalconHQ) for runtime call-trace patterns.

## Mitigations

- **Audit firms:** require checks-effects-interactions discipline as a structural review item; flag any external call preceding state finalisation; flag absence of `ReentrancyGuard` on functions sharing a state surface; treat ERC-777 / ERC-721 / ERC-1155 hook surfaces as implicit external calls and audit them as such.
- **Protocols:** adopt OpenZeppelin `ReentrancyGuard` (or equivalent) as a baseline; apply the lock across the *set* of functions that share state, not per-function; finalise state writes before external calls (checks-effects-interactions); avoid integrating tokens with reentrancy-relevant hooks (ERC-777, certain ERC-721/1155) in lending-style accounting paths without explicit hook-aware design; treat view functions read by third protocols as part of the reentrancy surface (read-only reentrancy mitigation).
- **Risk teams:** maintain per-protocol inventories of (a) which contracts use `ReentrancyGuard` and at what granularity, (b) which protocols accept ERC-777-style hooked tokens in collateral or accounting paths, (c) which view functions are consumed by third protocols. Treat protocols whose accounting paths integrate hooked tokens without explicit hook-aware design as elevated risk; treat any cross-protocol integration whose price / balance reads happen mid-transaction as an elevated read-only-reentrancy surface.

## Citations

- `[zhou2023sok]` — academic taxonomy.
- `[owaspscstop10]` — Reentrancy category in OWASP SC Top 10.
- `[daoreentrancy2016retrospective]` — see `citations.bib` (canonical DAO retrospective).

## Discussion

T9.005 is marked Maturity = stable rather than historical because the underlying failure mode — surrendering execution flow before finalising state — recurs in modern variants that the canonical mitigation does not fully address. OpenZeppelin's `ReentrancyGuard` (widely adopted post-2019) substantially closes the 2016-style single-function pattern, and pre-deployment static analysis routinely catches it; consequently, post-2021 reentrancy incidents are dominated by (i) cross-function reentrancy where the per-function lock did not span the shared-state surface, (ii) cross-protocol reentrancy where the attacker re-enters a *peer* protocol that reads stale state, frequently chained with T9.002 to acquire flash-loaned capital, (iii) hook-based reentrancy where the external call is implicit in a token-standard receive hook the protocol did not treat as an external call, and (iv) read-only reentrancy where no state mutation occurs in the re-entry but a third protocol's view-function read is the loss surface.

The Cream Finance October 2021 case is the v0.1 canonical example of the modern variant because it combines the cross-protocol pattern with flash-loan-enabled capital, making the T9.002 cross-reference unambiguous; The DAO is retained as the historical reference for the class origin and to ground the Ethereum-Classic fork context that newer contributors may not have lived through. Sub-Techniques may be split out in v0.x updates if the cross-protocol and read-only sub-patterns reach independent characterisation maturity (notably, read-only reentrancy is increasingly cited as a distinct sub-class in 2023–2025 audit-firm literature and may warrant its own technique ID).
docs.techniques/T9.006-subjective-oracle-reso:
# OAK-T9.006 — Subjective-Oracle Resolution Manipulation

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (Polygon-resident Polymarket / Ethereum-resident UMA DVM canonical at v0.1; cross-chain analogues across Kleros, Reality.eth, Augur REP)
**First documented:** Polymarket UMA whale-vote-capture March 2025; cohort scale-out 2025–2026 across Polymarket-class subjective-oracle prediction markets
**Aliases:** "subjective oracle attack", "DVM vote capture", "resolution-spec manipulation", "prediction-market oracle attack", "non-numeric oracle manipulation"

## Description

Smart-contract attacks against oracle systems that adjudicate **natural-language claims** rather than **numeric price feeds**. Distinct from T9.001 (price-feed oracle manipulation) at the artefact level: the "oracle" is a token-weighted vote over a natural-language proposition (UMA DVM, Kleros, Reality.eth, Augur REP), not a numeric reading of a market price. The mitigations OAK lists for T9.001 (TWAP, multi-venue feeds, deviation halts — OAK-M09) are mechanical and apply to numerical price feeds; **none of them apply to subjective-oracle resolution**.

Four properties make subjective-oracle systems qualitatively different from price-feed oracles:

1. **The oracle adjudicates language, not numbers.** "Agrees to a deal" / "wearing a suit" has no TWAP. Spec-ambiguity is the attack surface; the spec itself is the vulnerability.
2. **Oracle market cap < adjudicated volume.** UMA's market cap was approximately \$95M during the 2025–2026 dispute window; subsequent disputed markets (Zelenskyy-suit at \$237M) had volume larger than the oracle token's market cap. The economic cost of corrupting the vote is a fraction of the prize. This is a stable disequilibrium that worsens as the host platform scales.
3. **Voter–bettor identity overlap.** UMA tokenholders are not a separate pool from market participants; nothing prevents a whale from voting on a market they hold a position in. Classical oracle-defence assumptions (voter neutrality) are structurally absent.
4. **The resolution source itself is attackable off-chain.** When a market resolves on a real-world claim ("a deal was announced"), the resolution input — news headlines, official statements — can be manipulated upstream of the oracle.

T9.006 has **four structurally-distinct sub-patterns**, each anchored to one or more Polymarket-cohort worked examples at v0.1:

- **T9.006.001 — DVM vote capture by economically-interested holder.** Direct corruption of the oracle layer: a whale or coordinated tokenholder cohort with positions in the adjudicated market votes their holdings to drive resolution to their preferred outcome. Canonical: Ukraine mineral deal market (March 2025, ~\$7M, single whale ~25% of vote).
- **T9.006.002 — Resolution-spec ambiguity exploitation.** Attack on the spec layer: the natural-language claim is interpretively underdetermined; voters resolve against the bettor-majority's reasonable interpretation, citing spec ambiguity. Canonical: Zelenskyy-suit market (July 2025, ~\$237M).
- **T9.006.003 — Off-chain resolution-source coercion.** Attack on the resolution-input layer: a third-party off-chain reporter / journalist whose reporting is the resolution input is pressured (threats, extortion) post-publication to alter or suppress their account. Canonical: Times of Israel / Emanuel Fabian case, Iran-strike market (March 2026). **First publicly documented case of physical coercion of a human reporter as an oracle attack vector in crypto.**
- **T9.006.004 — Operational-insider trading on subjective-resolution prediction markets.** Attack on the **information-symmetry assumption between bettors**: a participant with classified-information access to (and operational involvement in) the underlying real-world event trades on that knowledge before public disclosure. The bettor is not just *informed* about the outcome — they are one of the *causal actors producing it*. Canonical: Van Dyke / Venezuela / Operation Absolute Resolve (DOJ + CFTC, January–April 2026); IDF reservist / Iran-strike timing (Tel Aviv District Court, February 2026).

T9.006 is **structurally distinct** from adjacent Techniques:

- **T9.001 (Oracle Price Manipulation)** — covers numeric price-feed oracle manipulation. Mechanical mitigations (TWAP, deviation halts, multi-venue feeds) apply. None apply to T9.006.
