- **T9.003 (Governance Attack)** — adjacent at the DVM-vote-capture layer (T9.006.001) but distinct in that T9.003 attacks protocol-governance decisions over protocol parameters / treasury / upgrades, while T9.006 attacks oracle-resolution decisions over external real-world facts. The voting-pool-vs-prize-pool disequilibrium that makes T9.006 structurally exploitable is a property specific to subjective-oracle adjudication.

## Observed indicators

- Sharp resolution-window probability movements on the disputed market (e.g., YES surge from <10% to 100% in the resolution window) inconsistent with the underlying real-world signal.
- Voter–position correlation: UMA voters with material holdings in the adjudicated market, particularly whale-scale positions.
- Resolution-spec language with unmachine-checkable predicates (interpretive language, fashion / wardrobe claims, "credible reporting consensus"-class sources).
- Single-source resolution input where a single journalist / single publication is the dominant resolution input.
- Newly-created accounts with sharp longshot wins in narrow time windows on markets gated by classified events (the operational-insider sub-pattern).
- Public characterisations from the host platform (Polymarket, Augur, Kleros) that the resolution was "incorrect" / "unprecedented governance attack" / "captured" — the platform's own admissions are first-class detection signals at the cohort level.

## Detection signals

- **Pre-event (market-design layer):** flag markets with unmachine-checkable resolution criteria; flag markets whose volume exceeds a fraction (e.g., 50%) of the oracle token's market cap as elevated dispute risk; flag markets with single-source resolution inputs.
- **At-event (vote-capture layer, .001):** voter-position-correlation analysis across UMA / Kleros / Reality.eth / Augur REP votes; cohort-attribution of large UMA holders to bettor positions.
- **At-event (resolution-spec layer, .002):** divergence between media-consensus-implied resolution and oracle-resolution outcome.
- **At-event (off-chain coercion layer, .003):** monitoring of high-volume markets where reporting cohorts are narrow; bettor-to-resolution-source social-media interaction patterns; preserved-evidence threat-monitoring on identified resolution sources.
- **At-event (operational-insider layer, .004):** account-age-vs-first-bet correlation on markets gated by classified events; sharp longshot wins concentrated in newly-created accounts; KYC linkage at the off-ramp (Kraken / brokerage account) plus account-creation metadata; counter-intelligence cross-reference on operationally-involved actors.
- **Cohort-level baseline (operational-insider, .004):** ACDC analytics published baseline rates — longshots win ~14% baseline cohort rate, ~51.8% in military / defense markets — establishing what counts as anomaly.

## Real-world examples

- **Polymarket UMA — Ukraine mineral deal market — Polygon — 2025-03 (canonical T9.006.001 case)** — \~\$7M in market volume resolved against the underlying real-world outcome. A single UMA whale cast ~5M UMA tokens via 3 wallets (~25% of resolution vote) driving YES outcome despite no real-world deal announcement. Polymarket publicly characterised the event as an "unprecedented governance attack" but cited UMA's decentralised structure to refuse refunds. First documented case of a Polymarket market resolved against its stated rules through coordinated UMA tokenholder voting. See [`examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md`](../examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md). Adjacent T9.006.001 cohort cases (UFO declassification December 2025, ~\$16M; Zelenskyy-suit market alongside its primary T9.006.002 framing at ~\$237M) reinforce the sub-pattern.
- **Polymarket Zelenskyy-suit market — Polygon — 2025-07 (canonical T9.006.002 case)** — \~\$237M in disputed market volume (CoinDesk secondary characterisation \~\$160M). Top 10 UMA voters held ~6.5M UMA (~30% of average vote). UMA market cap during the dispute window (~\$95M) was less than half the disputed market's volume — the **oracle was economically smaller than a single market it adjudicated**. Despite multiple credible outlets reporting Zelenskyy in a suit at NATO June 24, UMA voters resolved NO on July 1 citing lack of "credible reporting consensus." See [`examples/2025-07-polymarket-zelenskyy-suit.md`](../examples/2025-07-polymarket-zelenskyy-suit.md).
- **Polymarket Iran-strike market / journalist coercion — Polygon — 2026-03 (canonical T9.006.003 case)** — \~\$900,000 in bettor-stake forfeiture; the economically operative event is the **threats issued against Times of Israel correspondent Emanuel Fabian** demanding he rewrite his report to flip the market resolution. First publicly documented case in crypto-prediction-market history of physical coercion of an off-chain human reporter as an attack vector against the resolution layer. Threats documented in Fabian's first-person Times of Israel account; corroborated by Washington Post and Haaretz. Journalist did not change reporting; market resolved per his published account; perpetrators not publicly identified or prosecuted at v0.1. See [`examples/2026-03-polymarket-iran-strike-journalist-coercion.md`](../examples/2026-03-polymarket-iran-strike-journalist-coercion.md).
- **Polymarket / Van Dyke / Venezuela / Operation Absolute Resolve — Polygon — 2025-12 (operational decision) → 2026-01 (real-world execution) → 2026-04 (DOJ indictment) (canonical T9.006.004 case)** — US Army Special Forces master sergeant Gannon Ken Van Dyke, operationally involved in the planning and execution of Operation Absolute Resolve (the Maduro capture operation), opened a Polymarket account December 26, 2025, placed ~13 bets totalling ~\$33,034 on YES sides of Venezuela / Maduro markets December 27 – January 2, 2026. The bets resolved YES January 3–4, generating ~\$404K profit. DOJ SDNY indictment April 2026; CFTC parallel civil action; Kalshi blocked the same individual on its venue. **First publicly documented case in crypto-prediction-market history** in which a bettor with direct operational involvement in the underlying real-world event has been criminally charged for trading on classified knowledge. See [`examples/2026-01-polymarket-venezuela-maduro-soldier-insider.md`](../examples/2026-01-polymarket-venezuela-maduro-soldier-insider.md).
- **Polymarket / IDF reservist / Iran-strike timing — Polygon — 2025-06 (real-world execution) → 2026-02 (Israeli indictment) (T9.006.004 cohort case)** — Israeli Air Force major (reservist) briefed civilian co-defendant when warplanes were already en route to Iranian targets; civilian placed Polymarket bets on the timing of Israel's June 2025 opening strike. Pair extracted ~\$162,663 in winnings, agreed to split evenly. Tel Aviv District Court indictment February 2026; charges include severe security offences, bribery, obstruction of justice. Establishes the T9.006.004 sub-pattern as multi-jurisdictional (US + IL) within a four-month window. See [`examples/2026-02-polymarket-iran-strike-idf-reservist-insider.md`](../examples/2026-02-polymarket-iran-strike-idf-reservist-insider.md).

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — coverage **gap** at v0.1. The cohort-level detection surface (UMA whale-vote analysis; account-age-vs-first-bet correlation; sharp-longshot-win analysis) is closer to off-chain analytics than MG's runtime risk-engine scope. v0.x integration target via prediction-market-specific monitoring extensions.
- Vendor coverage outside MG: Polymarket internal surveillance partnership with Chainalysis (announced April 2026); ACDC analytics for cohort-rate baselines; Kalshi cross-venue blocklist coordination; investigative-journalism cross-reference (CoinDesk, The Block, Decrypt, NPR).

## Mitigations

- **Market-design (highest-leverage):** machine-checkable resolution criteria. Markets resolving on cryptographically-verifiable inputs (block-height events, on-chain state, signed oracle attestations) are not exposed to T9.006.001 / T9.006.002. Markets resolving on language are.
- **Oracle-design:** oracle-weight floor relative to adjudicated volume. A UMA-class oracle should refuse to adjudicate markets whose volume exceeds some fraction of the oracle token's market cap. This is a structural change to UMA, not a Polymarket-side mitigation.
- **Voter-position-disclosure with conflict-of-interest slashing:** voters with positions in the adjudicated market should be excluded or slashable. UMA does not currently enforce this.
- **Resolution-source multiplicity:** markets requiring two or more independently-published sources for resolution, with no single human reporter's account being load-bearing. Market-design mitigation, not platform-design mitigation.
- **Bettor-position disclosure to potential resolution sources:** journalists whose reporting is cited in a resolution spec should be informed that their account may have economic influence. Operationally awkward but the only way to extend traditional journalistic threat-modelling into the crypto-prediction-market context.
- **Platform-level threat-monitoring of high-volume markets where reporting cohorts are narrow:** identify markets where a single journalist or single publication is the dominant resolution input; warrant heightened monitoring of bettor behaviour around the resolution window.
- **Operational-insider-detection:** account-age-vs-first-bet correlation on markets gated by classified events; sharp-longshot-win analysis; KYC linkage at the off-ramp; counter-intelligence cross-reference on operationally-involved actors. Polymarket / Chainalysis surveillance partnership operationalised this in April 2026.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1: gap. v0.x candidates: "OAK-M-x machine-checkable-resolution-criteria invariant"; "OAK-M-x oracle-weight-floor-vs-adjudicated-volume threshold"; "OAK-M-x voter-position-conflict-of-interest slashing"; "OAK-M-x resolution-source-multiplicity invariant"; "OAK-M-x operational-insider-detection cohort surveillance".

## Citations

- See worked examples for primary citations. Ukraine mineral deal: CoinDesk, The Block, CoinMarketCap Academy, Webopedia, Orochi Network, UMA blog. Zelenskyy-suit: Decrypt, CoinDesk, The Defiant. Journalist coercion: Times of Israel (Fabian first-person), Washington Post, Haaretz. Van Dyke / Venezuela: DOJ SDNY indictment, CFTC complaint, NPR, CoinDesk. IDF reservist / Iran-strike timing: Tel Aviv District Court charging documents; Israeli and international media February 2026 reports.

## Discussion

T9.006 occupies a structurally distinct slot in OAK's T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit) Tactic. T9.001 (price-feed oracle manipulation — Mango Markets, BonqDAO, Vee Finance) is a *liquidity attack*: the attacker borrows or accumulates capital, moves a thin pool, and books the price reading. Mitigations are mechanical (TWAP, multi-venue feeds, deviation halts — OAK-M09). T9.006 has **no mechanical mitigation** because the oracle adjudicates language rather than numbers.

The four-sub-pattern split (.001 vote-capture / .002 spec-ambiguity / .003 off-chain-coercion / .004 operational-insider) is the analytically distinctive feature of the Technique. Each sub-pattern attacks a structurally different layer of the subjective-oracle stack:

- **.001** attacks the oracle layer (DVM vote)
- **.002** attacks the spec layer (natural-language interpretation)
- **.003** attacks the resolution-input layer (off-chain reporter)
- **.004** attacks the information-symmetry assumption between bettors (insider with classified knowledge of the underlying event)

The Van Dyke (.004) case is structurally novel because the bettor is one of the **causal actors producing the outcome** — this crosses from classical insider trading (informational asymmetry) into a class structurally adjacent to market-manipulation-via-action. The on-chain settlement substrate makes this attack class qualitatively different from traditional Section 10(b)/Rule 10b-5 insider trading: position and timing are publicly inspectable; wallet-attribution via off-ramp KYC is the load-bearing forensic step; geofencing is partial; the market itself surfaces the anomaly via account-age-vs-first-bet correlation.

**Note on T9.006.004 framing:** an earlier `TAXONOMY-GAPS.md` framing of T9.006.004 used a different sub-pattern (platform-override of oracle outcome — Barron Trump / DJT memecoin market). At v0.1 promotion the existing example anchors (Van Dyke; IDF reservist) self-categorise as T9.006.004 with the operational-insider definition; the platform-override sub-pattern lacks a worked-example anchor at v0.1 and is held as a v0.x candidate for a separate sub-Technique slot. Contributors should treat T9.006.004 as the operational-insider sub-pattern at v0.1 freeze and propose a separate ID for the platform-override sub-pattern at v0.x.

The **Polymarket cohort 2025–2026 is structurally the cohort-level anchor for T9.006**. The host platform (Polymarket on Polygon) plus the oracle layer (UMA DVM on Ethereum) is the substrate where all four sub-patterns are anchored at v0.1. Cross-platform analogues (Augur REP-token resolution, Kleros jurors, Reality.eth, Polymarket competitor venues) are pipeline anchor candidates for v0.x as those platforms scale to material volume.

The case generalises beyond crypto. Subjective-oracle prediction markets are economically equivalent to over-the-counter contingency contracts whose settlement criterion is a natural-language proposition. The same attack surface exists in any system where (a) the resolution input is non-numeric, (b) the resolution arbitrator is a token-weighted vote, and (c) the voting-pool economic weight is smaller than the adjudicated-market economic weight. T9.006 contributors should treat this as the structural framing and ensure the Technique's framing generalises beyond the Polymarket-cohort anchor.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once each sub-Technique carries multiple independent worked-example anchors. Currently .001 and .002 are anchored at the Polymarket-Ukraine and Polymarket-Zelenskyy cases respectively (with adjacent UFO-declassification and US-Iran ceasefire cases tracked as pipeline anchors); .003 is anchored at the Fabian / Iran-strike case alone; .004 is anchored at Van Dyke / Venezuela and IDF reservist / Iran-strike, establishing multi-jurisdictional confirmation but on a single host-platform substrate (Polymarket). The `emerging` classification at v0.1 reflects that the threat-class characterisation is mature and the per-incident anchor cohort is forming; `stable` will follow once each sub-pattern has multi-jurisdictional, multi-platform anchor coverage.
docs.techniques/T9.006.001-dvm-vote-capture.m:
# OAK-T9.006.001 — DVM Vote Capture by Economically-Interested Holder

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit); sub-Technique of OAK-T9.006 (Subjective-Oracle Resolution Manipulation)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (Polymarket on Polygon / UMA DVM on Ethereum canonical at v0.1)
**First documented:** 2025-03 (Polymarket Ukraine mineral deal)
**Aliases:** "UMA whale vote capture", "DVM governance attack", "oracle-vote corruption"

## Description

Direct corruption of the subjective-oracle layer by a tokenholder cohort with material positions in the adjudicated market. A whale or coordinated tokenholder group votes their UMA / Kleros / Reality.eth / Augur REP holdings to drive resolution to the outcome that maximises their bettor-side payoff, against the underlying real-world signal.

The structural feature is the **voter–bettor identity overlap**: subjective-oracle protocols typically permit any tokenholder to vote on any market regardless of whether they hold positions in that market. Classical oracle-defence assumptions (voter neutrality) are structurally absent.

The **stable disequilibrium** between oracle market cap and adjudicated market volume is the load-bearing substrate. UMA's market cap was approximately \$95M during the canonical 2025–2026 dispute window; subsequent disputed markets (Zelenskyy-suit at \$237M) had volume larger than the oracle token's market cap. The economic cost of corrupting the vote is a fraction of the prize.

Distinct from sibling sub-Techniques:

- **T9.006.002 (Resolution-Spec Ambiguity Exploitation)** — attacks the spec layer rather than the oracle layer. Voters in T9.006.002 may vote consistent with one reasonable interpretation of the spec; T9.006.001 voters vote *against* the resolution criteria entirely.
- **T9.006.003 (Off-chain Resolution-Source Coercion)** — attacks the resolution-input layer (the off-chain reporter); T9.006.001 attacks the oracle layer directly.
- **T9.006.004 (Operational-Insider Trading)** — attacks the information-symmetry assumption between bettors; T9.006.001 attacks the oracle's resolution decision.

Distinct from **T9.003 (Governance Attack)** at the artefact level: T9.003 covers protocol-governance over parameters / treasury / upgrades; T9.006.001 covers oracle-resolution governance over external real-world facts.

## Observed indicators

- Sharp resolution-window probability movement (e.g., YES surge from <10% to 100%) inconsistent with the underlying real-world signal.
- Voter-position-correlation: top-N UMA / Kleros / REP voters with material positions in the adjudicated market.
- Single-whale or small-cluster tokenholder concentration capturing a meaningful fraction (>20%) of resolution vote weight.
- Public characterisation from the host platform that the resolution was "incorrect" / "unprecedented governance attack" — the platform's own admissions are first-class detection signals.

## Detection signals

- Voter-position correlation analysis across the UMA DVM (or Kleros / Reality.eth / Augur REP) vote logs against bettor-side position holdings on the adjudicated market.
- Single-whale or cluster-attribution analysis of large UMA holders during the resolution window.
- Cohort-level monitoring of disputed-market volume against UMA market cap as a structural threshold.
- Cross-reference with Polymarket / host-platform public statements regarding disputed resolutions.

## Real-world examples

- **Polymarket UMA — Ukraine mineral deal market — 2025-03 (canonical T9.006.001 case)** — \~\$7M in market volume resolved against the underlying real-world outcome. Single UMA whale cast ~5M UMA tokens via 3 wallets (~25% of resolution vote) driving YES outcome despite no real-world deal announcement. Polymarket publicly characterised the event as an "unprecedented governance attack." See [`examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md`](../examples/2025-03-polymarket-uma-ukraine-mineral-deal.md).
- **Adjacent T9.006.001 cohort cases** (per parent T9.006 framing): UFO declassification market (December 2025, ~\$16M); Zelenskyy-suit market shows T9.006.001 sub-pattern alongside its primary T9.006.002 framing (top 10 UMA voters held ~6.5M UMA, ~30% of average vote, with UMA market cap ~\$95M < disputed market volume \$237M).

## Reference implementations

- See parent technique `T9.006-subjective-oracle-resolution-manipulation.md`. T9.006.001-specific tooling gap at v0.1.

## Mitigations

- **Voter-position-disclosure with conflict-of-interest slashing:** voters with positions in the adjudicated market should be excluded or slashable. UMA does not currently enforce this. Highest-leverage mitigation for T9.006.001.
- **Oracle-weight floor relative to adjudicated volume:** UMA-class oracle should refuse to adjudicate markets whose volume exceeds some fraction of the oracle token's market cap. Structural change to UMA, not Polymarket-side mitigation.
- **Machine-checkable resolution criteria** (parent-Technique mitigation): markets resolving on cryptographically-verifiable inputs are not exposed to T9.006.001.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1: gap. See parent T9.006 file for v0.x mitigation candidates.

## Citations

See worked example for primary citations. Ukraine mineral deal: CoinDesk, The Block, CoinMarketCap Academy, Webopedia, Orochi Network, UMA blog.

## Discussion

T9.006.001 is the canonical *first* of the now-documented Polymarket cohort of subjective-oracle resolution-manipulation cases. The Ukraine mineral deal case is structurally significant because Polymarket itself publicly conceded the resolution was "incorrect" and described the event as an "unprecedented governance attack" — but, citing UMA's decentralised structure, refused refunds. This is the strongest available admission that the oracle layer is not mechanically defensible against economically-rational voters.

The voting-pool-vs-prize-pool disequilibrium is a **stable property** of any subjective oracle adjudicating economically-meaningful prediction markets. The larger Polymarket grows, the more profitable each individual oracle-corruption opportunity becomes, while the cost of acquiring veto-equivalent UMA voting power stays bounded by UMA's market cap. This is qualitatively different from price-oracle manipulation (T9.001), where the manipulation cost scales with pool depth and the attacker must continuously fund a position. T9.006.001 manipulation is a one-time governance vote with a long-tail bounty.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once T9.006.001 carries multiple independent worked-example anchors beyond the canonical Ukraine mineral deal and adjacent Zelenskyy-suit / UFO-declassification cohort cases. The pipeline includes any 2026+ disputed market where voter-position correlation analysis reproduces the cohort signature.
docs.techniques/T9.006.002-resolution-spec-am:
# OAK-T9.006.002 — Resolution-Spec Ambiguity Exploitation

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit); sub-Technique of OAK-T9.006 (Subjective-Oracle Resolution Manipulation)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (Polymarket on Polygon / UMA DVM on Ethereum canonical at v0.1)
**First documented:** 2025-07 (Polymarket Zelenskyy-suit market)
**Aliases:** "spec-ambiguity attack", "natural-language oracle ambiguity", "interpretive-resolution exploitation"

## Description

Attack on the **spec layer** of subjective-oracle prediction markets. The natural-language claim defining the market's resolution criterion is interpretively underdetermined — there is no machine-checkable definition of the load-bearing predicate ("wearing a suit", "agrees to a deal", "credible reporting consensus"). Voters resolve against the bettor-majority's reasonable interpretation of the spec, citing spec ambiguity as the basis for the divergent ruling.

Distinct from sibling sub-Techniques:

- **T9.006.001 (DVM Vote Capture)** — voters in T9.006.001 vote *against* the resolution criteria entirely, in favour of the outcome that maximises their payoff. Voters in T9.006.002 vote consistent with one reasonable interpretation of an ambiguous spec; the attack is on the spec layer rather than the oracle's good-faith adherence to it.
- **T9.006.003 (Off-chain Resolution-Source Coercion)** — attacks the resolution-input layer; T9.006.002 attacks the spec layer itself.
- **T9.006.004 (Operational-Insider Trading)** — attacks the information-symmetry assumption; T9.006.002 attacks the spec definition.

The structural feature is that **markets resolving on language are exposed to T9.006.002 by construction**: any market whose resolution criterion contains a non-machine-checkable predicate has spec-ambiguity as a standing surface. The mitigation is at the market-design layer, not at the platform / oracle / voter layer.

## Observed indicators

- Resolution-spec language with unmachine-checkable predicates: fashion / wardrobe claims; political-status claims ("agreed to a deal"); subjective adjudications ("credible reporting consensus"); interpretive framing ("genuine," "official," "verified").
- Divergence between media-consensus-implied resolution and oracle-resolution outcome.
- Voter rationale citing spec ambiguity: "lack of credible reporting consensus", "no clear definition", "subjective interpretation".
- Bettor-side public dispute citing the platform's own ambiguous spec; high engagement on the disputed-market thread post-resolution.

## Detection signals

- Pre-event flagging of resolution-spec language during market-listing review: predicates that include subjective / interpretive language; predicates that lack a single canonical authoritative source; predicates that depend on natural-language interpretation rather than cryptographically-verifiable on-chain state.
- Cohort-level analysis of disputed-resolution rates by spec-language category (fashion / political / interpretive vs. cryptographically-verifiable).
- Media-consensus-vs-oracle-resolution divergence as a post-event detection signal.

## Real-world examples

- **Polymarket Zelenskyy-suit market — 2025-07 (canonical T9.006.002 case)** — \~\$237M in disputed market volume (CoinDesk secondary characterisation \~\$160M). Despite multiple credible outlets (Reuters, AP, AFP) reporting Zelenskyy in a suit at NATO June 24, 2025, UMA voters resolved NO on July 1 citing lack of "credible reporting consensus." Top 10 UMA voters held ~6.5M UMA (~30% of average vote). UMA market cap during dispute window (~\$95M) was less than half the disputed market's volume. Resolution criterion ("wearing a suit") had no machine-checkable definition. See [`examples/2025-07-polymarket-zelenskyy-suit.md`](../examples/2025-07-polymarket-zelenskyy-suit.md).

## Reference implementations

- See parent technique `T9.006-subjective-oracle-resolution-manipulation.md`. T9.006.002-specific tooling gap at v0.1; the load-bearing mitigation is at the market-spec design layer rather than the runtime detection layer.

## Mitigations

- **Machine-checkable resolution criteria (highest-leverage):** markets resolving on cryptographically-verifiable inputs (block-height events, on-chain state, signed oracle attestations) are not exposed to T9.006.002. Markets resolving on language are.
- **Pre-listing spec review:** flag market specs with unmachine-checkable predicates; require listing-time tightening of natural-language predicates.
- **Source-multiplicity in resolution specifications:** markets requiring explicit enumeration of acceptable resolution sources, with majority-or-supermajority among the named cohort as the resolution criterion.
- **Voter-position-disclosure with conflict-of-interest slashing** (cross-cuts T9.006.001): voters whose interpretive ruling correlates with bettor-side position holdings should be exposed or slashable.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1: gap. See parent T9.006 file for v0.x mitigation candidates.

## Citations

See worked example for primary citations. Zelenskyy-suit: Decrypt, CoinDesk ("This Isn't Decentralized" power-user piece), The Defiant.

## Discussion

The Zelenskyy-suit market made the structural disequilibrium visible: **the oracle's total economic weight (UMA market cap) was smaller than the prize available for corrupting it (the market's volume)**. This is not a transient mispricing — it is a stable property of any subjective oracle adjudicating economically meaningful prediction markets.

T9.006.002 is structurally the most diffuse of the four T9.006 sub-patterns because the attack surface is built into the market-spec design itself, not into the oracle layer or the voter cohort. A defender designing a UMA-class oracle should treat any market title that includes a fashion / language / interpretive claim as elevated dispute risk; a market-listing pipeline should refuse listings whose resolution criterion lacks machine-checkable predicates or fails source-multiplicity requirements.

The mitigation surface is structurally limited: voter-position disclosure addresses T9.006.001 vote-capture but not T9.006.002 spec-ambiguity. Source-multiplicity and machine-checkable criteria address T9.006.002 directly but require platform-side market-spec tightening that constrains the host's product flexibility.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once T9.006.002 carries multiple independent worked-example anchors beyond the Zelenskyy-suit case. The pipeline includes any 2026+ disputed market where the resolution divergence is anchored to spec-ambiguity rather than vote-capture.
docs.techniques/T9.006.003-off-chain-resoluti:
# OAK-T9.006.003 — Off-chain Resolution-Source Coercion

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit); sub-Technique of OAK-T9.006 (Subjective-Oracle Resolution Manipulation)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (Polymarket on Polygon canonical at v0.1)
**First documented:** 2026-03 (Times of Israel correspondent Emanuel Fabian, Iran-strike market)
**Aliases:** "journalist coercion", "off-chain reporter extortion", "resolution-input-layer attack", "oracle-input physical-coercion"

## Description

Attack on the **resolution-input layer** of subjective-oracle prediction markets. When a market's resolution depends on a single human reporter's published account or a narrow cohort of off-chain sources, the human reporter becomes an attack surface. Bettors with positions in the disfavoured outcome translate their economic bounty into pressure (threats, extortion, harassment) on the off-chain resolution source — demanding the reporter rewrite, retract, or suppress their account so the market resolves in the threatening bettors' favour.

The attack vector is not crypto-specific in technique — extortion of journalists has a long history outside crypto. What is structurally novel is that the **economic incentive for the extortion is created by the prediction market itself**. The market creates a bounty for a specific reporting outcome; bettors with positions translate that bounty into pressure on the human reporter.

This is a category-level new finding for OAK: an attack vector that operates **entirely off-chain, against humans, but whose economic motivation is generated on-chain**.

Distinct from sibling sub-Techniques:

- **T9.006.001 (DVM Vote Capture)** — attacks the oracle layer; T9.006.003 attacks upstream of the oracle, at the resolution-input layer.
- **T9.006.002 (Resolution-Spec Ambiguity Exploitation)** — attacks the spec layer; T9.006.003 attacks the input that the spec references.
- **T9.006.004 (Operational-Insider Trading)** — attacks the information-symmetry assumption; T9.006.003 attacks the resolution-input layer.

## Observed indicators

- Threats / extortion / harassment directed at named off-chain reporters or publications whose reporting is the resolution input for a high-volume disputed market.
- Bettor-to-resolution-source social-media interaction patterns: replies, DMs, mentions concentrated in the resolution window.
- Public first-person accounts from threatened reporters (Times of Israel / Fabian first-person account is the canonical reference).
- Single-source resolution input: markets where a single journalist or single publication is the dominant resolution input.

## Detection signals

- Pre-event flagging of high-volume markets where the reporting cohort is narrow.
- Bettor-position disclosure to potential resolution sources: journalists whose reporting is cited in a resolution spec should be informed before publication that their account may have economic influence.
- Platform-level threat-monitoring of bettor behaviour around the resolution window for markets with narrow reporting cohorts.
- Post-event corroboration: cross-reference between the threatened reporter's published account and independent news coverage (Washington Post / Haaretz corroboration of the Fabian case is the canonical pattern).

## Real-world examples

- **Polymarket Iran-strike market / journalist coercion — 2026-03 (canonical T9.006.003 case)** — \~\$900,000 in market positions held by Polymarket bettors who would have profited from a YES resolution. The economically operative event is the **threats issued against Times of Israel correspondent Emanuel Fabian** demanding he rewrite his report so the market would resolve in their favour. Fabian preserved evidence and published a first-person account in Times of Israel; corroborated by Washington Post and Haaretz coverage on March 17, 2026. The journalist did not change his reporting; the market resolved against the threatening bettors' position; the perpetrators were not publicly identified or prosecuted at v0.1. **First publicly documented case in crypto-prediction-market history of physical coercion of an off-chain human reporter as an attack vector against the resolution layer.** See [`examples/2026-03-polymarket-iran-strike-journalist-coercion.md`](../examples/2026-03-polymarket-iran-strike-journalist-coercion.md).

## Reference implementations

- See parent technique `T9.006-subjective-oracle-resolution-manipulation.md`. T9.006.003-specific tooling gap at v0.1; the mitigation surface is at the market-design and journalist-side awareness layers rather than the runtime detection layer.

## Mitigations

- **Source-multiplicity in resolution specifications (highest-leverage):** markets requiring two or more independently-published sources for resolution, with no single human reporter's account being load-bearing. Market-design mitigation, not platform-design mitigation.
- **Bettor-position disclosure to potential resolution sources:** journalists whose reporting is cited in a resolution spec should be informed that their account may have economic influence. Operationally awkward but the only way to extend traditional journalistic threat-modelling into the crypto-prediction-market context.
- **Platform-level threat-monitoring of high-volume markets where reporting cohorts are narrow:** identify markets where a single journalist or single publication is the dominant resolution input; warrant heightened monitoring of bettor behaviour around the resolution window.
- **Machine-checkable resolution criteria** (parent-Technique mitigation): markets resolving on cryptographically-verifiable inputs are not exposed to T9.006.003.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1: gap. See parent T9.006 file for v0.x mitigation candidates.

## Citations

See worked example for primary citations: Times of Israel (Fabian first-person account), Washington Post, Haaretz.

## Discussion

The Fabian case proves a structural property of subjective-oracle prediction markets that was previously theoretical: **when the resolution input is a single human reporter's published account, the human reporter becomes an attack surface**. OAK-T9.001 (price-feed oracle manipulation) does not contemplate this attack class; the mitigations OAK lists for T9.001 (TWAP, multi-venue feeds, deviation halts — OAK-M09) are mechanical and apply to numerical price feeds. None of them apply to "the resolution depends on a journalist's interpretation of an event."

T9.006.003 is structurally the most concerning of the four T9.006 sub-patterns because the attack vector extends into physical coercion of off-chain humans. The defensive surface is real (market-spec design, source-multiplicity, journalist-side awareness) but is operationally outside the technical-mitigation domain that OAK Mitigations otherwise cover.

**Attribution for T9.006.003 incidents is structurally hard.** The threats come from individual anonymous accounts with no single operator-cluster signature. OAK should expect future cases of this class to remain `inferred-strong` or `confirmed-without-attribution` rather than reaching the per-operator `confirmed` standard.

OAK's mission is to document the real adversary surface against on-chain assets — including the parts that are not technical exploits. The Fabian case is on the public record (Washington Post, Haaretz, Times of Israel), the technique is generalisable (any subjective-oracle prediction market with narrow resolution-source cohorts), and the defensive surface is real. Documenting it explicitly is the only way an open framework can claim to cover the actual adversary surface rather than the comfortable subset of it.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once T9.006.003 carries multiple independent worked-example anchors beyond the canonical Fabian / Iran-strike case. The class is structurally generalisable; pipeline anchors will surface in any 2026+ subjective-oracle prediction market whose resolution-source cohort is narrow and whose disputed-market volume is large enough to incentivise off-chain coercion.
docs.techniques/T9.006.004-operational-inside:
# OAK-T9.006.004 — Operational-Insider Trading on Subjective-Resolution Prediction Markets

**Parent Tactics:** OAK-T9 (Smart-Contract Exploit); sub-Technique of OAK-T9.006 (Subjective-Oracle Resolution Manipulation)
**Maturity:** emerging
**Chains:** EVM (Polymarket on Polygon canonical at v0.1)
**First documented:** 2025-12 (Van Dyke / Operation Absolute Resolve betting window) → 2026-01 (real-world execution) → 2026-04 (DOJ SDNY indictment + CFTC parallel civil action). Multi-jurisdictional confirmation 2026-02 (Tel Aviv District Court indictment of IDF reservist).
**Aliases:** "operational-insider Polymarket", "classified-information prediction-market trading", "causal-actor insider trading"

## Description

Attack on the **information-symmetry assumption between bettors** in subjective-resolution prediction markets. A participant with classified-information access to (and operational involvement in) the underlying real-world event trades on that knowledge before public disclosure. The bettor is not just *informed* about the outcome — they are one of the **causal actors producing it**.

This crosses from classical insider trading (informational asymmetry) into a class structurally adjacent to market-manipulation-via-action: the bettor's operational decisions on the world (when the raid is launched, on what date, with what force composition) directly determine which resolution outcome triggers their payout.

Distinct from sibling sub-Techniques:

- **T9.006.001 (DVM Vote Capture)** — attacks the oracle layer; resolution input is honest, the vote is corrupted. T9.006.004 leaves the oracle layer untouched; the resolution is correct given the public information available at the resolution time, but the attacker traded ahead of that information.
- **T9.006.002 (Resolution-Spec Ambiguity Exploitation)** — attacks the spec layer. T9.006.004 leaves the spec untouched.
- **T9.006.003 (Off-chain Resolution-Source Coercion)** — pressures third-party reporters post hoc. T9.006.004 acts on the underlying real-world event itself, before disclosure.

The on-chain settlement substrate makes T9.006.004 **qualitatively different from traditional Section 10(b)/Rule 10b-5 insider trading**:

1. **Position and timing are publicly inspectable.** Wallet address, deposit timestamp, share-acquisition ladder, and withdrawal pattern are all on-chain and forensically reconstructable by prosecutors. Traditional insider-trading detection relies on broker subpoenas; on-chain prediction markets give the regulator the trade record by default.
2. **Wallet-attribution is the load-bearing forensic step.** Real-world identification depends on KYC at the off-ramp (Kraken / brokerage) plus account-creation metadata. The on-chain data alone does not name the operator; it names the position.
3. **Geofencing is partial.** Polymarket nominally bars US persons; the participation pattern (US-based active-duty soldier creating an account) demonstrates that VPN-and-self-attestation geofencing does not survive a determined participant who has every motivation to conceal jurisdiction.
4. **The market itself surfaces the anomaly.** Sharp longshot wins by newly-created accounts in narrow time windows are detectable signatures; the public record now contains explicit baseline rates (ACDC analytics: longshots win ~14% baseline, ~51.8% in military / defense markets) that establish what counts as anomaly.

## Observed indicators

- New Polymarket account creation in narrow time window before a classified real-world event.
- Concentration of bets on YES-side of correlated markets gated by the classified event.
- Sharp longshot wins by newly-created accounts in narrow time windows.
- Same-day withdrawal of profits before public Polymarket-side scrutiny ramps up.
- Operational-actor involvement in the underlying real-world event (Special Forces NCO; reservist with classified briefing access; intelligence-cleared personnel).
- KYC linkage at off-ramp (Kraken / brokerage account) plus account-creation metadata correlating to cleared-personnel identity.

## Detection signals

- **Account-age-vs-first-bet correlation** on markets gated by classified events.
- **Sharp longshot wins concentrated in newly-created accounts** on correlated cohorts of markets.
- **KYC linkage at the off-ramp** (Kraken / brokerage) plus account-creation metadata.
- **Counter-intelligence cross-reference** on operationally-involved actors when sharp longshot wins are detected on correlated military / defense markets.
- **ACDC analytics baseline rates** as published reference for cohort-anomaly detection (longshots: ~14% baseline, ~51.8% in military / defense markets).
- **Polymarket / Chainalysis surveillance partnership** (operationalised April 2026) as the cohort-tracking surface.

## Real-world examples

- **Polymarket / Van Dyke / Venezuela / Operation Absolute Resolve — 2025-12 → 2026-01 → 2026-04 (canonical T9.006.004 case)** — ~\$33,000 wagered → ~\$404,000–\$410,000 in profits extracted. US Army Special Forces master sergeant Gannon Ken Van Dyke, operationally involved in the planning and execution of Operation Absolute Resolve (Maduro capture, January 3, 2026), opened a Polymarket account December 26, 2025 and placed ~13 bets totalling ~\$33,034 on YES sides of Venezuela / Maduro markets December 27 – January 2. Bets resolved YES January 3–4; on the day of the operation, Van Dyke withdrew the majority of proceeds, routed funds through a foreign cryptocurrency vault, and deposited them into a newly-created online brokerage account. Arrested April 2026; DOJ SDNY indictment for unlawful use of confidential government information for personal gain, theft of nonpublic government information, commodities fraud, wire fraud, and engaging in a monetary transaction in property derived from specified unlawful activity (up to 20 years on the wire-fraud count). Pleaded not guilty April 28, 2026. CFTC filed parallel civil enforcement action. Kalshi publicly stated it had blocked the same individual on its venue. **First publicly documented case in crypto-prediction-market history** in which a bettor with direct operational involvement in the underlying real-world event has been criminally charged. See [`examples/2026-01-polymarket-venezuela-maduro-soldier-insider.md`](../examples/2026-01-polymarket-venezuela-maduro-soldier-insider.md).
- **Polymarket / IDF reservist / Iran-strike timing — 2025-06 → 2026-02 (T9.006.004 cohort case, multi-jurisdictional)** — ~\$162,663 in profits extracted by a two-person scheme. Israeli Air Force major (reservist) briefed civilian co-defendant when warplanes were already en route to Iranian targets; civilian placed Polymarket bets on the timing of Israel's June 2025 opening strike. Pair extracted ~\$162,663, agreed to split evenly. Tel Aviv District Court indictment February 2026; charges include severe security offences, bribery, obstruction of justice. The pair continued betting across additional Iran-conflict markets (including a winning bet on the end-date of the 12-day war) until arrest. **Establishes T9.006.004 as multi-jurisdictional (US + IL) within a four-month window.** The Israeli case differs structurally from Van Dyke in one important way: the briefing was passed laterally to a civilian co-conspirator who placed the actual bets, decoupling the on-chain wallet identity from the cleared-personnel identity. Purely on-chain detection (account-age, deposit-time, bet-cluster) does not by itself identify the operational insider; the linkage runs through human intelligence on the briefing chain. See [`examples/2026-02-polymarket-iran-strike-idf-reservist-insider.md`](../examples/2026-02-polymarket-iran-strike-idf-reservist-insider.md).

## Reference implementations

- See parent technique `T9.006-subjective-oracle-resolution-manipulation.md`. T9.006.004-specific cohort-tracking surface: Polymarket / Chainalysis surveillance partnership (April 2026); ACDC analytics; Kalshi cross-venue blocklist coordination.

## Mitigations

- **Account-age-vs-first-bet correlation cohort surveillance** on markets gated by classified events.
- **Sharp-longshot-win cohort surveillance** with ACDC-class baseline rates as the reference threshold.
- **KYC linkage at off-ramp** as the load-bearing forensic step for wallet-to-real-world identification.
- **Cross-venue blocklist coordination** (Kalshi / Polymarket / future prediction-market venues) for identified operational-insider actors.
- **Counter-intelligence integration** for operationally-involved-actor cross-reference on flagged cohorts.
- **Geofencing reinforcement:** Polymarket's nominal bar on US persons via VPN-and-self-attestation is structurally insufficient; the operational-insider cohort has every incentive to evade. Stronger geofencing at the transaction-acceptance layer (rather than at the registration-self-attestation layer) is the structural mitigation candidate, but operationally hard for a fully-on-chain venue.

OAK Mitigation cross-references at v0.1: gap. v0.x candidates: "OAK-M-x operational-insider cohort surveillance"; "OAK-M-x sharp-longshot baseline-rate threshold"; "OAK-M-x cross-venue prediction-market blocklist coordination".

## Citations

See worked examples for primary citations. Van Dyke / Venezuela: DOJ SDNY indictment, CFTC complaint, NPR, CoinDesk. IDF reservist / Iran-strike: Tel Aviv District Court charging documents, Israeli and international media February 2026 reports.

## Discussion

T9.006.004 is structurally the most novel of the four T9.006 sub-patterns at v0.1 because the bettor is not just *informed* about the outcome — they are one of the **causal actors producing it**. This crosses from classical insider trading into a class structurally adjacent to market-manipulation-via-action.

**Note on T9.006.004 ID framing.** An earlier `TAXONOMY-GAPS.md` framing of T9.006.004 used a different sub-pattern (platform-override of oracle outcome — Barron Trump / DJT memecoin market). At v0.1 promotion the existing example anchors (Van Dyke / Venezuela; IDF reservist / Iran-strike) self-categorise as T9.006.004 with the operational-insider definition; the platform-override sub-pattern lacks a worked-example anchor at v0.1 and is held as a v0.x candidate for a separate sub-Technique slot. T9.006.004 is canonical for the operational-insider sub-pattern at v0.1 freeze; contributors documenting future platform-override cases should propose a separate sub-Technique ID (e.g. T9.006.005) at v0.x.

**On-chain settlement substrate creates novel forensic affordances.** The on-chain transparency that traditional finance lacks (wallet, timestamp, position ladder, withdrawal pattern publicly inspectable) makes T9.006.004 cases forensically tractable in ways that traditional Section 10(b)/Rule 10b-5 cases are not. The Van Dyke case was driven by a combination of public on-chain forensics (Bubblemaps, Sonar Pro, NPR-cited analytics firms initially flagging the ~\$400K profit cluster as anomalous) plus federal subpoenas to Polymarket and Kraken for KYC linkage, plus Defense-side records matching Van Dyke's role to bet timing.

**Multi-jurisdictional pattern is established.** The Van Dyke (US, April 2026) and IDF reservist (Israel, February 2026) cases together establish T9.006.004 as a multi-jurisdictional structural class within a four-month window. The pattern is now multi-jurisdictionally documented; future cases are likely to continue surfacing as KYC-cluster forensics become standard at prediction-market venues and at off-ramp exchanges.

**The IDF reservist case demonstrates a forensically harder sub-shape:** the briefing was passed laterally to a civilian co-conspirator who placed the actual bets. This decouples the on-chain wallet identity from the cleared-personnel identity. Purely on-chain detection does not identify the operational insider; the linkage runs through human intelligence on the briefing chain. Contributors writing future T9.006.004 worked examples should expect this lateral-disclosure sub-shape to recur as operational-insider trading scales, and should record the briefing-chain dimension as a first-class observable.

**The mitigation surface is partly off-chain.** Account-age-vs-first-bet correlation, KYC at off-ramp, cross-venue blocklist coordination, and counter-intelligence cross-reference are all real defender mitigations, but the load-bearing prevention is on the operational-actor side (security clearance briefings on prediction-market trading risk; clearance-loss as deterrent; pre-trade compliance enforcement at military / intelligence personnel offboarding events). Polymarket / Chainalysis surveillance partnership (operationalised April 2026) is the post-event detection layer; the prevention layer requires off-platform coordination.

Promotion to `stable` will follow once T9.006.004 carries additional cohort cases beyond the Van Dyke / IDF reservist anchor. The pipeline includes any 2026+ DOJ / CFTC / international prosecution of operational-insider trading on subjective-resolution prediction markets.
docs.mitigations/OAK-M01-source-bytecode-veri:
# OAK-M01 — Source-Bytecode Verification

**Class:** detection
**Audience:** vendor, risk-team, venue
**Maps to Techniques:** OAK-T1.001, OAK-T1.003, OAK-T1.004, OAK-T1.005, OAK-T2.004, OAK-T6.001, OAK-T6.003

## Description

Source-Bytecode Verification is the deterministic-recompile defence: for any deployed contract whose source has been claimed (Etherscan-style verified source, project-published GitHub commit, or audit-firm-cited commit), recompile the source under the declared compiler version and optimisation flags, and compare the produced bytecode against the bytecode actually deployed at the target address. The two should match within compiler-deterministic noise (metadata-hash trailers excluded). Anything else is a divergence between what defenders believe the contract does and what it actually does.

The defensive principle is that source-level review (manual audit, static analysis, Technique-level pattern matching) is only meaningful if the source actually corresponds to the deployed bytecode. Without that binding, every downstream defensive control reduces to a marketing claim. The mitigation works because the comparison is mechanical, falsifiable, and cheap relative to the harm prevented — a single recompile-and-hash converts a class of evasion patterns (T6.001 and its T1 / T6.003 combinations) from undetectable into deterministic.

The canonical-at-listing-time variant — where a venue or risk team performs the recompile as a *listing precondition* and stores the bytecode hash in a registry — is the highest-leverage deployment surface. It pushes the verification cost from per-trader (where it is rarely paid) to per-venue (where it amortises across every trader).

## How it applies

- **OAK-T1.001 (Modifiable Tax Function):** static-analysis-tool detection of fee-mutability paths only holds if the verified source compiles to the deployed bytecode; recompile binding is the precondition that makes downstream T1.001 fee-branch enumeration trustworthy.
- **OAK-T1.003 (Renounced-but-not-Really):** authority-graph enumeration over the verified source produces correct results only when the source matches the bytecode; without recompile binding, an attacker can publish a renounced-looking source while deploying a different bytecode that retains admin paths.
- **OAK-T1.004 (Blacklist / Pausable Weaponisation):** blacklist and pause predicates may be present in deployed bytecode but absent from the published source; recompile binding closes that gap.
- **OAK-T1.005 (Hidden Fee-on-Transfer):** asymmetric fee branches are commonly hidden behind a source-vs-bytecode mismatch; the recompile is the structural check that makes T1.005 branch enumeration meaningful.
- **OAK-T2.004 (Initial Liquidity Backdoor):** pool-creation-time admin paths must be evaluated against the actual deployed bytecode of the token and pool wrapper, not the announced source; recompile binding is a prerequisite for the creation-transaction trace check.
- **OAK-T6.001 (Source-Verification Mismatch):** this is the canonical Technique that OAK-M01 exists to defeat — the recompile is the unambiguous detection signal.
- **OAK-T6.003 (Audit of Different Bytecode Version):** recompile against the audited commit, hash the result, compare against the deployed-bytecode hash; non-match is the audit-vs-deployed gap that T6.003 exploits.

## Limitations

OAK-M01 does not cover proxy patterns where the proxy itself is verified-source-correct but the implementation it points to is unverified or has been swapped post-listing — that is OAK-M03 territory (continuous bytecode-diff monitoring). It also does not cover semantic equivalence: two bytecodes that match exactly are not necessarily *safe*, only *consistent with the source that was reviewed*. The mitigation is binding, not absolution. Compiler-determinism edge cases (Solidity metadata-hash variations, IR pipeline changes between minor versions, Vyper evaluation-order changes) require a normalised hash that strips known-variable trailers; naive byte-equality comparisons produce false positives. CREATE2-replay-after-`selfdestruct` requires a re-binding event at the point of redeploy, not just at original listing. And on Solana / non-EVM chains the verification surface is different (BPF program hashes, IDL-vs-binary comparisons) and OAK-M01 in its v0.1 form is EVM-centric.

## Reference implementations

- `mg-detectors-rs` — recompile-binding pipeline planned; current v0.1 status is gap (mirrors the T6.001 detection gap).
- Sourcify (`sourcify.dev`) — cryptographic source-verification via metadata-hash matching; the canonical open-source implementation of the recompile-bind primitive at scale.
- Etherscan / Blockscout / Routescan — block-explorer "verified source" UIs; partial-match vs full-match labelling is the relevant distinction defenders should consume programmatically rather than as a UI badge.
- Foundry `forge verify-bytecode` and Hardhat verification plugins — local recompile tooling suitable for per-listing pipelines.
- Audit-attestation registries (proposed, not yet standardised at v0.1) — would extend OAK-M01 from source-bytecode binding to audit-bytecode binding by publishing the audited-commit's recompile hash alongside the audit certificate.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2025rug]` — cohort-scale context on rug-pull retrospectives where source-bytecode mismatch is a recurring evasion modifier.
- `[slowmist2024report]` — industry-side enumeration of source-verification-mismatch patterns in 2024 incident corpus.
- `[certikfakeaudit]` — fake-audit-claim cohort context; relevant to T6.003-mode application of OAK-M01 (audit-bytecode binding).
- `[quillauditsbackdoor]` — backdoor-via-proxy patterns where source-bytecode binding (and OAK-M03 follow-up) is the relevant defence.
docs.mitigations/OAK-M02-static-analysis-pre-:
# OAK-M02 — Static-Analysis Pre-Deployment

**Class:** architecture
**Audience:** protocol, designer
**Maps to Techniques:** OAK-T1.001, OAK-T1.003, OAK-T1.004, OAK-T1.005, OAK-T6.001, OAK-T6.002, OAK-T9.004, OAK-T9.005, OAK-T13.001

## Description

Static-Analysis Pre-Deployment is the discipline of running automated source-level analyses (Slither, Semgrep with smart-contract rule packs, MythX, Securify) and, for high-assurance components, formal verification (Certora Prover, Halmos, hevm symbolic execution, K-framework specifications) against a contract's source *before* mainnet deployment. The output is a machine-readable enumeration of suspicious code patterns — modifiable fee paths, residual admin authorities, missing reentrancy guards, unguarded external calls, and access-control gaps — that the project team must triage before pushing the deployment transaction.

The defensive principle is that mainnet deployment is a one-way door for the cohort of users who interact in the first hours: by the time a manual audit or a community review surfaces a finding, capital is already at risk. Pushing detection upstream of deployment converts a class of post-incident forensic work into a pre-incident developer task, where the marginal cost of fixing a finding is orders of magnitude lower than the post-deployment cost of patching, migrating, or pausing.

The mitigation is most load-bearing for invariant-class properties — "this contract has no path that lets a non-owner mint", "this lending market's solvency invariant holds across every external-call boundary", "the access-control modifier covers every state-mutating function" — that are tractable for symbolic / formal tooling and intractable for manual review on a contract-system of any non-trivial size. It is necessary but not sufficient: static analysis is upstream of audit, not a replacement for audit.

## How it applies

- **OAK-T1.001 (Modifiable Tax Function):** Slither's `arbitrary-send` and custom Semgrep rules detecting setter functions on fee state variables surface fee-mutability before it ships.
- **OAK-T1.003 (Renounced-but-not-Really):** authority-graph enumeration via Slither's `pragma`/access-control detectors plus custom rules for residual admin paths catches single-axis-renouncement-with-residual-authority before the renouncement claim is made publicly.
- **OAK-T1.004 (Blacklist / Pausable Weaponisation):** Semgrep rules over transfer-logic predicates flag any transfer path gated by a settable blacklist mapping or pause flag without an associated time-lock.
- **OAK-T1.005 (Hidden Fee-on-Transfer):** symbolic-execution tooling (hevm, Halmos) can prove or disprove fee-symmetry across the transfer matrix in a way that manual review cannot at scale.
- **OAK-T6.001 (Source-Verification Mismatch):** static-analysis tooling is the principal *consumer* of verified source — its output is meaningful only when paired with OAK-M01 binding, but conversely the value of OAK-M01 is realised through OAK-M02 downstream analysis.
- **OAK-T6.002 (Fake Audit Claim):** static-analysis output published as a project-side artefact (the Slither / formal-verification report alongside the deployment) raises the falsifiability of audit claims even where a third-party audit is absent, narrowing the surface that T6.002 exploits.
- **OAK-T9.004 (Access-Control Misconfiguration):** Slither's access-control detectors and Certora-style rules prove "every privileged function is gated by the expected role"; this is the canonical pre-deployment mitigation for T9.004.
- **OAK-T9.005 (Reentrancy):** Slither's `reentrancy-eth`, `reentrancy-no-eth`, and `reentrancy-events` detectors plus Halmos-class symbolic checks for checks-effects-interactions ordering surface reentrancy vectors before deployment; for read-only reentrancy, custom rules are required since stock detectors miss the cross-protocol case.
- **OAK-T13.001 (Paymaster Compromise):** ERC-4337-specific static-analysis rule packs (validate-paymaster-userOp surface, postOp revert paths, gas-token assumptions) catch paymaster-class bugs before the paymaster contract is registered with an EntryPoint.

## Limitations

Static analysis catches structural / pattern-level bugs (missing guards, unsafe ordering, settable state on critical paths) but is weak on *protocol-economic* failure modes (oracle-manipulation surfaces in T9.001, governance-attack surfaces in T9.003, restaking-cascade surfaces in T14.003) where the bug is in the design, not the code. Formal verification narrows that gap but requires invariant specifications that themselves can be wrong or incomplete — Certora's bound is the spec, not the contract. False-positive rates on stock detectors (Slither in particular) are high enough that triage discipline is required; teams that rubber-stamp the report are no better off than teams that skip it. And static analysis says nothing about the source-bytecode binding (OAK-M01) — analysing source that does not match the deployed bytecode is the pathological case OAK-T6.001 exploits.

## Reference implementations

- Slither (Trail of Bits, open-source) — the canonical EVM static-analysis tool; runs detector packs over Solidity source.
- Semgrep (returntocorp, open-source) — pattern-matching engine with smart-contract rule packs (`semgrep-smart-contracts`).
- MythX / Mythril (ConsenSys Diligence) — symbolic execution and SMT-backed bug-finding for EVM bytecode.
- Certora Prover — commercial formal-verification platform with declarative rule language; widely used by major DeFi protocols (Compound, Aave, MakerDAO).
- Halmos (a16z) and hevm (DappHub / Foundry) — open-source symbolic execution; suitable for invariant proofs.
- `mg-detectors-rs` — runtime-side detectors; pre-deployment static analysis is out of scope for v0.1 but pairs naturally with the runtime layer.
- OpenZeppelin Defender, Forta — runtime monitoring; complementary to OAK-M02 rather than a substitute.

## Citations

- `[owaspscstop10]` — OWASP Smart Contract Top 10 enumerates the bug classes that pre-deployment static analysis principally targets.
- `[slowmist2024report]` — industry incident corpus; OAK-M02 gaps map directly to the recurring root-cause categories.
- `[chainalysiseuler2023]` — Euler 2023 retrospective; donateToReserves access-control gap was reachable by pre-deployment formal verification with the correct invariant.
- `[halborneuler2023]` — Halborn-side technical analysis of the Euler donateToReserves path.
- `[osecpaymasters2025]` — paymaster-class static-analysis findings; relevant to T13.001 application.
- `[ozaa4337audit]` — OpenZeppelin ERC-4337 audit notes; static-analysis surface for the AA stack.
docs.mitigations/OAK-M03-continuous-bytecode-:
# OAK-M03 — Continuous Bytecode-Diff Monitoring

**Class:** detection
**Audience:** vendor, risk-team

**Maps to Techniques:** OAK-T1.003, OAK-T1.004, OAK-T2.004, OAK-T6.001, OAK-T6.003, OAK-T9.004, OAK-T11.003

## Description

Continuous Bytecode-Diff Monitoring is the post-deployment counterpart to OAK-M01: instead of binding source-to-bytecode once at listing time, the defender continuously watches every contract that has been declared in scope (a venue's listed-token universe, a custodian's contract whitelist, a risk-team's protocol watchlist) for *changes* to the executing bytecode. The relevant signals are EIP-1967 admin-slot writes, transparent-proxy `upgradeTo` / `upgradeToAndCall` calls, UUPS-pattern proxy implementation pointer changes, beacon-proxy beacon updates, `selfdestruct`-then-`CREATE2`-redeploy at the same address, and on Solana the equivalent `Upgradeable BPF` program-data pointer changes.

The defensive principle is that the source-bytecode binding established at listing is a *snapshot*, not a property. A contract that was safe yesterday can be made unsafe today by a single proxy-upgrade transaction, and a contract that was reviewed against benign bytecode can be repointed at a backdoored implementation between blocks. Treating bytecode as immutable when it is in fact mutable is the systematic error this mitigation closes. The monitoring is most load-bearing for proxy-pattern contracts (where upgrades are *expected* and the question is whether each individual upgrade is *governed*) and for `selfdestruct`-permitting deployments (where redeploys at the same address are *unexpected* and any occurrence is a P0 signal).

OAK-M03 surfaces detection events fast enough that downstream defenders (venues delisting, wallet vendors warning, custodians pausing inflows) can act before the upgraded contract is exercised against held positions. The mitigation has two operating modes: alerting (push notification on event) and gating (programmatic block of dependent operations until the new implementation is reviewed).

## How it applies

- **OAK-T1.003 (Renounced-but-not-Really):** post-listing implementation swaps on proxy-pattern tokens are the canonical pivot from "renounced" to "controlled-again"; OAK-M03 catches the pivot transaction directly, before the new admin path is exercised.
- **OAK-T1.004 (Blacklist / Pausable Weaponisation):** an upgrade that adds a blacklist or pause predicate to an originally clean transfer-logic path is a T1.004 activation event; bytecode-diff monitoring surfaces the upgrade transaction, paired with OAK-M01 recompile-on-diff to confirm the new bytecode contains the gating predicate.
- **OAK-T2.004 (Initial Liquidity Backdoor):** for pool wrappers and LP-token contracts that are themselves upgradeable, OAK-M03 catches post-listing repointing toward backdoor-bearing implementations.
- **OAK-T6.001 (Source-Verification Mismatch):** the time-evolved variant of T6.001 — verified source matches at listing, then diverges via upgrade — is unreachable for OAK-M01 (which is one-shot) but is the canonical signal for OAK-M03.
- **OAK-T6.003 (Audit of Different Bytecode Version):** an audit performed against implementation v1 has zero defensive weight against implementation v2; OAK-M03 makes the implementation-vs-audited-bytecode drift legible by alerting at every implementation change.
- **OAK-T9.004 (Access-Control Misconfiguration):** misconfigurations introduced by upgrade (where v1 was correctly-configured and v2 ships a bug or a deliberate bypass) are caught at the upgrade-event boundary rather than only after exploitation.
- **OAK-T11.003 (Multisig Contract Manipulation):** unannounced contract-modification events on custodial multisigs are the structural T11.003 signal; OAK-M03 is the detection surface, paired with the mandatory-disclosure expectation from the custodian.

## Limitations

OAK-M03 detects the *event* but not its *intent* — a legitimate, governance-approved upgrade and a malicious admin-key-compromise upgrade present identically at the bytecode level. Distinguishing them requires out-of-band signal (announcement on the project's published change-control channel, governance-vote tally, time-lock window) which the detection layer cannot synthesize on its own. False-positive volume is high for protocols with active legitimate upgrade cadence, which forces alert-fatigue management and risks under-reaction at the moment of compromise. Monitoring is also chain-asymmetric: EVM EIP-1967 slots are well-standardised and easy to watch, while custom proxy patterns (Diamond / EIP-2535, custom-storage proxies) and non-EVM equivalents require per-pattern detector logic. Finally, the mitigation says nothing about the *new* implementation's properties — it tells you the bytecode changed, not whether the change is dangerous; OAK-M01 + OAK-M02 must run on the new implementation for the chain to close.

## Reference implementations

- OpenZeppelin Defender — production-grade EIP-1967 admin-slot monitoring with policy-driven alerts; one of the canonical commercial deployments of OAK-M03.
- Forta — open detection network with EIP-1967 / proxy-upgrade detector bots; suitable for self-hosted defender pipelines.
- Tenderly — alert framework with proxy-upgrade and admin-slot watchers as first-class primitives.
- `mg-detectors-rs` — runtime bytecode-diff and proxy-implementation-pointer change detectors planned; v0.1 status is partial coverage on Solana program-data pointer changes, EVM coverage in progress.
- Etherscan / Blockscout proxy-upgrade event surfaces — passive, UI-side; suitable for manual review but not for sub-block alerting.
- Custom event-listener pipelines over JSON-RPC (`eth_getLogs` filtered on the EIP-1967 admin / implementation slots) — the open-source baseline that the commercial offerings wrap.

## Citations

- `[nomicproxybackdoor]` — proxy-backdoor pattern retrospective; the structural class OAK-M03 is designed to catch.
- `[quillauditsbackdoor]` — proxy-and-backdoor combination patterns; downstream consumer of OAK-M03 alerts.
- `[chainalysis2025rug]` — cohort-scale evidence that proxy-upgrade-after-listing is a recurring rug-pull pattern.
- `[halbornwintermute2022]` — Wintermute 2022 retrospective; relevant to the broader theme of post-deployment authority changes that bytecode-diff monitoring surfaces (alongside OAK-M05 for authority graph).
docs.mitigations/OAK-M04-funder-graph-cluster:
# OAK-M04 — Funder-Graph Clustering

**Class:** detection
**Audience:** vendor, risk-team

**Maps to Techniques:** OAK-T2.001, OAK-T3.001, OAK-T3.002, OAK-T3.003, OAK-T8.001, OAK-T8.002, OAK-T1.001, OAK-T2.004

## Description

Funder-Graph Clustering is the attribution-side defence against fresh-address camouflage: cluster on-chain addresses by common funder ancestry — the wallet, exchange withdrawal, or bridge that supplied the gas / initial balance — and treat any set of addresses tracing back to a shared funder within a narrow time window as a *single operator entity* for the purposes of risk decisions. Where surface analysis sees twenty independent first-block buyers, funder-graph clustering sees one operator splitting an allocation across twenty wallets to evade per-buyer concentration limits.

The defensive principle is that an attacker controls address creation but cannot easily control the genesis of value: every wallet must be funded by *something* (a centralised-exchange withdrawal, a bridge inflow, an existing on-chain balance), and that funder is a structural attribution edge that survives address rotation. Clustering by funder reconstructs the operator-level view that the attacker is trying to hide, and in doing so re-enables the per-operator risk controls (concentration caps, position limits, listing thresholds) that fresh-address camouflage was designed to bypass.

OAK-M04 is the canonical capability under OAK-T8.001 (the Technique itself describes the *failure to apply* this mitigation as the relevant attack surface). It is also the load-bearing input for any sybil-class detection: rate limits, airdrop-eligibility checks, and concentration metrics that operate on *addresses* without first collapsing to *operators* are systematically defeated by sybil deployment.

## How it applies

- **OAK-T2.001 (Single-Sided Liquidity Plant):** when the entity providing initial liquidity also funds the first cohort of buyers from the same ancestor wallet, funder-graph clustering exposes the structural collusion that depth-snapshots alone cannot distinguish from organic interest.
- **OAK-T3.001 (Sybil Bundled Launch):** the canonical T3.001 pattern is N freshly-created wallets buying within the first M blocks; clustering them by common funder collapses the apparent diversity into a single operator and surfaces the concentration the operator was hiding.
- **OAK-T3.002 (Wash-Trade Volume Inflation):** wash-trade pairs that pass surface tests (different addresses, plausible pricing) collapse under funder-graph clustering when both sides trace to a common ancestor; this is a direct contributor to wash-adjusted volume metrics.
- **OAK-T3.003 (Pump-and-Dump Coordination):** coordinator wallets accumulating before promotional spikes are typically funded through a small set of ancestor wallets; clustering surfaces the coordinator cohort as a single concentration signal.
- **OAK-T8.001 (Cluster Reuse / Operator Continuity):** this is the canonical Technique that OAK-M04 implements. The Technique's "operator-cluster watchlist" formulation is the mature-deployment form of funder-graph clustering applied across launches over time.
- **OAK-T8.002 (Cross-Chain Operator Continuity):** funder-graph clustering extends cross-chain when bridge transactions are treated as funding edges; the defender then sees a unified operator-graph spanning chains, which is the canonical T8.002 detection surface.
- **OAK-T1.001 (Modifiable Tax Function) — contributory:** when a fee-mutability operator deploys repeatedly, funder-graph clustering ties the new deployment to known prior-cohort operators even when the contract surface is freshly deployed.
- **OAK-T2.004 (Initial Liquidity Backdoor) — contributory:** the LP-mint recipient and the deployer wallet are typically common-funder-clustered with the operator's broader infrastructure; OAK-M04 ties the per-deployment surface analysis to the operator-history layer.

## Limitations

Funder-graph clustering is defeated when the attacker funds wallets through high-mixing infrastructure (Tornado Cash and successors, CEX-deposit-and-withdraw cycles with sufficient delay, cross-chain bridges with embedded mixing). It also produces false positives when many users share a common funder by ordinary means — exchange hot-wallets fund millions of withdrawals daily, and a naive clustering algorithm will collapse all of them into a single "operator". Mitigation requires CEX-deposit-address recognition (so that a CEX hot wallet is treated as a *boundary*, not as a clustering ancestor) and time-window-bounded clustering (addresses funded by a common ancestor *within a narrow window* are stronger evidence than addresses funded over months). Privacy-respecting users who legitimately deploy multiple addresses from a personal hot wallet are clustered together, which is correct attribution but raises false-positive rates for any downstream control that treats clustering as inculpatory rather than informational. Finally, the mitigation is chain-aware: clustering quality is highest on EVM chains with rich exchange-address labelling, and weakest on chains with native privacy features (Monero, Zcash shielded pools, Solana confidential transfers).

## Reference implementations

- Chainalysis Reactor / KYT — commercial address-clustering and funder-graph traversal; the reference implementation in compliance-tooling form.
- TRM Labs, Elliptic Navigator, Crystal Blockchain — equivalent commercial offerings with overlapping but non-identical clustering heuristics; defenders integrating multiple providers see better recall.
- Arkham Intelligence — public-facing entity-attribution surface; funder-graph traversal is a first-class UI primitive.
- Breadcrumbs.app, Etherscan address labels (Nansen / Nansen Query) — open / freemium tooling suitable for case-by-case investigation rather than at-scale risk gating.
- `mg-detectors-rs` — funder-graph clustering against Solana operator-cluster lists is a v0.1 in-scope capability; EVM extension planned.
- Open-source GraphSense — academic / OSS clustering toolkit; suitable for self-hosted research deployments.

## Citations

- `[chainalysis2024dprk]` — Chainalysis 2024 retrospective on DPRK-attributed flows where funder-graph clustering was the principal attribution method; relevant to T8.001 / T8.002 application against state-actor operator cohorts.
- `[dydx2024sybil]` — dYdX sybil-cohort retrospective; canonical example of T3.001 patterns surfaced post-hoc by funder-graph clustering.
- `[chainalysis2025rug]` — cohort-scale evidence on rug-pull operator continuity across launches; the T8.001 deployment surface for OAK-M04.
- `[slowmist2024report]` — industry-side retrospective; recurring sybil and wash-trade patterns where clustering is the load-bearing detection.
- `[chainalysisprivacychain2024]` — privacy-chain hop patterns that defeat naive funder-graph clustering; the limitation surface OAK-M04 must acknowledge.
docs.mitigations/OAK-M05-authority-graph-enum:
# OAK-M05 — Authority-Graph Enumeration

**Class:** detection
**Audience:** custody-customer, risk-team, vendor

**Maps to Techniques:** OAK-T1.003, OAK-T1.004, OAK-T9.003, OAK-T9.004, OAK-T11.001, OAK-T11.002, OAK-T11.003

## Description

Authority-Graph Enumeration is the per-key inventory of every privilege a given signing key (or a given operator's set of signing keys) holds across the on-chain estate: every contract on which the key is `owner`, every role-bearing assignment (`DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE`, `MINTER_ROLE`, `PAUSER_ROLE`, `UPGRADER_ROLE`, custom roles) the key holds, every multisig of which the key is a signer, every proxy whose admin slot points at an address in the key's control, every Permit2 / `setApprovalForAll` allowance the key has been granted on user-side. The output is a single graph: nodes are addresses (keys, contracts, multisigs), edges are typed authority relations (signer-on, admin-of, role-bearer-on, allowance-from), and the graph is queryable by key.

The defensive principle is that the privilege held by a key is structural information about *what becomes exploitable if that key is disclosed*. Without an authority graph, "the key was compromised" is an under-specified incident: the responder doesn't know which contracts to pause, which multisigs to remove the key from, or which allowances to revoke before the attacker exercises them. With an authority graph, the response becomes an enumeration: walk every edge from the compromised key, rotate or revoke each privilege in priority order, watch each downstream contract for exercise during the window the rotation is in flight.

This mitigation is *load-bearing for rotate-on-disclosure* — the canonical Wintermute-2022 lesson, where a vanity-address key compromise was discoverable in advance, the privilege held by that key was knowable from chain state, but no operator-side authority graph existed to make the priority-order rotation legible until after the attacker had exercised it. The graph must exist before the disclosure event; reconstructing it under incident-response time pressure is too late.

## How it applies

- **OAK-T1.003 (Renounced-but-not-Really):** the authority graph for a token contract's deployer key surfaces every retained role and proxy-admin path *that survives surface-level renouncement claims*; this is the operator-side counterpart to OAK-M01's source-bytecode-binding approach to the same question.
- **OAK-T1.004 (Blacklist / Pausable Weaponisation):** the authority graph identifies who holds the blacklist-setter and pause-toggler roles; defender-side decisions to treat such roles as elevated risk hinge on the graph's correctness.
- **OAK-T9.003 (Governance Attack):** the authority graph of governance-token-holding keys (and the multisigs they sign on) surfaces the concentration that governance-attack analyses depend on; without the graph, the attack is invisible to per-address holding metrics.
- **OAK-T9.004 (Access-Control Misconfiguration):** the graph makes the misconfiguration legible at the *system* level — a key that holds `DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE` on a contract that should have been renounced is visible as a graph edge, regardless of whether the original deployment script intended to leave it.
- **OAK-T11.001 (Third-Party Signing Vendor Compromise):** when a custody vendor is breached, the authority graph for keys held in that vendor's infrastructure is the rotation playbook — *every privilege those keys hold must be rotated*, and the graph is what makes the enumeration possible. Wintermute is the canonical case study.
- **OAK-T11.002 (Wallet Software Distribution Compromise):** for end-user wallets compromised via the distribution channel, an authority graph at the user level (allowances held, smart-account permissions delegated, NFT `setApprovalForAll` grants) drives the revocation playbook in the same way the institutional authority graph drives institutional rotation.
- **OAK-T11.003 (Multisig Contract Manipulation):** the authority graph captures every multisig a given key is a signer on; on disclosure, the graph identifies which multisigs need a `removeOwner` proposal queued and on what priority order.

## Limitations
